game_explanation:
This is a long (23-round) public goods game with 4 players. Each round everyone either keeps all 20 coins (contributes 0) or leaves them all in the shared pot (contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split evenly, so cooperative contributions benefit the group but can be exploited by free-riders. Punishment is available and costly: sending 1 punishment unit costs the sender 1 coin and removes 2 coins from the target. Players see how much others spent on punishment (but not who targeted whom), and the game has a known finite length, so forward-looking incentives (and endgame defections) matter.

Likely dynamics: some players (GORILLA, DUCK) are predisposed to cooperate, at least early on; others (SLOTH, CHICKEN) are more likely to defect or be conditionally cooperative. Early rounds will test cooperation: cooperators may invest (20) while free-riders keep 0. Small, targeted punishment is the main enforcement tool: cooperators will punish defectors occasionally to signal that defection is costly. Because punishment is itself costly, it will be used sparingly. Periods of broad cooperation can appear after punishments, but repeated temptation and the finite horizon will slowly erode cooperation toward the end. In the final rounds, expect more defection because future punishment is less effective. Chat messages, when present, will be short: requests for cooperation, reminders about punishment, or short reactions.

game_explanation: This public-goods game lasts 29 rounds with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round players decide how much of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (their contribution). The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, creating a tension between private withdrawal (immediate private benefit) and group benefit from contributing. Because contributions are observed only after decisions, players form expectations about others’ cooperativeness; conditional cooperators tend to match others' contributions, while freeriders withdraw more. Rewarding is available but costly (3 coins to give 2 to a target), so players use it sparingly to reinforce cooperation or reciprocate helpful behavior. Uncertainty about others’ motives plus the known finite horizon (29 rounds) typically produces an early willingness to cooperate followed by gradual decline as end-game approaches; targeted rewards can stabilize cooperation for a while but are rare and modest. Below is a plausible transcript showing these incentives play out: moderate contributions early, occasional small rewards to encourage cooperation, intermittent recoveries when players reciprocate, and a late-game drift toward defection.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with four players. Each round each player either keeps all 20 coins (contribution = 0) or leaves all 20 coins in the pot (contribution = 20). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally. After payoffs from the pot are allocated, players may pay to punish or reward others; punishment is costly to the sender (3 coins per unit) and costly to the receiver (7 coins per unit), while reward costs the sender 3 but gives the receiver +4 per unit. Players do not see others' choices before deciding, and the game is finite (6 rounds).

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often start mixed as players test each other. Free-riders gain more immediately because they keep 20 plus their share of the pot; cooperators earn less unless many others also contribute.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly in typical games: cooperation is enforced by occasional punishments for free-riding and occasional rewards for cooperation. Because punishment is costly to both sides, it tends to be used mainly to signal disapproval and to shift behavior in future rounds.
- Players who are punished often switch to contributing in later rounds to avoid further penalties; some players may still test cooperation intermittently. In the final round, backward induction reduces the incentive to punish (no future rounds to influence) and cooperation often falls off, though some players still punish out of spite or fairness motives.
- Uncertainties: we can't observe private beliefs or emotions. How hard players punish, whether they prefer reward over punishment, and how quickly they reciprocate cooperation are unpredictable. The personas provide priors (e.g., CHICKEN—postgrad—may enforce norms; SLOTH—name suggests laziness—may test free-rides), but they are not deterministic.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with three players over six rounds. Each round every player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Individually, each contributed coin returns only 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 to its contributor — less than 1 — so the dominant selfish action is to contribute 0. However, group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes more. Typical dynamics: some initial cooperation from more pro-social players, exploitation by freeriders (who gain higher round payoffs), then downward drift as cooperators reduce contributions in response. Messaging can sustain cooperation if credible, but anonymous or unverifiable claims may not fully deter free-riding. Given the personas: GORILLA (more cooperative) will tend to contribute relatively more and try to coordinate; SLOTH (low effort) will under-contribute or free-ride; DUCK (moderate) will respond to others and sometimes reciprocate cooperation. Expect early moderate contributions, some exploitation by SLOTH, attempts at coordination by GORILLA, and modest fluctuations across rounds with a slight end-round uptick as players try to raise final-round payoffs.

game_explanation:
This three-person repeated public-goods game pits private incentives (keep 1 coin per coin contributed) against group incentives (each coin contributed is doubled and split among three, yielding 2/3 coin back to each player). Without binding agreements, we typically see some initial willingness to contribute (especially from conditional cooperators), followed by opportunistic reductions by free-riders and occasional targeted punishment to sustain cooperation. Punishment is costly to the sender (1 coin per unit) and reduces a target's payoff (2 coins per unit), so it is used sparingly — mainly by players who value fairness or want to deter persistent free-riding. Uncertainty about who will punish, and the limited visibility (players see totals but not punishers) also moderates punishment frequency.

Given the personas:
- SLOTH (young male) is biased toward lower contributions and opportunistic play; he may under-contribute periodically.
- GORILLA (older male) is steady and modestly cooperative but unlikely to aggressively punish.
- DUCK (older female) is more of a conditional cooperator — she contributes relatively high early and will occasionally punish clear under-contributors to protect returns.

Expect contributions to fluctuate: DUCK tends to lead with higher contributions; SLOTH will occasionally free-ride and get targeted punishment; GORILLA will mostly follow the group average and punish only rarely. Punishment events will be occasional, targeted at SLOTH when he under-contributes, and of modest magnitude (1–3 units).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This 4-player public goods game creates a classic tension: each coin a player keeps privately yields 1 immediately, but each coin contributed returns only 2.1/4 = 0.525 to each player on average. Because 0.525 < 1, an individual who cares only about their own immediate payoff has an incentive to withdraw everything (contribute 0). Repeated interaction and the ability to reward others can sustain contributions: conditional cooperators will increase contributions when they see others contribute and when they receive rewards; free-riders can be disciplined indirectly by withholding rewards. Uncertainty (players do not observe choices before deciding) tends to make first-round behavior driven by priors/personality; as rounds progress players update beliefs based on revealed contributions and small, costly rewards. Near the final rounds a typical "end-game" decline in cooperation is likely because incentives to build future reciprocity vanish. In this transcript I use the provided personas as priors: CHICKEN and GORILLA are more likely to contribute strongly and to reward cooperators, SLOTH is slower-to-start but responsive to positive signals, and DUCK is more likely to low-contribute/free-ride. Rewards are predicted to be sparse and small (1–2 units), mostly targeting consistently high contributors; withholding rewards serves as the primary negative signal.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (each player keeps any coins they withdraw from the shared pot) against a group benefit (the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally). The marginal per-capita return on each contributed coin is 1.8/4 = 0.45, so any single coin is privately worth 1 to the withdrawing player but socially yields 0.45 to the player — creating a strong temptation to free-ride. Because players can punish (costly to the sender, harsher to the target) and reward (also costly to the sender but beneficial to the target), enforcement and encouragement are possible but costly.

Given the four personas:
- SLOTH looks likeliest to start as a low contributor and respond slowly to social pressure (a "slacker").
- GORILLA is moderate and responsive but may drift downward as cooperation erodes.
- DUCK and CHICKEN are more cooperative and likelier to reward cooperators and occasionally punish persistent low contributors.

Typical unfolding I predict:
- Early rounds: DUCK and CHICKEN contribute strongly; SLOTH contributes little; GORILLA sits in the middle. Small, targeted punishments at first discourage blatant free-riding; small rewards encourage high contributors.
- Middle rounds: Because of punishment/reward and conditional cooperation, group contributions climb or stabilize at a moderate-to-high level for a while. SLOTH increases contributions after receiving sanctions or encouragement.
- Late rounds: Finite-horizon concerns and gradually realized temptation cause gradual decay in contributions near the end; punishment becomes rarer (because it's costly and retaliation/endgame reduces its deterrent value). Cooperation decreases but typically not to zero — there is some persistence from those valuing the group payoff or reputation.

Uncertainties: exact magnitudes of punishment/reward, individual responsiveness to sanctions, and whether players use reward more than punishment. I keep punishments and rewards sparse and targeted, consistent with most experimental PGG behavior.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players and 23 rounds. Each round each player either keeps all 20 coins for private use (contribution = 0) or puts the full 20 in the shared pot (contribution = 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally. Individually, withdrawing (defecting) always yields a higher payoff in that round regardless of others' choices (dominant strategy), but if everyone contributes every round, all players earn much more collectively (50 vs. 20 per round). Because players can send messages, brief cooperation can be established early, but the one-shot incentive to defect and the finite horizon (23 rounds known) make cooperation fragile. Expect initial cooperation attempts, a likely early break when someone defects to gain extra payoff, and then cycles of attempted rebuilding and exploitation. As the game proceeds and the end approaches, cooperation is less likely to persist; by the final round players are most likely to defect.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public‑goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN), 10 rounds, and monetary incentives for contribution, punishment, and reward. Each round each player decides how many of 20 coins to leave in the shared pot; those coins are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. Withholding coins increases private payoff but lowers the group return. Players can subsequently punish or reward specific peers: punishments are costly to the punisher (1 coin per unit) and costly for the target (3 coins per unit), while rewards cost the giver 1 coin and benefit the target 1 coin.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: people often start with moderate or generous contributions since expectations about others’ cooperation are uncertain. Conditional cooperators will match others over time.
- Free‑riding (low contribution) tends to invite sparse but targeted punishment from those who contributed more, because punishment preserves future cooperation despite its cost.
- Rewards are used even more sparingly than punishment, typically to reinforce generous behavior and build trust.
- Over time, contributions may stabilize if players reciprocate, but occasional defections or end‑game free‑riding (final rounds) are common.
- Uncertainties: players don’t observe others’ real‑time choices and can only react after rounds. Punishment is costly, so it will be used judiciously — enough to deter blatant free‑riding but not so often that punishers destroy their own payoffs.

Predicted behavioral pattern for these four players given the personas:
- SLOTH (30–49, female): starts somewhat low, but responds to punishment by increasing contributions; will sometimes punish obvious free‑riders lightly.
- GORILLA (18–29, female): tends to contribute relatively high and occasionally uses small rewards to encourage others.
- DUCK (30–49, male, postgraduate): leans toward higher contributions and enforces cooperation by occasional punishment when exploited.
- CHICKEN (30–49, male): mixed pattern — will reward cooperators occasionally but may experiment by withdrawing more in some rounds.

Below is a simulated, coherent transcript for all 10 rounds consistent with those tendencies. Numbers are shown to two decimals where redistribution produces non-integer payoffs.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated three-player public goods game (16 rounds). Each round each player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20 from their 20-coin endowment. The group sum is doubled and split equally across the three players, so each player's round payoff = (20 − own contribution) + (2 × total contributions / 3). The dominant one-shot incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride) because each coin you put in returns only 2/3 of a coin to you personally, while benefiting others. Over repeated interactions, two opposing forces shape behavior: (1) reciprocity and conditional cooperation — players who care about joint outcomes or fairness (or who are reputationally motivated) tend to contribute more early and sustain cooperation if others reciprocate; (2) exploitation and endgame effects — free-riding is individually profitable, and visible free-riding in previous rounds tends to erode contributors’ willingness to give, especially as the final rounds approach.

Uncertainties: we don't observe private motives; players only see past summaries (contributions and net payoffs) at the end of each round. That creates strategic inference: generous early contributors (Gorilla, Duck in their profiles) may try to sustain cooperation, while a more self-interested or “slothful” player (Sloth) may contribute little and reap outsized rewards early, prompting retaliation. A typical empirical pattern is moderate-to-high contributions early, then a gradual decline as conditional cooperators reduce giving in response to free-riding and approaching the endgame. With the given personas (SLOTH lower on contribution tendencies; GORILLA and DUCK more cooperative initially), I predict an initial cooperative move by Gorilla and Duck, Sloth under-contributes, and subsequent rounds show a general downward drift with occasional small cooperative attempts.

That creates strategic inference: generous early contributors (Gorilla, Duck in their profiles) may try to sustain cooperation, while a more self-interested or “slothful” player (Sloth) may contribute little and reap outsized rewards early, prompting retaliation. A typical empirical pattern is moderate-to-high contributions early, then a gradual decline as conditional cooperators reduce giving in response to free-riding and approaching the endgame. With the given personas (SLOTH lower on contribution tendencies; GORILLA and DUCK more cooperative initially), I predict an initial cooperative move by Gorilla and Duck, Sloth under-contributes, and subsequent rounds show a general downward drift with occasional small cooperative attempts.

Early rounds often establish norms: cooperative players signal or contribute a lot (to get the group return), while a smaller number may free-ride. Because punishment is costly to the punisher (4 coins per punishment unit) but inflicts a larger loss on the target (12 coins per unit), players typically punish sparingly — enough to signal that free-riding will be costly, but not so much that punishers lose too much. game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and 23 rounds. Each round every player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot or withdraws 20 for private use (so contribution is 0 or 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally among the four players, so contributors forgo private 20 but boost everyone's shared return. After redistribution, players can spend coins to punish others (1 coin cost to sender, 2 coins deducted from the target). Players cannot see choices before deciding, but they do see a round summary afterward (their own net payoff, what they spent on punishment, and what they lost to punishment; summaries of peers are also shown, though punishers are anonymous).

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- The marginal per-player return on a 20-coin contribution: with 4 players, each 20 contributed yields 50 total (20*2.5) so each player gets 12.5. A contributor loses 20 privately but causes everyone (including themself) to get +12.5, so individually contributing reduces their private payoff by 7.5 relative to free-riding. Collective welfare is highest when everyone contributes, but individually there's an incentive to free-ride.
- With punishment, cooperators can deter free-riding by imposing costs on defectors. Early rounds typically see testing behavior and light punishment targeted at visible defectors. If cooperators coordinate on punishing, contributions can stabilize. Toward the final rounds, the "endgame" effect makes cooperation harder to sustain — players anticipate fewer future repercussions and often defect more.
- Uncertainties: players don't know others' private tendencies, so initial rounds are exploratory. Punishment messages are anonymous, so players know they were punished but not by whom; this can create deterrence (fear of being punished) but also resentment and occasional retaliatory punishing.

Predicted broad pattern for these 23 rounds:
- Early rounds: Gorilla and Duck act as stable cooperators, punishing Sloth (who leans to free-ride). Chicken initially wavers but moves toward contributing after being punished, then later drifts back.
- Middle rounds: periods of stable cooperation (sometimes everyone contributes), punishment becomes less frequent once norms stabilize.
- Late rounds: cooperation unravels (especially in the final rounds), so contributions collapse and punishment fades because many expect others to defect and there's less incentive to spend to punish.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 3 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and decides privately how many coins to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the three players, so each coin you personally contribute returns 1.9/3 ≈ 0.63 to you. That is less than 1, so the individually rational (dominant) choice is to contribute 0 each round. However, the group earns more if players contribute, so there is a social dilemma: cooperative contributions increase everyone’s total payoff, but they can be exploited by free riders.

Typical dynamics here: some initial cooperation is common if players are willing to trust one another or make explicit promises/messages. If some players free-ride early and get higher individual payoffs, trust erodes and contributions tend to decline over rounds (the “end‑game” effect is especially strong because the game has a known finite horizon of 6 rounds). Communication can help sustain cooperation early, but it rarely prevents decline over all rounds. Uncertainties include beliefs about others’ preferences, noise in stated intentions versus actions, and how much weight players give future rounds.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game (PGG) lasts 16 rounds with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone gets 20 coins and privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a public pot. The pot is doubled and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 2/3 of a coin to its contributor — beneficial for the group but individually costly. After returns are distributed, players can spend coins to punish others: each punishment unit costs the punisher 1 coin and removes 2 coins from the target. Punishment is anonymous in the summaries, which typically makes retaliatory dynamics noisy.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others leads to moderate contributions as players test each other. Some players with a cooperative disposition (here DUCK) will contribute relatively high amounts; others (SLOTH or GORILLA) may be more cautious or opportunistic.
- Mid game: without enforcement, contributions often drift downward because the private marginal return (2/3) is less than 1. If someone persistently contributes much less, a motivated player may occasionally punish to enforce norms — but punishments are costly, so they tend to be small and infrequent.
- Late game: end-game effects (knowing the game ends) usually lower cooperation further; punishments may continue but are typically rare. Anonymity of punishers makes targeted retaliation less precise; players may reduce contributions rather than punish in return.
- Uncertainties: we can’t know exact motives or risk preferences. Punishment can sustain cooperation if used sparingly by someone willing to pay short-term costs; otherwise contributions decline.

Below is a round-by-round transcript (players listed in the fixed order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK).

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 3 players. Each coin a player contributes costs them 1 privately but only returns 2/3 to them (the group's pot is 2× total contributions split among 3), so the one-shot dominant action is to contribute 0 (free-ride). Over repeated play, cooperation can emerge if players trust each other or punish free-riding, but with a known finite horizon (16 rounds) incentives to defect grow toward the end — players often start with some cooperation, react to others' observed behavior, and drift toward lower contributions as the game proceeds. Uncertainties include not seeing peers' choices before deciding and incomplete information about the others' long-run strategies or tolerance for exploitation. Given the provided personas (SLOTH: older, postgraduate; GORILLA: older, college-educated; DUCK: younger, college-educated), I predict moderate early contributions, occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation after seeing exploitation, and overall decline toward zero by the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a finite (23-round) public goods game with 4 players. Each round every player effectively chooses whether to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment to the group project (contribute = 20) or withdraw it for private use (contribute = 0). The group total is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally. That means each contributed 20 yields 50 added to the group pot and 12.5 to each player’s round payoff. For a given number x of contributors:
- each player’s public share = 12.5 * x;
- a contributor’s net payoff = 12.5*x − 20;
- a non-contributor’s net payoff = 20 + 12.5*x.

The personal incentive is to withdraw (defect): keeping 20 privately plus still receiving the public share beats contributing in almost all cases. The group optimum (all contribute) would yield the highest payoff per person (x=4 gives 12.5*4 − 20 = 30 per contributor), but the one-shot dominant strategy is to withdraw. With repeated play and messaging, we can expect:
- Early rounds: at least one or two players who are dispositionally cooperative (DUCK, CHICKEN) will try to contribute and may send messages promising to do so. Selfish players (SLOTH, GORILLA) are likely to free-ride early.
- After observing payoffs, cooperators will quickly see they’re being exploited (contributors get much less than withdrawers). Unless communication credibly enforces cooperation, contributions will fall.
- Over time contributions will decay toward universal defection. Given a known finite horizon (23 rounds), standard backward-induction predicts collapse to all-defect by the end; practically, we’ll likely see cooperation for a few early rounds, then a breakdown and persistent defection for the remainder, with occasional short-lived pleas or attempts to restart cooperation.

Now the round-by-round transcript (players in the order given: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and 23 known rounds. Each round players either contribute the full 20 coins to the group pot or withdraw the full 20 for private use. The shared pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so individual incentives favor withdrawing privately (you keep 20 more than a contributor in the same group), while the group payoff is highest when everyone contributes. Because summaries show each player's net payoff after every round, players can infer exactly who contributed and who defected, enabling conditional strategies: reciprocity, punishment, and promises via chat.

Likely dynamics: early rounds will see attempts to coordinate cooperation (some players will propose "I'll contribute if you do"), but free-riding is tempting. If a single player free-rides and receives a large payoff, others will often punish by defecting the next round or threaten to punish via chat. This tends to create cycles: short bursts of full cooperation, punctuated by free-riding and retaliatory all-defection. Because the horizon is finite and known, cooperation typically unravels toward the end; expect more defection in the last several rounds. Chat will be sparse and mostly used for short promises, calls to punish free-riders, and occasional appeals to fairness.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). In each round each player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from their round allotment of 20. The contributions are pooled, doubled, and split equally; after payoffs are revealed players may spend coins to punish others (1 coin spent removes 2 coins from the target). Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Contributing benefits the group (higher pooled return) but costs the individual immediately; free-riding (low contribution) can yield higher immediate private payoff unless punished.
- Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it tends to be used sparingly to discipline persistent low contributors or to signal a norm.
- Players observe others' contributions and summary payoffs (including how much they spent on punishment and how much was deducted from them), but they do not see who punished them. That reduces direct retaliation and makes punishment a general enforcement tool.
- Given fixed 16 rounds, players may sustain cooperation early/midgame with intermittent punishment of repeat low contributors, and contributions often decline toward the final rounds (endgame effect).
- Uncertainties: exact thresholds of who enforces norms and how much a player tolerates free-riding. Based on the personas, expect GORILLA (college-educated) to be relatively cooperative and willing to punish occasional free-riding; DUCK (moderate) to be conditionally cooperative and sometimes punish; SLOTH (some college) to oscillate between low and moderate contributions, provoking punishment sometimes.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript (contributions listed in SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK order). Each round includes a short explanation, the contributions, any punishment actions (non-zero only), and the round summary as shown to players (net payoff for the round, amount spent on punishment, and amount deducted from them by punishment).

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private short-term incentives (take the 20 coins yourself) against group benefits (leave the 20 in the pot so everyone gets a share). The individual monetary return for contributing is 1.6 × 20 / 4 = 8 — less than the 20 you could keep — so pure self-interest favors withdrawing (contributing 0). However, with 6 repeated rounds and the option to punish (costly to the punisher but harsher to the target) and reward (costly to the rater but beneficial to the target), cooperation can be sustained if cooperators deter free-riding with occasional punishments or support each other with rewards. Because punishment/reward is visible in totals but sources are not revealed to targets, sanctions can deter defectors but make targeted retaliation harder. Expect some early testing (a mix of cooperation and defection), selective punishment of repeated free-riders (sparse punishments), some small rewards to steady cooperators, stabilization around cooperation mid-game, and erosion of cooperation in the final round as the future disappears.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits four identifiable players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) against the standard social dilemma: each round everyone can keep private coins or contribute to a pot that benefits all after multiplication. The immediate incentive is to withdraw (keep) as much as possible (free-riding), but collective payoffs rise if many contribute. Because contributions are revealed before rewards, players can use small costly rewards to reinforce cooperative contributors and withhold rewards (or give none) to low contributors. Rewards are expensive to the giver (3 coins/unit) and only partially beneficial to the receiver (2 coins/unit), so rewards are used sparingly.

Given the personas and common PGG dynamics, I expect:
- GORILLA and CHICKEN to start relatively cooperative and to use rewards to sustain cooperation.
- DUCK to be moderately cooperative and somewhat conditional.
- SLOTH to under-contribute early (free-ride) and rarely reward others.
- Early rounds show conditional cooperation: cooperators reward each other modestly, targeting visible high contributors. If exploitation by SLOTH persists, average contributions drift downward. Midgame may see occasional attempts to revive cooperation by targeted rewards. Near the end (final several rounds) contributions typically collapse as the “endgame” approaches and rewards largely disappear.

There is uncertainty in exact magnitudes and timing of recovery attempts. I model sparse, targeted rewards (mostly 1–2 units) and a gradual decline in contributions with a small midgame bump when cooperators try to prop one another up.

game_explanation:
This three-player public goods game creates a tension between private incentives (keep your 20 coins) and collective benefit (contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split). Because the multiplier < number of players, each coin contributed returns less than a full coin to the contributor personally but can raise group payoff, so conditional cooperation and punishment are typical dynamics.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds: without history, cooperative players (GORILLA, DUCK) will tend to contribute moderately/high; a more self-interested player (SLOTH) may contribute little. That produces a clear free-rider signal.
- Punishment is costly (4 coins per unit) but can deter free-riding because each unit removes 12 coins from a target. Expect sparse, targeted punishment from cooperators when someone clearly free-rides; punishment will be used enough to change behavior but not so much as to be routine every round.
- Conditional cooperation: once punished, a free-rider often increases contributions in later rounds to avoid further punishment, restoring cooperation and reducing future punishment.
- Endgame uncertainty: in the final round, forward-looking incentives to build reputation vanish, so contributions may drop and punishment is less likely (because it’s costly with no future gains), but some players still punish as retribution or fairness enforcement.

We’ll use these priors plus the provided personas: SLOTH (young male, more likely to free-ride), GORILLA (older, college-educated, inclined to cooperate and enforce), DUCK (young, college-educated, conditional cooperator). The transcript below shows plausible contributions, sparse chat, and modest punishment used to enforce cooperation over six rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This repeated public-goods game (6 rounds) puts players in a tension between private incentive (keep the 20 coins) and group benefit (contribute the full 20 so the pot grows and is shared). Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), choices are stark: a cooperator sacrifices immediate private coins for a multiplied group return; a defector secures the private coins and still gets a share of the public pot if others contribute. Rewarding is the only sanctioning mechanism: it is costly to the giver (3 coins per reward unit) but benefits the recipient (4 coins per unit); rewarders are anonymous in the summary. Typical dynamics: early rounds often see some cooperation and small reciprocal rewards among cooperators, while defectors exploit cooperators. If defectors go unpunished, cooperation tends to decline over rounds as players switch to free-riding. Because the game has a known finite horizon (6 rounds), expectation of endgame defection increases in later rounds. Chat is available and may influence norms, but messages can be cheap talk and are not guaranteed to change behavior. I therefore predict initial moderate cooperation by some players, sparse rewarding of cooperators, and a gradual decline in cooperation toward the final rounds with sparse, targeted rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-round public goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round every player effectively decides how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot (contribution = 0–20). The total contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally; this creates a social incentive to contribute (everyone benefits from the public pot) but a private incentive to withdraw coins for oneself (private withdrawal yields full value to the chooser). After payoffs from contributions are computed, players can spend coins to punish or reward specific peers. Punishment is costly to the punisher (1 coin per punishment unit) and removes 3 coins from the punished player per unit; reward costs 1 coin to the giver and gives 1 coin to the recipient.

Likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Early rounds often show moderate contributions as players test others’ willingness to cooperate. Observed low contributors can trigger targeted punishments or withholding of rewards.
- Punishments and rewards tend to be sparse and selective rather than widespread: players usually reserve punishment for clear free-riders and rewards for consistent cooperators.
- Conditional cooperation is common: players tend to adjust contributions after seeing others’ past behavior (increase if peers contribute, decrease if they free-ride).
- Uncertainty stems from heterogeneous preferences and limited sample size: some players may be more selfish, some more pro-social; punishment effectiveness depends on whether others reciprocate or retaliate.
- Over 10 rounds you often see an initial cooperation level that drifts depending on the use of sanctions: well-targeted sanctions and occasional rewards can sustain or even raise contributions; lack of sanctions tends to lead to gradual decline.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with four players. Each round each player either withdraws all 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each contributor increases every player's payout but loses on net privately (a contributor gives up 20 and yields each player 8 from that contribution; net private loss = 12). Individual incentive is to withdraw (defect) while the group is better off if many contribute. Punishment and reward are available after redistribution: a punishment unit costs the sender 3 and removes 7 from the target; a reward unit costs the sender 3 and gives the target +4. Messages are allowed, but choices are simultaneous and players cannot see who targeted them.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players test each other — some signal cooperation, some test by defecting. Cooperators may use sparse punishment to discourage free-riding, and occasional small rewards to reinforce cooperation.
- Middle rounds: if punishment/reward is credible, cooperation can stabilize (many contribute). If punishment is costly to punishers, cooperators might switch to mutual small rewards instead.
- Final round (round 6): backward-induction pressure and the lack of future consequences usually reduce cooperation — we often see a drop in contributions in the last round.
- Uncertainties: individual temperaments (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) may cause deviations: some players may be more forgiving, others more punitive; punishment is costly so it is used sparingly.

game_explanation:
This three-player public goods game pits private incentives (keep 1 coin) against group returns (each coin contributed yields 1.9 total, split across 3 players → 0.6333 return per player per contributed coin). Players start each round with 20 coins, choose an integer contribution 0–20, then may spend costly punishment units (4 coins each) that subtract heavily from targets (12 coins per unit). The game lasts six rounds; information is limited (players don't see others' choices before acting, and punishers are anonymous in summaries).

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds usually feature moderate, conditional cooperation: players contribute some positive amount to test others.
- Deviations (low contributions) tend to trigger targeted punishment, since punishment is the only mechanism to enforce norms here. Punishment is costly and therefore usually sparse and proportionate.
- After being punished, many players increase contributions to avoid future sanctions. Punishment is also reduced toward the end (final-round "endgame") because there's no future enforcement motive; last-round free-riding is more common.
- Chats, when present, are short: requests to cooperate, expressions of frustration after low contributions, or signals of intent to punish/forgive.

The transcript below simulates these tendencies: a mix of steady cooperation, a couple of short defections followed by measured punishment, and a modest endgame decline in contributions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a standard public-goods dilemma. Each coin a player leaves in the shared pot is multiplied by 2.1 and then split evenly across the four players, so each contributed coin returns 2.1/4 = 0.525 coins to its contributor. The individually dominant action is to withdraw all 20 coins (contribute 0), because you keep 1 coin privately versus receiving only 0.525 back per contributed coin. The group-efficient outcome is the opposite (everyone contributes), so cooperation requires trust or incentives.

Expectations and likely dynamics:
- DUCK and CHICKEN (higher-education profiles) are more likely to start relatively cooperative and try to sustain contributions.
- SLOTH and GORILLA are more mixed: they may begin moderately and then drift toward freeriding if others appear to contribute more than they do.
- Because players see contributions only after deciding, early rounds are uncertain; conditional cooperators will adjust based on observed group behavior.
- The reward stage lets players transfer small payoffs to reward cooperators. In practice rewards are used sparingly because they cost the sender 1 coin per unit; expect occasional 1- or 2-unit rewards targeted at visibly high contributors, used to foster reciprocity or signal approval. Rewards will appear most when DUCK and CHICKEN contribute consistently.
- Over ten rounds, a gradual decline in average contributions is likely (conditional cooperation + end-game effects), with occasional small rewards prolonging cooperation for a few rounds before final-round defection or large drop-off.

game_explanation:
This is a finite (10-round) public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player effectively decides how many coins (0–20) to leave in the public pot (their contribution). The pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally among the 4 players, so each coin contributed returns 2.1/4 = 0.525 coins to its contributor. That means the private incentive is to withdraw (contribute) 0 (since 0.525 < 1), while the group as a whole is better off if everyone contributes more (social return 2.1 > 1). Because contributions are simultaneous, players face uncertainty about others’ choices. The reward stage lets players transfer coins to others at a 1:1 rate (cost 1 to giver, +1 to recipient); rewards are visible and can be used to reinforce cooperation, but they are costly to give.

Likely dynamics: initial rounds usually show mixed behavior—some players start relatively cooperative, others free-ride. Small, targeted rewards tend to be used to reinforce higher contributors; rewarded players may keep contributing at similar or higher levels. Because the game is finite and known to end at round 10, cooperation often drifts down in the final rounds (backward induction / end-game effect). Rewards are typically sparse and directed toward clear cooperators; they are unlikely to be used to punish by negative transfers (not available here), so withholding rewards serves as a mild penalty. Uncertainty about others and individual differences (personas) will produce heterogeneity: some players will steadily contribute and reward, others will under-contribute and rarely reward.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 29 rounds and four players. Each round players decide privately how much of 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution) vs. withdraw for private use. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so the social return on a single coin contributed is 0.45 per player (0.45 total for each contributor to themselves) while the private return from withdrawing is 1. That creates the usual tension: the group does best if everyone contributes, but each individual gains more in the short run by withdrawing. Over many rounds, players can use costly punishment (3 coins cost to sender, -4 to target per unit) and costly reward (3 coins cost to sender, +2 to target per unit) to shape behavior.

Likely dynamics: GORILLA is the most consistent contributor and will act as an informal enforcer, occasionally punishing low contributors and sometimes rewarding cooperators; SLOTH tends toward low contributions (the name hints at that), provoking occasional sanctions; DUCK is middle-aged and moderate — sometimes rewarding cooperators; CHICKEN is variable (young male, some college), often drifting between moderate and lower contributions. Punishments and rewards are used sparingly (single-unit actions are most likely). Over time cooperation should fluctuate: occasional enforcement can stabilize contributions somewhat, but some decline or variability is likely. Because players see only their own round summary (their net payoff and punish/reward details about them), responses will be based on those private signals and the visible pattern of who punished or rewarded them.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 23 rounds, four players, and a binary contribution choice each round (contribute all 20 coins to the public pot, or withdraw all 20 privately). The public pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally among the four players, so each contributing player effectively forgoes 20 privately to increase everyone’s shared payout. If n players contribute (n ∈ {0,1,2,3,4}), each player's share from the multiplied pot is 12.5 × n. A defector (who withdraws 20) gets 20 + 12.5×n; a cooperator (who contributes 20) gets 12.5×n.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- There is a strong short-term incentive to withdraw privately (defect) because, holding others’ choices fixed, defecting adds 20 to your round payoff.
- If all cooperate every round, everyone gets a high payoff (50 per round). If all defect, everyone gets 20.
- Expect early rounds to include signals/commitments to cooperate and some exploitation by at least one free-rider. That can trigger punishment (retaliatory defection) and a collapse in cooperation for a few rounds.
- Because messages are allowed, players may coordinate recoveries: conditional cooperators (who respond to others' behavior) are likely to try rebuilding cooperation. The “sloth” persona suggests an individual more likely to shirk intermittently, which can destabilize cooperation. Near the end, standard endgame effects often produce increasing defection.
- Messages will be sparse and mostly strategic: short commitments, calls-out, offers to try cooperating, or final-round remarks.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 10-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round every player effectively decides how much of the 20 coins to keep privately (withdraw) vs. how much to leave in the shared pot (contribution = 20 - withdrawn). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so each player's private withdrawal yields a sure benefit to themselves but every coin contributed benefits the group by 2.1/4 = 0.525 to each player (so total group return 2.1 per coin). The individual incentive is to withdraw (free-ride) because each coin you keep gives you 1 privately but contributes less than 1 to your own return through the public good; however, mutual cooperation (high contributions by all) raises everyone's total payoff.

Uncertainties: players do not observe others' choices before deciding in a round, so early rounds tend to show moderate, cautious cooperation. Over repeated rounds, players form beliefs: cooperative players may reward cooperators, while others may withhold rewards from low contributors. Because rewards are costly (1 coin given = 1 coin lost but 1 coin gained by the target), they are typically used sparingly — mainly to reinforce cooperation or to reward particularly high contributors. With a finite 10-round horizon, there is also an "endgame" temptation to reduce contributions late in the game; conditional cooperators may try to sustain cooperation earlier with small rewards, but we should expect an overall slow decline in contributions and only occasional, small reward transfers.

Below I simulate a plausible transcript consistent with these incentives and the provided personas: SLOTH (tends to freeride), GORILLA (practical/conditional cooperator), DUCK (reciprocal/conditional cooperator), CHICKEN (more cooperative). Rewards are small and infrequent.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round each player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is doubled and split equally. Individually, each coin contributed returns 2/3 of a coin to the contributor (because 2 is split among 3 players), so the one-shot individual incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride), while the group welfare is highest when everyone contributes more. Over 16 rounds, we therefore expect tension between short-term self-interest (contribute little) and longer-run, conditional cooperation or retaliation (adjusting contributions in response to peers' previous behavior).

Using the provided personas as priors: GORILLA (postgraduate) is likely the most conditional/cooperative and strategic — he may start moderate and adjust to reward or punish. DUCK (younger) may begin more idealistic/cooperative but will reduce contributions if repeatedly exploited. SLOTH (30–49, associate degree) is given the label "SLOTH" and is plausibly more inclined to free-ride or make smaller contributions, exploiting others' cooperation. Because players see round summaries (peers' information shown) but cannot communicate, we expect early moderate contributions, then some decline if SLOTH free-rides, with GORILLA and DUCK adjusting (punishing or attempting to restore cooperation). There is uncertainty about exact numerical choices, so the transcript below shows a plausible dynamic: initial moderate cooperation, a period of exploitation and retaliation, then partial recovery and oscillation around moderate contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a 23-round public goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player either withdraws all 20 coins for private use (leaving 0 in the common pot) or withdraws none (leaving 20 in the common pot). The total shared pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally across the 4 players. That creates a tension: if everyone contributes, each receives 50 (2.5 * 80 / 4). But a single defector who takes the 20 privately and others still contribute will earn more that round (e.g., 57.5 when 3 others contribute). Because players move simultaneously, free-riding is tempting; because the game is repeated with a known 23-round horizon, cooperation can be sustained early by promises/messaging but is vulnerable to collapse and to endgame unraveling (players defecting as the last rounds approach). Messaging can help coordinate and sustain cooperation, but it can also reveal intentions and be ignored.

Using the provided personas as priors: SLOTH (older, postgraduate) is likely dispositionally cooperative and patient, often trying to restore norms. GORILLA (young, male) is more likely to test/opportunistically defect once the temptation is clear. DUCK (young, postgraduate) leans cooperative and will try to coordinate, but may punish defectors. CHICKEN (30–49, some college) is pragmatic — will contribute when it seems to work, but will stop if exploited. Expect early cooperation supported by a few messages, one or two defections that trigger retaliation and occasional attempts to rebuild. As the last rounds near, expect a drift toward defection.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either keeps the entire 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leaves them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so contributors help the group but individual incentives favor free-riding: a defector keeps 20 and still shares the multiplied pot. Because the game is finite (6 rounds), we should expect some end-game drift toward defection. Rewards allow positive transfers: each reward unit costs 3 to the sender and gives 4 to the receiver. Because rewards are costly, they are used sparingly — typically to reward cooperators or reciprocate. Players cannot see who rewarded them, but they do see totals (how much they spent on rewards and how much they received). Early rounds often show some cooperation with selective rewarding of cooperators; later rounds tend toward more defection as the horizon approaches. Given the personas, GORILLA (postgraduate) and DUCK (older) are somewhat more likely to sustain cooperation early on; SLOTH may be inclined to free-ride; CHICKEN is uncertain but responsive.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round they choose how much of their private 20-coin endowment to put into a shared pot. The pot is doubled and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 2/3 coin to each contributor privately — less than the private cost of 1 coin. That creates the classic social dilemma: group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes everything, but each individual has an incentive to free-ride.

Punishment is available after contributions: sending one punishment unit costs the sender 1 coin and reduces the target’s payoff by 2 coins. Punishment can deter free-riding and support cooperation, but it is itself costly. Because players do not see who punished them, punishment creates uncertainty about motives and can provoke retaliation or forgiveness.

Typical empirical dynamics I expect here:
- Early rounds: moderate cooperation (non-zero contributions) driven by optimism/conditional cooperation.
- Occasional low contributions by one player (likely GORILLA, given profile priors) trigger sparse, targeted punishments from others — enough to nudge behavior but not continuous heavy sanctioning.
- Punishment is used sparingly (a few 1-unit actions at key moments) because it’s costly and players often hesitate to punish too often.
- Over time contributions tend to drift downward (end-game effect), especially near the final rounds, as the incentive to punish weakens and the future returns to cooperation vanish.
- The overall pattern: moderate contributions with occasional punishments aimed at the player who under-contributes, some short-lived recovery when punishments are effective, and a gradual decline toward lower contributions by the very last rounds.

No one has reason to punish on round 1.

Players see round-1 contributions and small asymmetry (GORILLA gave slightly less). Conditional cooperators (SLOTH, DUCK) increase slightly; GORILLA reduces a bit further. DUCK responds to GORILLA's lower contribution with a small, targeted punishment to signal disapproval. Punishment is small because it's costly and information about punishers is anonymous.

After being punished, GORILLA may feel provoked or defensive and lowers contribution again. SLOTH holds fairly steady; DUCK remains cooperative. Repeated small sanctions are starting to influence GORILLA, who raises contribution modestly to avoid future cost. SLOTH and DUCK increase a bit given the visible minor recovery. No punishments this round as contributions are closer to the group average.

Cooperation stabilizes after a small improvement in GORILLA’s contribution. Players see the benefit of modest mutual contributions and refrain from punishing; they conserve coins while maintaining a cooperative level.
Another stable round: SLOTH and DUCK continue to be slightly more generous; GORILLA maintains a middling contribution. No reason to punish as discrepancies are small.

Mid-game restlessness: GORILLA drops contribution noticeably (tempted to free-ride), testing others’ willingness to enforce. SLOTH and DUCK respond to this deviation with clear but limited punishment intended to signal that under-contributing will be costly.
Punishments in round 8 push GORILLA back up modestly. Players move toward re-establishing cooperative behavior; no one punishes this round as contributions are more balanced.

As the horizon shortens, contributions begin drifting down — players discount future enforcement. GORILLA drops again a bit; SLOTH uses a small punitive action to deter persistent free-riding. DUCK holds steady but conservative.

End-game effects grow: all players lower contributions further. DUCK still briefly punishes GORILLA for repeated low contributions, but overall punishment frequency is decreasing as players prepare for the final rounds.
With only a few rounds left, cooperation declines further; players cut contributions to save coins for immediate payoff. No punishments this round because the expected future reciprocity is weaker.

The downward trend continues. Players expect little long-term benefit from sustaining the public good, so contributions drop. No one invests in punishment because it’s expensive and unlikely to change behavior near the end.

Very late phase: most players reduce contributions further. GORILLA contributes minimally. No punishments — players conserve coins rather than spend them on costly sanctions with little future payoff.

One round to go: near-universal decline. SLOTH gives a token contribution; GORILLA contributes very little. SLOTH registers one final small punitive action aimed at signaling disapproval, though its deterrent effect is small so late.
Final round: standard end-game collapse. There is no point in punishing because there's no future to influence, so all players free-ride fully and refrain from punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK), a small multiplication factor (2) and costly, pairwise punishment (1 to sender costs, 2 deducted from target per punishment unit). Typical dynamics: many players start with moderate contributions; some participants (here GORILLA and DUCK) are more willing to sustain contributions and occasionally use punishment to discipline low contributors. A player who contributes little (SLOTH early on) may be targeted by light punishment and then raise contributions. Over 16 rounds with a known end, cooperation often drifts downward toward the final rounds (end‑game effect). Punishment is costly and used sparingly to avoid eroding the punisher's payoff. There is uncertainty about exact moves: punishments are usually small and intermittent, and responses to punishment can be gradual rather than immediate.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round repeated public goods game (PGG) with three players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a group pot. The pot is doubled and split equally among the three players, so each player's round payoff = 20 - own contribution + (2 * total contributions) / 3.

Key incentives and likely dynamics:
- Individual incentive: the private return to putting one coin in the pot is 2/3 of a coin, so keeping a coin yields a higher private payoff than contributing it (2/3 < 1). That creates a strong immediate incentive to free-ride (contribute 0).
- Social incentive: higher total contributions increase the shared return for everyone. If others contribute a lot, it's beneficial collectively; but each player still gains less individually per coin contributed than they cost.
- Repeated play and information: players see round summaries (their own net payoff and corresponding information of peers). This allows conditional strategies: conditional cooperators tend to match observed group contribution levels; some may reduce contributions if they see free-riding; others may maintain cooperative norms. Uncertainty about others' types (selfish vs conditional cooperator vs altruist) will shape early rounds.
- Typical pattern: often moderate-high contributions early (hopeful cooperation), then decline as free-riding spreads; sometimes slight end-game increases if players choose to boost final-round payoffs since future punishment is irrelevant.
- Applying the personas as priors: SLOTH (older, possibly more conservative) is likely to contribute less and gravitate toward free-riding. GORILLA (younger) may be conditionally cooperative — willing to contribute moderately but responsive to others. DUCK (postgraduate) may start more cooperative/altruistic but will reduce contributions if others free-ride.

Below is a simulated transcript from round 1 through round 16, round-by-round explanations followed by predicted integer contributions (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK) and the round summary (each player's contribution and net payoff for that round). Payoffs are shown to two decimal places.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution 0–20 to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Because 1.9/3 < 1, an individual who contributes loses expected private payoff on each coin (private return < private cost), so the one-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, with repeated interaction and social preferences, players often start by contributing some amount and then reduce contributions over time when they observe others free-riding. Here players can send messages to coordinate or signal intentions, but messages are non-binding and visible to others before choices are made.

Likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Round 1: Expect modest-to-moderate contributions as players test each other. A player with cooperative leaning may propose a plan; others may tentatively match or free-ride a bit.
- Middle rounds (2–4): If any player benefits from contributing less (higher net payoff) and signals that, others will likely reduce contributions; cooperation typically decays.
- Final rounds (5–6): Because the horizon is known and finite, backward induction encourages defection; contributions often fall toward zero by the last round. Some players with strong social preferences may still give small amounts.
- Uncertainty: Individual tastes (altruism, inequality aversion) and response to messages vary. The transcripts below use the provided personas as priors: SLOTH (postgraduate, may be slightly cooperative but cautious), GORILLA (pragmatic, likely to freeride if it pays), DUCK (college grad, likely to try to sustain cooperation early but will reduce if exploited).

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either contributes the whole 20 (cooperate) or withdraws it (defect). Contributions are multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so a lone defector keeps a private 20 plus a share of the public good — defecting typically gives higher immediate payoff unless most players contribute. Because players can reward others (paying 3 per reward unit to give 4 to a target), cooperators can try to support each other, but rewards are costly and transfer efficiency is limited. Over repeated rounds we expect some initial cooperation from pro-social types (DUCK, CHICKEN), possibly some late switching by GORILLA, and persistent free-riding by SLOTH. Rewards will be used sparingly to shore up cooperators, but they usually won't fully compensate for the advantage of free-riding, so cooperation tends to erode toward the end as players anticipate the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone gets 20 coins and decides simultaneously how many coins (0–20, integer) to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the three players; the private return on each contributed coin is 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633, so the individually rational choice is to contribute 0, while group welfare is maximized by full contribution. After contributions are revealed and payoffs are computed, players can spend costly punishment points: each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and reduces the punished player's payoff by 12. Punishment is anonymous in terms of who applied it (players see totals but not which peer punished them). The game is repeated for 6 rounds and players know the horizon, so cooperation and punishment dynamics can shift (e.g., cooperation tends to decline toward the end). Given the player priors and typical behavior, I expect some initial cooperation by GORILLA and DUCK, SLOTH to start lower, a couple of targeted punishments of clear low contributors (used sparingly because punishment is costly), some recovery in contributions after punishments, and a modest endgame drop in contributions in round 6 because future retaliation is no longer effective. Chat will be sparse and mostly aimed at signaling intentions or reacting to punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players, 6 rounds, and private simultaneous contributions each round. Each player gets 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute (0–20). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.9, and split equally. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 < 1, so each coin contributed reduces the contributor's private payoff on that round (private incentive to free-ride). However, the group as a whole gets more if players contribute, creating a tension between private incentives (contribute 0) and collective benefit.

Common dynamics: with allowed chat and repeated interaction, players often start with positive contributions (trust/experimentation). If someone free-rides, conditional cooperators punish by reducing future contributions, causing a downward drift. Expect some early cooperation, at least one player (SLOTH) to free-ride more often given the persona name and profile, GORILLA (postgrad) to begin cooperative or conditional, and DUCK (younger) to try stabilizing cooperation but adjust in response to others. Because the game ends at round 6, anticipate more defection toward the final rounds (endgame effect). Messages will be sparse and used to signal intent or complain after observed outcomes.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round everyone effectively chooses either to contribute the whole 20 (so contribution = 20) or to withdraw it (contribution = 0). The shared pot (20 per contributor) is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so if k people contribute, each player receives 8*k from the public pot that round. Defection (withdrawing) gives a private 20 plus the public return; contributing sacrifices the private 20 but raises everyone's public share. After payoffs, players can spend resources to punish (cost 3 to sender, -7 to target per unit) or reward (cost 3 to sender, +4 to target per unit). Messages are allowed but choices are simultaneous.

Likely dynamics: early rounds often see a mix of cooperation and defection. Cooperators are sometimes rewarded, and defectors sometimes punished to sustain cooperation. Punishment is typically sparse because it is costly. As the horizon shortens, incentives to defect increase: final round defection often rises. Players who are punished often adjust toward cooperation in later rounds, while occasional testers (conditional cooperators) may defect to see reactions. Chat is used sparingly, mainly to pledge cooperation or call out defections.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 4 players and 29 rounds. Each round, players decide how many of the 20 shared coins to withdraw for private use (so contributions are the remainder in the pot). The public pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally (so each coin contributed returns 0.45 to each player), which means individual incentives favor free-riding (you personally get 0.45 from each coin you put in, < 1), while the group is better off if everyone contributes. Punishment is costly to the sender (3 coins per unit) and harsh to the target (-4 coins per unit); rewards are also costly (3 coins per reward unit) but give the target +2 per unit.

Given these payoffs, typical dynamics: initial rounds often show moderate contributions (optimism/cooperation). Without strong enforcement, contributions drift downward as free-riding pays off. Targeted punishment (used sparingly because it is costly) can sustain higher cooperation among some players; rewards are rarer but can reinforce cooperators. Because players can identify who punished/rewarded them, occasional retaliatory actions may occur. In later rounds, especially the final few, contributions often fall as future retaliation or reciprocity loses force. The four personas (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) suggest a mix of conditional cooperation and some strategic free-riding; expect most punishment and reward edges to be small and infrequent.

Because players can identify who punished/rewarded them, occasional retaliatory actions may occur. In later rounds, especially the final few, contributions often fall as future retaliation or reciprocity loses force. The four personas (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) suggest a mix of conditional cooperation and some strategic free-riding; expect most punishment and reward edges to be small and infrequent.

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a tension between private incentive and group benefit. Each coin a player keeps yields 1 privately, while every coin contributed is multiplied by 1.8 and split among four players (so each contributor gets only 0.45 of their own coin back). That means the individually rational move is to withdraw (free-ride), while the group optimum is higher contributions. Because players do not see current choices before deciding but observe past rounds and who rewarded them, we expect conditional cooperation: some players will contribute more early on, others will free-ride. The reward mechanism (cost 3 to giver, +2 to receiver per unit) allows players to signal approval of high contributors or reward reciprocators, but rewards are costly and therefore used sparingly. Over 29 rounds typical dynamics are: moderate cooperation early, gradual decline (especially as the end-game approaches), occasional targeted rewards that sustain cooperation among a subset (here likely GORILLA and DUCK), and low or inconsistent contributions from habitual free-riders (here SLOTH and sometimes CHICKEN). Uncertainty about others' choices and limited reward budgets keep most rewards small and infrequent.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits individual short-term incentives (keeping coins privately) against a group return from contributing to the shared pot. Each coin contributed yields 1.8 total to the pot but only 0.45 back to each player — so the private incentive is to withdraw (free-ride), while the group optimum is full contribution. Because players do not see others' current contributions before deciding and only see their own round payoff and any directed punishment/reward afterwards, information about others' behavior is noisy. That reduces the effectiveness and frequency of targeted sanctioning; punishments and rewards will therefore be used sparingly and often in response to perceived patterns rather than exact observed contributions. Expect a moderate-to-high initial contribution from inherently cooperative players (DUCK and CHICKEN), more conservative or self-interested choices from SLOTH and GORILLA, gradual drift downward over time as free-riding pays off, and occasional punishments or rewards used to try to correct obvious low/high behavior. Punishments are costly to the source (3 coins per unit) and inflict a larger hit (4 coins per unit) on the target; rewards cost 3 and give 2 — so sanctions tend to be small and infrequent.

This 6-round public goods game will likely show a mix of cooperation and opportunistic free-riding, with punishment used sparingly to enforce contributions. GORILLA (postgraduate) is the most likely to value group returns and to use punishment as an enforcement tool; DUCK (associate's degree) is moderately cooperative and will respond to patterns but avoid excessive punishment; SLOTH (some college, no degree) is most likely to under-contribute initially and adjust only after being penalized. Because punishment is costly (4 coins per unit) and reduces the target a larger amount (12 coins per unit), players will use it only to correct persistent low contributions or to signal that free-riding won't be tolerated. In later rounds, especially the final round, contributions often drop because future retaliation is impossible, so expect some end-game free-riding with limited punishment (players weigh whether it's worth the cost).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This 4-player public goods game pits private extraction (withdraw) against collective benefit: each coin left in the shared pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split evenly. The dominant individual incentive is to withdraw (contribute less) because each coin you keep yields 1 to you, while returning it yields only 2.1/4 = 0.525 to yourself from the public pot. However, higher group contributions increase everyone's payoffs. Players do not see others' choices before contributing, so expectations and observed past behavior drive conditional cooperation. The reward stage lets players transfer coins to others at 1:1 cost, which can be used sparingly to praise cooperators or compensate/reward reciprocity — but sending rewards is costly and typically limited. Personas suggest GORILLA (highly educated) will tend to contribute and reward cooperators; DUCK (older) may be moderately cooperative and reciprocate; SLOTH and CHICKEN (younger, lower education) are more likely to free-ride or behave unpredictably. Over ten rounds we expect some initial cooperation by GORILLA and DUCK, selective rewarding of high contributors, and a gradual decline in contributions as the endgame approaches. Rewards will be infrequent and targeted (few units, usually between GORILLA and DUCK).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and 23 rounds. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws the 20 coins for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so every player's payoff from the pot depends on how many in the group contributed (12.5 × number of contributors to each player). After redistribution, players can spend costly punishment units (1 coin per unit sent) that reduce a target's payoff by 2 coins per unit. Players see only their own round net, how much they spent punishing, and how much they lost to punishment (but not who punished them). The game lasts a finite 23 rounds, so players may respond to punishments early to sustain cooperation but may defect toward the end (endgame effect). Because punishment is costly, we expect sparse use: mostly used to discipline persistent defectors early and restore cooperation; some retaliation or mistaken targeting can cause short flurries of tit-for-tat. With the given personas, SLOTH and CHICKEN lean cooperative and more willing to punish defectors; GORILLA is more likely to defect occasionally and sometimes retaliate; DUCK is pragmatic, mixes behavior and occasionally punishes in response to being defected against. Over the 23 rounds I predict cycles of cooperation being enforced by a few targeted punishments, occasional retaliation or collapse near the end (rounds 21–23) when backward induction reduces incentives to cooperate.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 29 rounds, four players, and a costly reward stage. Because contributions are simultaneously chosen each round and players can only reward after seeing revealed contributions, behavior will be shaped by conditional cooperation and the shortfall between private incentive and group benefit. Multiplication by 1.8 and equal splitting means contributing benefits the group but is individually costly; many participants start fairly cooperative but gradually reduce contributions when others free-ride. Rewarding is costly (3 coins per unit given) and generates less than it costs (2 coins per unit to receiver), so rewards will be used sparingly — mainly to reciprocate or to signal approval for consistently high contributors. Early rounds are likely to show higher contributions from the more pro-social/conditional types (here GORILLA and DUCK), with sporadic reciprocation between them. Over many rounds cooperation will drift downward; rewards will become rarer, concentrated early and occasionally used mid-game for reputation/reciprocity, with very little reward activity late in the 29-round horizon.

game_explanation:
This 4-player online public goods game (PGG) repeats 23 rounds. Each round every player effectively chooses whether to contribute all 20 coins (cooperate) or withdraw all 20 (defect). Contributions are multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so the more cooperators, the higher the per-player return from the common pot. Defectors keep their 20 plus they also share the multiplied pot, so short-run temptation to defect is strong — but when the group punishes defectors (at a cost to the punisher), defectors lose additional coins, which can change incentives.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty. Some players (GORILLA) are inclined to cooperate; CHICKEN looks ready to enforce norms and may punish early defectors; SLOTH, whose name suggests freeriding temptation, will test cooperation by defecting early. DUCK is a conditional cooperator who tends to follow the group’s norms and will sometimes punish.
- Midgame (rounds ~5–18): cooperation can stabilize if punishments deter free-riding. Punishment is used sparingly because it costs the punisher, but a few targeted punishments can sustain high cooperation for several rounds.
- Endgame (last few rounds): backward-induction logic and weariness from costly punishment typically reduce cooperation. Punishment declines as it's seen as futile; many players start defecting in the final rounds.

Uncertainties:
- How much punishment will cooperators invest (how costly for them)?
- Whether SLOTH will adapt to punishment or continue to exploit the group repeatedly.
- Whether CHICKEN’s enforcement is enough to sustain cooperation late into the game.

Full transcript (round 1 → 23):

game_explanation: This is a 10-round repeated public goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round players decide privately how much of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (their contribution); the remaining coins are kept privately. The total contribution is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. After payoffs are realized, players may spend coins to punish (cost 1, remove 3 from the target per punishment unit) or to reward (cost 1, give 1 to the target per reward unit). Because the horizon is finite and known (10 rounds), incentives to free-ride grow toward the end; early and middle rounds typically show more cooperation if people expect reciprocity or use punishment/reward to enforce norms. Punishment is costly to the punisher and thus used sparingly—often to deter repeated low contributions—while rewards are a cheaper way to reinforce high contributions. There is uncertainty each round because players do not observe others' current choices before deciding; they rely on past behavior and short-run incentives. With the personas as priors: SLOTH (name suggests lower effort but educated) will tend to under-contribute relative to others; GORILLA tends to be a reliable high contributor and may pay to enforce cooperation; DUCK is moderately cooperative and uses small rewards; CHICKEN is cautious and may drift between modest cooperation and defection. Expect contributions to start moderate, be supported by occasional small rewards and rare punishments, then slowly decline as the end approaches.
