game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and 23 known rounds. Each round each player either leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws all 20 for themselves (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so each cooperator gives up a private 20 to produce 50 distributed among the group (12.5 per player for each cooperator). Individually, defecting always yields 20 more than cooperating in the same round, so the one-shot incentive is to defect. Group payoff is highest if everyone contributes (each gets 50). In the repeated setting with communication, we usually see some early cooperation sustained by messages and reciprocity, but because the horizon is finite (23 rounds) cooperation typically unravels toward the end (endgame effect). Messaging can slow the decline—players who identify free-riders may try to punish by withholding contributions, or attempt to coordinate cooperative norms. Given the four personas, I expect CHICKEN (strategic, postgraduate) to try to coordinate cooperation early, SLOTH (older, more patient) to be cooperative early on, DUCK to be a conditional cooperator who punishes after being exploited, and GORILLA (young, some college) to be more likely to free-ride. So likely pattern: moderate-to-high cooperation first several rounds, occasional punishment and attempts to re-establish cooperation, and a clear collapse to universal defection as the end approaches.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 10-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player decides how many of the 20 coins to withdraw for private use; the remainder is contributed to the shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.1 then split equally, so the per-player return on each contributed coin is 2.1/4 = 0.525, which is less than 1. That makes full defection (contributing 0) the short-term best response, while contributing more benefits the group overall. Because the game includes a costly reward stage (1 coin spent gives 1 coin to another), players can selectively reward higher contributors and potentially sustain cooperation, but rewards are typically sparse in practice. Knowing the game lasts 10 rounds also creates an end-game incentive to reduce contributions near the end.

Given the player personas and typical behavior patterns:
- Some players (like DUCK) will tend to be relatively cooperative and reward others selectively.
- Others (GORILLA) may free-ride more when it looks profitable.
- SLOTH and CHICKEN are likely conditional cooperators who adjust based on others’ contributions and received rewards.

Expect initial moderate contributions, a few small positive reward transfers to signal approval for higher contributors, and gradual erosion of contributions as free-riding proves profitable — with the largest drop in the final round. Rewards will be small and used sparingly, usually to acknowledge above-average contributions rather than to punish.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with three players for 16 rounds. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is doubled and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players may pay to punish others (each punishment unit costs the punisher 1 coin and removes 2 coins from the punished player's round payoff). Key incentives: contributing benefits the group but costs the individual; punishment is costly but can deter free‑riding. Uncertainties include beliefs about others' conditional cooperation and the untraceability of punishers (players see punishment effects but not who punished them), which makes retaliation imprecise.

Given the three personas: GORILLA (young, postgraduate) is predisposed to be strategic and often cooperative (likely to lead with higher contributions and occasionally punish low contributions). SLOTH (30–49, high school) and DUCK (similar demographics) are more uncertain — SLOTH’s name and profile suggest a tendency toward lower effort or opportunistic moves, while DUCK is likely a conditional cooperator who mirrors others. Expect initial testing behavior (mixed contributions), conditional adjustment in early rounds, occasional low-cost punishment targeted at clear free-riders, and a gradual unraveling of cooperation toward the final rounds (endgame effect), with punishment used sparingly because of its cost.

game_explanation:
This public goods game has a simple but sharp incentive: each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws the whole 20 for private use (contribute 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so a cooperating group earns more total return, but any single defector keeps the 20 private coins and still receives a share of the multiplied pot — creating a strong temptation to withdraw (defect) each round. Punishment after redistribution lets players impose costs on defectors (1 coin cost to the punisher per unit, each unit deducts 2 coins from the target), which can make defection less attractive but also is itself costly to the punishers.

Given the 23-round finite horizon, strategic uncertainty matters. Backward induction predicts mutual defection in the final rounds because punishment is costly and retaliation or reputation effects end with the game. But in practice, small groups often sustain cooperation for many rounds early on through norms and occasional punishment; defections tend to be punished, leading to phases of high cooperation, occasional “tests” where someone defects to see if they can exploit the group, and then renewed punishment or shifting behavior. Player priors suggest Gorilla and Duck (higher education) are more inclined toward cooperation and norm enforcement; Sloth (named persona and lower engagement implied) is more likely to defect occasionally; Chicken is mixed and sometimes tests cooperation.

Uncertainties: how costly and frequent will punishment be? Will punishers sustain the cost long enough to deter repeat defectors? Will the group maintain high cooperation until near the end, or will end-game unraveling begin around the last few rounds? Expect sparse chat (requests, short accusations, apologies) and relatively rare but targeted punishment, concentrated on repeat defectors. As the final rounds approach, expect a modest increase in defections and reduced willingness to pay punishment costs.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and full knowledge of a 23-round horizon. Each round the group pot starts with 20 coins; each player either contributes those 20 (so contributes 20 to the public pot) or withdraws them (so contributes 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally among the four players. That structure creates a social dilemma: if everyone contributes, each gets a large group payoff (each contributor sacrifices 20 privately but receives a larger share of the multiplied pot). However, given others' contributions, any single player can do better by withdrawing (free-riding). With known finite horizon, typical dynamics are:

- Early rounds: some players often try to establish cooperation by contributing or by pledging to contribute. Others may exploit that by withdrawing.

- Endgame: as the final round approaches, the incentive to contribute collapses (backward induction), so contributions typically fall to zero.

Uncertainty here comes from private simultaneous choices each round (players can't see current-round choices before deciding) and from noisy signals about others' intentions (only payoffs are shown, not explicit contributions). With the provided personas, I expect SLOTH to free-ride consistently, DUCK to try to sustain cooperation early, GORILLA to start cooperative but switch to defecting after being exploited, and CHICKEN to cooperate early and punish or give up later. Chat will be sparse and used to plead/coax or to call out free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a standard 4-player public goods game with a multiplier of 2.1 and equal split. Each coin contributed returns 2.1 total to the group, but only 0.525 to each contributor personally (2.1 / 4). That means contributing is socially efficient (group payoff rises when everyone contributes), but each individual's private incentive favors withdrawing (free-riding). The reward stage allows players to transfer coins to specific peers at a cost of 1 per coin; this can be used to reward cooperators and partially offset the private incentive to defect.

Given the personas and the one-shot-per-round structure (no communication), I expect heterogeneous behavior: at least one player (CHICKEN, with a postgraduate background and likely more pro-social priors) will start relatively cooperative and use rewards sparingly to bolster cooperation. One player (SLOTH) is likely to contribute less consistently. The middle two (GORILLA and DUCK) will tend to respond to incentives and to rewards: if they get rewarded for cooperating they will increase contributions; if they are not rewarded they will drift toward freeriding. Over rounds, rewards are used infrequently (1–2 coins at a time) and primarily to reinforce generous contributors. There is uncertainty: players may experiment, or try to "buy" cooperation with small rewards, and a final-round decline (endgame) is likely as the incentive to invest in future reciprocity disappears.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives against a group benefit. Each round each player decides how much of the 20-coin pot to keep (private) and how much to leave as a contribution. Every coin contributed gets multiplied by 1.8 and the multiplied total is split evenly, so each coin contributed returns 0.45 coins to each player. Because 0.45 < 1, a single player maximizes private payoff by withdrawing all 20 coins (contributing 0). But if everyone contributes more, the group earns more overall. The punishment and reward stage creates a second-order enforcement channel: players can pay to reduce others' payoff (punish) or pay to boost others' payoff (reward). Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) but inflicts a larger harm (−4 per unit) on the target; rewards cost 3 to the giver and give +2 to the recipient. These instruments let players try to sustain cooperation but are also costly and can provoke retaliation.

Typical dynamics we expect:
- Early rounds often see moderate-to-high contributions as players "test" each other.
- When someone free-rides sharply, others may respond with a small, targeted punishment to signal disapproval; punishments are usually sparse because they are costly.
- Rewards are used even more sparingly, usually to reinforce consistently high contributors.
- Players can identify who punished/rewarded them, so occasional retaliation is possible, especially after visible punishments.
- As the final round approaches, contributions often decline (endgame effect) because punishment has no future deterrent value.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game puts four players in repeated tension between private gain (withdrawing the full 20 coins) and group benefit (contributing 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so each cooperator increases everyone’s return but personally forgoes the 20 private coins. Punishment lets players spend coins to reduce others’ payoffs (1 coin spent removes 2 coins from a target), which can sustain cooperation by making defection costly — but punishment itself is costly and anonymous (players see how much others spent punishing and how much punishment they received, but not who targeted whom). Because the game has a known, finite horizon (23 rounds), the incentive to defect grows as the end approaches, and we typically expect: some initial cooperation, intermittent defections that get punished, a period of relatively high cooperation if punishment is credible, and a breakdown toward the final rounds as players stop investing in punishment and revert to selfish choices. Individual tendencies from the personas (SLOTH tends to freeride, GORILLA and CHICKEN enforce norms, DUCK is somewhat conditional) make it likely that GORILLA and CHICKEN will punish defectors repeatedly, DUCK will oscillate in response to punishment, and SLOTH will defect more often early and late.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players and six rounds. Each round each player either keeps the 20 coins (contribute 0) or leaves them in the public pot (contribute 20). The public pot is multiplied by 1.6 and shared equally, so each contributor adds 8 coins to each player's round return. The individual incentive each round is to withdraw (defect): keeping 20 plus receiving the benefit from others' contributions usually yields a higher immediate payoff than contributing. The institutional twist is a reward stage after contributions: a player can pay 3 coins per reward unit to give 4 coins to another player. Rewards let players reward cooperators (or indirectly “punish” defectors by withholding rewards). The game lasts 6 rounds (known), so subjects may try to build cooperation early and then anticipate end‑game unraveling.

Likely unfolding and uncertainties:

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players and 23 rounds. Each round starts with 20 coins in the shared pot and every player can either withdraw all 20 (private payoff this round) or leave them in the pot (contribute 20). The total contributed is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally among the 4 players. That means a contributing player gets only the equal share from the multiplied public pot, while a withdrawing player gets that same share plus the 20 they took. Individually, withdrawing (free-riding) strictly dominates contributing in a single round, because keeping 20 always gives you 20 more than contributing while everyone gets the same split of the public pot. Socially, the group does best if everyone contributes (each would get 50 when all contribute).

Because the game is finitely repeated and decisions are perfectly observed only through summary payoffs (not true actions before decisions), there is an incentive to test cooperation early (promises, short messages) but also strong pressure toward defection as players anticipate others' incentives. Expect an early attempt or two at cooperation from at least one or two players; free-riders will benefit and that typically undermines sustainment of cooperation. Chat may contain small promises or complaints early on and then become sparse as rounds proceed and defection becomes common. By the later rounds most players are likely to withdraw (0 contribution) every round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either contributes all 20 (cooperate) or withdraws all 20 (defect). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so defecting gives a private benefit in the same round unless the group punishes you afterward. Punishment is costly to the sender (3 per unit) but harsh on the recipient (-7 per unit). Rewards are also costly (3 per unit) and provide modest gains to recipients (+4 per unit). Players know there are 6 rounds, so early rounds often see more cooperation sustained by the threat of punishment; defections sometimes arise in later rounds (endgame effect). Chats are possible and can signal intent or shame, but choices remain simultaneous. Because punishment/reward are costly, players tend to use them sparingly—punish to deter repeated free-riding and reward to encourage cooperation. Expect an initial mix, movement toward cooperation if punishments deter defectors, occasional targeted rewards, and some opportunistic defections near the end.

game_explanation: This public goods game pits four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) against a repeated choice: keep the 20 coins privately (defect) or leave them in the group pot (cooperate). With 1.6 multiplication and equal splitting, a single cooperator helps the group but is vulnerable to free-riders. Because the game lasts six rounds and players can punish or reward after seeing contributions (but not who exactly took those actions), early rounds often feature mixed behavior while players probe each other's willingness to enforce cooperation. Punishment is costly to the sender but can deter repeated defection; rewards are also costly but can reinforce cooperation. Toward the final rounds (finite horizon), incentives to defect rise because reputation benefits disappear; punishment tends to decline in influence, so defections often increase in the last rounds. Given the provided personas, I expect DUCK and CHICKEN to be relatively cooperative and somewhat willing to use small incentives; SLOTH to be more likely to defect sometimes; GORILLA to test aggressive/competitive options (defecting at times) and potentially resist sustained punishment unless it becomes costly.

This is a 6-round repeated binary public goods game with 10 players. Each round each player can either contribute all 20 coins or keep them; the group pot multiplies contributions by 6.2 and divides the result equally among the 10 players. Because the per-coin return to the group (6.2/10 = 0.62) is less than 1, contributing is costly to the individual in every round even though collective welfare is higher when more people contribute. That creates a tension: socially beneficial mutual contributions are undermined by the temptation to free-ride.

Typical dynamics in such games: some players start by contributing (trust/other-regarding preferences), but they get exploited because non-contributors receive a higher round payoff. Seeing that exploitation often leads conditional cooperators to reduce contributions in later rounds, producing a downward trend. Occasional appeals or reminders can temporarily increase contributions, but the endgame (final rounds) usually sees widespread defection because there is no future punishment or reciprocity.

Given the player personas, I predict a moderate initial cooperation from a subset (those with more pro-social priors), early exploitation by some free-riders, subsequent decline in contributions as contributors adapt, a few scattered attempts to revive cooperation mid-game, and near-universal defection in the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute; contributions are multiplied by 3.8 and shared equally. After contributors are revealed, players may spend coins to punish or reward specific others (punish: source pays 3 per unit, target loses 12 per unit; reward: source pays 3 per unit, target gains 2 per unit). Players do not know how many rounds remain, so they balance immediate payoff against establishing norms. Common unfolding dynamics:

- Early rounds: moderate contributions as players test one another.
- Free-riding (low or zero contributions) appears occasionally because it boosts a single-round private return; when visible it often elicits sparse punishment from some players.
- Punishment is used sparingly because it’s costly, but a few punishers can reduce repeated free-riding. Punishment can provoke retaliation in subsequent rounds.
- Rewards are rarer than contributions/punishments, used to encourage cooperative behavior or mend relations.
- Over many rounds groups often oscillate between cooperation and occasional defections; if cooperation is re-established, rewards and few punishments sustain it.
- Because players don’t know when play ends, end-game collapse is less predictable, but last-round free-riding is common if players infer a late-stage decline — we model a sharp drop in the final round.

Below is a round-by-round transcript (26 rounds). I include brief round explanations, sparse chat lines, each round's contribution vector (in the exact player order: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT), the non-zero punishment / reward actions as tuples (source, target, unit) with positive = reward, negative = punishment, and a summary shown to players (net payoff and action receipts/costs).

game_explanation: This public-goods game has a tight tension: each player privately decides whether to keep the full 20 coins (contribute 0) or to leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among the 7 players. Individually, withdrawing (free-riding) always yields more private return when others contribute, because 2.6 < 7; collectively, everyone is better off if many contribute. Players cannot see others' choices before deciding, and they do not know how many rounds remain, so early rounds typically feature testing (some cooperate, some free-ride). The reward stage (cost 2 to giver, +2 to recipient per unit) allows cooperators to reward each other (supporting cooperation) but is costly and thus used sparingly in practice. Expect sparse chat, a small set of reward transfers focused on supporting cooperators, and a pattern where a core of players sustain cooperation while some players consistently free-ride. Over rounds, cooperation may ebb and flow as players respond to observed payoffs and rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players who each choose either to keep the full private amount (withdraw 20 → contribute 0) or to leave the full 20 in the public pot (contribute 20). The public pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so an individual who defects (keeps 20) gains the private 20 plus an equal share of the multiplied pot; a cooperator (contributes 20) gives up the private 20 but still gets the shared return. Rewards are the only peer sanctioning mechanism: each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Because reward is costly to the sender and gives equal benefit to the recipient, rewards do not change the group’s total but serve as social signaling and reinforcement.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds show uncertainty: a small core of conditional cooperators will contribute, while some players free-ride. Cooperators tend to reward other cooperators modestly (1 unit) to signal approval.
- If rewards and messages reinforce cooperation, contribution rates can rise over several rounds as conditional cooperators join.
- Toward the middle of the sequence, cooperation can become widespread, with reward use becoming sparse (cooperation sustained through norms rather than continuous rewarding).
- Near the end of a short sequence, anticipation of the game ending (even if players don’t know the final round count) often triggers an end-game decline in cooperation: some players defect, reducing group returns. Rewards remain sparse and mostly appreciative rather than punitive.
- Chats are rare and used to encourage, thank, or complain about free-riding.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game (6 rounds) pits the short-run incentive to free-ride against the long-run benefit of mutual cooperation. Each round players decide privately whether to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally, so contributing increases everyone’s payoff but leaves the contributor worse off relative to a free-rider that round (a free-rider gains the same share without paying 20). Because punishment is available after contributions are revealed (costly to the punisher, costly to the punished), players who value future cooperation or fairness may punish free-riders to deter shirking. Early rounds are uncertain—some will test cooperation, some will free-ride. If punishments are sufficiently frequent and targeted, cooperation tends to rise in middle rounds. In the final round, with no future repercussions, some players are likely to defect again (the "end-game" effect), and punishers often restrain themselves because there’s no longer a future to protect. Chats tend to be sparse and used to signal intent, complain, or retaliate.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits private short-term incentive (keep your 20 each round) against group benefit (contribute 20 to a pot that is multiplied and shared). Because the multiplier (6.2) makes the group return on each 20 contributed larger than 20 when many contribute, cooperative clusters can yield higher group payoffs, but an individual who defects still gets a higher private payoff within the same round. Punishment allows cooperators to impose a cost on free-riders at a cost to themselves, which can sustain cooperation across rounds — especially early on — but punishment is costly and loses deterrence in the final round when there is no future. Expect initial uncertainty and mixed play, selective punishment by cooperators to enforce contributions, improving cooperation mid-game, and some end-game retreat to free-riding (and a bit of spiteful punishment) in round 6. Chat will be sparse: commitments, calls for contribution, and short reactions after punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game over six rounds with 10 players. Each round everyone privately chooses 0 or 20 to put into a pot that is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all players. A contributor sacrifices their private 20 in hopes the multiplied pot (shared by everyone) returns them more than 20 in collective benefit; a non-contributor keeps 20 and also benefits from others' contributions. Early rounds are characterized by uncertainty: some players (especially those with postgraduate backgrounds) are more prone to try cooperating to test reciprocity, while others (younger or less trusting profiles) may free-ride. If contributors are exploited (they earn less than non-contributors), contributions tend to fall in subsequent rounds. Communication can sustain cooperation if believable commitments are made and honored, but in the final round standard strategic logic predicts many will defect because there's no future punishment or reward.

Expect initial partial cooperation (a mix of contributors and free-riders), then decline as free-riding is observed, occasional attempts to coordinate recovery, and an erosion of cooperation toward the final round — often culminating in widespread defection in round 6. Chat will be sparse: a few pledges, disappointments, and short coordination attempts.

game_explanation:
This public goods game will likely start with mixed contributions: some players contribute moderately, others a bit less. Small acts of punishment and reward early on (used sparingly) will signal social norms: punishments target persistent low contributions, rewards reinforce high contributors. Because the multiplication factor is greater than 1 per player share, contributing yields group returns that typically encourage cooperation over time. Expect contributions to drift upward as players learn peers' tendencies and respond to sanctions/rewards; punishment will remain rare and modest (1 unit) because it is costly to the sender, while rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce cooperative behavior. A single defection mid-game may be punished by a couple of players, after which the defector often returns to higher contributions.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players. Each round everyone either keeps the 20 coins (withdraws 20 → contributes 0) or leaves them in the pot (withdraws 0 → contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so cooperators (who contribute 20) lower their private withdrawal but increase the shared return for everyone; defectors (who withdraw 20) keep a private gain while still receiving an equal share of the multiplied pot. Because rewards are available (positive transfers that cost 2 to the giver and add 2 to the recipient), we expect some players to use these sparingly to reinforce cooperation or to encourage converts. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so reputation-building and conditional cooperation can sustain contributions early on; free-riding gives higher immediate payoffs and can erode cooperation unless cooperators coordinate to reward contributors. Chat is possible but typically sparse and used to thank, encourage, or call out behavior.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with voluntary contributions and a post-decision reward stage. Material incentives alone favor free-riding (each coin contributed returns less to the contributor than it costs), so cooperation is fragile. Because players can reward others (at a personal cost) and see who rewarded them, we should expect conditional cooperation: many players start by contributing a positive amount, then adjust based on others' behavior and occasional rewards. Rewarding will be used sparingly to signal approval of high contributions and to sustain cooperation; it will usually come from players who are somewhat prosocial or who want to encourage reciprocation. Chat messages will be rare and practical (requests to cooperate, short thank-yous, or mild calls-out). Over 26 rounds, contributions may rise if rewards successfully incentivize cooperation, dip when some players free-ride, and then stabilize as social norms form — with intermittent small rewards reinforcing contributors. Because players do not know the number of rounds, no sharp endgame collapse is expected; instead behavior drifts slowly.

Rewarding will be used sparingly to signal approval of high contributions and to sustain cooperation; it will usually come from players who are somewhat prosocial or who want to encourage reciprocation. Chat messages will be rare and practical (requests to cooperate, short thank-yous, or mild calls-out). Over 26 rounds, contributions may rise if rewards successfully incentivize cooperation, dip when some players free-ride, and then stabilize as social norms form — with intermittent small rewards reinforcing contributors. Because players do not know the number of rounds, no sharp endgame collapse is expected; instead behavior drifts slowly.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits seven players against a simple binary choice each round: withdraw the full 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so cooperating helps the group but is individually costly up front; free-riding yields immediate private benefit plus the shared return. After seeing payoffs, players can punish (cost 2 to source, -3 to target per unit) or reward (cost 2 to source, +2 to target per unit). Players do not know how many rounds remain, so behavior will often be cautious and responsive to recent history.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: a mix of cooperation and free-riding. Contributors may punish visible free-riders to enforce fairness; punishments are costly and thus used sparingly. Small positive rewards may be given to consistent cooperators.
- Middle rounds: if punishment deters free-riding, cooperation tends to rise toward full contribution. Free-riders sometimes test the group; if punished, they may switch.
- Late rounds: with no knowledge of the end, players may continue reciprocity; occasional defections are met with targeted punishment, and a few small rewards maintain pro-social norms.
- Chat will be sparse: short appeals to cooperate, brief acknowledgements, or calls for fairness when someone defects.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players. Each round each player either keeps all 20 coins (contributes 0) or leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. Because 2.6/7 ≈ 0.371 < 1, withholding coins (free-riding) is individually attractive: a defector keeps 20 privately while only raising each player's share by ~7.4 if they had contributed. That creates a tension: group welfare is higher when many contribute, but individual temptation is to withdraw.

Players can punish (costly to source, costly to target) or reward (costly to source, beneficial to target) after seeing contributions. Punishment and reward are typically used sparingly in real games: they are present mainly to enforce cooperative norms or signal approval. Because the cost of punishing is borne immediately by the punisher, expect punishments only when defecting behavior recurs or provokes moralizing players. Rewards are used selectively to reinforce cooperation.

Given the provided personas, I predict:
- A few players (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT, MOOSE, DOG) will bias toward cooperation early; SLOTH (the name hints at lower effort) is likelier to test free-riding early on.
- When a clear defector appears (SLOTH or an occasional other), some norm-enforcers (GORILLA, PARROT, DUCK) will punish lightly (1–2 units); a few players may give small rewards to cooperators (DOG or others).
- Over rounds the group will largely converge toward cooperation with occasional defections that get small punishments. Chat will be sparse — short admonitions or thanks.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds. Each round each player can either contribute their full endowment (20) to the public pot or keep it (0 contributes). The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and shared equally, so cooperative contributions help everyone but are individually costly. Because players observe contributions and can punish (at a private cost of 2 coins per punishment unit, which imposes -2 on the target per unit), cooperation can be sustained by conditional cooperators who punish free-riders. Early rounds tend to feature some probing and modest punishment to enforce cooperation; mid-game cooperation often stabilizes if punishments deter defectors. In the final rounds (“endgame”), incentives to free-ride grow because future retaliation is less effective, so we may see more defections and less punishment. Chats are sparse and aimed at coordination, signaling intent, or reacting to punishments. Punishment is used sparingly because it is costly to send.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone privately decides how many of 20 coins to contribute; contributions are multiplied by 3.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns about 0.633 coins to each player. That means the private incentive is to keep money (since 0.633 < 1), while the group is better off when people contribute. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, conditional cooperation and norm enforcement (punishment and reward) are likely: many players will start by contributing moderately if they expect others to do so, but free-riding can appear. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) but very costly to the target (12 coins per punishment unit), so punishments are a strong deterrent and used sparingly. Rewards are also costly to give (3 coins per unit) and add only 2 coins to the recipient, so they serve more as positive reinforcement than as efficient transfers.

Given the six player personas: SLOTH is predisposed to contribute less; GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, and PARROT are more likely to be conditional cooperators (with DUCK and PARROT tending toward higher contributions). Expect the group to begin with moderate contributions, react negatively to persistent low contributions (targeted punishments), and use a few rewards to reinforce cooperation. Chats will be sparse and purposeful — short calls for cooperation, occasional complaints about free-riders, and occasional thanks after rewards. Because punishments are powerful, a few well-targeted sanctions early can move a low contributor toward higher contributions later; yet players will punish infrequently.

game_explanation:
This is a classic voluntary contribution public-goods game with 10 players, repeated for 6 rounds. Each round, players choose whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally, so group-payoff is maximized if everyone contributes, but any individual who withholds (free-rides) always gets a higher personal payoff in the same round than a cooperator. That creates a tension: cooperating benefits the group but invites exploitation.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: Some players (those with stronger pro-social preferences or trust) will test cooperation and may communicate to encourage contributions. Others will free-ride to gain the immediate advantage.
- Over rounds: Seeing that defectors earn more in the same round tends to erode cooperation. Without strong enforcement or binding agreements, contributions typically decline as the game progresses.
- Messaging can sustain cooperation if it builds credible reciprocity, but with a known finite horizon (6 rounds), backward induction pressures mean cooperation often collapses toward the middle or end. Still, a few players often continue contributing for intrinsic or reputational reasons even when it is personally costly.

Uncertainties:
- A few players may be stubborn cooperators (keep contributing despite losses).
- Some players may try last-round cooperation (despite backward induction) or try to coordinate a partial comeback.
- Chat is usually sparse; expect short appeals, brief reports of intent, or short expressions of frustration.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 rounds and an option to punish after seeing contributions. Each round players choose either to contribute their whole 20-coins or keep it. Contributions are multiplied strongly (6.2) and shared equally, so cooperating increases group payoff but defecting yields higher personal payoff within a single round unless punished. Because the game repeats and players can identify punishers, we expect dynamics typical of PGGs with costly punishment: some early free-riding, a few targeted punishments of defectors, and then increased cooperation in subsequent rounds. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it is used sparingly — enough to signal that free-riding will be punished, but not so much that punishers erase their own gains. In later rounds, cooperation tends to rise after punishments, and in the final round some players may return to free-riding because there is no future enforcement — producing a slight drop in cooperation again.

game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game creates a tension between private incentive to keep your 20 coins (and free-ride) and the collective benefit of contributing to the shared pot (which is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally). Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20) and players see only aggregate outcomes after decisions, early rounds usually show a mix: many people cooperate initially out of fairness or reciprocity, while some test by free-riding. Punishment lets cooperators sanction free-riders at a cost to themselves, which can sustain cooperation for a few rounds. However, with a known finite horizon (6 rounds), incentives to punish and to cooperate typically decline toward the end (backward induction), so cooperation often falls in the final round. Messages (chat) are used sparingly — to signal intentions, warn, or call out defectors.

I predict: moderate-to-high cooperation early (some free-riders), selective punishment targeted at persistent defectors, temporary improvement in cooperation after punishment, then gradual decline and a marked drop in the final round. Punishment is used sparingly — enough to signal consequences but not so much that players spend large shares on punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a standard binary public goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds. Each round every player can either keep their 20 coins or contribute all 20 to a common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players. Individually, keeping your 20 always yields a higher personal payoff in that round (because each 20 contributed returns only 12.4 to each player), so the dominant incentive is to free-ride. The group-optimal outcome is full contribution (everyone contributes each round) because that maximizes total group payoff.

Likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Early rounds often feature a mix: some players (conditional cooperators or pro-social types) contribute to signal cooperation; others free-ride to exploit those contributions. Because messages are allowed, leaders can try to coordinate cooperation, but messages are cheap talk and cannot commit others.
- Over rounds, observing payoffs (everyone sees peers' round payoffs) gives indirect information about how many contributed. If free-riding is common, cooperative players tend to reduce contributions (retaliation or discouragement). If some players successfully signal and others reciprocate, cooperation can be sustained or recover.
- In last rounds, standard "end-game" incentives push more players to defect (no future consequences), so contributions typically fall in later rounds unless strong intrinsic cooperation persists.
- Uncertainty arises from mixed motives in the group. Personas (education/age cues) suggest a few committed cooperators (postgrads: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, RABBIT, OWL) who may start contributing or try to coordinate, while others (younger players with less formal education or different backgrounds: DOG, PARROT, CHICK) may be more inclined to free-ride. Some middle-aged players (CHICKEN, MOOSE) may lean pragmatic and adapt to others' behavior.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits a small group (7 players) against the classic tension: contributing (leave the 20 coins in the shared pot) helps the group but lets others free-ride, while withdrawing (take the 20 privately) benefits the chooser immediately. The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly, so defecting tends to pay more in the short run when many others contribute. The reward stage (cost 2 per reward unit to the giver, +2 per unit to the recipient) allows players to promote cooperation by rewarding cooperators. Because only positive transfers are allowed, enforcement works indirectly: cooperators withhold rewards from defectors and reward cooperators, creating reputation-based incentives. Players don't know how many rounds remain, so early experimentation is common; over multiple rounds I expect:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions as players test others.
- Middle rounds: cooperation rises as cooperators reward each other and defectors are socially isolated (via withheld rewards).
- Final rounds: if cooperation looks stable, most players will settle into cooperating and occasional small positive rewards to cement norms.
Chat will be sparse and functional (announcements of intentions, short calls-out), and rewards will be used sparingly—small positive units targeted to cooperators who switch or sustain cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players who each receive 20 coins each round, decide simultaneously how many coins to contribute to a common pot (0–20), then see the pot multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After redistribution, players may spend costly rewards (3 coins per reward unit) that give 2 coins to a chosen peer. Players can send sparse chat messages. Because the number of rounds is unknown to players, behavior usually shows a mix of caution and experimentation early on, some reciprocity and targeted rewarding to sustain cooperation, and gradual drift toward lower contributions when free-riding appears. Rewards are used sparingly because they are costly; they tend to be directed at reliable contributors or used reciprocally. With this group of six, I expect moderate initial contributions, occasional targeted rewards to sustain the most cooperative players, and overall slow fluctuation in cooperation rather than a dramatic collapse (since rewarding preserves some cooperation).

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a tension between private gain (withdrawing the 20 coins) and group benefit (leaving them in the shared pot). Each round everyone’s choice is binary (contribute all 20 or withdraw all 20), the total contributed pot is multiplied by 2.6 and then evenly split among all 7 players, so a single cooperator increases the group return but risks being exploited by free riders. Punishment (cost 2 to the sender, -3 to the recipient) and reward (cost 2 to the sender, +2 to the recipient) let players sanction or reinforce peers after seeing contributions. Because players don't know how many rounds remain, we expect:

- Early rounds: some uncertainty and mixed behavior — some players test cooperation, others free-ride.
- Mid-game: visible sanctions (even small ones) tend to raise cooperation as defectors are penalized; some players use sparse punishment to enforce norms and sparse rewards to reinforce cooperators.
- Late rounds: if cooperation has been established, it often persists because players don't know the end; occasional tests/temptations still occur and are usually met with targeted, sparse punishment.
- Chats are typically short and purposeful; punishment and reward actions are infrequent and targeted.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round players choose either to contribute their full endowment (20) to a group pot or keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally, so contributions help everyone but are individually costly. Because choices are simultaneous, free-riding (contributing 0 while others contribute) can be profitable short-term. However, players can punish others after outcomes are revealed: punishment is costly to senders and harms targets, which can deter free-riding across rounds. Expect early rounds to show substantial cooperation from many players, with punishments targeted at clear defectors. Punishment will be used sparingly but visibly to enforce cooperation; some defectors may persist and be repeatedly punished, while others switch to contributing to avoid sanctions. Approaching the final round, some players may defect expecting no future retaliation (endgame), so cooperation often falls in round 6. Chat messages will be few but will include promises, complaints, and occasional threats or apologies.

Chat will be sparse and used to request coordination or to comment on behavior.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round everyone privately chooses how many of 20 coins to contribute. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 3.8, and split equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players may spend some of their remaining coins to punish or reward others (punish costs 3 per unit and removes 12 per unit from the target; reward costs 3 per unit and gives the target +2 per unit). Players can also send messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some tentative cooperation (moderate contributions), with a few low contributors testing the group. Strong cooperators (GORILLA, DUCK) try to signal for high contributions; more opportunistic or less-engaged players (SLOTH, occasionally PARROT or DOG) test by contributing less.
- Enforcement: Punishments will be used sparingly by players who value cooperation (GORILLA and sometimes PARROT or DUCK), mainly in response to obvious free-riding. Because punishment is costly to the punisher, punishments are typically small (1 unit) and targeted at repeat low contributors.
- Rewards: Rewards appear rarely as encouraging gestures when someone contributes noticeably more than the group average. Rewards are also used sparingly because they are costly to the sender.
- Over many rounds: contributions fluctuate. Conditional cooperators (CHICKEN, DUCK) respond to observed group behavior: if others generally cooperate and punish defectors, contributions rise. If punishments are absent and free-riding becomes tolerated, contributions drift down. Because players don't know when the game ends, there is no guaranteed endgame unraveling; instead behavior shows ebb and flow with occasional corrective punishments and sparse rewards, with some social messaging to coordinate.

reflection:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round everyone privately chooses how many of 20 coins to contribute. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 3.8, and split equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players may spend some of their remaining coins to punish or reward others (punish costs 3 per unit and removes 12 per unit from the target; reward costs 3 per unit and gives the target +2 per unit). Players can also send messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some tentative cooperation (moderate contributions), with a few low contributors testing the group. Strong cooperators (GORILLA, DUCK) try to signal for high contributions; more opportunistic or less-engaged players (SLOTH, occasionally PARROT or DOG) test by contributing less.
- Enforcement: Punishments will be used sparingly by players who value cooperation (GORILLA and sometimes PARROT or DUCK), mainly in response to obvious free-riding. Because punishment is costly to the punisher, punishments are typically small (1 unit) and targeted at repeat low contributors.
- Rewards: Rewards appear rarely as encouraging gestures when someone contributes noticeably more than the group average. Rewards are also used sparingly because they are costly to the sender.
- Over many rounds: contributions fluctuate. Conditional cooperators (CHICKEN, DUCK) respond to observed group behavior: if others generally cooperate and punish defectors, contributions rise. If punishments are absent and free-riding becomes tolerated, contributions drift down. Because players don't know when the game ends, there is no guaranteed endgame unraveling; instead behavior shows ebb and flow with occasional corrective punishments and sparse rewards, with some social messaging to coordinate.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players. Each round every player either leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (cooperates = contribution 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (defects = contribution 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly, so cooperation raises the group's return but leaves defectors temporarily better off (they keep 20 plus the shared return). Because players can punish or reward after seeing contributions, we expect:
- Early rounds: mixed behavior and some norm enforcement. A few players will experiment with cooperation; norm enforcers will punish visible defectors to signal that free-riding is costly.
- Middle rounds: with some punishments and occasional rewards, cooperation tends to increase as players respond to sanctions and positive reinforcement.
- Later rounds: occasional lapses/temptation to defect reappear (some players revert to free-riding), and targeted, sparse punishments follow. Rewards remain relatively rare and small.
Punishments are sparse because they cost the punisher; rewards are also used sparingly to encourage cooperators. Chat messages will be occasional and purposeful (requests to cooperate, acknowledgement of punishment/reward).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds, binary contributions (0 or 20). Each coin contributed yields 6.2 times the contributed amount to the group pot, split equally among all 10 players. Individual incentives: contributing 20 gives the group a return of 124 (=6.2*20) or 12.4 per player; the contributor therefore gets back 12.4 but loses the private 20 (net -7.6 compared to keeping it). A defector who keeps 20 receives the same per-person pot share but also keeps the 20, so defecting strictly dominates contributing in a one-shot calculation. However, because the multiplier is high, collective payoffs are maximized when many contribute.

Likely dynamics: initial rounds often see some conditional cooperation—several players will contribute out of pro-social preferences or reciprocity hopes. Observing higher individual payoffs for free-riders (who keep 20 plus pot share) creates pressure to defect in later rounds. Messages can sustain cooperation early, but the "end-game" (round 6) tends to produce full defection. Given the personas, expect a moderate level of early cooperation from players with higher education / older age (DUCK, DOG, RABBIT, DUCK and DOG as especially steady contributors). Younger or more opportunistic players (SLOTH, PARROT, MOOSE, CHICK) are more likely to free-ride, especially after seeing payoffs that reward defection. Uncertainty arises from not seeing others' choices before moving and from potential cheap-talk in messages.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits short-run incentives to withdraw the full private endowment (free-ride) against group incentives to leave the endowment in the shared pot. If everyone contributes their 20, the pot is multiplied and split so each gets 52, which is collectively best. If a single player free-rides while others contribute, the free-rider earns more that round (keeps 20 plus their share) — so unilateral defection is tempting. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, early rounds often see experimentation: promises and a few cooperators, some free-riding, and sparse use of costly punishment and rewards to shape behavior. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and hurts the target more (3 coins per unit), so it’s used sparingly to deter persistent free-riding. Rewards (cost 2 to giver, +2 to receiver) are similarly used sparingly to reinforce cooperation.

Given the seven-player mix and personas, I predict a modest cluster of steady cooperators (SLOTH, PARROT, MOOSE, DOG) who try to sustain contributions, intermittent free-riding by a few (initially GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN), and occasional targeted punishment early on that gradually encourages more contributions. Rewards are used occasionally to reinforce switches to cooperation or to thank cooperators. Chat is sparse and mostly functional.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated online public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone privately chooses how much of a 20-coin endowment to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players may spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs the giver 3 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins). Players see per-round summaries (their net payoff, rewards spent, rewards received, and who rewarded them). They do not know how many rounds there will be.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty leads to moderate contributions from several players, while a couple may test free-riding (low contribution) to see reactions.
- Conditional cooperation: many players respond to the group average — if others contribute, they sustain or increase contributions; if others defect, they lower theirs.
- Rewards are used sparingly because they are costly, but they serve as signals: small positive rewards to high contributors and withholding of rewards toward free-riders help stabilize cooperation.
- Chats are sparse and usually either informational (e.g., "I contributed X") or normative (e.g., praising high contributors).
- Because the number of rounds is unknown, players are less prone to a sharp endgame collapse; cooperation tends to fluctuate but often settles around a modest-moderate level. Rewarding remains selective and limited (typically 1 unit per tie).

game_explanation:
This public goods game features seven players who each decide whether to leave the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw them all for private use (contribute 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and then split equally, so the public payout per player increases with the number of contributors, but any single player's contribution yields a private cost of 20 while returning only 2.6*20/7 ≈ 7.43 to themselves — so unilateral defection (keeping the 20) is individually attractive even though group payoffs rise with more contributors. Players can send costly rewards (each unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins) after seeing contributions; rewards are the only social sanction/rewarding mechanism. Players can also chat, but they do not see decisions before making their own and do not know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty and testing. A few players (often those with prosocial tendencies in the personas) will try contributing; others will free-ride. Cooperators get smaller direct payoffs but may be modestly compensated by selective rewards.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperation emerges. Players who see recurring cooperators may start to contribute and selectively reward cooperators to encourage more contributions. Rewards are sparse and targeted (1–2 units), mainly to reinforce cooperators.
- Later rounds: unstable end-game behavior may appear. If players suspect an end, some may defect to capture higher private returns. Because rewards cost the sender, they are used sparingly; reward links tend to reinforce cooperators rather than punish defectors.

Now the play-by-play transcript across eight rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with six players. Each round everyone privately chooses how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; each coin contributed yields a group return of 3.8 coins, split evenly among the six players, so each contributed coin returns only about 0.63 to the contributor — contributing is socially beneficial but individually costly, so there is a tension between cooperation and free-riding. After contributions are revealed and the public return is distributed, players can use costly rewards to encourage cooperation: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Rewards are therefore a private sacrifice to benefit others and can sustain cooperation if targeted at contributors. Players can also send messages at any time; communication can create coordination or signaling, but messages are cheap and may be unreliable.

Uncertainties: players do not know how many rounds remain, which encourages conditional strategies (e.g., reward cooperators, withhold from free-riders). Expectation: early rounds typically show moderate contributions as people test each other, with some targeted rewarding to encourage cooperators. If rewarding is used sparingly and targeted at reliable contributors, cooperation should gradually increase. A few players may initially free-ride and be left unrewarded; social pressure (messages and later rewards) can bring them up slowly. Rewards should be sparse (1–2 units typical) because they are costly to the sender.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round each player has 20 coins and chooses either to contribute all 20 to the public pot or keep the 20. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players. The one-shot dominant move is to keep the 20 (free-ride) because, for any fixed number of contributors, a non-contributor retains the 20 and still gets the shared return. However, if many players coordinate to contribute, everyone (including contributors) can end up much better off than repeated free-riding would deliver.

Given the personas and the ability to chat, I expect some initial cooperative attempts (cheap-talk commitments), followed by exploitation of cooperators early on and a gradual decline in contributions. A few players who are more other-regarding or committed are likely to contribute repeatedly (holding to a principle), but many will switch to free-riding once they see contributors get lower net payoffs. Because the game has a known finite horizon (6 rounds), we might see a small coordinated comeback attempt near the end — players may use chat to try a last-round joint contribution, with mixed compliance. Chat will be sparse and mostly short commitments, complaints, or one-line attempts to coordinate.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins, chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a public pot, the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally, and then players can spend coins to punish (cost 3 per punishment unit, deduct 12 per unit from the target) or reward (cost 3 per reward unit, give +2 per unit to the target). Players don't know how many rounds remain, so behavior often stabilizes: conditional cooperators contribute moderate amounts if others do; clear free-riding tends to be met with occasional punishments from cooperators; targeted rewards are used sparingly to encourage cooperation. Chat is usually sparse; punishment and reward are used sparingly to enforce norms.

With these personas, I expect:
- SLOTH will sometimes contribute low (occasional free-rider) and respond to punishment by increasing contributions.
- CHICKEN and PARROT (both more educated and pro-social) will often contribute higher amounts and occasionally punish low contributions or reward consistent cooperators.
- DUCK and DOG will be steady mid-level contributors; DOG may reward cooperators.
- GORILLA will be moderately cooperative but will sometimes test with low contributions and then be punished.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and must decide privately whether to contribute all 20 or keep it. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 6.2, and split equally. The social optimum is that everyone contributes (each gets a large share), but the individual incentive each round is to free-ride (keep 20 and still receive a share). Because players see round-by-round payoffs and can send messages, early cooperation may appear (trust/reciprocity), but successful free-riding tends to undermine cooperation over time. Uncertainties include players' types and whether messages are credible — some high-education players may be more pro-social, others may act strategically. Expect initial cooperation by many, some exploitation by a few, gradual erosion of contributions, intermittent repair attempts via chat, and a drop in the final round as backward-induction incentives dominate.

game_explanation:
This public goods game gives each player 20 coins each round and a binary choice: keep all (0) or contribute all (20) to a group pot. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among the 10 players, so the per-player return from the pot equals 0.62 times total contributions. Because the private cost of contributing is 20 and the individual return per contributed coin is only 0.62, an individual who contributes loses in that round relative to a free rider if others' behavior is unchanged. That creates a strong short-term incentive to free-ride (keep the 20 and still earn a share of the pot).

Uncertainty about others' choices (simultaneous moves) makes the decision strategic: some players may lead by contributing to try to sustain cooperation, others may respond to visible past behavior (seen at round summaries) and chat messages. Over repeated rounds we typically expect:
- Early rounds: a mix of contributions and free-riding as players test each other.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators may follow visible cooperation, while free riders exploit cooperative history, possibly causing fluctuations.
- Final round (endgame): cooperation often declines because there is no future punishment or reward; many players defect.

Given the 6-round horizon and the personas, I predict modest initial cooperation, some back-and-forth as players react to payoffs and sparse chat, occasional attempts to rally cooperation, and a drop in contributions in the final round.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game places six players in repeated anonymous interactions where each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute (0–20) to a common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 3.8 and split equally; this creates a social return greater than the private return (any contributed coin returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to each person, so the group benefits if people contribute). However, every player individually gets less back (≈0.633 per contributed coin) than they keep by not contributing (1 coin), so the temptation is to free-ride. Because rounds are repeated but the total number of rounds is unknown, we expect a mix of cooperative contributions and occasional defections. Players who care about fairness or expect reciprocity will tend to contribute more; those who prioritize immediate private payoff or test the group will contribute less.

Punishment (cost 3 to sender, −12 to target per unit) and reward (cost 3 to sender, +2 to target per unit) allow players to sanction or reinforce peers after seeing the round outcomes. Punishments are costly and tend to be used sparingly to deter persistent free-riding; rewards are also costly and used selectively to incentivize cooperation. Uncertainty about others' motives and the unknown game length mean players will adapt: conditional cooperators will increase contributions if others are cooperative; persistent low contributors will be punished occasionally, which can push them back toward cooperation. Chat is possible but typically sparse; messages are used to call out defectors or encourage cooperation.

Overall predicted dynamics: moderate-to-high cooperation most rounds, occasional unilateral or small defection episodes by one or two players (especially the player labeled SLOTH or CHICKEN), and intermittent targeted punishment by DUCK, GORILLA, or DOG to discipline free-riders. Occasional small rewards acknowledge consistent contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods setting with a small multiplier (2.6) split across 7 players. Each player can either keep the full 20 (withdraw = defect) or leave the full 20 in the shared pot (contribute = cooperate). Privately, keeping 20 is individually attractive because each coin returned to the group yields only about 0.371 to each contributor (2.6/7), less than one-to-one. Socially, though, mutual contribution yields much higher group payoffs (each person would get 52 if everyone contributes 20).

Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we can expect early signalling and conditional responses: some players will contribute to signal cooperation; some will free-ride opportunistically. When contributors see others free-ride, they are likely to use sparse punishment to discourage defection (punishment is costly but deters freeriding). Rewards are used less often but can appear to reinforce cooperation. Punishment and reward typically happen sparingly (one or two units) and are targeted at obvious defectors or occasional cooperators who deserve praise. reflection:
This is a standard repeated public goods setting with a small multiplier (2.6) split across 7 players. Each player can either keep the full 20 (withdraw = defect) or leave the full 20 in the shared pot (contribute = cooperate). Privately, keeping 20 is individually attractive because each coin returned to the group yields only about 0.371 to each contributor (2.6/7), less than one-to-one. Socially, though, mutual contribution yields much higher group payoffs (each person would get 52 if everyone contributes 20).

Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we can expect early signalling and conditional responses: some players will contribute to signal cooperation; some will free-ride opportunistically. When contributors see others free-ride, they are likely to use sparse punishment to discourage defection (punishment is costly but deters freeriding). game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players. Each round everyone starts with 20 coins in the shared pot but privately chooses either to withdraw all 20 (defect, leaving 0 contribution) or withdraw 0 (cooperate, contributing the full 20). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally across all players. Individually, contributing is costly because each coin you contribute returns 2.6/7 ≈ 0.371 to yourself — less than the 1 coin you keep if you defect — so the one-shot dominant incentive is to withdraw (defect). However, the group optimum is everyone contributing (higher total payoff). Because players do not know the total number of rounds, and because they can reward others (at a cost of 2 coins per 1 reward unit which gives the target +2 coins) and can see who rewarded them, social incentives and reputation can support cooperation over time.

Given typical behavior in such settings, I expect:
- Early rounds: uncertainty and some free-riding (withdrawals) while a few players test cooperation.
- Sparse, targeted reward use: players reward cooperators (or switchers) rather than wastefully rewarding defectors. Rewards are used to signal approval and build reciprocity.
- Gradual increase in cooperation as rewards reinforce contributors; occasional defections still occur as strategic tests.
- Chats will be brief and used mainly to signal intent or to call out patterns.

Now the simulated transcript from round 1 through round 8.

game_explanation:
This public goods game mixes a high multiplication factor (6.2) with binary contributions (0 or 20) and costly peer punishment (2 coin cost to sender per punishment unit; 2 coin loss per unit to recipient). With 10 players, contributions generate large shared returns but the individual short-term incentive is to free-ride (keep 20, still get the group share). Punishment can deter free-riding but is itself costly, so expect:
- Early rounds: some cooperation but a few free-riders; cooperators may use limited punishment to discipline defectors.
- Middle rounds: cooperation may stabilize if punishment is reasonably targeted; persistent defectors may continue to be punished.
- Endgame (rounds 5–6): cooperation tends to fall as future consequences vanish, so defections often rise and punishment becomes less frequent or more symbolic.
Chats are typically sparse and used to threaten, demand contributions, or register displeasure. Punishment is used sparingly — mostly directed at repeat defectors — because it reduces the punisher's own payoff.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods setting with uncertainty about the total horizon and a small group (7 players). Each player faces a clear temptation: withdraw the entire 20 coins (keep private payoff + public share) or leave them in the pot (forgo 20 but increase the public return to everyone). The public multiplier (2.6) gives a social return greater than individual cost only if enough people cooperate, but individual incentives favor withdrawing. Because players can punish or reward after seeing contributions, we expect social enforcement to shape behavior over rounds:

- Early rounds: some players will test incentives and may free-ride; others will cooperate to build norms.
- Punishment is costly for the punisher (2 coins per unit) but inflicts larger loss on the target (3 coins per unit), so a few targeted punishments can deter repeat free-riding even though punishers pay a price.
- Rewards cost the same to the giver as punishment (2 coins) but give less leverage per coin than punishment (reward adds +2 to target; punishment removes -3 per unit).
- Because the number of rounds is unknown, players often adopt conditional strategies (punish defectors, reward cooperators) rather than purely one-shot exploitation.
- Expect relatively sparse but strategic punishment and occasional rewards to stabilize cooperation after initial rounds of experimentation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with voluntary contributions and one-sided rewarding. Players get 20 coins each round and choose how much to contribute to a shared pot (0–20). The pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs 3 to the giver and gives 2 to the recipient. Players do not know how many rounds will be played, so they are likely to experiment early (testing others' cooperativeness) and settle into conditional strategies: cooperate if others do, free-ride if many defect. Rewarding is costly, so it will be used sparingly and targeted to reinforce cooperators or to incentivize marginal contributors. Given the player personas, I expect DOG and PARROT (college-educated, prosocial) to contribute near-maximum frequently and to reward cooperators modestly; CHICKEN and SLOTH will be moderate and conditional cooperators; GORILLA and DUCK start with some free-riding tendencies but may increase contributions if rewarded. Chat will be sparse and mostly encouraging or signaling intentions. Over 26 rounds we should see occasional swings when free-riding appears, with rewards used to support and stabilize cooperation; rewards are typically 1–2 units and directed toward consistent contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated binary public goods game with punishment and reward. Each round every player's decision is simply whether to withdraw the 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or to leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly among the 7 players, so a lone defector gets an immediate private gain (they keep 20) while everyone shares the public return. Because punishment is costly to the punisher but imposes a larger loss on targets (punisher pays 2 per unit; target loses 3 per unit), small punishments can deter defection if enough players are willing to pay the cost. Rewards are also possible (cost 2 to giver, +2 to receiver) and can reinforce cooperative switches.

Likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Early rounds are exploratory: cooperators may contribute to signal willingness, defectors may exploit. Observed punishments often cluster on clear defectors.
- Punishment tends to be sparse but targeted; a few players (often those who identify strongly with cooperation) will punish defectors to enforce norms. Rewards are rarer but used to welcome back or thank cooperators.
- A defector who is punished sufficiently is likely to switch to cooperation in later rounds; subsequent rewards can help stabilize that change.
- Because players do not know the total number of rounds, classic end-game collapse is less likely; cooperation can be sustained if punishment and reward create stable incentives.

I will simulate 8 rounds with these priors: most players are conditional cooperators (contribute), GORILLA starts by defecting, a few players (DOG, MOOSE, CHICKEN) are willing to punish early, and some players occasionally send small rewards to encourage cooperation. Chat messages will be sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds, binary contributions (0 or 20). If all contribute, the group pot grows strongly (6.2×) and everyone benefits; if some free-ride (give 0), they keep their 20 and still receive a share of the multiplied pot, so short-run incentives favor defection. Because players can punish after seeing contributions (at a cost to themselves), we expect a mix of dynamics:

- Early rounds: many players will try cooperating (contribute 20) to signal collective benefit; a few may test free-riding. If cooperators care about fairness, they will use small punishments to sanction obvious free-riders.
- Middle rounds: punishment tends to steer the group toward higher cooperation if sanctions sufficiently reduce the payoff advantage of defecting. Punishment is costly to send, so it will be used sparingly and selectively.
- Final round: backward induction and lack of future rounds make defection more attractive; some players may defect in round 6. Because there's no future to influence, punishment in the last round tends to be lower or more expressive (small, spiteful) rather than enforcement-oriented.

Uncertainty remains about how costly each player finds punishment and how many players will accept the private cost of sanctioning. Expect sparse chat (short normative or strategic comments), relatively few punishment edges each round, and a gradual movement toward cooperation mid-game with a small uptick in defection in round 6.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20). The social optimum is everyone contributing (each 20), because the pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split across 10 players — each contributed 20 adds 12.4 to the group pot which yields 1.24 to each player, so cooperation yields higher group payoffs. However, individually, a one-shot incentive to free ride exists (keep 20 privately while still receiving a share of the multiplied pot). Because players see contribution outcomes each round and can punish specific players (at a cost), we expect conditional cooperation: many players will contribute early to establish norms, and a few may free-ride occasionally. Punishment will be used sparingly to sanction clear defectors, especially early on, and this tends to increase cooperation in intermediate rounds. In the final round punishment incentives are weaker (no future rounds), so defections are more likely and punishments somewhat reduced or spiteful.

Given the player personas, I predict an initially cooperative profile with a small number of free-riders. Punishments will target obvious free-riders (1-unit punishments are common), and will mostly succeed in nudging those players toward cooperation in subsequent rounds. Chat will be present but sparse — a few short normative or reactive messages.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players. Each round each player either contributes all 20 coins to the public pot (cooperate) or withdraws all 20 for private use (defect). The public pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so defecting gives a private advantage in the short run (defectors keep 20 plus the shared return) while cooperators rely on the multiplied public good. After the sharing stage players can spend coins to give positive rewards to others (each reward unit costs the giver 2 coins and gives the receiver 2 coins). Players can also send short messages.

Likely dynamics: at first many players will try contributing, but a few may defect to free-ride. Cooperators will respond by selectively rewarding those who contribute (and withholding rewards from defectors). Because rewards are costly, they tend to be small and sparse — used mainly to signal appreciation or incentivize change. Over rounds some conditional cooperators may switch to contributing if rewarded/encouraged; persistent defectors can be isolated (receive few or no rewards) and may either continue free-riding or eventually switch. Messages are sparse and typically urge cooperation, call out free-riders, or thank contributors.
