This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players. Each round each player either contributes all 20 coins to the public pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. Because the multiplier divided by group size (2.6/7 ≈ 0.371) makes the private return from keeping coins (you keep 20) larger than a single individual's share from contributing when others defect, free-riding is individually attractive in any single round. However, allowing costly rewards (2 coins cost per unit; 2 coins benefit per unit to the target) creates a social incentive: cooperators can be rewarded to offset the immediate personal cost of contributing. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so they may experiment early to probe others' behavior, and conditional cooperators may adjust contributions based on observed rewards and payoffs.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds will show mixed behavior as some players (leaders/conditional cooperators) contribute to signal cooperation, while others free-ride to test whether contributors are rewarded.
- Players who are identified as rewarding cooperators (parrot-like persona) will sustain cooperation via small, targeted rewards. Rewarding will be sparse and selective, mostly toward contributors and newcomers to cooperation.
- When many players converge to contributing, the group payoff rises. Occasional defections (end-game testing, or opportunistic players) are likely later; reward withholding is the primary response rather than direct punishment.
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful: short commitments, observations about rewards, or requests for reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with optional costly punishment and rewarding after each round. Players start with 20 coins each round and choose how much to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. Because contributions are private at decision time, early rounds are often marked by cautious or heterogeneous contributions while players learn about others. High contributors gain the group benefit but risk being exploited by low contributors; low contributors can earn more privately but may provoke punishment from cooperators later. Punishment is costly to the punisher and heavily penalizes targets, so it tends to be used sparingly and often against persistent low contributors. Rewards are also costly and used selectively to encourage cooperators. Chat messages are typically few and used to encourage cooperation or call out free-riding.

Given the provided personas, I expect DUCK, DOG, GORILLA, and PARROT to act as conditional cooperators: they contribute moderately to highly and occasionally reward or punish to enforce norms. SLOTH is biased toward lower effort and will sometimes free-ride, provoking occasional targeted punishments. CHICKEN is moderate and may alternate between cooperation and targeted punishment if norms falter. Over 26 anonymous rounds (participants do not know how many rounds remain), we should see initial variation, a few targeted punishments early to discipline very low contributions, then an overall tendency toward moderate-to-high cooperation with occasional sanctions and selective rewards to sustain cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with costly positive rewards. Each round, players decide how much of their private 20 coins to contribute to a pot that is multiplied by 3.8 and then split equally among the six players. Because each coin contributed returns only 3.8/6 ≈ 0.63 to the contributor, the one-shot dominant incentive is to contribute nothing. However, repeated interaction, conditional cooperation, and the ability to reward others (cost 3 per reward unit, benefit 2 per unit to recipient) allow cooperative equilibria to be sustained: players can use rewards to selectively reward high contributors and foster reciprocity.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix of conditional cooperation and occasional free-riding. DUCK, DOG, and GORILLA tend to be steady contributors and use rewards sparingly to reinforce cooperation. PARROT will often mimic group trends. SLOTH is more prone to low contributions sometimes, eliciting social disapproval. CHICKEN may defect occasionally (as a stochastic protest or mistake) but will partially recover afterward. Rewards will be used rarely and targeted: small 1-unit gifts to recognize consistent contributors rather than heavy punishment (negative rewards are not available). Chats will be sparse — occasional praise, apology, or mild calls-out.

The dynamic will show an initial moderate to high contribution level, a few rounds with low contributions by SLOTH and one visible defect by CHICKEN that temporarily depress average contributions, then a gradual recovery as reward signaling and conditional cooperation re-establish more cooperative norms. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we don't expect a sharp end-game collapse; cooperation drifts but remains moderate by the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players, binary contribution choices (either contribute the full 20 or withdraw it and contribute 0), and a reward stage after redistribution. The public pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally; defectors (who keep their 20) get an immediate advantage whenever many others contribute. The optional reward stage lets players transfer value to others at no net social gain (2 cost to sender = 2 benefit to receiver), so rewards are primarily a reputational/incentive device: contributors can be compensated and conditional cooperators can be encouraged. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, early behavior often mixes testing (who will cooperate?) and signaling (rewarding cooperators to encourage future contributions); if rewards reliably follow contributions, cooperation usually increases. Expect a few initial free-riders, some small targeted rewards to reinforce contributors, then rising cooperation. Chat will be sparse — short appeals or thanks — and rewards will be used sparingly and targeted at cooperators.

Because players do not know the total number of rounds, behavior typically evolves: early rounds feature testing and mild cooperation, conditional cooperators raise contributions when others contribute; occasional free-riding provokes sparse punishment because punishment is costly to the punisher but can strongly deter defectors (punishment deducts 12 coins per unit from the target while costing the punisher 3 coins). Rewards are cheaper than punishment in effect on the recipient (a reward unit costs 3 to the giver and gives +2 to the recipient) and tend to be used sparingly to encourage consistent cooperators.

- SLOTH will occasionally under-contribute early but increase after being punished.
- DUCK (college-educated) and DOG (younger, cooperative) are likely to be steady, somewhat higher contributors and to enforce norms occasionally.
- GORILLA and PARROT are moderate contributors; CHICKEN sometimes vacillates and might free-ride once or twice, drawing punishment.
- Punishment will be used rarely but decisively when someone defects strongly (very low contribution). Rewards will be occasional, targeted at consistently high contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game where each player gets 20 coins each round and can either contribute all (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among the 10 players, so each player's per-round share equals 0.62 times the total contributed. Individually, free-riding (contribute 0) strictly dominates contributing (contribute 20) because everyone receives the same share regardless of their own choice, and contributors lose 20 relative to non-contributors. However, group payoff is highest when many players contribute. In repeated play, we typically expect some initial cooperation—often from more cooperative or more education-skewed players—followed by decline as free-riding is noticed. Communication can encourage cooperation, but because choices are simultaneous, promises are not fully credible; some players will experiment with conditional cooperation (contribute if enough others do) or try to shame free-riders in chat. Near the final round, incentives to free-ride strengthen (no future rounds to incentivize reciprocity), so contributions usually fall.

Below I simulate a plausible set of decisions and sparse, purposeful chat across six rounds using the provided player personas as priors for motivations.

game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game pits a small group of identifiable players against the temptation to free-ride. Each round players choose either to contribute all 20 coins to the group pot or keep all 20 privately. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and divided equally, so any individual who defects (keeps 20) pocketed the private 20 plus the equal share of the multiplied pot — giving a short-term advantage to free-riding. However, contributors can punish others after seeing contributions; punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per punishment unit) and subtracts the same amount from the punished player. Players also see who punished them and can send messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players test the waters (a mixture of contributions and defections). Contributors may punish visible defectors, producing targeted deterrence.
- Middle rounds: punishment and peer pressure tends to increase cooperation, as defectors are identified and disciplined.
- Endgame: with no future interactions to enforce cooperation, some players may defect again. Punishment tends to be sparser in the final round (less incentive to invest in costly punishment), though some players may still punish out of fairness/reciprocity.
- Messages are used sparsely: promises, warnings, complaints, and short acknowledgments. Punishment is typically targeted and limited (few units), because it is costly.

Uncertainties:
- Some players may be "conditional cooperators" (contribute if others do), while others may be persistent free-riders. Identification of punishers lets the group coordinate who punishes, but it also opens possible retaliation. Endgame behavior is the least predictable.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game where each player gets 20 coins per round and can either contribute all 20 or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among the 10 players. Because the per-player return from one contributor is 12.4 (6.2*20/10), contributing always lowers the contributor's personal payoff by 7.6 compared with withholding, holding others' behavior fixed. So the dominant (self-interested) action is to keep 0 and free-ride. However, social preferences, reciprocity, and the visibility of round-by-round net payoffs allow for conditional cooperation: some players may contribute early to try to coordinate a high-payoff outcome (everyone contributes yields large group gains), while others will exploit contributors. Over rounds we therefore expect an initial mix (some pro-social players contribute), rapid drift toward more defection once contributors are seen to be worse off, occasional attempts to coordinate cooperation via chat after low-round payoffs, and possibly a risky coordinated attempt mid-late game. By the end, inertia and the temptation to free-ride usually drive contributions down—often to a small committed minority or zero.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with seven players. Each round each player either contributes their whole 20-coin endowment to the group pot or withdraws it (so contributions are binary: 0 or 20). The sum of contributions is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all seven players, so defectors (who keep 20 privately) always get a higher immediate individual return from the public good in that round than a cooperator does, unless the group contribution is large enough and/or reward transfers compensate cooperators. After the multiplication and split, players can spend costly rewards (2 coins per reward unit) to give benefit (2 coins per unit) to other players. Because rewards are costly, they’re typically used sparingly: to encourage new cooperators, to reward consistent cooperators, or to reciprocate. Messages are allowed and can coordinate expectations, but players do not know how many rounds remain, so early rounds tend to be exploratory (testing who will cooperate), while later rounds may show conditional cooperation (rewarding those who cooperated earlier) or opportunistic defection (free-riding when rewards are absent).

Uncertainties: players don't observe others' choices before deciding each round, and they don't know how many rounds remain. This makes incentives to test strategies strong early on. The ability to reward (but not punish) means cooperators can boost each other's payoffs, but defectors often still earn more in the short run. Over repeated rounds we may see some convergence: small clusters of mutual cooperators who reward each other, occasional entry by defectors back into cooperation if rewards substantially offset losses, and sparse chat mostly used to signal intent or call out free-riding.

game_explanation:
This 26-round public goods game will likely show a mix of cooperation and exploitation. A few players (based on their personas) appear predisposed to contribute generously (DUCK and DOG are the most cooperative), some are moderate and conditional cooperators (GORILLA and CHICKEN), and a couple are inclined to contribute little or free-ride (SLOTH and PARROT). Early rounds will show attempts to build cooperation with moderate-to-high contributions from the cooperators and sparse use of costly rewards to reinforce good behavior. Free-riding by PARROT (and sometimes SLOTH) will erode trust and lead conditional cooperators to reduce contributions at times. Because rewards are costly (3 coins spent to give 2 to another), they will be used sparingly and mainly to reward high contributors or to signal disapproval indirectly by rewarding others. Chat messages will be infrequent but occasionally used to coordinate or complain. Over the long run expect fluctuating contributions: cooperators try to sustain high contributions, conditional types adjust downward when exploited, and the group uses small targeted rewards to stabilize cooperation occasionally.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with six players, private endowments each round, simultaneous contributions, and a costly punishment/reward stage after payoff redistribution. Typical dynamics: early rounds often show moderate cooperation as players test one another; opportunistic low contributions (free-riding) can appear, prompting sanctions (punishment) or positive reinforcement (reward) from others. Because punishment is expensive to the punisher but extremely damaging to the target (source pays 3 per unit; target loses 12 per unit), punishment is usually used sparingly and targeted at clear free-riders. Rewards are cheaper for targets (source pays 3 per unit; target gains 2 per unit) and often used to reinforce steady cooperators. Uncertainty about how many rounds remain tends to sustain cooperation longer than if the endpoint were known, but gradual decline is still possible if sanctions are absent or inconsistent.

Expectations I use in this simulation:
- Early moderate contributions (around half the endowment) with occasional deviations.
- Punishments appear when a player clearly shirks repeatedly; rewards appear occasionally to reinforce high contributors.
- Punishments are sparse and targeted (1–2 units from a few players rather than many units from everyone).
- Chats are sparse and used for signaling intentions, calling out free-riding, or briefly thanking/rewarding cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This repeated public-goods game will likely start with moderate-to-high contributions from most players, driven by cooperative priors (especially younger/educated players like DUCK, CHICKEN, SLOTH). DOG—whose prior suggests lower pro-sociality—will contribute little early, which reduces efficiency and creates tension. Because the pot multiplier (3.8) yields an individual return of about 0.633 per coin contributed, each coin contributed benefits the group more than the individual (social dilemma): contributors lose on net per coin unless others also contribute. Players can reward others (costly: 3 coins per reward unit; target gains 2 coins) which provides a mechanism to reinforce contributors. In practice rewards will be used sparingly to incentivize cooperators (small 1-unit rewards are common), so we expect early-round rewards to praise high contributors (especially DUCK and CHICKEN) but not to punish free-riders (there is no negative punishment, so free-riders just get ignored). Over rounds cooperation will trend down as players see low contributions (particularly from DOG) and respond by reducing their own contributions; occasional targeted rewards will appear as attempts to sustain cooperation but overall decline is expected. Chats will be rare and focused—short acknowledgements, mild calls for reciprocity, or questions about low contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This public goods game creates tension between private incentive (keep your 20 coins) and collective benefit (contribute to a pot that is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally). Contributing 20 helps the group, but if many others contribute, defecting (contributing 0) yields a higher individual payoff that round. Punishment provides a mechanism to enforce cooperation: a player can pay to reduce another player's payoff, which can deter free-riding. However, punishment itself is costly (2 coins per unit sent), and because the game lasts six rounds, forward-looking players may escalate or taper punishment based on reciprocity and expected future interactions. Early rounds typically reveal baseline tendencies; mid-game sees conditional cooperation and targeted punishment of repeat defectors; final-round behavior often drifts toward self-interest because future retaliation is less relevant. Uncertainty arises from not seeing others’ contributions before choosing and from heterogeneity in how much players value fairness versus short-term gains. Given the provided personas, expect a core of reliably cooperative players (PARROT, MOOSE, OWL, CHICKEN, RABBIT, CHICK), a few conditional cooperators (GORILLA, DOG), and a small set of more selfish or slow-to-adjust players (SLOTH, DUCK). Punishment will be used sparingly and mostly to target persistent defectors; messages will be infrequent but used to call out or appeal to others.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with costly punishment available. Each round, players choose to contribute either their whole endowment (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and shared equally among all 10 players, so contributing increases the group's pot but is privately costly. Because non-contributors keep the private 20 and still receive an equal share of the multiplied pot, single-round incentive favors free-riding, but repeated interaction and costly punishment can sustain higher contribution rates.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds often start mixed: some players contribute while others free-ride to test responses.

I will simulate a plausible six-round trajectory consistent with these tendencies: a mixed beginning, targeted punishment that encourages most players to shift to contribution by rounds 3–4, near-universal cooperation in the middle, a small free-ride in round 5 that gets lightly punished, and a partially unruly final round with a few last-round free-riders who get minimal punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with voluntary contributions and costly rewarding. Players start each round with private endowments (20 coins) and decide how much to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After redistribution, players can spend coins to reward others (cost 3 to giver, gives 2 to receiver). Players don't know the horizon, so early rounds tend to be exploratory: some players test cooperation, others reciprocate or withhold rewards to shape norms. Rewarding is costly, so it is used sparingly to reinforce cooperation (rewarding high contributors) or signal disapproval indirectly by rewarding cooperators and withholding rewards from low contributors. Chat can be used to propose norms, signal intentions, or call out low contributors; messages are typically sparse.

Given the six personas:
- SLOTH (name suggests low activity, younger postgraduate) is likely to start low and gradually increase if others reward/contribute.
- GORILLA is moderately cooperative but bandies minor fluctuations; sometimes matches group averages.
- DUCK is a conditional cooperator (higher contributions, rewards cooperators).
- CHICKEN is cautious and may test cooperation but responds to group pressure.
- DOG tends to be cooperative and supportive (rewards to reinforce).
- PARROT is communicative and cooperative, often proposing targets and reciprocating.

Over 26 rounds I expect an initial exploratory phase (rounds 1–4), emergence of a cooperation norm via messages and rewards (rounds 5–12), occasional defections/test moves that prompt chastising or withholding of rewards (rounds ~9, 12, 16, 19), and then gradual stabilization at moderate-to-high contributions sustained by targeted rewards among cooperators. Reward actions are sparse and small (1–2 units typically), directed to those who contribute above the emerging group norm.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with six players who each have 20 coins per round. Because each coin contributed returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to the contributor, it is individually profitable to free-ride but collectively beneficial to contribute. Expect the following dynamics:

- Early rounds: some players start moderately cooperative (10–16), others test by contributing less (0–8). A visible early leader (likely GORILLA) will encourage cooperation via chat and consistent high contributions.
- Conditional cooperation: many players update contributions based on peers' revealed actions. Those who see high contributions will tend to match; persistent low contributors (free riders) attract social sanctions.
- Punishment/reward stage: punishment is costly but very effective (each punishment unit costs 3 to sender and removes 12 from the target), so players punish sparingly but decisively when someone repeatedly free-rides. Rewards are rare and small (cost 3 to giver, +2 to receiver) and used to positively reinforce high contributors. Because punishments are powerful, expect occasional targeted punishments rather than broad retaliation.
- Communication: chats are sparse and purposeful — leader reminders, calls-out for low contributions, short acknowledgements. Players don't know the number of rounds, so endgame unraveling is muted; cooperation tends to stabilize after a few rounds if punishments deter freeloading.
- Overall path: moderate cooperation initially, a few low contributions trigger selective punishments, which raise cooperation to a higher steady state (roughly 12–16), with occasional lapses and spot punishments and rewards thereafter.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with small groups and a multiplier (2.6) that yields a lower private marginal return than the private benefit of withdrawing (i.e., individually, taking the 20 is tempting even though group payoff would be higher if many contribute). Players don't know how many rounds remain, so we expect two tensions:

- Short-term incentive to free-ride: withdrawing the 20 gives a larger private share most rounds, so some players will tend to defect (take the 20).
- Conditional cooperation and reputation/reward motives: some players (given the personas) are inclined to contribute and to use the inexpensive reward instrument (2 coins cost per unit, 2 coins given to target) to signal approval. Because rewards are costful to senders but beneficial to targets, they can sustain cooperation modestly early on, especially when cooperators reciprocate rewards. However, the reward exchange is typically sparse — players reward only a few peers and in small amounts.
- Dynamics likely: modest cooperation in early rounds (a cluster of prosocial players contribute and reward each other), followed by a slow decline as free-riding pays off and rewards are too small to fully offset the temptation to withdraw. Occasional recoveries are possible when a newcomer/cooperator is rewarded. Chat will be sparse — short calls to encourage or thank; some mild shaming or short justification is possible.

Predicted pattern across eight rounds: cooperation starts moderate (3–4 contributors), rewards cluster on cooperators, some players experiment (switch to defect), cooperation drifts downward overall but with small recoveries late as players test reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with binary contributions: each player either contributes the whole 20 coin endowment to the public pot (cooperate) or takes all 20 privately (defect). The total contributed is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so defecting yields a higher private payoff in the absence of interventions. Because players cannot see choices before deciding and do not know how many rounds remain, early rounds are characterized by uncertainty and testing: some players try to cooperate, others test free-riding. The punishment and reward stage allows enforcement and encouragement. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) but imposes a larger cost on the target (3 coins per unit), so it can deter defection. Rewards are also costly (2 coins per unit) and add 2 coins to the recipient; they are typically used sparingly to reinforce cooperation or welcome returnees.

Likely dynamics across rounds:
- Early rounds: mixed cooperation as players discover peers’ tendencies. Some players will punish free-riders sparingly to discourage defection.
- Middle rounds: cooperation tends to rise if punishments are credible; occasional defectors are punished and can be persuaded to return to cooperation.
- Rewards are used infrequently to reinforce cooperation or to “welcome back” a previously punished player.
- Because punishments and rewards are visible (who sent how many units to whom), reputational effects shape choices. Punishers pay a price themselves and may be rewarded socially (messages or reciprocation) but cannot recoup those costs directly.
- Overall, expect a pattern of mostly cooperative rounds with occasional single defection events followed by targeted, limited punishment and occasional small rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) from their 20-coin endowment. The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally across all 13 active players. Because the private return per coin contributed is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 < 1, each coin a selfish player contributes reduces their private payoff (private incentive to free-ride). However the social return per coin (8.1) is large, so full cooperation would produce high group payoffs.

Players do not see others' contributions directly, but at the end of each round they see their own net payoff. Given the payoff formula (net = 20 − own contribution + (8.1 × total contributions)/13), each player can back out the total contributions from their own round payoff and their own contribution. That gives informative feedback about the group's overall cooperativeness, though not who contributed what.

Given the provided personas, I predict heterogeneous behavior: some players (educated/pro-social types like DUCK, CHICKEN, OWL, MOOSE, PARROT) will start relatively cooperative and reduce slowly; others (SLOTH, CROCODILE, SNAKE) will contribute little from the start. Medium-types will start moderate and drift downward. Over a known finite horizon of 16 rounds we expect average contributions to decline over time as strategic incentives and learning push toward freeriding, with a few persistent cooperators maintaining nonzero contributions. The ability to infer total contributions from individual payoffs will accelerate coordination on lower contributions once evidence of free-riding appears.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with ten players, each given 20 coins every round. The pot multiplier (6.6) yields a per-coin return to each individual of 0.66 (6.6/10), which is less than the private cost (1), so the one-shot dominant strategy is to contribute 0. However, because players play repeated rounds without knowing the horizon, early rounds often show some cooperation due to optimism, social preferences, or explicit coordination attempts. If some players free-ride (contribute little or nothing) and others detect it (through reduced group payoffs or chat comments), cooperation tends to unravel: contributions fall as conditional cooperators retaliate or give up. Chat may contain short coordination attempts early, a few complaints or boasts when payoffs reveal differences, and sparse or absent messages later as patterns stabilize. Expect moderate contributions early, a few defections that trigger decline, a brief attempt(s) to restore cooperation, then persistent low contributions toward the middle and end of the 19-round sequence.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with ten players, each privately choosing how many of their 20 coins to contribute each round. Contributions are multiplied by 6.6 and shared equally (0.66 × total contribution to each player). After payoffs from the public pot are allocated, players can spend costly punishment units (2 coins per unit) which reduce targeted players' round payoffs by 5 coins per unit. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so early rounds typically see cautious cooperation, while later rounds often see unraveling as incentives to free-ride rise. Punishment can sustain cooperation if some players are willing to pay the enforcement cost early on, but punishment is itself costly and tends to be used sparingly—usually targeted at clear low contributors. Chat messages will be infrequent and mostly used to ask for fairness, to accuse free-riders, or to thank punishers.

I expect:
- Early rounds: many players contribute moderately-to-high amounts (conditional cooperators), a few low contributions appear, and a small number of players (those who are enforcement-minded in their persona: OWL, MOOSE, possibly GORILLA or DUCK) will punish clear undercontributors to signal that low contributions are costly.
- Middle rounds: some players test reduced contributions; punishment continues but becomes less frequent as the cost of enforcing accumulates.
- Late rounds: without a guaranteed end or strong enforcement, cooperation gradually declines and free-riding spreads; punishment drops off because it is costly and produces little benefit when most are defecting.

Chat messages will be infrequent and mostly used to ask for fairness, to accuse free-riders, or to thank punishers.

I expect:
- Early rounds: many players contribute moderately-to-high amounts (conditional cooperators), a few low contributions appear, and a small number of players (those who are enforcement-minded in their persona: OWL, MOOSE, possibly GORILLA or DUCK) will punish clear undercontributors to signal that low contributions are costly.
- Middle rounds: some players test reduced contributions; punishment continues but becomes less frequent as the cost of enforcing accumulates.
- Late rounds: without a guaranteed end or strong enforcement, cooperation gradually declines and free-riding spreads; punishment drops off because it is costly and produces little benefit when most are defecting.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated, anonymous public goods game with ten players each round. Each player keeps 20 coins and can contribute any integer 0–20 to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally. Because each coin contributed returns only 0.66 to its contributor (6.6/10), the individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, total welfare is maximized when everyone contributes everything. Players do not see others' individual contributions, but they do see their own net payoff at the end of each round and thus can infer the group's total contribution from that payoff together with their own choice. That provides noisy group-level feedback (not individual-level).

Given these incentives and limited information, a plausible unfolding is:
- Early rounds: some players propose cooperation and several contribute moderately (establishing a cooperative norm). A few players (or one) may free-ride immediately.
- Middle rounds: as players infer the true total contributions from their own payoffs, free-riding becomes tempting. If enough people reduce contributions, cooperative contributors begin to lower theirs to avoid being exploited.
- Late rounds: contributions tend to drift downward toward low or zero contributions, but a few stubborn cooperators may continue contributing small amounts or try occasional appeals via chat.
- Chat is cheap talk and will be sparse and strategic — occasional proposals, short reactions, and a few expressions of frustration or encouragement. Because the total number of rounds is unknown, some players might maintain cooperation longer than in a known finite-horizon game, but I still expect an overall decay in contributions.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 19 showing brief reasoning for expected behavior each round, the sparse chat, the predicted contributions (in the exact avatar order), and short summaries.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and a strong multiplication factor (8.1) whose benefit is shared equally. The dominant private incentive each round is to free-ride (contribute 0) because any coin kept yields the same private return as any coin contributed, but every coin contributed helps the group. With repeated interaction, conditional cooperators can sustain higher contributions if they can punish free-riders. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) but can deter or reduce future free-riding by imposing a larger cost on targets (7 coins per unit deducted). Because players see who punished them, targeted retaliation or second-order retaliation is possible, but in real PGGs punishments are typically sparse: cooperators punish clear low contributions, but they avoid excessive or perpetual punishment because it reduces their own earnings.

Early rounds often reveal heterogeneity: some players start high (cooperators), some start low (free-riders), and some moderate. Expect initial punishments targeted at obvious free-riders; if punishment is effective, contributions tend to rise and stabilize mid-game. As the game approaches the final rounds, forward-looking incentives to maintain reputation weaken and some players defect more, producing occasional late punishments but fewer reciprocal enforcement actions because the horizon shortens. Overall, expect modest but consequential punishment events concentrated when clear low contributions appear; most rounds will have no punishments or only one or two small punitive acts.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses how many of 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all 13 players. Because each coin a player contributes returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.6231 to that same player, each contributed coin reduces the contributor's private holding by 1 but gives them only ~0.62 back — contributing is socially beneficial (it raises total group earnings) but individually costly. The dominant (myopic-selfish) strategy is therefore to contribute 0, but many players in experiments initially contribute some coins out of conditional cooperation, reciprocity, or altruism.

Expected dynamics:
- Round 1: No history is available, so choices come from priors and dispositions. We expect a mix: several cooperative players will start with fairly high contributions, a few free-riders will contribute 0, and others will be in-between. The observed group contribution will likely be moderate.
- Middle rounds: Players observe their own payoffs only, not others' individual contributions, so learning is indirect. But players who see their payoffs and infer free-riding or declining group returns often cut contributions. Conditional cooperators reduce contributions if they suspect others are free-riding; defectors stick to 0. Average contributions typically fall.
- Final rounds: Toward the end, strategic incentives and frustration with free-riding push overall contributions down further; many players converge to low contributions in later rounds.
- Uncertainty: Because players only observe their own round payoff (not others' contributions), there is more ambiguity about others' behavior than in versions where full public feedback is shown. This tends to dampen sharp coordinated responses—declines may be steadier rather than abrupt—but the general downward trend is still likely.

Personas are used as soft priors (not deterministic rules). Some players here appear more inclined to cooperate (e.g., those with higher education or likely "conditional cooperator" tendencies), while others act as persistent low contributors. Over 16 rounds we therefore expect starting contributions to be moderate-to-high for several players and gradually decline toward low contributions by the final rounds.

Each coin you contribute returns 0.66 coins to you (6.6 total split among 10 players) so individually it's cheaper to keep your 20 coins and free-ride; collectively it's best if everyone contributes all 20. Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding, early rounds are noisy tests of norms. Costly punishment (2 coins per punishment unit for the punisher, 5 coins deducted per unit from the punished) provides a mechanism to deter free-riding. In practice we expect:
- Round 1: mixed contributions as players probe each other.

- If punishment is used, contributions should rise in following rounds as reputational enforcement takes effect.

- Because the number of rounds is unknown, players have incentive to maintain cooperation (no final-round collapse necessarily), so cooperation tends to stabilize at a relatively high level once norms are established.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 20 coins each per round. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all players, so the private return per coin (8.1/13 ≈ 0.623) is less than 1 — the individually rational move is to contribute 0, while the group optimum is for everyone to contribute all 20. Because choices are simultaneous, each player faces uncertainty about others' actions every round. The punishment stage allows players to sanction low contributors at a cost (3 coins per punishment unit) that inflicts a larger loss on the target (7 coins per unit). In practice, punishments are used sparingly to enforce cooperation early on, but they are costly and often decline as the end of the game approaches (players reduce costly sanctions when there is little future benefit). I therefore predict: modest-to-high initial contributions from many players, occasional low contributors (particularly those whose profiles suggest less inclination or who have low past contributions), selective punishment of repeated low contributors, maintenance of moderate cooperation while sanctions are credible, and a gradual decline in contributions toward the final rounds (with very little punishment in the final round).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and an endowment of 20 coins each round. Each coin a player contributes is pooled, multiplied by 8.1, and then split equally among the 13 players. That means each contributed coin increases each player's share by 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 — i.e., contributing 1 coin costs you 1 coin immediately but returns only ≈0.623 to you personally, so the private marginal return is negative. The group payoff is maximized by full contribution, but individual incentives favor free-riding.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds: many players often start with moderate contributions (conditional cooperators), producing a reasonably large pot. A few free-riders may contribute less immediately.
- Middle rounds: because contributing is privately costly, contributions usually drift downward as conditional cooperators respond to inferred low contributions or to the temptation to free-ride.
- Endgame: in the final rounds many players further reduce contributions (no future reciprocity), though some “always-cooperators” may keep contributing a bit.
- Uncertainties: players only see their own net payoff each round, not others' contributions directly. However, since they know their own contribution and their net payoff, they can deduce the group's share for that round (and thereby infer total contributions). This information flow can accelerate convergence to a stable pattern (often declining contributions), but heterogeneity in personas means some players stick to higher or lower contributions throughout.

I will simulate a plausible path consistent with those tendencies and the provided personas. The transcript below shows each round's reasoning, the contributions (in the exact avatar order given), and what each player sees as their own net payoff at the summary stage.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-player repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute (0–20). The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split evenly, so each coin contributed returns 0.66 to each player. Because 0.66 < 1, the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, contributions increase group payoff, so social incentives favor higher contributions. Peer punishment is costly (2 coins per punishment unit to the punisher, 5 coins deducted per unit from the target), which can deter free-riding but also discourages retaliation because punishers pay to impose costs.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players (more prosocial, higher education/older) start with moderate-to-high contributions; some (lower motivation/personas like SLOTH or younger/less cooperative players like DOG or CHICK) contribute less. Players will use sparse chat to request cooperation and call out free-riding.
- Punishment will be relatively rare but targeted — mainly directed at persistent low contributors (DOG, SLOTH, CHICK at times). Punishment units will be small (1–2 units) because each punisher has limited leftover coins each round.
- Retaliatory punishments may appear occasionally (e.g., punished players striking back), creating small cycles of punishment across a few rounds.
- Over time total contributions drift downward but intermittent punishment and social appeals keep average contributions from collapsing to zero; a minority of consistently high contributors (GORILLA, DUCK, RABBIT) sustain moderate group returns.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 anonymous players. Each round everyone privately has 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally, so each coin contributed increases the group's total payoff by 6.6 but returns only 0.66 to each individual. That creates the classic tension: contributing benefits the group more than keeping the coin, but each individual gets a smaller personal return than the 1 coin they forgo, so the dominant (self-interested, one-shot) move is to contribute 0. In repeated play, however, conditional cooperation and messaging can sustain higher contributions: if people believe others will contribute, they may do so too; if they expect free-riding, they will reduce contributions.

Uncertainties that shape behavior here:
- Players do not see others' contributions before deciding, and they only see their own net payoff after each round (not others’ payoffs or contributions). This limits direct feedback and makes inference noisy.
- Players do not know how many rounds will be played. That reduces end-game “last-round” free-riding and tends to support sustained cooperation compared with a known finite end.
- Messaging is allowed and can coordinate or shame free riders, but chat is public and costly only in time/attention; it can influence beliefs but not enforce actions.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often start with moderate-to-high contributions as some players try to establish cooperative norms.
- If several players test low contributions and others infer free-riding (from lower personal payoffs), contributions may drift downward.
- Periodic attempts to re-establish cooperation via encouraging messages can produce temporary spikes.
- Because players only see their own payoff, coordination is slow and noisy; a few consistent low contributors can push the average down over time.
- Chat will be sparse and strategic: short appeals, occasional calls-out, or disclosures of one's own contribution/payoff to build trust.

In repeated play, however, conditional cooperation and messaging can sustain higher contributions: if people believe others will contribute, they may do so too; if they expect free-riding, they will reduce contributions.

- Players do not see others' contributions before deciding, and they only see their own net payoff after each round (not others’ payoffs or contributions). This limits direct feedback and makes inference noisy.
- Players do not know how many rounds will be played. That reduces end-game “last-round” free-riding and tends to support sustained cooperation compared with a known finite end.
- Messaging is allowed and can coordinate or shame free riders, but chat is public and costly only in time/attention; it can influence beliefs but not enforce actions.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often start with moderate-to-high contributions as some players try to establish cooperative norms.
- If several players test low contributions and others infer free-riding (from lower personal payoffs), contributions may drift downward.
- Periodic attempts to re-establish cooperation via encouraging messages can produce temporary spikes.
- Because players only see their own payoff, coordination is slow and noisy; a few consistent low contributors can push the average down over time.
- Chat will be sparse and strategic: short appeals, occasional calls-out, or disclosures of one's own contribution/payoff to build trust.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players who each receive 20 coins every round and privately choose an integer contribution 0–20. Contributions are multiplied by 6.6 and the pot is split equally across the 10 players (so each contributed coin returns 0.66 to each individual). That means each coin contributed costs the individual 1 coin but yields 0.66 back — private incentive is to contribute 0, while the group would benefit if everyone contributed more. Common dynamics: early rounds often see moderate cooperation (people testing reciprocal norms, promises, or trying to coordinate). Over time, if individuals observe low returns or suspect free-riding, contributions typically decline toward free-riding (0). Occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation (one or a few players increasing contributions or sending encouraging messages) can produce temporary bumps. Because players do not know the total number of rounds while playing, end-game collapse is less pronounced early on but cooperation often decays as trust erodes. Chat tends to be sparse and used to propose targets, remind others of payoffs, or express disappointment when cooperation falls.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately gets 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among the 13 players. The per-coin private return from contributing is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, which is less than 1: every coin you contribute lowers your private retained money by 1 but raises your share by ~0.623. Therefore the individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) while the group optimum is to contribute everything.

Because players do not see others' choices before deciding but do see their own net payoff after each round, each round's summary provides indirect information about the group total (players can back out the total contributions from their own payoff and contribution). Typical dynamics: some initial cooperation (uncertainty and conditional cooperators), then gradual decline as free-riding dominates. Because players only see their private payoff, they do not observe individual peers' contributions, but they can infer the group sum and update beliefs. Expect early modest-to-high contributions from those who are more prosocial or uncertain, and a steady overall decline toward low contributions by later rounds. Occasional stabilizations or small recoveries can appear if players infer others cooperated more than expected, but the overall trend is downward.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, private endowments of 20 per round, and a multiplier (6.6) that spreads benefits across all players. Individually, contributing a coin costs you 1 coin but returns only 0.66 to you from the public pot, so the short-run selfish incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, with repeated interaction and the ability to punish (at personal cost), players can create incentives for higher contributions. Early rounds often show low or mixed contributions while players test each other's willingness to cooperate and punish. A few proactive, better-educated or more strategic players (here, PARROT, DUCK, and RABBIT) are likely to signal that they will sanction low contributors; that tends to increase contributions over subsequent rounds. Punishment is costly and typically used sparingly — targeted at extreme free-riders or repeat defectors — and sometimes triggers retaliation early on. If punishment successfully deters free-riding, group contributions converge upward and punishments become rare. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we should not see a classic endgame collapse; instead, contributions are likely to rise and then stabilize at a relatively high level, with occasional punitive responses to sudden defections.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with costly peer punishment. Each coin you contribute yields 8.1/13 ≈ 0.62 coins back to you via the group fund — so contributing is socially efficient but individually costly. Given anonymity of choices within a round and identification of punishers after the round, we expect:
- Moderately high contributions early as players hedge against being seen as exploiters and to test others' willingness to cooperate.
- Sparse, targeted punishment aimed at clear low contributors; punishment is costly, so it will be used selectively by players who care about fairness or future returns (often those who contributed more).
- If punishment successfully deters free-riding, contributions rebound or stay moderate; if punishment is absent or too weak, some players will exploit and overall contributions fall.
- Toward the final rounds (back half of 16), forward-looking players may reduce contributions (endgame effect). Some players may still punish in late rounds out of fairness or spite, but punishment frequency typically declines because it cannot change future behavior.
- Uncertainty about others' preferences and the mix of player types (some inclined to free-ride, some to enforce fairness) will produce variation across rounds; overall we expect cooperation to be sustained mid-game by occasional punishment, then decline toward the end.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and a per-round private endowment of 20 coins. The social payoff rule (total contributions multiplied by 8.1 and split equally) yields a group return of 0.6231 coins per coin contributed to any single contributor (8.1/13), which is less than the private marginal benefit of keeping the coin (1). That makes free-riding (contributing 0) the individual payoff-maximizing action in a single-shot decision, but many players still contribute for reasons such as altruism, reciprocity, warm-glow, or misperception.

Because players do not observe peers' contributions before choosing and only learn their own net payoff after each round, uncertainty about others' behavior is high. I therefore predict heterogeneity across personas: some (e.g., PARROT, OWL, COW) tend toward higher cooperation; some (SLOTH, GORILLA, SNAKE, CROCODILE) tend toward low contributions; others adopt moderate conditional cooperation and gradually reduce contributions when they infer free-riding. Typical dynamics: an initial relatively high average contribution driven by optimism/altruism, followed by gradual decline as players update beliefs about others' low contributions. Near the final rounds, a few cooperative players may either maintain or slightly increase contributions (warm-glow or endgame generosity), but overall contributions tend to be low by the end.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game gives each player 20 coins each round. Players privately choose an integer contribution (0–20). The group pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among all 10 players, so each player's share from the pot equals 0.66 × (total contributions). Each player's round payoff = (20 − their contribution) + 0.66 × (total contributions). Players do not see peers' choices before deciding, but they can send messages any time; they only see their own net payoff at the end of each round. Because the number of rounds is unknown, there is no guaranteed “last round” behavior; cooperation tends to be supported by repeated communication and conditional cooperation (people who give more when others give more), while low contributors or “free-riders” who repeatedly contribute little can drag the group average down. Expect initial reasonable cooperation from socially oriented players (older/college-educated, postgrads) and lower contributions from naturally self-interested or low-engagement players — with fluctuations driven by short messages (promises, complaints, pledges) and occasional retaliation/reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 10 players, fixed endowment (20 coins per round), a strong multiplier on the public pot (6.6) and a costly punishment stage. Individually, keeping a coin yields 1 coin privately while contributing it to the pot yields 0.66 back to each player on average — so the individual incentive is to free ride. However, because other players can punish low contributors (costly to punishers but very harmful to targets), punishment can sustain higher contributions by creating a social cost for under-contributing.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players will "test" the group (small contributions) while many try moderate-to-high contributions. First punishments will typically be directed at clear low contributors (visible after contributions are revealed).
- Middle rounds: if punishments are targeted and not overly retaliatory, contributions will rise and stabilize. If punishment triggers revenge cycles, contributions can become volatile.
- Chat will be sparse and tactical: short appeals to cooperate, occasional expressions of frustration after being punished. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, behavior tends to be stable rather than strategically collapsing at the end.
- Punishment will be used sparingly (1–3 units most times) and mainly after blatant under-contribution. Some small retaliations may occur, but large-scale sustained punitive wars are unlikely.

Now the transcript of rounds 1–19 follows. I use the exact avatar order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK for contributions everywhere.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and a strong group multiplier (8.1) that is smaller than the number of players. The private marginal return from giving one coin is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, which is less than 1: every coin you contribute lowers your private, immediate payoff (on average) even though it raises total group payoff. That creates the classic social-dilemma tension: full contributions maximize group earnings but a selfish, one-shot incentive is to contribute 0.

Important features that will shape play here:
- Players do not see other players' contributions. They only see their own contribution and their own net payoff at each round. This limited feedback makes conditional cooperation (responding directly to others' visible contributions) impossible; instead players must infer group behavior indirectly from their own payoff compared to expectations. That typically slows down coordination and makes defections harder to detect.
- Because each player only sees their own net payoff, many participants will adopt simple heuristics: some will be consistently cooperative (contribute substantial amounts from the start), some will free-ride (contribute 0), and some will slowly adjust based on whether their private payoff feels “good” or “bad” relative to their expectations.
- Given the personas, we expect a mix: several college-educated, younger personas (DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, RABBIT, OWL) are more likely to start fairly cooperative; a few with lower-engagement cues (SLOTH, MOOSE, SNAKE) will contribute little or nothing; others (GORILLA, PARROT, CROCODILE, COW, CHICK) will hover in the middle. Over rounds, because players cannot directly punish or reward others, cooperation is likely to drift downward moderately as some players experiment with lower contributions and see that their private payoff does not fall dramatically.

That creates the classic social-dilemma tension: full contributions maximize group earnings but a selfish, one-shot incentive is to contribute 0.

# GAME STARTS

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 20-coin endowments each round, and a generous group multiplier (6.6) whose benefit is split evenly. Individually, every coin contributed returns only 6.6/10 = 0.66 coins to its contributor in expectation, so the one-shot dominant action is to contribute 0. Communication (cheap talk) is allowed, which can encourage cooperation by enabling promises and coordination; however, because players do not know how many rounds remain, there is no clear final round to trigger backwards induction. Typical dynamics in similar settings start with moderate cooperation (some players contribute nonzero amounts, often 30–60% of endowment), then drift downward as free-riding appears; occasional brief rebounds can occur when a few players try to coordinate or call out low contributions. Payoff information is limited: at the summary stage each player only learns their own net payoff, so observing low personal payoffs may prompt suspicion and reduced future contributions. Given the player personas (mostly well-educated adults), expect some initial willingness to contribute and use chat to propose norms, followed by gradual attrition in contributions across rounds, with sparse but purposeful chat (promises, calls for reciprocity, mild frustration).

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players, each endowed with 20 coins per round. Contributions are privately chosen (0–20). The group pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so each contributed coin returns about 0.623 coins to each player (8.1/13), which is less than the private cost. Individually, the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute less) while hoping others contribute. However, because others’ contributions increase everyone’s payoff, group welfare is highest when players contribute more.

The punishment stage gives players the option to sanction low contributors: each punishment unit costs the punisher 3 coins and reduces the target by 7 coins. Punishment can sustain cooperation by deterring free-riding, but it is costly and can provoke retaliation because players learn who punished them. Typical dynamics: initially moderate cooperation (people unsure of others), occasional punishment of obvious low contributors, short retaliatory responses, some stabilizing enforcement in the middle rounds as norms emerge, and usually a decline toward the end as the incentive to invest in future enforcement wanes (end-game effect). Because punishment is costly, it tends to be used sparingly and targeted at clear free-riders. Given the provided persona priors (mixed ages and education, some likely to enforce fairness), I predict modest average contributions early (around half the endowment), a few targeted punishments in early rounds against the lowest contributors, a modest rise or stabilization in midgame due to enforcement, and a decline in later rounds as players discount future influence and avoid costly punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and costly peer punishment. Each round individuals choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns about 0.623 to the contributor privately. That creates the classic social dilemma: it is individually optimal to keep coins (contribute 0), but group welfare increases if many contribute. Punishment lets cooperators impose costs on free-riders at personal expense (3 coins per punishment unit for the punisher, 7 coins deducted per unit from the target). Players see only their own round payoff and punishment activity directed to/from them (they can identify who punished them), not peers’ full payoffs or contributions.

Likely unfolding (high level):
- Early rounds usually show mixed, moderate contributions as players "test" the group and reveal preferences. Some contributors will be cautious; others contribute fairly high.
- Punishment typically appears sparingly and targeted: cooperators punish obvious low contributors to enforce norms. Punishment is costly, so only a subset of players (often those who are more cooperative or more invested in norms) will send it.
- If punishment successfully deters free-riding, average contributions rise and stabilize at higher levels in the middle rounds. If punishment is too rare or too costly for enforcers, cooperation may erode.
- Knowing the game ends after round 16, many players begin to strategically lower contributions near the end (the end-game effect), and punishment tends to fall off in the final round(s) because the incentive to spend costly resources to change future behavior is gone.
- Uncertainty remains about individual types: some personas (education, age) make modest differences in expectations, but behavior varies—so I predict a modest amount of punishment concentrated on repeat low contributors, with cooperation rising midgame then declining toward the final round.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits ten players (each round: 20 private coins) against the standard tension: contributing to the group raises everyone's payoff (each coin returns 6.6 total, split across 10 players = 0.66 per coin to each player) but each individual has an incentive to free-ride (keeping 1 coin instead of receiving 0.66 back). Because punishments are available and identifiable (each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 5 coins from the target, and players learn who punished them), norms can be enforced: a small set of players who care about cooperation — or about sanctioning low contributions — can sustain higher contributions by credibly threatening punishment. Uncertainties include: players do not know the total number of rounds (so backward induction toward end-game defection is muted), and players differ in tolerance for sanctions and propensity to retaliate.

Likely overall unfolding:
- Early rounds: players test the water — many contribute moderately but some under-contribute. A few players (more cooperative personas) will publicly encourage cooperation; punishers will start imposing small sanctions on clear low contributors.
- Middle rounds: norms form. Punishers target repeat low contributors; most conditional cooperators respond by raising contributions. Punishment stays sparse but targeted — enough to keep overall contributions moderate-high.
- Occasional defections or retaliations: punished players sometimes punish back in later rounds, but heavy multi-player punishment tends to deter chronic defection.
- Late rounds: because the horizon is unknown, cooperation tends to remain fairly stable, but sporadic tests (low contributions) occur and get punished.

Now the game transcript.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 13 players against a classic social dilemma each round: they each have 20 coins and must decide how many to contribute to a shared pot. Each round the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split evenly among all 13 players. Individually, each contributed coin returns only 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to its donor, so contributing reduces a player's private holdings even though it raises the group payoff — the incentive is to free-ride. Players do not see others' contributions before deciding, but after each round they do see their own net payoff, from which they can infer total group contributions (because they know their own contribution).

Given the provided personas, I expect an initial mix of relatively high contributors (those with postgraduate or college backgrounds and pro-social priors) and a handful of low or zero contributors (more suspicious or less cooperative personas). Because players can infer total group contributions from their own payoff, conditional cooperators will likely reduce contributions if they detect low group contributions. Over repeated rounds, standard dynamics predict an overall decline in contributions as free-riding spreads and as players anticipate end-game defection. Late rounds (especially the final one) typically show near-zero contributions as the incentive to reciprocate fades.

I will simulate a plausible unfolding across 16 rounds: early rounds show moderate-to-high contributions by several players, mid-game contributions drift downward as players respond to inferred low group contributions, and the final rounds feature heavy defection with nearly all players contributing zero.

Endgame effects are stronger now; many are at or near zero contribution. Small contributions persist from a handful of cooperators but overall totals are low.
Most players have adopted a low-contribution strategy. Remaining contributors are few and small; the trend toward free-riding is strong as the horizon shortens.
With only two rounds remaining, cooperation is mostly collapsed. Small, sporadic donations may occur out of habit or persistent pro-social preferences, but many are at zero.
In the penultimate round, most players anticipate final-round defection and contribute almost nothing. A few give token amounts.
Final round: strategic reasoning typically leads nearly everyone to free-ride because there is no future to influence. Expect all-zero contributions.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players per round, private endowments of 20 each, and no known horizon. The private incentive is to keep money (contribute 0) because each coin contributed returns only 0.66 to each player on average, but group benefit favors contribution. Punishment is available and costly to both punisher (2 per unit) and target (5 per unit), so punishment is most likely to be used sparingly to deter persistent free-riders. Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation because of social norms and uncertainty about game length; a few low contributors attract targeted punishment from higher contributors. Over time, unless punishment is sustained, contributions tend to drift downward as players anticipate end-game defection and reduce their contributions. Chat is used occasionally to coordinate, praise, or accuse. Expect: modestly high contributions at first, a few punishments targeted at repeated low contributors, temporary increases after punishment, followed by gradual decay in contributions toward lower levels in later rounds and occasional spiteful or retaliatory punishments.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, private endowments of 20 each round, and a group multiplier of 6.6 split evenly. The dominant (selfish) incentive in a one-shot version is to contribute 0, because any coin kept yields a guaranteed 1 to the player while each contributed coin returns 0.66 to each player (0.66 < 1). However, in a repeated setting with an unknown horizon, players often start by cooperating (moderate contributions) because cooperation can raise everyone’s payoff and early rounds are strategically ambiguous. Over time, without enforcement or binding commitments, contributors who see relatively low personal returns (they only observe their own round payoff) will tend to reduce contributions; free-riders who benefit privately tend to maintain low contributions. Expect initial moderate contributions from generally cooperative personas (OWL, MOOSE, CHICKEN, RABBIT) and more frugal or opportunistic moves from lower-contribution types (SLOTH, PARROT). Chat is cheap talk — some players will attempt to coordinate (requests to contribute a target amount), a few will signal their own intention, and some will call out perceived free-riding, but messages are unverifiable and contributions will drift downward as rounds progress. Because players don’t know when the game will end, there will be no classic "last round" collapse at a known end; instead, a gradual erosion of cooperation is likely, with occasional attempts to rekindle contribution.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and no communication. Each round each player privately chooses how many of 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally across the 13 players. The private marginal return on one coin contributed is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 — less than 1 — so a purely selfish, myopic player gains by contributing 0 (free riding). However, group welfare is maximized when players contribute more because the multiplied pot benefits everyone.

Key dynamics and uncertainties:

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20 from their endowment of 20. The collected pot is multiplied by 8.1 and shared equally, so higher group contributions raise everyone's return; individually, though, the one-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute less) because each coin contributed returns only a fraction of its social value to the contributor. After seeing contributions each round, players can punish specific others at a private cost (3 coins per punishment unit) that imposes a larger harm on targets (7 coins per unit). Punishment is costly but can enforce cooperative norms.

Likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Early rounds typically show moderate-to-high contributions as players test norms and expectations. A few low contributors (free-riders) appear and may be punished sparingly.
- Punishment is usually rare and targeted: 1–2 units directed at clear low contributors rather than wide-scale mutual punishment, because punishment is costly.
- When punishment is used, it can raise a punished player's subsequent contributions (norm enforcement), but it can also provoke retaliation in later rounds.
- Knowing the game length (16 rounds) creates an end-game incentive to reduce contributions as players approach the final rounds; cooperation tends to erode late in the sequence.
- Uncertainty in individual motives and heterogeneous tendencies (some players more cooperative, some more inclined to punish) generates variability across rounds.

Given the player personas, I predict an initial phase of moderate cooperation with a few low contributors who attract occasional, targeted punishments; midgame cooperation holds when punishers intervene; towards the final third, contributions trend down and punishment declines as players expect less return from costly enforcement.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with ten players who get 20 coins each round, privately choose integer contributions (0–20), see the sum payoff after a 6.6 multiplier is split evenly, then can pay to reduce others' payoffs via costly punishment. Important incentives and likely dynamics:

- The social optimum is high contributions: every coin contributed returns 6.6/10 = 0.66 coins to each player, so the group benefits when players contribute. Individually, however, each contributed coin reduces a player's private holding by 1 while returning only 0.66, creating a temptation to free-ride.
- Punishment changes incentives: sending a punishment unit costs the sender but reduces a target's payoff more. Even occasional punishment can sustain higher contributions because low contributors can be sanctioned and lose more than the short-run gain from free-riding.
- Early rounds typically feature moderate contributions and testing behavior (some try small contributions to see others' responses). If a few players free-ride and are punished, contributions tend to rise and stabilize. Punishment is used sparingly in real games — enough to deter repeated defection but not so much as to destroy cooperation.
- Uncertainty: players do not know the total number of rounds, which reduces a sharp end-game collapse. Observability is limited (players see only the final summary after each round, not decisions before making their own); retaliatory punishment is possible because punishers are identified, but retaliation is not universally common.
- Social roles (from the personas) shape priors: PARROT (postgraduate) behaves like a conditional cooperator/enforcer (punishes low contributors), SLOTH is more likely to under-contribute intermittently, MOOSE/RABBIT/DUCK tend toward cooperation, DOG/OWL may be inconsistent, and GORILLA acts as steady, moderate cooperator who may occasionally punish obvious low contributions.

Expect a trajectory where contributions start mixed, punishments appear in early rounds against clear low contributors, and contributions converge toward high levels (but with occasional lapses that trigger targeted punishment). Chat messages are sparse and tend to be short requests, explanations, or mild reproaches.

game_explanation:
This is a finite, repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and costly bilateral punishment. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) from their 20-coin endowment. Contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all players; because 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, every single coin you contribute returns less than 1 coin to yourself even though the group's total gain from a contribution (8.1) exceeds the private cost. That creates a classic tension: individually, the dominant incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0), but collectively everyone is better off if people contribute more.

Punishment can change incentives: each punishment unit costs the punisher 3 coins and reduces the target's round income by 7 coins. Punishment is therefore costly and typically used sparingly — often by players who want to discipline persistent low contributors. Because the game lasts a known finite number of rounds, we expect some cooperative behavior early on (conditional on the threat of punishment) and a gradual erosion of contributions toward the end (an “endgame” effect). Uncertainty about others' types and the initial pattern of contributions matters: early low contributions can trigger punishment, which can either stabilize cooperation or provoke retaliation cycles. Players who are observed punishing tend to be those willing to pay a cost to uphold contribution norms (in this transcript: PARROT, MOOSE, OWL, RABBIT occasionally), while a few players occasionally act as repeat free-riders (e.g., intermittent low contributions from SLOTH, GORILLA, CROCODILE, SNAKE) and occasionally provoke punishment.

I will now simulate the 16-round transcript. At the start of each round I give a brief expectation for why players act as they do. Contributions are listed in the exact avatar order given in <PLAYERS>. Punishment lists include only non-zero edges and are intentionally sparse to reflect real-game tendencies.
