game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game creates a tension between individual incentives and group benefit. Each coin a player keeps yields a sure personal value of 1, but every coin contributed increases the group's pot by a factor of 6.6 and that total is split equally among the 10 players (so each contributed coin returns 0.66 to each player). The private marginal return to contributing one coin is therefore 0.66 for the whole group but only 0.66 to the contributor — less than the 1-coin private cost — so the strictly dominant action is to contribute 0. In contrast, if everyone contributed, payoffs would be much higher for all.

Because players only see their own round payoff (not others' payoffs or individual contributions), feedback is noisy: a high personal payoff can come either from one's own contribution combined with others' cooperation or from free riding on others' large contributions. That ambiguity tends to produce initial experimentation and conditional cooperation (some players try to set a cooperative norm), followed commonly by decline in contributions as free riding becomes attractive and hard-to-detect. Communication can help sustain cooperation if credible, but because players can misreport their own payoffs or intentions, messages are only partially persuasive.

Given the provided personas, I predict an initial phase where several idealistic/conditional-cooperators (RABBIT, CHICK, DUCK, PARROT, SLOTH) start with moderate-to-high contributions and try to encourage others. Some more skeptical or pragmatic players (OWL, GORILLA, MOOSE) contribute little from the start. Over rounds we should see contributions fall as free riding appears profitable; occasional messages by cooperative types trigger short-lived recoveries, but without hard enforcement the overall trend drifts downward toward low contributions by later rounds.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game presents a classic collective-action tension. Each round, players privately decide how many of 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among all active players. The marginal per-capita return from contributing is 0.66 per coin (less than 1), so any single player maximizes their own round payoff by contributing 0; however, the group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes more. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, reciprocity, reputation-building and conditional cooperation can support higher contributions early on; conversely, observing low returns or hearing evidence of free-riding tends to trigger decline. Communication is allowed but non-binding; sparse, earnest messages from more cooperative-seeming players can sustain cooperation briefly, while a few persistent low contributors (or a single clear defection) commonly initiates a downward spiral. Expect initial moderate contributions with some leadership (a few players contributing above average), a few conditional cooperators who adjust up or down in response to the group, and gradual drift toward lower contributions as trust erodes — punctuated by occasional attempts to re-coordinate via chat.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and a return multiplier of 8.1 split evenly across players. Each coin you contribute returns, on average, 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to yourself through the group pot — less than the private value of 1 — so the dominant short-run incentive is to keep coins (free-ride). However, if many players contribute, everyone can earn much more collectively. Because contributors can punish low contributors at a cost (3 coins per punishment unit to the punisher, 7 coins removed from the target), punishment can be used to enforce higher contributions: cooperators sometimes pay to sanction defectors to sustain cooperation.

Key dynamic expectations I use to generate the transcript:
- Early rounds typically show moderate contributions (people test others), and visible low contributions are sometimes met with small punishments from those who prefer cooperation.
- If punishment is effective, mid-game contributions often rise and stabilize as defectors are deterred; punishments are sparse and typically small.
- Toward the end (final rounds), the "end-game effect" often reduces incentives to punish (no future interactions) and contributions tend to drift down as some players free-ride.
- Punishment is costly and thus used sparingly; only a few targeted punishments occur in rounds with clear low contributions.
- Individual heterogeneity matters: some players (based on the personas) are slightly more likely to punish or stick to cooperative contributions, others defect occasionally.

The transcript below follows these expectations: modest initial contributions, a few targeted punishments early on to deter clear defectors, higher cooperation in the middle rounds with sparse sanctioning, and some decline near the end with limited punishment.

Some players adjust after round 1 feedback. The previously punished low contributor (CROCODILE) tends to raise contribution modestly; a couple of low contributors may still be singled out for light sanctions.
With a pattern emerging, cooperators increase contributions slightly. Persistent low contributions (here, DOG) may draw slightly stronger punishment to signal norm enforcement.
Punishment appears effective: low contributors respond by increasing contributions. With cooperation rising, fewer punishments are needed and many rounds can have none.
Cooperation is generally high, but occasional downward deviations occur. A small number of players may receive a short reminder sanction if they dip too low relative to the group.
Punished players often revert to the group norm. Small targeting of low contributors (when they appear) continues; otherwise rounds remain largely sanction-free.

Mid-to-late game: cooperation remains fairly strong. When contributions are broadly similar, punishment becomes very rare because the norm is stable.

As the end approaches, some players begin to test the norm again (end-game effects). Small targeted punishments may reappear but are generally limited because punishers also weigh costs in a finite-horizon game.

With just a few rounds left, contributions show modest decline for some; cooperators may still apply a small sanction to particularly low contributors, but overall punishment remains light.
As the horizon shortens further, more players reduce contributions anticipating the final rounds. Punishment continues to be sparse because it is costly and has limited future benefit.
Penultimate round: many anticipate the final round and trim contributions; punishments are usually very limited as the return on influencing future behavior declines.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the total contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all 13 players. Individually, each coin contributed returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.62 to the contributor, which is less than 1, so the selfish short-run incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride). Socially, maximal collective payoff occurs when everyone contributes more.

Given the player personas (many postgraduate, mixed ages) and the large group size, I expect an initial moderate level of contributions driven by social preferences and uncertainty about others. Because players do not see others' choices and only learn their own net payoff each round, information about others' behavior is noisy; players must infer group behavior from personal payoffs. That typically slows precise coordination and encourages some to reduce contributions over time. Over rounds we should therefore see an early cooperative phase followed by a gradual (then accelerating) decline toward low or zero contributions as free-riding dominates. Occasional small experiments or brief rebounds are possible, but the overall trend is downward. In the final rounds many players are likely to contribute very little or nothing.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each round. Each coin a player contributes is multiplied by 6.6 and then split equally among the 10 players, so each coin contributed returns 0.66 to each player. The social optimum is for everyone to contribute everything (each coin yields 6.6 total), but the private, per-coin return (0.66) is less than keeping the coin (1), so there is a strong individual incentive to free-ride. Communication is allowed, which can sustain cooperation via promises or coordination, but messages are non-binding and players do not see others' contributions directly—only their own round payoff is shown at the summary stage. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect some mix of conditional cooperation and opportunistic free-riding:

- Early rounds: friendly, hopeful messages and relatively high contributions from cooperative players (PARROT, MOOSE, CHICKEN, CHICK) and tentative signals from leaders (GORILLA, RABBIT).
- Middle rounds: occasional defections as strategic players test free-riding; some players reduce contributions in response (conditional cooperators), producing lower total contributions.
- Late rounds: mixed behavior — attempts to rebuild cooperation via chat (PARROT), occasional short-run defections by strategic players, and modest fluctuations rather than full collapse. Because players can't tell how many rounds remain, we expect no guaranteed final-round “all defect,” but some endgame increases in selfishness can appear.

Transcript below shows predicted chat (sparse) and the contributions each round in the exact avatar order: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0 to 20 (from their 20-coin endowment). The group pot (sum of all contributions) is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among the 13 players, so the private return on each coin contributed is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623. Because 0.623 < 1, the individually rational (myopic, selfish) best response is to contribute 0 each round; yet if everyone contributes more, the group payoff is larger (social optimum: all contribute 20).

Given these incentives and the fact that players do not see other players' contributions (they only see their own net payoff at the end of each round), we expect:
- Early rounds: some players will experiment with positive contributions (especially those whose priors/personas suggest more cooperative tendencies), while others will try low or zero contributions to free-ride.
- Mid game: because players can infer something about total contributions from their own net payoff but not who contributed, conditional cooperation is harder. Many cooperative players will see that contributing reduces their private earnings relative to low contributors and will reduce contributions over time. Overall average contributions will tend to decline across rounds.
- Late rounds / endgame: with horizon known (16 rounds), players will increasingly defect, and most will contribute 0 in the final rounds.
- Heterogeneity: some players (postgraduate, older, or more “pro-social” personas) will persist with small positive contributions across many rounds, but they will typically be a minority. Others (younger, lower-education, or risk-averse personas) will gravitate to low or zero contributions early and stay there.

Uncertainties: because players only observe their own net payoff each round, they must infer total group behavior from that single number. That limits precise conditional strategies and favors straightforward heuristics (give a little, give none). The transcript below simulates a plausible path consistent with these dynamics: an initially mixed cooperation level that gradually declines toward widespread free-riding by the end, with a few consistent but small contributors remaining.

game_explanation:
This public goods game will likely show tension between cooperation (high contributions that raise everyone’s payoff via the 6.6 multiplier) and individual incentives to free-ride (keep endowment privately). Early rounds will feature testing and some tentative cooperation. Because punishment is available and identifiable, players who care about group returns (or about fairness) will punish clear low contributors, but punishment is costly and therefore used sparingly. Some players (those with “sloth” or skeptical personas) will test by contributing little occasionally; others (young/college-educated, or postgraduate players) will tend to contribute more and sometimes bear punishment costs to enforce norms. Over 19 rounds we should see cooperation fluctuate: initial moderate-to-high contributions, occasional low contributions triggering targeted punishments, temporary improvements in cooperation after punishment, and modest erosion or variability late in the game as players continue testing. Chat will be sparse and targeted (requests to cooperate, mild reprimands, brief thanks).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and private feedback: after each round players learn only their own net payoff (which lets them infer the group's total contribution but not who contributed what). Individually, contributing costs 1 coin per coin contributed but returns only 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 coins to the contributor, so the dominant-strategy (selfish) choice is to free-ride (contribute 0). However many players have conditional or social preferences and may start by contributing; observing their own payoff (which reflects the group's total) allows them to update beliefs about others and adjust in subsequent rounds.

Likely dynamics: early rounds will show a mixture of fairly high contributions from socially-minded / trusting players and near-zero contributions from some free-riders. Because players can infer the group total from their payoff, conditional cooperators will gradually reduce contributions if they infer others are free-riding; free-riders will typically maintain low contributions. Over repeated rounds we therefore expect an overall downward trend toward low contributions, with a few consistent contributors (slowly decreasing) and several persistent zeros. Toward the final rounds, some players may give a small final contribution if they value the public good or behave altruistically, but the group average should be low by the end.

Players infer a high group total from their payoffs. Some who contributed little may persist in low contributions (free-riding), while conditional cooperators may remain high but slightly reduce contributions expecting some freeriding. Slight overall decline is likely.

Conditional cooperators have seen that not everyone contributed as much as in round 1; they tend to trim contributions. Persistent free-riders stay low. The group total declines further but remains moderate because several high contributors persist.

With further evidence of declining group totals, many cooperative players reduce contributions more noticeably. Some resilient contributors still put in substantial amounts but overall the trend is downward.

Conditional cooperators react to inferred lower group totals; contributions slide further. Those who consistently free-ride remain at or near zero. A few mid-range contributors remain willing to contribute but at reduced levels.
The private-payoff feedback makes it clear the group total has been declining. Many conditional cooperators reduce further; a core of contributors remains but the group share continues to fall.
The downward drift continues: contributors trim contributions further, while several players maintain zeros. The group total is now clearly much lower than in the early rounds.
By round 8, many players have converged toward low contributions. A small core still gives modest amounts, but the group total is now a fraction of round 1's level.
Many players now contribute very little or nothing. Returns from the pot are substantially smaller, and private incentives to free-ride dominate for most players.

Towards the middle-late part of the game, most players expect others to give little. Contributions are low and the marginal payoff from the pot is small; a few contributors still give modest amounts, perhaps from inertia or pro-social preference.
As the game approaches the final third, contributions are low; conditional cooperators who saw little cooperation earlier contribute minimally or zero. A small core still gives tiny amounts.

Most players now behave almost selfishly; a few small contributors keep giving token amounts. The total is small and many players get payoffs close to the end-of-game fallback (their private holdings plus a small share).
Very low contributions now; many give 0. A few remaining contributors give tiny amounts out of principle or habit, but the public good is minimally funded.
Near the final rounds cooperation collapses for most. A couple of players give a token amount, perhaps from stubborn pro-social preferences or to signal a last-minute contribution.
Final two rounds produce minimal contributions. Some players give a last small amount out of preference; many give zero, anticipating no return and limited future interaction.

Last round: with no future repercussions, most players revert fully to the selfish motive and contribute 0; a tiny handful might give one last coin for intrinsic reasons, but the group total will be negligible.

game_explanation:
This 16-round public goods game pits 13 players against the temptation to free-ride. Each round, players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players can pay to punish others (each punishment unit costs the punisher 3 coins and reduces the punished player's tokens by 7). Because punishment is costly but can enforce cooperation, we typically see: some initial heterogeneity in contributions, scattered punishment aimed at clear low contributors, and gradual convergence toward higher contributions as sanctions discipline persistent free-riders. However, late in the game players may exploit the endgame by free-riding because future retaliation is limited. Punishment is usually used sparingly — a few 1-unit sanctions from cooperators to obvious defectors — and retaliatory cycles can appear but are not constant.

Below I simulate a plausible full transcript. Each round begins with a short explanation of expected incentives and then lists the predicted contributions and any punishments observed that round. Contributions follow the exact avatar order given. Punishment entries only show non-zero actions as tuples (SOURCE, TARGET, UNIT).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 20 private coins each round, an 8.1 multiplication of the group pot, and costly bilateral punishment after contributions are revealed. Key incentives:
- Contributing increases the group's pot, but individual private return from one coin contributed is (8.1 / 13) ≈ 0.623, less than 1, so the self-interested best reply is to contribute 0 given beliefs others' contributions. Collective best outcome is full contribution.
- Punishment lets players deter free-riding by imposing a heavy cost on targets (−7 per punishment unit) at a smaller personal cost (−3 per unit). Punishment is costly to the punisher but can sustain cooperation if used selectively.
- Uncertainty: players do not see others' contributions before deciding, so contributions reflect priors and learning from past rounds' summaries (and any punishments they suffered). Because punishers are identifiable, punished players can retaliate later.
Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: moderate to fairly high contributions by socially motivated or well-educated players; a few low contributors appear.
- Punishment will be used sparingly and targeted on clear low contributors to enforce norms. This will cause some short-term retaliation, producing a small sequence of counter-punishments.
- Over time, contributions typically drift down as cooperation is hard to sustain without strong, costly enforcement; punishment becomes less frequent late in the game as its cost is not worth it when the horizon is short.
- Final round: little punishment (no future leverage) and contributions tend to be low.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game creates a tension between individual incentives (keep private endowment) and group incentives (contribute to a pot that gets multiplied by 6.6 and shared equally). Because contributions are made privately each round, players face uncertainty about how cooperative others will be. Typical dynamics: some players start cooperative (or idealistic) and others free-ride; cooperators then react to observed free-riding, producing a gradual decline in contributions unless group coordination or repeated appeals sustain cooperation. Messages can help stabilize cooperation if enough players trust each other, but one or two persistent free-riders usually erode contributions over time. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there is no obvious final-round collapse, so behavior is driven by reciprocity, persuasion, and occasional fatigue.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, each given 20 coins per round. Contributing one coin returns 6.6 coins to the group pot which is split equally among the 10 players, so each contributed coin gives 0.66 back to every player. Private incentives favor free-riding (0.66 < 1 net private return per coin), but group welfare rises with contributions. Because players can punish others at a cost (2 coins per punishment unit paid by the punisher, 5 coins deducted from the target per unit), we expect some targeted sanctioning of low contributors. Players do not know how many rounds the game will include, so there's no guaranteed end-game unraveling — decisions will evolve from beliefs about others’ typical contributions and previous punishments.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: mixed behavior. Some players (say PARROT, CHICKEN, DOG, RABBIT) will favor higher contributions; a few (DUCK, OWL, MOOSE at times) may test by contributing very little.
- Punishment will be used sparingly but effectively: when someone contributes near 0 repeatedly, several cooperators will spend punishment units to deter free-riding. Punishment is costly, so it's used mostly against clear, repeated low contributors rather than small deviations.
- Communication will be sparse and functional: short pledges, accusations, or appeals to cooperate.
- Over many rounds contributions may fluctuate: occasional enforcement will maintain a moderate level of cooperation, but without strong centralized coordination contributions tend to drift downward, with occasional recoveries after coordinated punishment or persuasive messages.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment. Each round, every player privately chooses either to contribute their full endowment (20) or to keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six players, so cooperative contributions benefit the entire group but are individually costly. Because players can punish after seeing contributions, the game usually features a tension between short-term incentives to free-ride (contribute 0) and longer-term enforcement of cooperation via punishment. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per punishment unit) and even costlier to the punished (5 coins per unit), so players will tend to punish sparingly — mainly to deter repeated free-riding or to retaliate against punishers. Messaging can be used to signal intent or to call out free-riders; such messages are typically short and strategic.

Given the provided personas, I expect that most players will cooperate frequently (contribute 20) because the multiplier makes cooperation worthwhile collectively and because punishments are available. A few early defections are likely as some players test the group; these will typically provoke targeted, modest punishment (1 unit) from multiple contributors. That pattern usually pushes defectors to return to cooperation. Occasional retaliatory punishments can occur (especially by someone who was punished before), but overall punishment rates remain low after cooperation is established.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with optional costly rewards. Each player gets 20 coins per round and decides how many to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally among the six players. Players know the game lasts two rounds, so early-round signals and rewards can shape second-round contributions. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others' intentions before choosing contributions, from the costliness of rewarding, and from the fact that reward recipients cannot identify who rewarded them. Expect moderate-to-high contributors to be selectively rewarded (sparsely) — especially clearly pro-social players — and for some players to adjust contributions upward in round 2 if they see rewards concentrate on cooperators. game_explanation: This public goods game puts six players in repeated choices where each round everyone begins with 20 coins, withdraws some privately (keeping that amount), and leaves the remainder as a group contribution. The total contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly, so individual incentives push toward withdrawing (private gain) while group gains come from leaving coins in the pot. Because contributions are not observable, players can't directly punish or reward identified free-riders — reward decisions will be based on priors, expectations, and how players feel about reciprocity. Rewards are costly to give (2 coins per reward unit) but directly benefit recipients (2 coins per unit), so they're used sparingly to signal appreciation or to encourage cooperators. Over 19 rounds we typically expect some early cooperation from more prosocial players, some free-riding (especially by a player with a “SLOTH” profile), a few targeted positive rewards among perceived cooperators, and a gradual erosion of average contributions as players learn they can't reliably identify or discipline defectors — with a likely end-game drop in cooperation near round 19.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated binary public goods game with unknown horizon. Each round everyone chooses 0 or 20 from a private 20-coin endowment. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six players. Because contributors forgo their private 20, non‑contributors who still receive the group share typically out-earn contributors in mixed groups. That creates a tension: group-optimal behavior is to all contribute, but individual incentives push toward free-riding if others contribute. Early rounds often show some cooperation (people try to build trust), but visible payoff differences between contributors and non‑contributors tend to erode cooperation over time unless players coordinate with messages. Given the personas: GORILLA (assertive/postgrad) is likely to keep pushing for contributions and to send encouraging messages; DUCK and SLOTH may contribute initially but can defect if they see exploitation; CHICKEN and DOG are more likely to defect early and stay defecting; PARROT may be chatty and switch between contributing and free-riding. Expect a mix: initial cooperation, a drop as free-riding is rewarded, some attempts to rebuild cooperation via messages, occasional short-lived recoveries, and at least one coordinated push to full cooperation if persuasion succeeds.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 6 players, fixed partner matching and 19 known rounds. The key tensions:

- Incentive: With 1.8 multiplier and equal split, contributed coins yield less private return per coin than keeping them (private keep = 1 coin retained; public return per contributed coin = 1.8/6 = 0.3 to each player, or 1.8 total split among 6). So purely self-interested myopic play favors withdrawing everything (contributing 0). But collective payoffs are higher when many contribute.
- Dynamics: In early rounds we often see conditional cooperation — many players start moderate-to-high hoping to establish cooperation. Low contributors (free-riders) tend to appear and, if visible over rounds, can provoke social responses: messages, occasional rewards for cooperators, or punishment for free-riders.
- Punishment/reward: Punishing is costly (2 to punish for -3 to target) and imperfect (players don't see who punished them in the summary), so it's used sparingly to deter persistent free-riding. Rewards are cheaper in effect (2 cost → +2 to target) and are sometimes used to encourage cooperation.
- Uncertainties: Players don't know others' intentions; chat can coordinate but is non-binding. As the known end approaches, cooperation typically unravels (end-game effect): contributions decline in later rounds because incentives to punish or reward drop and backward induction reduces cooperation.
- Expected overall path: Moderate contributions early (12–16), occasional targeted punishments or small rewards if a persistent low contributor emerges, stabilization of relatively high contributions for several rounds if enforcement works, then a gradual decline through the late rounds and a collapse in the final round(s).

game_explanation:
This is a finite repeated public-goods game in which each player privately chooses either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to a shared pot or to keep it. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across all six players. Because an individual's personal return from contributing (3.7*20/6 ≈ 12.33) is less than the private cost of contributing (20), the one-shot dominant strategy for a selfish player is to keep their 20 (free-ride). However, everyone contributing maximizes total group payoff (socially efficient). Key dynamics and uncertainties to expect:

- Early rounds often show some level of cooperation as players test each other and signal intent (especially when chat is available).
- Because players cannot see choices before deciding and do not know the total number of rounds, cooperation can persist if players believe others will reciprocate. But typical behavior is a decline in contributions over rounds as free-riding spreads or cooperative signals are exploited.
- Chat allows for promises, coordination attempts, and short grudges. Messages tend to be sparse and targeted (e.g., "I'll contribute" or "Why did you defect?").

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with optional costly punishment and costly rewards. Each coin a player contributes returns less than 1 coin to themselves (the group multiplier divided by 6), so the selfish one-shot incentive is to contribute 0. However social preferences, norms, and the ability to punish or reward others mean we usually see some positive contributions. With punishment available (costly to sender but quite damaging to the target: sender pays 4, target loses 10), players who care about fairness or want to sustain cooperation are likely to use a small number of punitive actions against obvious free-riders. Rewards are also possible (cost 4, target gains 3) and tend to be used more sparingly because they are relatively inefficient for the sender.

Typical unfolding here:
- Round 1: Some players will contribute relatively high amounts (those inclined to cooperate), a few will contribute modest or zero amounts. A small number of punishments are likely targeted at the clearest free-riders; a couple of rewards may be sent to high contributors. Chat is sparse — one or two normative statements or requests to cooperate.
- Between rounds: Players see everyone's contributions and their own round payoff/punish/reward activity (but not who acted against them). That information will cause modest adjustments. Free-riders punished in round 1 may raise contributions in round 2 to avoid further punishment, or they may continue to under-contribute (especially since round 2 is the last round). Because this is the final round, the forward-looking incentive to cooperate is lower; however, moralizing and punitive motives can still produce punishment in round 2, though typically less often than in longer games.

Uncertainties: exact contribution levels and who sends punishments/rewards are noisy — some profiles are more likely to free-ride, others more likely to lead or to punish. I'll simulate a plausible sparse pattern consistent with these tendencies.

game_explanation: This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly. Individually, the best action is to contribute 0 (avoid the personal cost) while the group does best if everyone contributes everything. Because the game has two rounds and a punishment/reward stage after contributions, players can try to enforce cooperation or retaliate across rounds. Punishment is harsh on targets (−10 per unit) but costly to the punisher (−4 per unit); rewards are modestly beneficial to targets (+3 per unit) while also costing 4 per unit. Typical dynamics: some players (conditional cooperators) will contribute moderately and may punish obvious free-riders sparingly to deter them; strongly cooperative types may contribute a lot and sometimes reward fellow cooperators. Free-riders may either remain uncooperative or respond to punishment by increasing contributions in round 2. Chats are usually sparse and focused on intentions or short responses. Punishments and rewards tend to be limited in number because they’re costly.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game with six players, known horizon (19 rounds), and the option to reward others normally produces a mix of cooperation and attrition. Early rounds typically show relatively high contributions as players try to establish a cooperative norm. Because rewards cost the sender and benefit only the recipient, they are used sparingly to reinforce high contributors (positive reciprocity) rather than to punish free-riders directly; cooperators reward other cooperators, which can sustain cooperation but at a personal cost. Mid-game often shows fluctuation: a few low contributors can cause others to reduce contributions or step up targeted rewards to shore up cooperative behavior. Toward the end of the game (final few rounds), forward-looking players tend to withdraw cooperation (the “endgame” effect) because future retribution or reciprocation is limited. Chat is sparse and usually aimed at coordinating or calling out low contributors; rewards are relatively rare and targeted.

game_explanation:
This 19-round public goods game gives players a repeated opportunity to decide how much of a common 20-coin endowment to withdraw privately (the remainder is contributed to the public pot). Contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally across the six players. After seeing their own round payoff they can spend coins to punish (cost 2 per unit to sender, -3 to target) or reward (cost 2 per unit to sender, +2 to target) others. Players cannot see others' decisions before choosing their own contribution and cannot tell who punished/rewarded them afterward — they only see their own net payoff and their own punishment/reward spending/receipts.

Likely unfolding and incentives:
- Early rounds typically feature exploratory behavior: many contribute at moderate-to-high levels while a few test the waters by withdrawing more (free-riding). Because the multiplier (1.8) gives a social return of 0.18 per coin per other player (1.8/6 = 0.3 per coin to each including sender), contributing is collectively beneficial but individually costly relative to withdrawing.
- Punishment and reward let cooperators try to enforce norms. In practice, punishments are costly and used sparingly; rewards are also limited, but used to reinforce high contributors.
- Over repeated rounds, we expect conditional cooperators (who match others) to sustain moderate cooperation, while occasional defectors (e.g., low contributors) get targeted with a few punishments which often raises their contributions afterward. Rewards flow occasionally to high contributors.
- Toward the end (final few rounds), backward-induction and declining incentives usually reduce contributions; players often cut back because future enforcement is limited.

I simulate plausible contributions and the sparse, targeted use of punishment and reward across 19 rounds, showing chat when players send brief messages. Each round shows contributions (in the exact avatar order), any punishment/reward edges, and the private summary that each player would see for themselves.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game where each player must choose to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or to keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly among the six players, so cooperators increase everyone’s payoffs but defectors (who keep their 20) can gain more in a single round unless other players punish them. Because punishment is costly to the sender (2 coins per unit) but costly to the target (5 coins per unit deducted), players will typically use punishment sparingly and mainly to deter repeated free-riding. Expect early testing (one or two players free-riding), swift targeted punishments by at least a couple of cooperators, and then a pattern of mostly cooperation with occasional one-off defections followed by short punishment responses. Chat will be scarce and utilitarian: quick requests, reproaches or brief apologies. Players who were punished will be able to see who punished them; that information will guide whether they switch to contributing in subsequent rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 2-round public goods game with a weak group multiplier (2.2) shared among 6 players, so each coin contributed returns about 0.367 coins to the contributor but increases the group's pot. The dominant selfish action in any single round is to contribute 0, but social preferences, fairness, and the availability of punishment and reward can sustain positive contributions. Because the game lasts only two rounds, forward-looking incentives to cooperate are limited: players may use round 1 to signal or sanction, but in round 2 many will free-ride knowing there is no future. Punishment is costly to the punisher (4 coins per unit) but inflicts a larger loss on the target (10 coins per unit), so players willing to enforce norms may use small punishments against obvious free-riders. Rewards are relatively inefficient (cost 4 to give 3), so they tend to be used even more sparingly than punishment. Chats are likely to include short statements of intent or complaints after punishments. Overall I expect a mix of moderate first-round contributions with one or two targeted punishments of an evident zero contributor, followed by lower contributions and little to no punishment in the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public goods game where each player decides each round whether to contribute their whole endowment (20 coins) or keep it. Contributing benefits the group because contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and shared, but contributors are individually exploited when others keep their endowment. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect the following dynamics:

- Early rounds: some players will attempt to build cooperation by proposing contributions. Those who try to cooperate can be exploited and will likely reduce their contributions afterward.
- Mid-game: cooperation will tend to be fragile — occasional small coalitions of contributors will form when trust briefly reappears, often led by a few repeat cooperators.
- Later rounds: without knowledge of the endpoint, cooperation can re-emerge sporadically (experiments, reciprocity), but overall many players will default to free-riding because that gives higher immediate payoff unless a clear, stable coalition forms.
- Chat will be sparse and used mainly to propose cooperation, to complain about being exploited, or to signal willingness to try again.

Expected incentives: a unilateral defector (keeps 20) always earns 20 more than a unilateral contributor given the same group contributions (because contributors pay 20). The group payoff is higher when more contribute, but individual temptation to defect is strong. Players observe round payoffs of everyone at the summary stage, so they can infer who contributed in past rounds and respond accordingly.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players for 19 rounds. Each round everyone effectively decides how much of the 20 coins to keep privately (withdraw) and how much to leave in the shared pot (contribution). The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly, so high contributions increase everyone's shared return but are individually costly. After redistribution players can spend costly reward units (each unit costs the giver 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins). Players also can send chat messages; reward receipts are anonymous (players see amounts they received but not who gave them).

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players test the waters. Some will pledge cooperation publicly via chat and contribute high amounts; others (or a few) may free-ride to see whether they are punished or socially sanctioned.
- Mid-game: if cooperative signals and some rewarding behavior reinforce contributors, cooperation can stabilize or rebound. If free-riding persists and rewards are sparse (or costly), contributions tend to fall.
- Late-game (final few rounds): standard endgame incentives predict declining cooperation as players stop expecting future reciprocal benefits. Even so, some players may attempt a late cooperative push or symbolic rewards.
- Rewards will be used sparingly because they are costly, typically to encourage or thank visible cooperators (from chat pledges) rather than to perfectly enforce cooperation. Chat will be infrequent but used for pledges, reminders, and occasional accusations or thanks.

The transcript below uses the player order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT for every contribution list. Contributions are integers 0–20. Reward lists show only non-zero edges as (source, target, unit).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players and 19 known rounds. Each player decides how much to keep (private) and how much to contribute to the shared pot (0–20). The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each player's per-round payoff depends both on their own kept coins and the group's total contribution. After seeing contributions, players can punish (costly to sender, harsher to target) or reward (costly to sender, beneficial to target). Players also can send messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Some players will behave as consistent cooperators (high contributions) because they value the multiplied return for everyone and may want to sustain cooperation.
- Others will test or exploit the group by contributing little (free-riders), gaining larger private amounts while still receiving the public return.
- Early rounds often show mixture of cooperation and free-riding. If free-riding persists, cooperators sometimes use targeted punishment to deter it; punishment is used sparingly because it is costly to the punisher.
- Rewards are used even more sparingly — typically to acknowledge improvements or steady cooperators.
- With a fixed end at round 19, contributions tend to fall near the final round(s) (last-round effect), as the incentive to maintain reputation disappears.
- Messages will be sparse but may include requests, short appeals to cooperate, or shaming after low contributions.

Below is a round-by-round transcript predicting contributions, occasional chat, targeted punishments and rewards (only non-zero edges shown), and what each player sees in their private round summary.

game_explanation:
This six-player public goods game combines a simple contribute-or-defect decision (20 coins or 0 each round) with costly punishment after outcomes are revealed. With a multiplier of 3.7 and equal split, contributors increase the group pot but individually earn less from the public pot than a defector does in the same round, so free-riding is privately attractive. Because players don't know how many rounds remain, early rounds typically show uncertainty: some players contribute to signal cooperation, while others test for exploitable patterns. Punishment is the mechanism to enforce cooperation: costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) but punitive for the target (5 coins per unit). Expect sparse, targeted punishments early (warnings) and sharper, more coordinated responses if a player repeatedly defects. Chat will be used occasionally to signal intentions, call out defectors, and coordinate informal norms. Over 12 rounds I predict oscillation early (mixed contributions, selective punishment), followed by periods of near-full cooperation with occasional single defections that are usually punished modestly to maintain cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players. Each round every player has 20 coins and must choose either to contribute all 20 to the group pot or keep all 20 privately. The group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and divided equally among all 6 players. The individual return from contributing (3.7/6 ≈ 0.617 of your 20 → about 12.33 per coin-20 contribution) is less than the private retention (20), so the dominant (selfish) incentive is to free-ride: keep your 20 while others possibly contribute.

That creates the classic tension: if everyone contributes, all players get more than if everyone defects (collective benefit), but any single player can improve their own round payoff by defecting while others contribute. Because players do not know how many rounds will be played, typical dynamics often begin with some cooperation (experimentation, optimistic signals) and then cooperation tends to decay as contributors see they were exploited. Cheap talk (chat) can sometimes sustain cooperation if groups coordinate, but messages are non-binding. Expect sparse chat: occasional offers to cooperate, a few expressions of frustration after being exploited, and sporadic attempts to rebuild cooperation; overall trend likely toward more frequent defection over time, though isolated "tests" or small cooperative clusters may appear.

game_explanation:
This is a standard binary public goods game with 6 players. Each round every player chooses either to contribute their entire endowment (20) to a group pot or to keep it (0 contribution). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so one contributing 20 increases the group pot by 74 total, which yields about 12.33 extra for each person. Because 12.33 < 20, a lone contribution (or contributing when others' choices are fixed) reduces an individual's private payoff; free-riding is the individually safe (dominant) action. Socially, however, everyone would be best off if all contributed (each would get ~74).

Given uncertainty (players do not know how many rounds remain) and visible personas, I expect early attempts at cooperation by a few players who are more pro-social/engaged (PARROT and DUCK), with occasional help from CHICKEN, while some players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DOG) are more likely to free-ride. Because contributors get systematically lower round payoffs when not enough others join, those cooperative attempts will be punished (exploited) and cooperation will decline. Chat will be sparse — short promises, pleas to cooperate, and a little frustration after exploitation. Over 12 rounds the most likely pattern is a few early cooperative rounds with 1–3 contributors, then a collapse to widespread free-riding, with PARROT (and maybe DUCK) occasionally trying to rebuild cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a standard binary public goods game with costly punishment and cheap communication. Each round each player chooses whether to contribute their entire 20 coins or contribute nothing. The social multiplier (3.7) makes group welfare highest when everyone contributes, but any individual who defects (keeps 20) earns more in the short term if others contribute. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, they may behave as if future retaliation or reciprocity matters.

We should expect initial uncertainty and some mixed behavior in early rounds: a few players will contribute immediately (intrinsically cooperative or conditional cooperators), while others test the waters by free-riding. When cooperators observe defectors, they have an incentive to use punishment to deter future defections: punishment is costly to the punisher but reduces the target's payoff substantially, so it can sustain cooperation if used sparingly and credibly. Chat messages can be used to coordinate norms ("please contribute") or to justify punishment.

Given the personas: DUCK and DOG are likely to cooperate early; PARROT and GORILLA are more likely to test defection; SLOTH may oscillate (labels suggest low effort, but education implies conditional cooperation); CHICKEN (older male) is likely to support enforcement. So I predict initial mixed contributions, some targeted punishments to signal enforcement, then convergence to high cooperation for many later rounds, with occasional one-off defections that are punished once and corrected.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players over 19 rounds. Each round, players decide how many of the 20 coins to withdraw for themselves (the remainder is contributed). Contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally; then players may spend coins to punish or reward others. Punishment is costly (2 coins to the punisher, -3 to the target per unit). Reward costs 2 and gives 2 per unit to the target. Players cannot see others' choices before deciding, so decisions reflect expectations about peers.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions. A few players start as consistent cooperators (high contributions), some as free-riders (low contributions), and some as conditional cooperators. Social enforcement (especially by players with reputational or "leader" personas like PARROT and DOG) will be used sparingly to deter persistent free-riding.
- Punishment will be rare and targeted (one or two units) because it's costly to send and harms the target more than the sender pays. Rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce high contributors.
- Over repeated rounds, conditional cooperators and those who are punished/rewarded adjust toward higher contributions. By mid- to late-game contributions trend up and punishment declines. In the final rounds, there is less incentive to punish (endgame effect), so enforcement drops and contributions stabilize at a higher level from prior rounds.

The transcript below simulates rounds 1–19 in the order specified. Each round starts with a short explanation of why that round likely unfolds as shown. Contributions are given in the exact avatar order: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT. Punishment/reward entries show only non-zero actions; negative unit = punishment, positive unit = reward. Chat is sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with uncertain horizon: each player privately chooses either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (cooperate) or keep it (defect). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six players. Because the multiplier (3.7) is less than the group size (6), the group-maximizing action is for everyone to contribute (every player would receive 74 coins if everyone contributes), but the individual incentive is to free-ride: a defector keeps 20 and still receives a share of the public pot, so defecting strictly dominates contributing in a single round.

Typical dynamics in such settings: initial rounds often show some cooperation driven by social preferences or trust; visible payoff differences (defectors earn more than cooperators) create incentives to switch to defection. Because players do not know when the game ends, there is no predictable end-game where everyone defects only at the last round; instead cooperation usually decays gradually as participants respond to being exploited or try to punish defectors. Chat (cheap talk) can sustain cooperation if credible and persuasive, but it is often sparse and not fully effective.

Given the provided personas, I predict a moderate early level of cooperation from the socially inclined players (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT), and persistent defection from more self-protective players (SLOTH, CHICKEN, DOG). Over the 12 rounds cooperation will erode: after repeated exploitation a few cooperators stop contributing, leaving one or two lone contributors for a while; eventually everyone defects and stays there.

Because players do not observe others’ choices before deciding and do not know the number of rounds, we should expect testing behavior early (mix of contributions and defections), occasional punishment targeted at perceived free-riders, and gradual stabilization around higher cooperation if punishment is used sparingly and credibly. Expect the group to start with mixed contributions (some try cooperating, others freeride), a few targeted punishments in early rounds to enforce contribution, and then a pattern where most players contribute most of the time with occasional defections that attract small punishments. Punishment events will be relatively rare and low-intensity (1 unit) to avoid excessive cost to punishers.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with costly punishment and rewarding. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a public pot; contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players may spend coins to punish (cost 4 to sender, -10 to target) or reward (cost 4 to sender, +3 to target). Because punishment is an efficient deterrent (large penalty to target), we should expect a few targeted punishments against clear low contributors, especially in round 1 if someone free-rides. Rewards are less efficient (costly to giver, smaller benefit to receiver), so rewards tend to be used sparingly and often symbolically to acknowledge high contributors.

Given the short horizon (only 2 rounds), players anticipate limited future repercussions; however, punishments in round 1 can still change round 2 behavior. Typical dynamics: moderately high contributions from some players, a small cluster of low contributions (or a single low contributor) gets punished lightly in round 1, leading that player to increase contributions in round 2. Most players will punish/reward sparingly because sending costs are non-trivial.

game_explanation:
This is a finite (19-round) public goods game with 6 players. Each round players decide privately how much of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (their contribution). The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed yields 0.3 coins back to each player. After payoffs from the pot, players can spend coins to punish or reward others: each punishment unit costs the sender 2 and reduces a target by 3; each reward unit costs the sender 2 and gives the target +2. Players cannot identify who targeted them. Because the game ends after 19 rounds, behavior tends to show: relatively cooperative play early (people try to build a norm), occasional free-riding followed by sparse punishments or targeted rewards to enforce cooperation, and a steady decline in cooperation as the horizon approaches (players reduce contributions in later rounds and final-round defection is common). Chat messages are sparse and usually used to encourage contributions or to call out low contributions; punishments/rewards are used sparingly because they are costly.

game_explanation:
This is a standard finite public-goods game with costly peer rewards. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Private incentive: each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to the contributor (because the 2.2×pot is split among 6 players). That is less than 1, so the dominant private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0).
- Social incentive: full cooperation (high contributions by all) raises total group payoffs, so conditional cooperators may give positive contributions.
- Reward stage: each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 3 coins. Rewards are therefore payoff-inefficient (group net loss per reward unit = 1 coin) but can be used strategically to reward cooperators or punish defectors; because rewards are costly, players will use them sparingly.
- Information and timing: players do not see others' choices before deciding; they can chat at any time (cheap signaling). The game lasts only 2 rounds, so backward induction pushes toward more free-riding in round 2 (final-round effect). Expect moderate contributions in round 1 by some (conditional cooperators, high-education personas) and lower contributions in round 2. Rewards will be sparse, aimed at acknowledging high contributors in round 1 and possibly small reciprocal gestures in round 2.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public goods game with six players and 19 rounds. Each round players choose how many coins to withdraw (private payoff) and the remainder is contributed to a common pot that is multiplied by 1.8 and shared equally. After seeing contributions, players can spend coins to positively reward others (each reward unit costs 2 to the sender and gives 2 to the recipient). Players know the finite horizon (19 rounds), so endgame effects (declining contributions near the last rounds) are likely. Early rounds typically show moderate-to-high cooperation as players test each other's tendencies; some will free-ride occasionally and others will respond by withholding rewards or selectively rewarding cooperators. Because rewards are costly, they will be used sparingly — usually to reinforce consistently high contributors rather than to punish directly. Uncertainty about motives (who will reciprocate, who will defect later) and the anonymity of reward sources (players can't identify who rewarded them in summaries) will keep communication and rewards strategic and infrequent. Overall I expect: initial mid-to-high contributions, occasional sharp drops by a few free-riders, selective rewarding of steady contributors, and a gradual decline toward the end as the game approaches round 19.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits private incentives (taking money for yourself) against group incentives (leaving coins in the pot so everyone benefits after the 1.8 multiplier). Early rounds often feature testing/cooperation: some players contribute generously to signal trustworthiness, others free-ride to see if cooperators will be exploited. Rewarding is the only direct sanction/bonus mechanism here: it costs 2 coins per reward unit to the sender and gives 2 coins per unit to the recipient, which can sustain cooperation if cooperators reciprocate or if norm-enforcers reward high contributors. Because the game has a known finite horizon (19 rounds), expect some gradual decline in contributions (endgame effects) and sparse, targeted rewarding (players reward selectively rather than broadly). Uncertainty remains about how strongly each persona follows cooperation norms: some (e.g., GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) are more likely to contribute; others (e.g., SLOTH) may contribute less. Chats will be rare and tactical, and rewards will be used sparingly to encourage cooperation early and may taper off toward the end.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players, private endowment 20 each round, contributions multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, followed by an optional costly reward stage (cost 4 to give 1 unit, recipient gets +3). With only two rounds, dynamics will reflect both conditional cooperation and end-game incentives. In round 1, players often experiment or try to signal cooperative intent — moderate contributions are likely from some (especially those with higher education or who value reputation), while younger or less invested players may contribute less. Because rewards are costly and anonymous to recipients, players tend to reward sparingly, usually directing small rewards to visibly high contributors to reinforce cooperation.

By round 2 (the final round), short-run incentives to free-ride increase because there's no future to incentivize reciprocity; contributions often fall somewhat. However, players who enjoyed social preferences (warm-glow) or who received rewards in round 1 may still contribute and may send a last-round reward to reinforce fairness or “pay back” the group norm — despite anonymity, players sometimes reward high contributors out of reciprocity or to feel fair. Overall expect moderate cooperation in round 1, slight decline in round 2, and a small number of sparse reward actions each round targeted at the highest contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with costly punishment and costly reward. Incentives:
- Contributing benefits the group (multiplier 2.2) but is individually costly, so free-riding is tempting.
- Punishment is a strong deterrent (each punish unit deducts 10 from a target, but costs 4 to the source) and can be used to sanction low contributors; it is costly and inefficient overall.
- Reward is mildly positive for targets (adds 3) but also costly to give (cost 4), so it’s used sparingly to reinforce cooperation.
- Because the game lasts only 2 rounds and identities of punishers/rewarders are hidden, forward-looking incentives to sustain cooperation are limited; however, players may still use punishment in round 1 to signal norms (and they may adjust round 2 contributions in response to the observed punish/reward outcomes).

Uncertainties:
- Players don’t see contributions before choosing, and punishers/rewarders aren’t identified, so strategic retaliation is imprecise.
- Some players (given the personas) will be inclined to contribute more (e.g., PARROT, CHICKEN), others to free-ride or contribute less (e.g., SLOTH, GORILLA). This leads to mixed contributions and selective sanctions.

Predicted dynamic:
- Round 1: a mix of moderate and low contributions. A couple of players punish obvious free-riding (zero contribution) and one or two players reward high contributors.
- Round 2: some downward drift in contributions because the game ends, but punished players may modestly increase contributions to avoid further sanctions; punish/reward remain sparse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players, small discrete choices (contribute 0 or 20), and costly punishment (sender pays 2 per unit, target loses 5 per unit). The multiplication factor (3.7) and equal split make contributing socially efficient but individually risky: a lone defector gains substantially in a round unless others punish. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, conditional strategies (contribute when others do; punish defectors) and occasional opportunistic free-riding are both plausible. Punishment tends to be used sparingly to enforce cooperation: contributors will (sometimes) spend a little to sanction defectors, which usually restores cooperation in later rounds. Chats are typically short — warnings, requests, or brief protests.

Given the supplied personas, I predict a pattern of mostly cooperative rounds with occasional single or pair defections. Contributors will issue targeted, modest punishments to deter repeat defection. Punishments are sparse and mostly aimed at restoring cooperation rather than maximizing short-run monetary returns.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each player can keep some of the 20 coins privately and contribute the rest to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly each round. After seeing their own round payoff (but not others' choices), players can spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs the giver 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins). Messages are allowed and can shape expectations.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically start with moderate cooperation as players test each other. Some players will contribute more to signal cooperation (and to earn reciprocal rewards); others may free-ride early to see responses.
- Because rewards are costly but directly beneficial to recipients, they tend to be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation (reward cooperators) or to reciprocate generosity. Since rewards are zero-sum across senders and receivers, they redistribute payoffs but don't increase total group earnings.
- Over many rounds with finite horizon (19 rounds), cooperation often declines gradually as the end approaches, with a possible small uphill effort mid-game by pro-social players trying to stabilize cooperation via rewarding. A late-game unraveling is likely, with contributions falling toward zero in the final rounds.
- Chat messages can encourage cooperation (from leaders) or justify low contributions; they will be sparse and typically assertive or reactive.
- Players only see their own round payoff and their own reward actions/receipts; they infer others' behavior from chat and reward patterns, so visible reward patterns (who rewards whom in chat) will influence subsequent behavior.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This two-round public goods game puts each player in tension between private payoff (keeping coins) and group payoff (contributing to the shared pot). Because contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and shared equally, a group-optimal contribution is positive, but individuals have an incentive to free-ride. In round 1 there is uncertainty about others' preferences, so contributions tend to spread: some players will contribute a lot (to signal cooperation), some little (to free-ride or test others). Because the game lasts only two rounds, players who want to influence the other round have an incentive to reward in round 1; in round 2, behavior often shifts toward rewarding and contributing more among reciprocating types, while persistent low contributors may continue to free-ride. Chat messages, when present, will be few and used to signal intent or thank others.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This public goods game lasts 19 rounds with six players. Each round everyone starts with 20 coins and chooses how many to withdraw (so contributions are integers 0–20). The common pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can punish (costly to the punisher, more costly to the punished) or reward (costly to the sender, beneficial to the receiver), and players may chat at any time.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players start moderately cooperative (above 50% of endowment) to test reciprocity. A few players may free-ride with lower contributions.
- Mid-game: cooperators begin to use modest punishments/rewards to discipline freeriders and sustain contributions; small targeted punishments are used sparsely because punishment is costly.
- Late rounds: a mild endgame effect reduces cooperation as incentives to support future rounds decline; overall contributions trend downward, and punishment becomes rarer because it yields less future benefit.
- Chat will be sparse and functional: brief calls for cooperation, occasional calls-out, and short acknowledgments.

I'll present each round with a brief explanation, the chat entries, the contributions (in SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT order), the punishment/reward edges (only non-zero actions), and then the summary stage followed by any short chat.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with punishment and reward. Each round, players decide how much of their private 20 coins to contribute to a common pot that is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. Because returns from the pot are shared, there is an incentive to free-ride (contribute little and keep private money) while benefiting from others' contributions. Punishment and reward let players impose social costs or give bonuses after seeing contributions; punishment is costly to the punisher and very damaging to the punished, while rewards are costly to the giver but give modest benefits to the receiver.

Likely dynamics over two rounds:
- Round 1: Many players will contribute moderately (signal cooperation), but at least one player may test the group by contributing little or nothing. Other players may punish that free-rider to enforce norms despite the cost. A few targeted rewards may be used to encourage high contributors.
- Round 2: Observing round 1 punishments and rewards, formerly punished players often raise contributions to avoid further sanctions, while punishers may be less willing to punish in the final round (no future rounds to enforce), so overall contributions may increase modestly or stabilize. Rewards may be used again but punishments will be rarer in the final round.

Expect some sparse chat: short commitments or warnings before contributions, brief justifications or apologies after summaries.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This game is a standard one-shot repeated public goods game with incomplete horizon (players don't know how many rounds remain). Each round each player can either keep their 20 coins or contribute the full 20 to the common pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across the 6 players. Individual incentive: keep the 20 and benefit from others' contributions (best single-round payoff if others contribute). Group efficiency: if everyone contributes, everyone gets a higher payoff than if nobody contributes.

Because players do not know the total number of rounds, early rounds often support cooperation (people hope cooperation will continue), but cooperation tends to be fragile — free-riding pays off in the short run and can trigger conditional cooperators to reduce contributions. Chat allows limited coordination: a few committed cooperators (or a persuasive leader) can sustain contributions for stretches. Given the six personas, I expect initial goodwill (many contribute), one or two early free-riders will appear and get higher immediate payoffs, leading some conditional cooperators to respond by reducing contributions. Periodic short messages (promises, pleas) will produce partial recoveries but not full, steady cooperation; toward the middle rounds cooperation will ebb and flow, and near the end some players may defect more often. GORILLA and DUCK (college-educated profiles) are likely to act as conditional cooperators/leaders; CHICKEN (name + profile) is more likely to free-ride repeatedly; PARROT and SLOTH oscillate; DOG tends toward modest cooperation but is responsive to payoff signals.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players. Each round each player chooses to contribute either all of their 20 coins or nothing. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so a single cooperator's contribution raises everyone's share but costs the contributor 20. Without enforcement, we expect some players to free-ride (contribute 0) because contributing is individually costly. However, this version allows costly punishment after contributions are revealed: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and reduces the target by 5 coins. Punishment can deter future free-riding but is itself costly, so players typically punish sparingly and target clear free-riders. Players also can send brief messages, usually few and strategic (warnings, explanations, thanks).

Given the provided personas, I expect most players to be conditional cooperators: they will often contribute, tolerate a small number of defectors at first, and use punishment selectively to discipline repeat defectors. One or two players may attempt occasional defections to test the group; these will be met with targeted punishments by norm-enforcing players (especially GORILLA and CHICKEN). Over the 12 rounds we should see overall cooperation maintained, punctuated by occasional defections and short punishment responses that restore cooperative behavior.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with costly punishment and costly rewards. Each player gets 20 coins each round and decides simultaneously how much (0–20) to contribute to the group pot. Contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally among the six players. After contribution returns are allocated, players can spend coins to punish (cost to sender = 4 per unit, cost to target = 10 per unit) or reward (cost to sender = 4 per unit, benefit to target = 3 per unit) others. Messages can be sent at any stage.

Likely dynamics:
- In round 1, players often try a mix of cooperation and testing: some contribute relatively high to signal cooperativeness (especially those with postgraduate education or strong prosocial priors), while others contribute less to test enforcement. Expect moderate average contributions.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly. We can expect a small number of punitive actions aimed at clear low contributors and a couple of small rewards to reinforce cooperation.
- Because the game lasts only two rounds, round 2 can go either way: some players may increase cooperation in response to punishment or reciprocity, but many will reduce contributions in the final round (endgame effect). Punishment in round 2 is usually limited because it no longer signals future behavior, though fairness concerns may still motivate some punitive acts.

Given the six player personas:
- DOG (postgrad, male) is likely the strongest pro-social enforcer (higher contributions, likely to punish free-riders).
- SLOTH and CHICKEN (postgrad and college grad) will lean cooperative.
- GORILLA and PARROT (younger, less education) are more likely to experiment with lower contributions but can respond to punishment.
- DUCK (older, associate degree) is likely moderate/cooperative.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each round everyone decides, simultaneously, how much of their 20 coins to contribute; contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.8, and split equally. After contributions are revealed each round, players may spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the receiver 2 coins). Players see contributions before rewarding, so rewards will be used to reinforce high contributors and (sparingly) to withhold benefits from low contributors. Because the multiplier yields a per-player return of 0.3 times the group total, individual incentives push toward free-riding (keep coins privately) while group incentives push toward contributing.

Typical unfolding: early rounds show mixed contributions as players learn about others. Those who contribute consistently (CHICKEN, PARROT, DUCK) get modest positive reinforcement via occasional rewards from peers; lower contributors (GORILLA early, SLOTH intermittently) get fewer rewards. Over rounds, brief cycles of punishment and recovery appear: occasional low contributions trigger a few targeted rewards to high contributors, which encourages cooperation; known endgame logic makes contributions decline in the final round(s). Chat is sparse but sometimes used to praise cooperation or call out low contributions; rewards are used sparingly (1–3 units) because they cost the sender as much as they benefit the recipient.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with six players who each choose either to contribute all 20 coins or keep them all. The shared pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. Individually, free-riding dominates: keeping 20 and letting others contribute yields a higher personal payoff given any set of others' contributions. Socially, however, everyone is better off if all contribute (everyone would get 74 each vs 20 if nobody contributes).

Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we expect initial rounds to show some cooperation from those who favor reciprocity or fairness (college/postgrad backgrounds and those who signal intentions). Others who are more self-interested or less trusting will be inclined to free-ride immediately. Cheap talk (chat messages) can slightly increase early cooperation if believed, but it can also be exploited, and visible exploitation typically reduces cooperation over subsequent rounds. So the likely dynamic is: modest cooperation in early rounds (some players contributing), followed by gradual decline as contributors observe they are being exploited and switch to free-riding. Chat will be sparse and mainly used early to declare intentions and mid-game to express frustration.

game_explanation:
This is a standard binary-contribution public goods game with costly, targeted punishment. Each round each player decides to keep their 20 coins or put all 20 into the shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per punishment unit) and harsher to the target (5 coins deducted per unit). Players see contributions and who punished whom each round, but decisions are simultaneous. Because the per-player share from a single 20-coin contribution (3.7*20/6 ≈ 12.33) is less than 20, the individual monetary incentive is to free-ride; social incentives and reputation — plus the ability to punish identified free-riders — push toward cooperation. Punishment sustains cooperation but is itself costly, so we expect cooperation to be high but punctuated by occasional defections and sparse punishment responses targeted at repeated or salient free-riders. Chat messages are sparse and used mostly to signal expectations or protest punishments. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect conditional strategies (switching after being punished) rather than guaranteed endgame defection. Below is a plausible round-by-round transcript for 12 rounds with the exact avatar order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT.
