game_explanation:
This public goods game pits a small group against a classic social dilemma: each player can keep coins privately or contribute them to a pot that yields a multiplied return shared equally. Because the per-player return from contributing (1.8/6 = 0.3 per coin) is less than 1, each individual has an incentive to withdraw (free-ride). However, the group benefits if many contribute, so we expect tension between short-term self-interest and group-minded behavior.

Uncertainties and likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show somewhat cooperative behavior as players test one another. Expect a mix: some high contributors (social types), some low contributors (free-riders).
- Rewards allow costly transfers (2 coins cost per reward unit, 2 coins gained by recipient) and primarily serve as social signals: rewarding cooperators to encourage them, or transferring to allies. Because rewards are costly to the giver and do not increase total group payoff, they will be used sparingly.
- If cooperators are rewarded, contributions can be sustained. If some players repeatedly withdraw much, cooperators may reduce contributions over time.
- Endgame effect: because the game ends at round 19, many players will defect in the final rounds. Expect a decline toward the end and particularly a strong drop in round 19.
- Chats will be sparse and purposeful: brief praise, requests, or complaints.

reflection:
This public goods game pits a small group against a classic social dilemma: each player can keep coins privately or contribute them to a pot that yields a multiplied return shared equally. Because the per-player return from contributing (1.8/6 = 0.3 per coin) is less than 1, each individual has an incentive to withdraw (free-ride). However, the group benefits if many contribute, so we expect tension between short-term self-interest and group-minded behavior.

Uncertainties and likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show somewhat cooperative behavior as players test one another. Expect a mix: some high contributors (social types), some low contributors (free-riders).
- Rewards allow costly transfers (2 coins cost per reward unit, 2 coins gained by recipient) and primarily serve as social signals: rewarding cooperators to encourage them, or transferring to allies. Because rewards are costly to the giver and do not increase total group payoff, they will be used sparingly.
- If cooperators are rewarded, contributions can be sustained. If some players repeatedly withdraw much, cooperators may reduce contributions over time.
- Endgame effect: because the game ends at round 19, many players will defect in the final rounds. Expect a decline toward the end and particularly a strong drop in round 19.
- Chats will be sparse and purposeful: brief praise, requests, or complaints.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 19-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round every player can withdraw some coins (private) and leave the rest as contribution to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each player gets 0.3 times the sum of contributions. That multiplier (1.8) is smaller than the group size, so the individually optimal move is to withdraw everything (contribute 0) while group-optimal is to contribute all. Expect a tension: some players will try to cooperate (higher contributions, conditional on others), while others will free-ride. Because choices are simultaneous, players who prefer cooperation often rely on punishment or occasional rewards to enforce contributions. Punishment is costly (source pays 2 per unit; target loses 3 per unit) and rewards are also costly (source pays 2 per unit; target gains 2 per unit), so both are used sparingly. Early rounds often show moderate cooperation or testing; mid-game may see targeted punishment of persistent low contributors; late rounds may show some endgame free-riding. Chat will be sparse — short appeals, warnings, or thanks. Punishments/rewards will be occasional and targeted rather than widespread.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 2-round public goods game with a subsequent costly punishment/reward stage. In round 1 players often try a mixture of cooperation and testing: some will contribute moderately to build goodwill, while a few may free-ride. Because contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split among all 6 players, contributing benefits the group but costs the individual immediately. After contributions, players can use costly punishments (4 coins to sender, -10 to target per unit) or rewards (4 coins to sender, +3 to target per unit). Punishment and reward are typically used sparingly: rewards to acknowledge high contributors and punishments to sanction clear free-riding. Messages are usually few and used to encourage cooperation or call out low contributions.

Because players know there are only 2 rounds, there is an "end-game" pressure: in round 2 many players tend to reduce contributions (expecting others may defect and that future retaliation is impossible). However, some players who value reciprocity or have been rewarded may still contribute moderately in round 2. Overall expect moderate cooperation in round 1, selective rewarding/punishing of obvious low/high contributors, then lower contributions in round 2 with minimal punishment/reward.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly, so each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.3667 coins to each player — less than the private value of 1 coin but beneficial if many players contribute. That creates a tension: individually it's best to keep coins (free-ride), but group earnings are higher when people contribute.

Uncertainties include players' beliefs about others' cooperativeness and how much they will punish or reward. Because the game lasts only 2 rounds, incentives to support cooperation via punishment are weaker in the final round (no future retaliation), so we typically see higher cooperation in round 1 than round 2. Early punishments can shift beliefs: low contributors who are punished may increase contributions later to avoid future sanctions. Punishment is costly to the punisher (4 coins per unit) and severe to the target (-10 per unit), so punishments tend to be sparse and targeted at clear low contributors. Rewards are also costly (4 per unit to giver, +3 to receiver) and usually used sparingly to reinforce good contributors.

Given the player personas, I expect a mix: a few moderately-high contributors (DUCK, GORILLA, DOG) willing to sustain cooperation, some middle contributors (CHICKEN, PARROT), and one low initial contributor (SLOTH) who may be punished in round 1 and respond by increasing contribution in round 2. Punishment/reward activity will be modest: a couple of punishments targeted at very low contributions in round 1 and some small reciprocal rewards across rounds; round 2 has fewer punishments.

Each round players trade private use (withdrawals) against a shared pot benefit that is split equally. Short-term incentives push toward withdrawing everything (maximize private coins), while group-level incentives favor contributing more because each contributed coin returns 0.3 to every player (so the group earns 1.8 but each individual recovers only 0.3). Because players observe contributions before deciding on punishments and rewards, costly sanctions and selective rewards let the group try to sustain cooperation: punishments reduce free-riding but are costly to the sender; rewards are also costly but increase recipients’ payoffs. Expect some early variation in contributions, occasional targeted punishments against low contributors, a phase where reciprocity and modest rewards stabilize contributions, and then a decline toward the final round (endgame) as forward-looking incentives vanish. Chat will be sparse and typically used to call out behaviors or thank cooperators. Punishments and rewards will be used sparingly and targeted to obvious defectors or to reinforce high contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public goods game where each player must either contribute all (20) or nothing (0) each round. Every contributed 20 becomes 74 (20 × 3.7) to the group pot and that pot is split equally among the six players. Individual incentives: for any given round, keeping your 20 and letting others contribute strictly gives you a higher individual payoff than contributing yourself (you get your 20 plus a share of the pot). The social optimum (everyone contributes) yields the highest group payoff (each person would get 74), but because contributors forgo their private 20, they are vulnerable to free-riding.

Uncertainties that shape behavior here:
- Players do not see others' choices before deciding, only payoff summaries after the round, so decisions are based on beliefs and past payoffs.
- The number of rounds is unknown, so players may try to build cooperation early (no guaranteed last-round defection).
- Messages are allowed and can sustain cooperation if persuasive or credible; however, messages can be sparse and unverifiable.

Typical dynamics expected:
- Early rounds often see some voluntary cooperation from pro-social players (here, PARROT and possibly younger or more social players). Others may test cooperation by free-riding to see the payoff advantage.
- If free-riding repeatedly yields higher payoffs, cooperation tends to decay over time toward lower contribution rates.
- Occasional bursts of cooperation can occur if players coordinate via chat or if conditional cooperators reciprocate a previous cooperative move.
- With these personas, expect PARROT to be a frequent contributor and pro-social communicator; some others (GORILLA, DUCK) to experiment with cooperation; risk-averse or pragmatic players (CHICKEN, DOG) to mainly free-ride; SLOTH to be passive but may occasionally contribute.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with six players. Each round players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After contributions are revealed and payoff shares assigned, players can spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives the recipient 3 coins). Because the game lasts only two rounds, incentives are mixed: without future interaction beyond the final round, some players may be tempted to free-ride (contribute little) to keep private coins; others who value group payoff or reciprocity may contribute more, especially if they expect rewards for cooperating. Rewards create a direct immediate incentive to reward cooperators and reciprocate earlier rewards, but they are costly to give, so we expect only a few small reward actions. Early chat can be used to coordinate but cannot bind contributions; it may slightly encourage cooperation. Overall, expect a mix of moderate cooperation from some (educated or more strategic players) and low contributions from others (free-riders), with sparse rewarding targeted at those who contributed relatively more.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with six players who each choose either to contribute their whole endowment (20) or nothing (0) each round. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so high group contributions raise everyone's earnings — but each player individually gains more by free-riding on others' contributions in the short run. Because players can punish (pay 2 coins per punishment unit to reduce a target by 5 coins per unit) and punishers are identifiable, we should expect the following dynamics:

- Early rounds: players will signal willingness to cooperate (messages), and many will contribute. Occasional free-riding will occur (temptation), and visible punishment will be used sparingly to deter repeat free-riders.
- Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it will be targeted and limited (often 1 unit). Repeat offenders are more likely to receive stronger or repeated punishment.
- Because the number of rounds is unknown, there is no guaranteed end-game collapse; players continue conditional strategies (punish defectors, cooperate when others cooperate).
- Chats will be sparse and used to encourage cooperation or call out defectors. Punishment events will be infrequent but visible and will tend to reduce defection over time (though a stubborn defector may re-offend occasionally and get punished again).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 rounds and a small group (6 players). Each round each player decides how much of the 20 coins to withdraw privately; the remainder is contributed to a group pot that is multiplied (×1.8) and split evenly. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players may spend part of their earnings to send costly rewards (each reward unit costs 2 coins to the sender and gives 2 coins to the recipient). Players are anonymous with respect to who rewarded them, and they see only their own round payoff and their own reward activity in the summary.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players test each other. Some (CHICKEN, PARROT, SLOTH) are more inclined to contribute high amounts; DUCK tends to free-ride (low contribution) and benefits immediately. GORILLA and DOG are conditional cooperators, responding to others' behavior.
- Rewarding is costly, so players will use it sparingly — typically to reinforce cooperators or reciprocate helpful behavior. Positive reinforcement helps sustain somewhat higher contributions than in a game without rewards.
- Over the middle rounds, a cooperative cluster may stabilize (moderate-to-high contributions from CHICKEN, PARROT, SLOTH, sometimes GORILLA and DOG). DUCK often continues contributing little and usually receives no rewards.
- Toward the end, the "endgame" effect appears: contributions gradually decline as future punishment/reward incentives weaken, and in the final round players tend to withdraw more (contribute less). Rewards likewise taper off in late rounds.
- Chat is likely sparse and used to praise cooperators or call out free-riding; it may slightly coordinate behavior early on but has limited power late in the game.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with optional costly rewards. In each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly among the six players. After contributions are revealed each round, players can spend costly reward units (4 coins each) to give other players a small benefit (3 coins per unit). Players can also send chat messages at any time.

Likely dynamics:
- Round 1 is uncertain: some players will try to signal cooperative intentions (promises in chat) while others may free-ride or wait to see behavior. Because the multiplier (2.2) makes group returns positive but personal incentive favors lower contributions, we expect mixed contributions.
- Rewards are costly and typically used sparingly. Players often use rewards in later rounds to reinforce cooperation or reward high contributors, especially since this game ends after Round 2. Expect few reward actions, concentrated on rewarding high contributors rather than punishing (there is no direct punishment, only withholding rewards).
- Round 2 decisions are influenced by Round 1 contributions and chat: cooperators may sustain high contributions, free-riders may remain low or slightly increase if they try to repair reputation. Rewarding in Round 2 is more likely as a final redistribution to encouragers or reciprocators.
- Because players see contributions after each round, reward choices will target visible cooperators. Rewarding will be selective and modest (1 unit is common).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, anonymous contributions, and an optional costly reward stage. Because contributions are split and multiplied by 1.8, contributions benefit the group but cost the individual. With 19 rounds known in advance, expect an initial period of uncertainty with somewhat cautious cooperation, followed by coordination among conditional cooperators if they observe others contributing. The reward stage introduces a way to incentivize cooperation: players who value group payoff or reputation (even anonymously) may send small positive rewards to high contributors to encourage them; free-riders will tend to receive fewer rewards. Because rewards are anonymous in the summary, direct reciprocity is imperfect, but conditional cooperators will still reward high contributions. Over time, contributions typically rise as cooperators reinforce each other, peak in the middle rounds, and then decline toward the end (an endgame effect) as incentives to invest in future reputation diminish. Chat is sparse and strategic: brief signals to cooperate or call out free-riding may appear early or midgame. Rewards are used sparingly (small units) and targeted mainly at those who contribute above the group average.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment and binary contributions (0 or 20). Early rounds typically show some mix of cooperation and free-riding as players test one another. Punishment is a tool to deter free-riding but itself is costly to the punisher, so expect punishment to be used sparingly and targeted at clear defectors. Over time, punishment often stabilizes cooperation: a few early sanctions can increase average contributions, but occasional defections or retaliatory punishment can reintroduce conflict. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we expect no sharp endgame unraveling; instead the group should move toward a norm of mostly contributing, with intermittent punishments when someone defects or “tests” the group.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with punishment and reward. Each round players decide how much (0–20) of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly. Contributions increase everyone's return but are privately costly, so there's a tension: cooperating (contributing) benefits the group but invites exploitation by free riders. Punishment lets players impose costly fines on low contributors (source pays 4 per unit, target loses 10 per unit); reward lets players pay to boost others (source pays 4 per unit, target gains 3 per unit). Because contributions and sanctions are costly and information about who punished/rewarded is anonymous, expect modest initial cooperation from conditional cooperators (those who expect others to contribute), some low contributions from likely free riders, and targeted, sparse sanctions: cooperators are likeliest to punish low contributors and to reward high contributors. Because the game only lasts two rounds, second-round behavior will lean toward less cooperation (last-round decline) and fewer costly sanctions, though some players may still punish or reward to express preferences or to equalize payoffs. Uncertainty remains about others' types and how many will sanction, so players hedge — moderate contributions are common, and punishment/reward tend to be used sparingly and targeted.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with a reward stage after contributions. In round 1 players are uncertain about others' actions, so many will choose moderate contributions to probe cooperation and potentially signal intent. Some will make explicit promises in chat (cheap talk) to encourage reciprocation. After contributions are revealed and payoffs redistributed, players can spend costly reward units to benefit others; typically rewards are used sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior (reward cooperators) rather than to punish free-riders, because rewards are costly to the giver. Because players see contributions and the amount of reward they personally received (but not who gave it), rewards can shape expectations heading into round 2.

Round 2 is the final round, so backward induction weakly predicts a drop in contributions: with no future rounds, there's less incentive to sustain cooperation. However, players who were rewarded in round 1 may reciprocate by contributing more, while those who received nothing (or who were low contributors) may reduce contributions or free-ride. Rewards in round 2 are likely to be sparse and targeted to those who contribute above the group mean or to reciprocate past rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with six players who each choose either to contribute all 20 coins or keep all 20 each round. Contributions earn a group return (sum multiplied by 3.7 and split equally), so individual incentives favor free-riding but group payoffs favor contribution. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, cooperation tends to be higher early on and enforcement (punishment) is used sporadically to deter repeated free-riding. Punishment is costly to the sender (2 coins per punishment unit) but penalizes targets by 5 coins per unit, so it is typically used sparingly — enough to signal disapproval and reduce exploitive gains but not so often as to waste senders' resources. Communication is allowed and will be used infrequently to call out or warn others; some players will experiment (test others), and those who are punished may change behavior or occasionally retaliate. Expect mostly cooperative contributions interspersed with occasional single defection rounds; punishments will target clear defectors and be modest (1–2 units) unless a defector repeats.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment and no known number of rounds. Each round players choose between contributing their whole 20-coin endowment or nothing. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six players. Punishment lets players spend 2 coins per punishment unit to reduce a target's round payoff by 5 coins per unit; punishment is observed and sources are identifiable.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players test the waters (free-ride) while others contribute. Because punishment is available and players can be identified, at least one player who values cooperation (e.g., GORILLA) is likely to punish early free-riders to signal enforcement. Punishment is costly so it will be used sparingly.
- Middle rounds: punishment should raise cooperation. With a few enforcement signals, most players will switch to contributing, producing higher shared payoffs. Punishment episodes will be occasional and targeted at occasional defections (mistakes or opportunism).
- Late rounds: because players don't know the end, classic end-game collapse may be muted, but some sporadic defections can still occur. Punishment may continue but may be less frequent due to cost and fatigue. Chat messages will be sparse and communicative (requests, short reproaches, apologies).
- Uncertainties: exactly who defects and when is probabilistic. Some players (SLOTH) are more likely to free-ride early or late; more conscientious players (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) are more likely to contribute and to use punishment at least once to enforce norms.

Transcript below simulates 12 rounds following these tendencies. Numbers are given per round: contributions, minimal chat, punishments (if any), and a round summary showing each player's net payoff, how much they spent punishing others, and how much they lost to punishment.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with incomplete information about the total number of rounds. Each round every player has 20 coins and chooses either to contribute 20 or keep 0 to their private holdings. The total contributed is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six players. Because the per-contribution return to the group (3.7*20 / 6 = 12.33) is less than the private cost (20), the one-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, repeated interaction with messaging creates room for cooperation: players can try to signal willingness to contribute, punish defectors by withholding contributions in later rounds, and coordinate on mutual cooperation.

Likely dynamics here: early rounds often show some voluntary cooperation from the more prosocial players (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG). Free-riders (SLOTH, PARROT) can gain relatively large payoffs when they exploit cooperators, and visible round summaries (net payoffs for every player) allow contributors to spot and call out defectors. That tends to trigger punishment (drop in contributions) and then occasional attempts at rebuilding cooperation. Because players don't know when the game ends, there can be intermittent attempts to re-establish cooperation, but overall contributions typically trend downward as trust erodes. Chats will be sparse and targeted: pledges, short calls-out, and occasional appeals for coordination.

game_explanation:
This is a six-player repeated public goods game with a binary choice each round: contribute your whole endowment (20) or keep it (0). The shared pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among all six players. Individually, every coin you contribute yields only 3.7/6 ≈ 0.617 back to you, so the private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). The social optimum (all contribute) would maximize group payoffs, but individual incentives push toward zero contribution. Uncertainty and strategic dynamics arise because:
- Players do not see others' choices before acting.
- The number of rounds is unknown, so strategies like “build cooperation early” or “exploit late” are possible.
- Players can send messages to try to coordinate or signal intentions; messages can build trust but are noisy and costly (cooperators may be exploited).
Given the personas, I predict a mixed dynamic: a few conditioned cooperators (Duck, sometimes Dog, sometimes Parrot) will try to kick-start cooperation early and intermittently. Others (Sloth, Gorilla, Chicken) will often defect or test cooperation opportunistically. Cooperation will appear in early rounds and recur in isolated attempts, but repeated exploitation and the private incentive to keep the 20 will lead contributions to fall back to 0 for most rounds. Chat will be sparse and focused on short coordination attempts and short complaints after bad payoffs.

game_explanation:
This is a small-group public goods game (6 players) with a multiplier of 2.2 on the group pot. Because 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 < 1, each coin you personally contribute returns less than one coin to you directly — so the individual incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride). However, group welfare is highest when everyone contributes, and some players may value reciprocity, fairness, or signaling. The reward stage lets players pay 4 coins per reward unit to give 3 coins to a chosen peer; this is costly and usually used sparingly to recognize high contributors or to reciprocate previous rewards. Because the game lasts only 2 rounds and players know it, there is an endgame effect: strategic players may reduce contributions in round 2 because no future punishment or reward is expected beyond the final interactions. Uncertainty comes from heterogeneous preferences (some players are more cooperative or reciprocal) and from whether others will use the costly reward mechanism.

Given the personas, I expect mixed behavior: a subset will contribute substantially in round 1 (to signal cooperation or because they prefer fairness), a few will contribute little or nothing. Rewards will be few and directed at visibly high contributors. In round 2 contributions will generally decline (endgame), though a few players who were rewarded or who care about norms may still give moderate amounts. Rewards remain sparse in round 2, usually reciprocal or reinforcing the prior round’s recognitions.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits six players against the classic tension: keeping coins for yourself vs contributing them to a common pot that benefits everyone once multiplied. Early rounds often show moderate cooperation as players test each other's behavior. Because the multiplier (1.8) and equal split dilute the private return of contributing, there's an incentive to free-ride; however, social preferences and the possibility of punishment/reward can sustain contributions. Punishment is costly to both parties (cost 2 to punisher, −3 to target per unit), so players will use it sparingly — typically to deter persistent free-riding. Rewards are less punitive (cost 2, benefit 2) so they may be used occasionally to encourage cooperators. Uncertainty comes from anonymity (players don't see who punished or rewarded them) and from the short horizon (19 rounds) which often produces declining contributions toward the end as the incentive to reciprocate fades.

Expectations for dynamics:
- Early rounds: relatively high contributions and some positive chat to coordinate.
- Middle rounds: one or two players experiment with low contributions; a few targeted punishments appear to stabilize cooperation.
- Late rounds: gradual erosion of cooperation as players anticipate the end, fewer costly sanctions, and lower average contributions.
- Chat: sparse, mostly short normative reminders or brief reactions.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with post-decision punishment and reward. Players have an incentive to free-ride (contribute 0) because each coin they keep returns privately only the fraction of the multiplied pot divided by 6; socially it helps everyone but privately it can be costly. Typical dynamics: some players start cooperative, some start low; cooperators may try to enforce contributions through targeted punishment or encourage contributors with rewards. Because punishment is costly to the source but very damaging to the target, a few punishments can strongly deter free-riding. Rewards are also costly but smaller in effect per unit. With only 2 rounds and identifiable patterns from round 1, expect adaptions in round 2: punished low contributors often increase contributions; cooperators may stay high or increase; unpunished low contributors may continue to free-ride. Chats tend to be sparse and used to signal intentions or protest punishments.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 19 rounds. Each player chooses a contribution (0–20) out of an initial 20; the group sum is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After payoff redistribution, players can spend resources to punish (cost 2 to sender, -3 to target per unit) or reward (cost 2 to sender, +2 to target per unit). Players cannot link specific punishments/rewards back to senders.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds usually show moderate cooperation (mid-level contributions), as people test one another.
- Occasional defections (low contributions) will prompt sparse punishment by some players to deter free-riding. Punishment is usually small (1 unit) and not every player punishes.
- Small rewards may be used to encourage return to cooperation after a defection.
- Cooperation tends to stabilize when punishments/rewards enforce norms; without them contributions drift downward.
- Near the final rounds, backward-induction pressure leads to declining contributions (unraveling), so contributions often fall toward the end. Players rarely spend on punishment in the final round because it has no future deterrent value, but some players still may act normatively earlier.
- Chats will be few and typically used to call out behaviors or encourage cooperation.

Now the round-by-round transcript.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard finite-horizon public goods setting with private endowments of 20 each round and a marginal per-player return from each 20 contributed of 20*3.7/6 = 12.33 (approx). That means each contribution helps the group but is costly to the contributor: individually it's always better to free-ride on others' contributions. Typical dynamics: some early rounds often see a few players test cooperation (cheap talk + initial goodwill), then contributions tend to fall as cooperators are exploited and switch to free-riding. Occasional attempts to restart cooperation can appear (people promising to contribute), but without reliable enforcement these attempts often fail or are only intermittently successful. Because players do not know the number of rounds, there is not the typical endgame collapse based on backward induction, but realized patterns still often decline. Messages will be sparse, mostly short commitments or remarks about being let down.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with a small reward mechanism and 19 fixed rounds. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Each player balances private withdrawal (immediate private payoff) against the group return from contributions (multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly). Contributing benefits the whole group but is individually costly relative to keeping coins.
- Rewarding lets players transfer coins to others (2 coins cost per 1 reward unit, giving the target +2). Because the cost equals the benefit to the recipient, rewards are purely redistributive and are typically used to (a) encourage cooperators, (b) reciprocate past generosity, or (c) signal approval. There is no direct costly punishment (only withholding rewards can function socially as “punishment”).
- Early rounds often see cooperators “test” whether others will also contribute. Given the player personas, expect most postgraduate-educated players (GORILLA, DOG) and other cooperative-looking players (DUCK) to lead with high contributions. SLOTH and PARROT (some college) are more likely to start lower and increase slowly if rewarded or shamed indirectly.
- Rewards will be sparse and focused: small, targeted transfers to high contributors. Over middle rounds, reciprocity and selective rewarding can sustain high cooperation among several players. Towards the end (especially rounds 17–19), players are likely to reduce contributions (end-game decline), and reward activity will drop.
- Communication is allowed but costs nothing; expect a few concise messages to coordinate norms or call out behavior (e.g., “good job,” “let’s keep going,” or “final rounds — don’t trust others”).
- Because players only see their own summary (their net payoff and rewards used/received), public signaling and reputational effects are imperfect — this supports a modest level of rewarding but not extreme policing.

Transcript below simulates these tendencies across 19 rounds. Contributions are integers 0–20 in the order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT. Reward tuples are (source_avatar, target_avatar, unit) with only non-zero actions listed.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with an added costly reward stage. In each round players decide privately how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and divided equally. After seeing the contributions and payoffs, players can spend coins to reward others at a net group cost (each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 3). Because rewards are costly overall (group loses 1 coin per reward unit), they are typically used sparingly and strategically: to reinforce cooperation, signal reciprocity, or encourage higher contributions in the second round. Players who contributed highly in round 1 are the most likely recipients of positive rewards; low contributors are unlikely to be rewarded. Messages are usually short and used to promise cooperation or thank anonymous rewarders.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix of cooperative and cautious behavior: DUCK, CHICKEN, and PARROT (higher education / younger) are likely to contribute relatively high in round 1; GORILLA and DOG will be moderate; SLOTH will contribute little. Rewards will be sparse, directed mainly from cooperators to other cooperators. In round 2 cooperators will mostly maintain or slightly increase contributions if they received rewards; low contributors will tend to remain low or adjust minimally.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This online public goods game pits individual incentives against the group's interest. Each round every player has 20 coins and chooses either to contribute all 20 to the public pot or keep all 20 privately. Contribution multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly across all 13 players means that, if many contribute, everyone benefits more than if no one contributes; however, at the individual level there is a short-run incentive to free ride (keep your 20 while others contribute). After contributions are revealed, players can use costly punishment (each unit costs the sender 3 and removes 11 from a target) and costly reward (each unit costs the sender 3 and gives the target +4). Punishment and reward are anonymous (players see totals but not who did it).

Likely dynamics: early rounds often show moderate cooperation as social norms are still forming. Some free riders appear because defection is tempting. Sparse punishment is used to discipline persistent defectors; sparse rewarding is used to encourage cooperators or those who shift to cooperation. Because punishments are costly and anonymous, they tend to be used sparingly and focused on repeat defectors. Over the 14 rounds cooperation may climb initially as punishments deter defection, possibly reach near-full cooperation mid-game, and then some exploitation can re-emerge later; because identities are fixed across rounds but actions are anonymous, players respond to aggregate signals (levels of punishment/reward and payoffs) rather than targeted retaliation. Uncertainty about who does the punishing keeps retaliation diffuse, and rewards supplement social enforcement.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game for 26 rounds with 12 players. Each round each player either contributes all 20 coins to the shared pot (contribution = 20) or withdraws them (contribution = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly; individually, withdrawing is the dominant action (it yields higher private payoff given the multiplier and group size), while contributing is socially beneficial only if many others contribute. Because contributions are publicly revealed each round, players can condition future choices on observed cooperation. Rewarding is anonymous (recipients cannot identify who rewarded them) and costly (1 coin spent adds 1 coin to the target), so rewards are typically used sparingly to signal appreciation or to try to sustain cooperation.

Likely dynamics here: a subset of players will start cooperating (contributing 20) out of pro-social tendencies or optimism; others will free-ride immediately. Early rounds will show moderate cooperation and a few small positive rewards aimed at cooperators. As the rounds progress and free-riding becomes visible, conditional cooperators will reduce their contributions and cooperation will fall. A few consistent cooperators (here, especially MOOSE) will persist in contributing; they may occasionally reward others as a signal, but rewards remain sparse because they are costly and anonymous. By the late rounds most players will be free-riding, with only one or two reliable cooperators remaining. Chat messages are expected to be infrequent and generally short—requests for reciprocity, short encouragement, or occasional frustration.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and costly punishment/reward. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws it for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly, so the private return from contributing is much smaller than the private gain from defecting. Without enforcement, defection is the dominant strategy. However, punishment (costly to sender, costly to target) and reward (costly to sender, benefits target) can sustain cooperation. Because players know the game length, cooperation often starts moderately high, may be supported early by targeted punishments/rewards, and then gradually decays toward the end as endgame effects kick in. Chat is sparse and used for appeals, complaints, or short coordination. Punishment and reward are used sparingly in real experiments; expect a few punishments of clear free-riders early on and occasional rewards to encourage contributors — with occasional escalation if defection becomes common. Near the final rounds players often reduce contribution because future retaliation is limited.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, and binary contribution choices each round (0 or 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally: a 20-coin contribution yields a personal return of 4 coins (20 * 2.6 / 13 = 4), so contributing is privately costly (net -16) but collectively beneficial. The dominant selfish action in any single round is to contribute 0, but in repeated settings some players start by cooperating, and a few conditional cooperators and prosocial types may use costly rewards or punishments to sustain cooperation. Punishment is expensive for the punisher (cost 3 per unit) but heavily penalizes the target (−11 per unit). Reward units cost 3 and give +4 to the recipient. Players do not see others’ choices before deciding, and they cannot trace who punished or rewarded them (they see only totals), which creates uncertainty about linking costly sanctions to specific behaviors.

Typical likely dynamics here: many players begin by contributing (trust / conditional cooperation), producing a positive social return but individual losses. Over rounds contributions tend to decline as some players exploit cooperators. A few players who value cooperation (or who are reputational/punishing types in their priors) will occasionally spend small amounts on rewards to encourage contributors and occasionally punish clear defectors; in practice punishments are used sparingly because they’re costly and noisy (anonymous). As the finite horizon approaches, cooperation usually unravels (fewer contributors) and sanctioning becomes less frequent, with most players defecting in the final round(s). Uncertainty about who is sanctioning reduces precise retaliation or targeted enforcement; expect low-magnitude rewards/punishments (mostly single units) and few total sanction actions.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits short-term private incentives against a group benefit. Each player privately chooses either to leave the entire 20-coin endowment in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw it all for private use (contribute 0). Every contributed coin yields only about 0.2417 coins back to each player after the multiplier and equal split (2.9 / 12 × 1). That means contributing is costly to the individual in the short run but increases total group payoff. Because contributions are revealed before the reward stage, players can reward (costly) those who contributed — a way to sustain cooperation indirectly. Rewards are costly to the sender (1 coin per unit) and give 1 coin to the recipient; they are therefore used sparingly. Players cannot see who rewarded them in their summary, which softens direct retaliation dynamics but still allows reputation-building via messages and visible rewarding behavior.

Typical likely dynamics here: some players (conditional cooperators) start by contributing, and a small set of them spend a few coins rewarding fellow contributors. Others (consistent defectors) repeatedly withhold contributions. If rewards are focused and frequent enough, cooperation may be sustained early on; otherwise, cooperative players will gradually defect as they get exploited. As the finite horizon approaches, we expect an end-game decline in cooperation (fewer contributors, fewer rewards). Chat is usually sparse but sometimes used to coordinate or shame free-riders; reward actions are also relatively sparse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with voluntary binary contributions (0 or 20) and a reward stage. The social-return logic is straightforward: every 20 contributed increases the group pot by 52, which gives every one of 13 players +4 coins from that single contribution. Individually, however, contributing costs you 20 while raising your own pot share by only 4, so the selfish one-shot incentive is to free-ride (not contribute). Over 14 rounds, two opposing forces will shape play:

- Strategic incentives and end-game effects: Because players know the game length, there's pressure toward defection late in the game (backward-induction logic). Many real participants nonetheless keep contributing for a while — often until the final few rounds.
- Social and informational incentives from rewards: After contributions are revealed players can pay to give rewards (3 cost to giver per unit, 4 gain to recipient). Because players see peers' contributions and who rewarded whom in aggregate (but not who individually rewarded them), rewards can be targeted to contributors and used sparingly to encourage cooperation. Rewards are relatively costly to the giver, so they tend to be occasional and directed at consistent cooperators.

Given typical behavior, I predict an early-to-middle increase in cooperation as players experiment and reward reliable contributors, then a gradual unraveling toward the end as some players start free-riding more often. Rewarding will be sparse (one or two 1-unit gestures most rounds, occasionally 2 units), mainly from cooperators toward other cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and binary contribution choices (0 or 20). The shared pot starts at 20 coins each round, is multiplied by 2.9, and split equally among active players; thus cooperation (contributing 20) benefits the group but costs the individual short-term. Punishment (costly to the punisher, doubly harmful to the target) and reward (less damaging to payers, beneficial to recipients) are available after contributions, and a chat channel allows social signaling.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: a mixture of cooperation and defection. Some players (older, higher education) have priors favoring cooperation; some younger/less socially invested players may defect initially. Early punishments are the main mechanism to discourage defection.
- Mid game: if punishments and occasional rewards are used selectively and credibly, cooperation tends to stabilize for many players. Those who persistently defect will be punished occasionally; cooperators may be rewarded sporadically.
- End game: toward the final rounds, the incentive to sustain cooperation weakens (backward induction / endgame effect). Unless a strong reputation/punishment regime is established, some players will begin to freeride more. Punishment often drops late, which accelerates decline in cooperation.
- Uncertainties: who chooses to punish, how much, and whether punishing is sustained; players don't see who punished them, reducing direct retaliation but also making social enforcement noisier. Chat can coordinate norms but is non-binding.

I will simulate plausible behavior consistent with these patterns: sparse, targeted punishments early to enforce cooperation; occasional rewards to encourage contributors; otherwise short messages to call out or thank players; cooperation mostly high through mid-game, then eroding in the final few rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and a multiplier (2.9) that makes the group benefit of contributing greater than the private use only if many contribute — but each coin contributed returns less than 1 to the individual (2.9/12 ≈ 0.242 per coin). That creates a classic tension: socially optimal cooperation vs. private incentive to free-ride. Typical dynamics: some players will begin cooperating out of reciprocity or fairness, free-riders will exploit the public pot and cause frustration, and a small subset may use costly punishment or rewards to sustain cooperation for a while. Punishment is costly to the punisher and costly to the target (1 cost to sender per unit, -2 to recipient per unit), so it is used sparingly. Rewards are cheaper for the recipient (1 cost to sender, +1 to recipient) and are also used occasionally to signal approval.

Expectations for this game:
- Early rounds: moderate cooperation as social norms form (several players contribute 20).
- Middle rounds: occasional punishments target persistent defectors (SLOTH, SNAKE, DOG, CROCODILE at times) and occasional rewards reinforce contributors; cooperation then fluctuates.
- Late rounds: standard end-game effect — cooperation declines as players anticipate fewer future consequences; punishments become rarer and freeriding increases. By the final rounds most players defect.
- Chat will be sparse: a few polite requests and a few expressions of frustration or thanks. Punishment and reward actions will be present but relatively limited and targeted.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 14-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round players privately choose whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so each additional 20-coin contribution raises every player's share by 4 coins (2.6*20/13 = 4). That means contributing costs the contributor 20 but returns only 4 to themselves directly — a clear individual incentive to free-ride. Collective payoff is maximized when everyone contributes, but unilateral incentive pushes toward defection.

Punishment and reward allow players to change incentives socially: punishment is expensive for the punisher (3 coins per unit) but very costly to the punished (11 coins per unit), while reward costs 3 to the giver but gives 4 to the recipient. Because punishments remove large amounts from targets, they can deter persistent free-riding, while rewards cheaply reinforce cooperation. However, punishments and rewards are visible in aggregate summaries but not attributable to specific senders, creating uncertainty about who enforced norms.

Typical unfolding: early rounds often mix cooperation and free-riding. If a few players consistently defect and earn more, cooperators may begin to use occasional punishment or rewards to enforce contribution norms. If enforcement is used sparingly and effectively, cooperation tends to rise and stabilize; if enforcement is absent, defection may persist. Rewards may also be used to sustain cooperation. Below is a plausible transcript that follows these dynamics: initial mixed contributions, a few targeted punishments to deter obvious free-riders, gradual movement toward near-universal cooperation, and sparse, strategic use of rewards to sustain cooperation.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game (14 rounds) with a binary contribution choice (0 or 20) and a rewarding stage (cost 3 per unit to giver, +4 per unit to recipient). Because the pot multiplier (2.6) and group size (13) imply each player's share equals 0.2 × total contributions, every contributing player effectively increases every player's payoff by 4 (per contributor) but pays 20 themselves. That creates a strong short-run incentive to free-ride: if others contribute, you gain 20 relative to contributing. However, the reward stage allows cooperators to be selectively compensated, which can sustain cooperation if enough players are willing to spend to reward contributors.

Typical dynamics here will be: some initial cooperative contributions from a subset of players, occasional small-scale rewarding directed at cooperators to reinforce cooperation, and gradual erosion of cooperation as defectors exploit contributors. Toward the final rounds, backward induction and mounting net advantage for defecting make contributions rare. Rewards are expected to be sparse (1-unit gestures or reciprocal 1-unit exchanges), focused mainly on those who contribute, and used intermittently rather than widely.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, a binary contribution choice (0 or 20), a pot multiplier of 2.6 and costly rewarding (3 coins per unit given, 4 coins received per unit). Individually, contributing 20 yields a personal immediate net loss compared with keeping the 20, because each contribution returns only 4 coins to each player (including the contributor) when split across 13 players. So the dominant one-shot incentive is to free-ride. However, players often exhibit conditional cooperation: many will contribute if they expect others to contribute. The reward mechanism allows players to incentivize cooperators (or, indirectly, to “punish” defectors by rewarding cooperators more), but rewards are costly and typically used sparingly.

Given the personas and typical PGG dynamics:
- Expect a mix of early contributions as some players (conditional cooperators, socially oriented) start contributing while others free-ride.
- Small, targeted rewards will be used intermittently to reinforce contributors — usually 1 unit at a time, occasionally 2 — rather than widely or heavily, because reward is costly.
- Cooperation tends to climb if early cooperation and some rewarding are visible, as conditional cooperators join in. But anonymous interactions and imperfect information create uncertainty, so not everyone will join.
- Toward the end of the 14-round horizon, classic end-game effects make defections more likely; some previously cooperative players will reduce or stop contributing, and rewarding becomes rarer.
- Because rewards are anonymous in the summary, reciprocity is blunt and reputational dynamics are limited; the reward instrument is primarily a short-run behavioral nudge.

Predicted path: a modest start with several contributors, rising cooperation through rounds 2–8 as conditional cooperators join and sparse rewards reinforce behavior, then a gradual unraveling in the final rounds as players anticipate the end and reduce contributions; rewards remain sparse throughout and largely target visible cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players and 26 rounds. Each round every player either keeps all 20 coins (contributes 0) or puts all 20 into the group pot (contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly, so a lone cooperator is costly to themselves but benefits the group; group cooperation raises everyone’s payoffs. Because players can reward others (cost 1 to give 1), cooperation can be sustained by positive incentives: cooperators reward cooperators to create benefits for good behavior. Communication is allowed but anonymous; messages help coordinate norms but cannot perfectly identify who rewarded whom.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: mixed behavior as players test others. Some will signal cooperation; a few will free-ride.
- If cooperators selectively reward other cooperators, cooperation tends to grow and stabilize mid-game.
- Communication can reinforce norms (brief “I’ll contribute” or “please contribute” messages).
- Toward the end (final rounds), backward-induction pressure makes defection more attractive; cooperation often erodes in the last several rounds.
- Rewards are typically sparse and targeted (a few 1–2-unit rewards per round), used to reward reliable cooperators rather than to punish directly (there’s no formal punishment, only withholding rewards).

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round binary public goods game with 13 players. Each round each player privately chooses to contribute 20 (cooperate) or 0 (defect). All contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. Individually, contributing is costly (you pay 20 but get only 4 per fellow player per 20 contributed, so the personal return is lower than keeping the 20), so the short-run incentive is to free-ride. However, social motives and conditional cooperation often produce intermediate levels of contribution early on. Because players can punish (costly to sender, heavy penalty to target) and reward (costly to sender, modest benefit to target) after seeing revealed contributions, we expect:

- Early rounds: a mix of contributors and defectors. Some players punish or reward sparingly to enforce cooperation or reward cooperators.
- Middle rounds: if punishment/rewards succeed, cooperation can rise; otherwise it drifts down as free-riding pays off.
- Late rounds (end game): cooperation typically declines (backward induction / end-game effect) as the incentive to invest in future reciprocity fades.
- Punishment and reward actions are rare and targeted (a few 1-unit actions), because sending costly sanctions is itself costly and anonymity of punishers reduces direct retaliation.

The transcript below simulates one plausible unfolding that follows these tendencies: moderate cooperation early, some punish/reward to steer behavior, mid-game higher cooperation, and eventual decline toward defection in later rounds. Each round shows the contribution vector (using the exact avatar order in <PLAYERS>), then any punishment/reward actions (tuples of (source_avatar, target_avatar, unit); negative unit = punishment, positive = reward). After that is the round summary shown to players: for each avatar the summary lists contribution, coins spent on punishing, coins spent on rewarding, amount deducted by punishment (coins lost), amount received in rewards (coins gained), and the player's net payoff this round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players, each choosing whether to keep the full 20 privately (contribute 0) or leave it in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly, so a single cooperator helps the whole group but bears the private cost of giving up private 20. Because the game lasts a known 26 rounds and players can punish or reward others after seeing round outcomes, we can expect these dynamics:

- Early rounds: Many players will try to signal cooperation to earn group returns and avoid being targeted. A few players (testing or opportunistic) will defect; they will often be punished by several cooperators to enforce norms. Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce consistent cooperators.
- Middle rounds: If punishment/reward remains low-cost enough relative to perceived benefit, cooperation can stay moderate-high. Occasional retaliatory or exploratory defections will be met with selective punishments, but identity-unobservable punishments reduce coordinated retaliation.
- Late rounds: As the end approaches, forward-looking incentives to sustain cooperation decline. Expect a gradual drift toward more defection (the classic end-game unraveling), with punishments and rewards becoming rarer and less effective. By the final round most players are likely to defect.

Players will send few, purposeful chat messages (promises, calls for punishment, short complaints). Punishments are typically sparse and targeted at repeated defectors; rewards are even rarer and usually directed at consistently cooperative players.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, full information about the rules, and a known finite horizon of 26 rounds. Individually, taking the 20 coins (defecting) is a dominant one-shot choice because you keep 20 and still receive a share of the multiplied public pot; contributing (leaving 20 in the pot) benefits the group but costs you personally. However, with the ability to punish and reward, some players can sustain higher cooperation early on by imposing small costs on persistent free-riders or by rewarding cooperators. In practice we expect a modest level of cooperation at the start (some players try to set a norm), occasional targeted punishments of repeat defectors, and sparse rewards to signal appreciation. If enforcement (punishment/reward) is effective, cooperation can rise for a while. As the end approaches, backward-induction incentives make defection more attractive, punishers may stop bearing costs, and cooperation typically unravels in the later rounds. Chat messages will be sparse and mostly normative appeals or brief reactions.

game_explanation:
This is a 13-player repeated public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) each round. The group pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so a single 20-coin contribution raises the total pot by 52 coins and gives each player 4 coins. Because an individual's private cost of contributing is 20, the private marginal return is 4 < 20: the one-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Group welfare is highest if many contribute, so cooperation requires social motives or mechanisms. The reward stage (cost 3 per reward unit to sender, +4 to receiver) allows players to reward cooperators (or retaliate), which can encourage cooperation by changing payoffs and signaling preferences — but rewards are costly and often used sparingly. Players do not see others' current-round choices before deciding, and reward sources are anonymous in summaries, which weakens direct targeted reciprocity; however, observing peers' past round payoffs and reward amounts across rounds can create conditional cooperation and reputational dynamics.

Common unfolding to expect: some players start cooperating (intrinsic cooperators), others start by free-riding. Early small rewards are used to encourage cooperators; if rewards successfully boost cooperators' net payoffs, more players may switch to contributing in middle rounds. Near the end (final few rounds), backward-looking incentives and anonymity usually cause cooperation to decline (endgame effect). Rewards tend to be few per round and modest in size (1–2 units) and primarily directed at cooperators.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game pits individual short-run incentives against group benefits. Each round every player receives 20 coins and must either keep them (contribute 0) or put them into a shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all 13 players, so the marginal per-capita return on a 20-coin contribution is (2.6/13)*20 ≈ 4 coins back to the contributor. That means contributing 20 directly costs a contributor 16 coins in that round compared to keeping the 20 — so the one-shot dominant action is to defect (keep 20). However, if many players contribute, everyone can get high payoffs (the social optimum is all contribute).

The reward stage allows players to transfer costly tokens to others: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 4 coins. Rewards are a positive but costly way to incentivize cooperation (senders pay, recipients gain). Because the game is repeated for 14 rounds, reciprocity and conditional cooperation can sustain contributions: players who expect others to cooperate may keep cooperating; those who see defectors may stop cooperating or withhold rewards. Uncertainty exists because choices are simultaneous within rounds (no pre-play information) and reward givers are anonymous in each player’s summary (players learn amounts given/received but not who sent them), so signalling and reputation are imperfect.

Given the player personas as priors, I expect a mix of conditional cooperators and some consistent free-riders. Early rounds will show fairly high cooperation from more educated/postgrad players (e.g., GORILLA, RABBIT, OWL, CROCODILE, COW, PARROT) and some gradual learning from others. Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation (usually 0–2 small, targeted units to cooperators each round). As the game approaches the final rounds, cooperation will decline (end-game unraveling), with fewer contributors and fewer reward transfers.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds. Each round players choose either to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment to the public pot (cooperate) or withdraw it privately (defect). The common pot is multiplied by 2.9 and shared equally, so your round income comes from whatever private withdrawal you kept plus the equal share of the multiplied public pot. After payoffs are calculated, players may spend coins to reward others (1 coin spent transfers 1 coin to the target). Rewards are visible as totals and recipients but recipients cannot identify who gave them rewards in their summary; senders pay the cost immediately.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: With no prior information, many players default to selfish (withdraw for private use). A few players with stronger cooperative priors may try contributing; because rewards are possible, early cooperators may use small rewards to encourage others.
- Middle rounds: If a critical mass of cooperators forms and participants use rewards to reinforce cooperation, cooperation tends to increase. Players who benefit from the public pot but do not reward others may be seen (implicitly) as free riders — that can reduce cooperative momentum unless cooperators use rewards strategically.
- Reward use: Because each reward costs as much as it transfers, players will reward sparingly and mainly to signal appreciation or to encourage conditional cooperators. Rewards are usually small (1–3 units) and directed at cooperators.
- Endgame (final rounds): As the known horizon approaches, cooperation tends to decay (last-round defection), because rewards cannot credibly alter future behavior. Expect cooperation to drop in the last few rounds.
- Uncertainties: Individual variation in risk preference and beliefs about reciprocity makes behavior noisy: occasional defectors reward to signal a desire to coordinate, and occasional cooperators withdraw unexpectedly.

This transcript simulates plausible choices (contribute 0 or 20), sparse chat, and modest reward use across 26 rounds, showing gradual growth of cooperation mid-experiment and a decline toward the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, binary contribution (leave the full 20 in the shared pot, or withdraw all 20 privately), redistribution, then optional costly punishment (1 to punish, -2 to target per unit) and costly reward (1 to reward, +1 to target per unit). Players know the game lasts 26 rounds but cannot identify who punished or rewarded them. That creates typical dynamics:

- Early rounds often start with relatively high cooperation as players test norms and hope others will contribute.
- Free riders benefit immediately (they keep 20 privately and still receive a share of the multiplied pot), so without deterrence cooperation tends to erode.
- Punishment can sustain cooperation but is costly to the punisher; because punishers are anonymous, the signaling value is imperfect and punishment tends to be used sparingly.
- Rewards can encourage cooperators at lower direct harm than punishment, but are also costly.
- Expect fluctuations: early cooperation, occasional punishment spikes when defectors are common, temporary recoveries when punishers/rewarders enforce norms, and a gradual drift toward more withdrawal (defection) late in the game as the cost of enforcement accumulates and players anticipate endgame defections.
- Chat is typically sparse and used to call out behavior or coordinate norms; a few players (those predisposed to cooperate or to police) will send most messages.

I'll simulate these tendencies across 26 rounds. Contributions will be given in the exact avatar order. Punishment/reward tuples use (source_avatar, target_avatar, unit) where unit > 0 is reward, unit < 0 is punishment. Only non-zero edges are listed.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 14-round public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so group benefit from contributions is real but individual incentives favor free-riding each round. Because players can punish (costly to punisher, much larger harm to target) and reward (cheaper to receiver than cost to giver), we expect enforcement and positive reinforcement to be used sparsely to sustain cooperation early and mid-game. Typical dynamics:

- Early rounds: some baseline cooperation from norm-followers, but a few unconditional free-riders may appear. Cooperators will occasionally use small punishments or rewards to signal norms; punishments tend to be rare and small.
- Middle rounds: cooperative respondents often return contributions after being punished; this can raise contribution rates. Rewards appear occasionally to reinforce cooperators. Punishment occurs mainly directed at persistent defectors.
- Late rounds (toward round 14): end-game incentives reduce the effectiveness of sanctions; cooperation commonly unravels as the future value of enforcing norms vanishes. Defection increases and sanctions taper off (players avoid wasting resources on last-round punishments).

Uncertainties: exact individual conditional strategies (who is perfectly conditional, who is stubborn) are unknown; players' personas give priors but not certainties. Punishment/reward decisions are private and anonymous about source-target mapping, so players infer motives only from outcomes.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 26 rounds, binary contribution choices (0 or 20), anonymous rewards, and full knowledge of the horizon. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Individual incentive each round: withdrawing all 20 (contribute 0) gives you the private 20 plus whatever you receive from the multiplied public pot; contributing 20 is costly privately but increases the total group pot (which is multiplied by 2.9 and shared). Without enforcement, defecting (0) is a dominant one-shot action.
- Rewards are the only active enforcement tool. They cost the sender 1 coin per unit and give 1 coin to the target. Because reward-givers are not identified by recipients, rewards are primarily useful to signal and sustain cooperation among senders who want to reward cooperators (and to show appreciation), but this signal is diluted by anonymity.
- Early rounds typically see experimentation and some cooperation from those inclined to contribute (education/postgrad personas tend to contribute more in similar settings). Some players will free-ride early. If cooperators organize informal norms via sparse chat and selective rewarding, cooperation can grow and be sustained for many rounds.
- Finite-horizon unraveling: as the end approaches, incentives to contribute fall (backward induction). Even with rewards, most groups see declining contributions in the final 5–7 rounds, often ending with widespread defection in the last round.
- Uncertainty: players cannot see others' individual contributions before choosing, and they do not see who rewarded them later. This reduces perfect reciprocity; rewards will therefore be modest and concentrated among a small core group committed to sustaining cooperation.

I predict: a moderately cooperative start with a core of contributors (especially those with postgraduate backgrounds). Rewarding will be sparse but targeted among cooperators. Cooperation rises to a strong midgame (rounds ∼8–16) driven by repeated mutual rewarding, then erodes toward the end as players anticipate the endgame. Chat will be sparse, used mainly to praise or complain, not to coordinate formal agreements.

game_explanation:
This public goods game sets up a standard social dilemma: each player can either contribute their whole endowment (20 coins) to a group pot or keep it. The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and then split equally among the 13 players, so each additional contributor increases everyone's share by a fixed amount (4 coins per contributor). Individually, however, contributing is costly (you forgo 20 coins), so the one-shot incentive is to free-ride. Because the horizon is finite (14 rounds) and decisions are anonymous within a round, I expect an initial level of cooperation from several players (especially those whose background/persona suggests conditional cooperation), followed by a gradual erosion of contributions as free-riding pays off and others respond. The reward stage provides a way to reinforce cooperation: sending reward units is costly to the sender (3 coins per unit) but benefits the recipient (4 coins per unit). Because rewards are visible in aggregate (each player's total given and received are shown) but not linked to who rewarded whom, they can sustain cooperation a bit by signaling and reciprocation, but they are used sparingly in real experiments because they are costly. Overall I predict: moderate initial cooperation, some fluctuations as players experiment, a general downward trend across the middle rounds, occasional short rebounds when rewards signal reciprocal intent, and lower contributions toward the final rounds (endgame defection).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and a large benefit-to-cost ratio for public contributions (each contributed 20 becomes 2.9× and then split). Individually, withdrawing (defecting) is profitable against cooperators, but mutual cooperation yields much higher group payoffs. With no anonymity breakdown and no binding commitments, typical dynamics are:

- Early rounds: many players try cooperating (contributing 20) to see if others reciprocate. A few will test free-riding by withdrawing privately (contribute 0).
- Mid-game: cooperators may punish repeat defectors (costly but can enforce cooperation). Rewards may be used occasionally to encourage contributors. Punishment and reward are used sparsely because they cost senders real coins.
- Retaliation and conditional strategies: some defectors who are punished may switch to cooperating; some punished players retaliate with punishment in later rounds, producing a small amount of conflict.
- Endgame: because the horizon is known and finite, cooperation tends to unravel toward the last rounds; many players stop contributing and punish less in the final rounds. Expect a gradual decline in contributions and an uptick in defection in the last ~5 rounds, culminating in near-universal defection by round 26.

I will simulate a plausible run: initial strong cooperation with a few persistent free riders (DOG, SNAKE, PARROT), occasional punishments by norm-enforcers (SLOTH, GORILLA, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL), a few small rewards to reinforce contributors, some retaliation episodes, and a steady decay of cooperation toward the end. Chat messages are sparse and purposeful (calls to cooperate, naming defectors, short reactions).

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (keep 20) against a group benefit (pooled contributions multiplied by 2.6 and split equally). With 13 players, contributing 20 will help the group but can be individually exploited: if many contribute, an individual who withholds nets more that round. Typical dynamics: moderate initial cooperation, some free-riding, occasional targeted punishments or small rewards to signal norm enforcement or appreciation, and a gradual decline in contributions as the end of the 14-round horizon approaches (an “endgame” effect). Because punishments are costly to send and very costly to receive, players punish sparingly; rewards are also used sparingly because their payoff effect on recipients is smaller per cost. There is uncertainty about who will enforce norms and whether rewards/punishments will be enough to sustain cooperation — so behavior drifts between cooperation and defection over rounds.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game gives each player a binary choice each round: contribute the full 20 coins to the shared pot, or withdraw them all (contribute 0). Contributions are multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so as usual a defector (who keeps 20) always gets a higher immediate private payoff than a contributor unless compensation arrives through rewards. After payoff redistribution, players can spend coins to give 1-for-1 rewards to others; sending a reward costs the sender and benefits the recipient. Rewards are anonymous in receivers' summaries, so rewards can encourage and recognize contributors but do not create clear individual reputations.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: uncertainty. Some pro-social players (those with postgraduate backgrounds and higher pro-social priors) will try contributing and use rewards to support fellow contributors. Others will free-ride to earn the private 20 plus their share, observing whether reward flows compensate contributors.
- Middle rounds: contributors who are not sufficiently compensated will start defecting; overall cooperation tends to decline unless a stable cluster of rewarders sustains it. Because rewards are costly and anonymous, they will be used sparingly and mainly to sustain a small group of cooperators.
- Late rounds / endgame: as the horizon shortens, contributions typically fall; rewards are used less, and many players defect. There can be occasional resurgences if a few players try to "restart" cooperation with visible rewards and chatter, but without identifiable reciprocity these attempts are fragile.
- Uncertainties: players might experiment, try coordinated signals in chat, or spend rewards to maintain small cooperative groups. The anonymity of reward origin makes it hard to tie rewards to specific actions, reducing the power of conditional strategies.

The transcript below simulates 26 rounds (players: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE). At each round I note why I expect the choices, show contributions (0 or 20 per player in avatar order), show a sparse chat, record the (sparse) reward actions, and give each player's round summary (their own net payoff, reward sent, reward received). Numerical payoffs are shown to two decimals.
