game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, and binary contributions each round (0 or 20). The group multiplier (2.6) and equal split mean the marginal per-coin return to the contributor is 2.6/13 = 0.2. That is, for every coin a player contributes they get back only 0.2 coins from the pot — so the individual monetary incentive each round is to free-ride (contribute 0) while hoping others contribute. The social optimum (maximizing group payoff) is for everyone to contribute, because the group benefits more than individual cost overall.

Because choices are simultaneous and punishers/rewarders are anonymous, uncertainty about others’ behavior is key. Early rounds often include some experimentation (a mix of contributors and free-riders). Conditional cooperators (people who reward/punish or follow others) will tend to respond to observed summaries. Punishment is costly to both parties (source pays 3 per unit to reduce 11 from target), so in practice punishments are used sparingly and usually to discipline persistent free-riders. Rewards are also costly but give positive returns to the target (source pays 3, target gets +4 per reward unit) and are used selectively to reinforce cooperation.

Given the personas: several players (postgraduates and college-educated) are likely to start cooperating or shift to cooperating when rewarded/punished; players with less formal education or younger participants may test the waters or free-ride early. Expect: (1) an initial mix of contributions with a handful of defectors; (2) modest, sparse punishments/rewards in early rounds to signal norms; (3) mid-game convergence toward higher cooperation as defectors are discouraged and cooperators reciprocally reward; (4) some opportunistic lapses mid-game; (5) a typical end-game unraveling in rounds 13–14 where private incentives dominate and many players defect, with punishment used less because it is costly and less effective at the very end.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round repeated public goods game with 12 players. Each round each player either contributes all 20 coins to the shared pot (cooperate) or withdraws them all for private use (defect). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 (so each contributed 20 yields 58 to be divided among 12 players, i.e. a per-player increment of about 4.8333 per cooperator). After payoffs are distributed, players may send positive rewards (each costing the sender 1 coin and giving 1 coin to the target). Players can also send chat messages; messages are public but nonbinding. Reward receipts are shown to recipients at the end of the round but recipients do not see who rewarded them.

Typical dynamics to expect here:
- Immediate incentive: defecting gives you 20 private coins plus your share of the multiplied pot; this usually beats contributing in a single round. So in early rounds, conditional cooperators and social preferences often produce some cooperation but not full cooperation.
- Positive rewards can sustain cooperation by compensating cooperators for the shortfall vs. defectors. Because rewards are costly, they tend to be sparse and targeted to a few cooperators each round.
- Communication can encourage cooperation but is not fully credible. Expect short, purposeful messages early (calls to cooperate, thanks) and more complaints as cooperation falls.
- Over time, with a known finite horizon (26 rounds), backward induction tends to push cooperation down toward the end; rewards may slow that decline but seldom prevent a late-stage collapse.
- Uncertainty: players differ in priors (some more likely to be conditional cooperators, some more self-interested); anonymity of rewards in summaries weakens direct reciprocity but does not prevent targeted rewarding right after contributions are revealed.

I predict initial rounds will show a reasonable level of cooperation (a majority cooperating by round 5–6, sustained with occasional rewards). From around the midpoint players will start pulling back, and cooperation will decline steadily; by the final round(s) cooperation will disappear.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players and 26 rounds. Each round every player effectively decides whether to keep their 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly, so contributors bear the private cost but can increase everyone's payout if enough people contribute. Because contributions are revealed before rewarding, players can use rewards to encourage cooperators or indirectly signal approval of cooperative choices. Rewards are costly to give (1 coin per unit) but pass the same amount to the recipient; they are the only punishment / reward instrument available (no negative transfers).

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often see a mix of cooperation and defection because participants are uncertain about others' types. Some players (the "consistent cooperators") will contribute almost every round; some are opportunistic and will defect if they see free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and binary contribution choices each round (either keep all 20 privately or leave it all in the shared pot). The multiplier (2.9) makes the group return on each contributed coin positive but the individual return per coin less than the private 1 coin, so the one-shot incentive favors withdrawing (free-riding). Over repeated interaction, communication, and the ability to punish/reward, we typically see:

- Early rounds: mixed behavior. Some players (intrinsically cooperative types) contribute immediately; others wait or defect.
- Middle rounds: social norms can emerge. Sparse, targeted punishments deter persistent free-riders; small rewards encourage cooperators. Cooperation often rises if punishment/reward are used selectively.
- Late rounds (end-game): a tendency to unwind cooperation as the horizon approaches. Some players start defecting in the final few rounds because future retaliation/reward has limited value.

Uncertainties include who will take on the costly role of punisher, how many will respond to messages, and whether some players will change strategy unpredictably. I’ll simulate a plausible path where a core of cooperators helps steer the group to high cooperation midgame, with a small number of defectors who get occasional punishments; cooperation mostly holds until the final rounds when some retreat to selfish play.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 anonymous players, 14 rounds, and a binary contribution choice each round (0 or 20 coins). Individually, contributing is costly (you lose 20) while the public return to each player from one contributor is only 4 coins (20 * 2.6 / 13), so the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride. Socially, however, mutual cooperation yields higher joint payoffs and can benefit each player if many others contribute.

Typical dynamics: some players start by cooperating (altruists or conditional cooperators), while others free-ride. Cooperators sometimes try to sustain cooperation by using the costly reward mechanism (3 coins per reward unit, giving 4 coins per unit to the recipient). Rewards are usually sparse because they are costly; they tend to flow from cooperators to cooperators to reinforce cooperation. Over repeated rounds, conditional cooperators may reduce contributions if they see many free-riders. Near the end of a 14-round finite game, contributions often decline further (end-game effect) because future reciprocity is impossible. Rewarding can slow decline if visible, but in this game rewarders are anonymous, so its effect is weaker. I therefore predict moderate initial cooperation concentrated among a subset of players, gradual erosion of contributions through the middle rounds, occasional attempts to bolster cooperation by rewarding, and low contributions toward the final rounds with only occasional altruistic contributions.

game_explanation:
This 13-player public goods game pits private incentives (keep 20) against group benefit (contribute 20 to a pot that is multiplied by 2.6 and shared equally). Contributing is socially efficient if many do it, but individually costly each round. Because contributions are revealed each round, players can condition future behavior on others' past choices. The reward stage (cost 3 per unit to sender, +4 to recipient) lets players privately reward or compensate others, which can sustain cooperation but is itself costly. Typical experimental dynamics: some initially cooperative players (especially those with prosocial priors) contribute early; freeriders exploit them and earn higher immediate payoffs; over rounds cooperation tends to decline unless rewards effectively compensate cooperators. Because the game has a known finite horizon (14 rounds), expect gradual unwinding of cooperation toward the end (end-game effect). Uncertainties include how strongly a few prosocial players will persist in contributing and whether the reward stage will be used enough and targeted precisely to sustain contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game (14 rounds) with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses to contribute either 0 or 20 of their 20-coin endowment to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally; with 13 players that means each contributed 20 adds 4 coins to every player (2.6*20/13 = 4). Individually, however, keeping your 20 is better in the short run because each coin you put in returns only 0.20 to you (2.6/13), so myopic incentive is to free-ride. Mutual cooperation yields the highest group payoff (if many contribute), but unilateral deviation is attractive. After returns are distributed, players may spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs a sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 4 coins. Rewards can therefore help sustain cooperation by privately incentivizing contributors, but they are costly to give and are typically used sparingly.

Given typical human behavior and the provided personas as priors, I expect initial rounds to show a moderate level of conditional cooperation (several players contribute), with a few players free-riding and receiving higher immediate payoffs. After returns are distributed, players may spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs a sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 4 coins. Rewards can therefore help sustain cooperation by privately incentivizing contributors, but they are costly to give and are typically used sparingly.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 12 players, fixed identities, 26 known rounds, and a simple binary contribution choice each round (contribute all 20 to the public pot, or withdraw all 20 for private use). The public pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so group-optimal behavior is to contribute, while individual incentives push toward free-riding. Because players see only summary information (their own net payoff, their own reward giving, and amount they received) and cannot identify which peer rewarded them, rewards serve as a coarse tool to (a) reinforce cooperators, (b) reward those who have been generous, and (c) signal intentions through messages. Rewarding is costly (1 coin per unit), so expect sparse, targeted reward transfers.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players test cooperation and typically start fairly cooperative (experimenter effect), while a few test free-riding. Sparse rewarding appears to reinforce cooperators.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators respond to observed payoffs and rewards; if defectors persist without being sanctioned (no negative rewards here), cooperation may decline. Some players attempt to coordinate via chat and selective rewarding.
- Late rounds: end-game effect — cooperation tends to fall as the horizon approaches because future reciprocity wanes. Rewarding becomes rarer; last-round defection is common. Occasional players keep cooperating out of principle or to signal identity.

Uncertainties remain: we don't observe exact payoffs shown to each player in the transcript (players do), and players differ in dispositions. The provided personas suggest a mix of likely conditional cooperators and some more self-interested or inconsistent players; this will produce a gradual erosion of cooperation with intermittent pockets of sustained cooperation supported by targeted rewards and brief chats.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and binary contribution choices each round (keep all 20 privately or leave 20 in the common pot). The public pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so any single player's private payoff is higher in a one-shot view if they withdraw (free-ride), but if enough people contribute the group payoff is higher. Because the horizon is finite and known (26 rounds), classic backward-induction predicts collapse toward defection near the end; in practice, experiments usually show some cooperation early and decay as the end approaches.

Expectations for the dynamic in these rounds:
- Early rounds: moderate cooperation from players who are dispositionally more pro-social (RABBIT, OWL, MOOSE, DUCK, DOG), supported by small selective rewards to reinforce contributors. Some players (SLOTH, PARROT, CROCODILE, CHICKEN) will free-ride from the start.
- Middle rounds: cooperation drifts down as conditional cooperators respond to observed free-riding and as the effectiveness of small rewards wanes. Rewards remain sparse, mostly used to reward consistent contributors or to punish chronic free-riders, but because reward is costly (1 coin per unit), it's used selectively.
- Endgame (last few rounds): cooperation falls substantially. By the final round (26), nearly everyone defects (withdraws private coins). Some isolated contributions may persist for reputation or altruism, but overall contributions trend to zero.
- Chat will be sparse and utilitarian: brief appeals to cooperate, a few thank-you messages, occasional short threats or complaints about free-riders.
- Rewards will be small and targeted (1–2 units), used occasionally to sustain cooperation early and occasionally to punish persistent defectors. Many rounds will have no rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, binary contributions (0 or 20), a pot multiplier of 2.6, and decentralized costly punishment and reward. Individually, contributing is costly: each 20-coin contribution returns only 2.6/13 ≈ 0.2 per coin to the contributor, so free-riding dominates myopically. However, with repeated interaction and the ability to punish or reward, cooperative contributions can be sustained: a few early rewards and targeted punishments of clear free-riders tend to raise cooperation mid-game. Punishment is expensive for sources (3 coins) but very damaging to targets (−11 coins per punishment unit), so players use it sparingly and usually against persistent or obvious defectors. Rewards are cheaper for targets (+4) but still cost senders (3), so they are also used sparingly to reinforce good behavior.

Expectations for dynamics:
- Early rounds: mixed play (some contribute, some free-ride). Small amounts of punishment/reward begin to shape behavior.
- Middle rounds: cooperation rises as players who contribute reward each other or punish repeat defectors; payoffs equalize somewhat.
- Late rounds: "endgame" or unraveling effect — contributions fall as future retaliation/reputation loses leverage; punishment and reward decline because of diminishing future influence.
- Throughout: few punishments/rewards per round, focused on clear defectors or to reinforce reliable contributors. Actions are anonymous in summaries, so players only see totals received/deducted.

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round binary public goods game with 13 players. Each round each player chooses to contribute 20 (full cooperation) or 0 (defection). The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally across all players, so each 20 contributed increases the total pot by 52 and raises every player's share by 52/13 = 4. That means an individual's own contribution gives them back 4 while costing 20 — so purely selfish incentives push toward free-riding (defection). However, repeated interaction, conditional cooperation, and the availability of costly punishments and rewards can sustain cooperation: players who value reciprocity or norms may contribute to encourage others, while targeted punishments (costly to sender, large hit to target) can deter persistent defections; small rewards can reinforce contributors.

Uncertainties:
- Players cannot see others' current-round choices before acting, so expectations are formed from past behavior.
- Punishments/rewards are anonymous to recipients (they don't know who sent them), which limits targeted retaliation but also makes sanctions somewhat blunt.
- Real experimental behavior usually shows an initial cluster of contributors, followed by decline as some players free-ride; occasional punishments or rewards can stabilize cooperation mid-game, and end-game declines often occur (last rounds see more defection).
- Punishments and rewards are typically used sparingly (both costly to the sender), so I predict relatively few non-zero sanctioning actions per round, concentrated when some players consistently defect.

Given the player personas, I'll treat DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, and OWL as relatively more likely to begin cooperating; SLOTH, GORILLA, DOG, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, and COW are more likely to defect initially. Over rounds there will be some ebb and flow: early cooperation, mid-game decline, a few targeted punishments and small rewards restore cooperation partially, and a final-round collapse or isolated acts of spite.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players for 26 rounds. Each round each player either leaves the 20-coin endowment in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws it for private use (contribute 0). The group pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly; this gives a per-cooperator incremental benefit to everyone (about 4.8333 coins per cooperator) but a private incentive to withdraw 20 (keep it privately). The punishment/reward stage lets players impose small costs on others (punishment: 1 coin cost per unit, -2 coins to target per unit) or give small positive transfers (reward: 1 coin cost per unit, +1 coin to target per unit). Messages can be used to communicate intentions, warnings, or thanks.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often feature a mix of cooperation and free-riding. When free-riders are visible in payoffs (even though identities of punishers/rewarders are private), some players respond by punishing or rewarding, which nudges others toward cooperation.
- Players who punish reliably (or are perceived to be willing to punish) help sustain cooperation over the middle rounds. Rewards are used selectively to reinforce cooperators.
- Toward the end of the 26-round horizon, incentives to defect grow (end-game effect). Cooperation typically declines in the final rounds because future punishment is less effective; some players may still cooperate out of fairness or habit, and a few punishing players may continue to sanction defectors even late in the game.
- Punishments and rewards are sparse: only a few edges per round. Chats are sparse and purposeful: warnings, offers, thanks, or end-game taunts.

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round public-goods game with 13 players. Each round every player chooses between contributing their whole 20-coin endowment to a shared pot or keeping it. The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and then split equally among all players; with 13 players that means each contributor increases every player's payout by 4 coins (20 * 2.6 / 13 = 4 per contributor). Thus contributing benefits the group (each contributor adds 52 coins total to the pot, which becomes +4 to everyone), but keeping the 20 privately gives an immediate individual advantage of +20 relative to a contributor. That sets up the classic temptation to free-ride: if others contribute you can keep 20 and still get the pot share.

The reward stage lets players use some of their round earnings to send positive transfers to others: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the receiver 4 coins. Rewards therefore are costly, so they tend to be used sparingly to encourage cooperators (rewarding contributors) rather than to punish defectors (which you cannot do directly). Because players do not see who rewarded them, rewards can signal appreciation but create uncertainty about which specific players were being targeted.

Given these incentives and the finite 14-round horizon, the common pattern is:
- Early rounds: moderate-to-high cooperation as many players try contributing to see mutual benefits.
- Mid-game: cooperation often drifts down as defection pays better and rewards are sparse and noisy.
- End-game: contributions typically decline further as the incentive to invest in future reciprocity disappears.
- Rewarding behavior is usually limited: a few 1-unit rewards to cooperators each round, often reciprocated between pairs; large-scale rewarding is uncommon.

Uncertainties here include individuals' priors (some players may be more trusting or more strategic), the inability to identify which peer rewarded whom (which reduces targeted reciprocal strategies), and noisy signals in the reward stage. Expect a gradual decline in contributions over rounds with occasional small rebounds if some players reward cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round public goods game with 12 players. Each round each player either withdraws their private 20 coins (contribute 0) or leaves the 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so full-group cooperation maximizes group payoff but each individual gains more in the short run by free-riding. Because contributions are revealed before punishment/reward, players can target sanctions or rewards to influence future behavior, but punishments and rewards are anonymous to recipients (they see only totals). Typical dynamics: some players start cooperative, others free-ride. Cooperators may use small punishments to deter persistent free-riders and occasional rewards to encourage newcomers; this can build toward high cooperation. Over time, however, as the end of the game approaches, the incentive to free-ride strengthens (endgame), punishments become less effective or less frequent, and cooperation often unravels toward the final rounds. Expect sparse chat, occasional targeted punishments early to establish norms, a period of relatively high cooperation mid-game, then gradual decline and collapse near the last few rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, and an anonymous reward stage after contributions are revealed each round. Incentives: contributing 20 coins to the public pot helps the group (pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally), but any single contributor typically gets a lower immediate private payoff than free-riders because the benefit from one person's 20 is diluted across all 13 players. This creates the typical tension: group welfare rises with more contributors, but individual incentive is to defect each round. Because rewards are costly to the sender (3 coins per reward unit) but beneficial to the recipient (4 coins per unit), rewards can be used to sustain cooperation by rewarding contributors and withholding rewards from defectors. Uncertainties include beliefs about how many others will contribute, whether rewards will compensate cooperators, and end-game effects (as the 14th round approaches, forward-looking incentives to cooperate fall).

Likely unfolding: early rounds typically see moderate-to-high cooperation (some players try contributing and reward reciprocally). Contributors may be rewarded modestly (1 unit) by a few peers; rewards are sparse because each 1-unit costs 3 coins. Over middle rounds, if rewards successfully signal reciprocity, cooperation can be sustained at an intermediate level. Toward the final rounds (especially the last 2–3 rounds), forward-looking defections increase and cooperation collapses, and reward activity declines. Given the provided personas, expect a set of reliably cooperative players (GORILLA, DUCK, MOOSE, OWL, COW, RABBIT, DOG) who contribute more often early on, and a cluster of more variable or defecting players (SLOTH, CROCODILE, SNAKE, PARROT, CHICK, CHICKEN) who fluctuate or drop out over time.

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round: some players test cooperation. Many of the more prosocial personas try contributing to signal willingness to cooperate; others free-ride to see how the group responds. Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperators.
Seeing some mutual rewards and moderate cooperation, a few previously hesitant players (e.g., PARROT, CHICKEN) try contributing. Rewarding remains reciprocal among cooperators to sustain behavior.
Some fluctuation: PARROT pulls back, but many core cooperators stay contributing. Rewards are still mostly reciprocal among those who consistently contributed in prior rounds.
A few more players experiment with contributing (SNAKE joins this round). Reciprocity and a small number of targeted rewards continue to prop up cooperation, but not everyone sustains contributing.
Group cooperation remains fairly strong as players reciprocate rewards. Some players who tested cooperation earlier continue; reward activity stays modest and focused on reciprocation.
Some volatility returns (PARROT drops out again). Cooperators continue mutual small rewards, but overall reward frequency slightly declines.
Mid-game: some players reduce their contributions (CHICKEN stops), perhaps testing free-riding again. Rewards remain but are sparser as players conserve resources or respond to defections.
Cooperation begins to erode: OWL stops contributing, and reward activity falls further. Contributors who remain try to support each other but do so less frequently.
Defection is spreading: DOG stops contributing. With fewer cooperators left, rewards become rarer and more selective.

The end-game begins to influence choices. Some players (e.g., DUCK) stop contributing to maximize near-term payoffs; a small core still contributes (GORILLA, MOOSE, RABBIT, COW). Rewards are now mostly between the last cooperators.
As the horizon shortens, only a couple of players keep contributing (RABBIT and COW). They reciprocate modestly, but most players defect.

With two rounds left, cooperation collapses further: only COW continues contributing (perhaps from a preference for cooperation or error). Rewarding largely stops because there are few viable recipients and senders anticipate no future return.
Penultimate round: almost everyone defects to maximize immediate gain; rewards are effectively absent because players prefer conserving their endowment.
Final round: standard backward-induction predicts universal defection. No one expects future reciprocation, so no one contributes or spends on rewards.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, binary contribution choices (20 = full contribution, 0 = withdraw everything). The group return factor (2.9) divided by 12 makes the marginal per-capita return of a contributed coin less than 1 (2.9/12 ≈ 0.242), so individually it's always profitable to withdraw (defect) but collectively everyone is better when most contribute. That tension typically produces a mix of conditional cooperation, occasional punishments to deter free-riding, and some end-game unraveling.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty. Some players (conditional cooperators and prosocial types) will contribute to test others; a few will defect immediately. Punishment is used sparingly to discipline clear free-riders (especially by the more punitive/inequity-sensitive players like RABBIT and OWL).
- Middle rounds: cooperative norm stabilizes if punishment and occasional rewards successfully deter defectors. Rewards (cheap to target) are used occasionally to reinforce cooperators; punishments remain limited because they cost the punisher.
- Late rounds (final ~4–6 rounds): expect more defection as the end approaches (backward induction / unraveling). Punishment declines because it's costly and signals of future retaliation are less credible; you may see some last-ditch punishment from norm enforcers but not enough to fully prevent defection.
- Messaging is sparse and purposeful: short calls for cooperation, brief warnings, a few thank-you messages, and occasional endgame candid messages (“last round, I’m taking mine”).

I will simulate these tendencies. The transcript below shows each round, a short explanation of expectations, sparse chat messages, the contributions in the exact avatar order, and only non-zero punishment/reward actions as tuples (source_avatar, target_avatar, unit). Positive unit = reward; negative unit = punishment.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game where each player has 20 coins and may either keep all 20 or contribute all 20 to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players, so each coin contributed returns 0.19 coins to the contributor personally. That means the individual monetary incentive is always to keep coins (free-ride), while the group as a whole would be better off if many people contributed. Typical dynamics: some initial cooperation or promises may appear, but because contributors get lower immediate payoffs when others free-ride, contributions tend to decline over rounds. Messages can change expectations: a convincing promise or credible pattern of repeated contributions can sustain cooperation for a while, while defections or silence usually accelerate decline. Uncertainty about others' types and the low marginal per-person return (0.19) means cooperation is fragile; expect a few persistent cooperators and many free-riders, with occasional short-lived attempts to revive contributions through chat.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players, a 20-coin endowment per round, and a group multiplier of 3.7. Each coin contributed returns 3.7 total to the pot but only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 to each individual — less than the private value of 1 — so the dominant (selfish) incentive is to keep coins rather than contribute. However, social incentives exist: group payoffs rise with contributions, and the optional reward stage (cost 4 per reward unit to giver, +5 to recipient) lets players reward cooperators at a net group gain (a rewarded unit increases group wealth by 1 overall: -4 +5 = +1 to group total), but reward-giving is itself costly to the sender.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Initial uncertainty and heterogeneity: many players will start with moderate contributions (conditional cooperators), while a few will contribute little (free-riders).
- Conditional cooperation: players who see many contributions will tend to contribute more in subsequent rounds; those who see low contributions will reduce theirs.
- Rewards are used sparingly because they are costly. When they appear, they are typically one or two small positive signals aimed at consistent cooperators (not broad distribution), which supports cooperation among a core subset.
- Because the total number of rounds is unknown to players, some decline toward later rounds is likely as participants become cautious or suspect the game is ending. But persistent small rewards can stabilize a moderate cooperative core.
- Expect overall moderate cooperation (not full contributions) that drifts downward across rounds, with a small, consistent pattern of positive rewards aimed at the most cooperative players.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game pits 15 anonymous players against a social dilemma each round: each has 20 private coins and must decide how many to contribute to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so the social return on contributing is positive (it helps the group) but the private return is less than the cost (so selfish incentive is to free-ride). After returns are distributed, players can spend costly reward units (each unit costs 4 to the sender and gives 5 to the receiver) to reward others; reward decisions are visible in aggregate but not traceable to revealers. Players do not know how many rounds remain, which typically encourages more sustained cooperation early on and makes endgame unwinding less certain.

Likely dynamics here: early rounds are exploratory—many test moderate-to-high contributions to see if others reciprocate. Some players will free-ride or contribute little throughout, extracting a private benefit. Because reward is costly, it is used sparingly to reinforce cooperation: a few players consistently reward high contributors (small units, often 1 or 2). Those who receive rewards tend to maintain higher contributions. Absent systematic punishment, cooperation will drift downward over time but can be stabilized somewhat if a subset of players consistently reward cooperators. Rewards will mainly flow to visibly high contributors in previous rounds; however anonymity of reward sources limits precise reciprocity, so we should expect modest, intermittent rewarding rather than heavy investment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 anonymous players. Each round every player gets 20 coins and decides privately how many to contribute (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and evenly split among all 15 players, so each coin contributed returns 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 coins to its contributor — less than 1. That means the individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) while the group benefit would be maximized if everyone contributed a lot. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect typical conditional-cooperation dynamics rather than a clear endgame collapse: many players will start by contributing moderately (they prefer mutual cooperation), some will free-ride, and others will adjust up or down based on observed payoffs and the use of sanctions.

Punishment and reward are available after contributions are revealed. Because punishment is costly to the sender (4 coins per unit) and inflicts a larger harm on the target (-7 coins per unit), and because rewards also cost 4 and deliver +5 to a target, players tend to use them sparingly. Expect occasional punishment directed at persistent low contributors and occasional rewards to signal or reinforce cooperative behavior. Punishment is typically scarce (used to deter clear free-riding), and rewards are also rare but used to support high contributors.

Uncertainties: players do not know the total number of rounds and cannot trace who punished/rewarded them. This reduces strategic “final-round” retaliation but also makes targeted reciprocal strategies harder. Over rounds we therefore expect: some stable cooperators (moderate–high contributions), some persistent low contributors (occasionally punished), and scattered use of rewards to sustain cooperation. Punishment and reward patterns will be sparse and targeted.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player chooses either to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment to the public pot or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all players. After payoffs are revealed, players may spend costly punishment to reduce other players' round earnings (each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and reduces the target's earnings by 8 coins). Players can send short messages at any time. The finite 15-round horizon creates incentives that typically produce the following dynamics:

- Early rounds: many players experiment and try cooperating. Small numbers of defectors (0 contributors) are common; these are often punished by a few players who value maintaining cooperation. Punishment is used sparingly because it is itself costly.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is credible, cooperation stabilizes. Persistent defectors may be repeatedly punished, and some retaliation can occur, but frequent punishment lowers overall payoffs.
- Late rounds: as the end approaches, the incentive to punish weakens (punishing is less valuable when there are few future rounds). Defection tends to increase in the penultimate and final rounds; costly punishment decreases. In the last round, the standard prediction is universal defection because there is no future to preserve, so punishment usually vanishes.

There is uncertainty about individual tendencies: some players (based on personas) are more predisposed to punish and sustain cooperation (e.g., PARROT, DUCK), while some may defect intermittently (e.g., CHICKEN, SLOTH). Expect sparse chat, occasional threats or calls to cooperate, selective punishment of repeat defectors, and a drift toward more defection in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round binary public goods game with costly punishment. Each round, players decide whether to contribute their whole 20 coins to a shared pot or keep all 20. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split evenly across all 10 players, so when many contribute cooperatively the group payoff rises but defecting can be individually tempting (you keep your 20 plus a share of others' contributions). Because punishment is available (4 coins per punishment unit paid by the sender, 8 coins deducted from the punished player per unit), cooperators can try to enforce contributions, but punishment is itself costly and can provoke retaliation. Players know the game length (15 rounds), so backward induction typically reduces cooperation toward the end. Early rounds tend to show some cooperation and sparse punishment to discipline obvious free-riders; mid-game cooperation can stabilize if punishment credibly deters defectors; late rounds usually see unraveling as future enforcement potential declines. Chats are sparse and used for encouragement, short reprimands, or short pledges.

game_explanation: This public goods game pits individual incentives against group benefit. Each round every player decides privately whether to contribute their whole 20 coins or nothing. If many contribute, the multiplied pot (1.9× total contributions) gives everyone a meaningful return; if few contribute, defecting (keeping your 20) typically yields a higher individual payoff. Expect some initial cooperation driven by social preferences or promises, but also early free-riding because defectors immediately earn more when others contribute. Over repeated rounds players will learn from observed round payoffs: conditional cooperators will reduce contributions after seeing many defectors, while persistent cooperators may continue contributing out of principle. Toward the end of the 15 rounds, the incentive to free-ride grows (the "endgame"), so overall contributions will tend to decline. Sparse messaging can sustain cooperation briefly (pledges and calls for fairness), but given the multiplier (1.9) relative to group size (10), unilateral contribution is costly, so I predict a gradual erosion of cooperation with occasional lone or small-group contributions and mostly defecting play by the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone chooses between giving all (20) or nothing (0) to the public pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so contributions help the group but free-riders gain more locally unless punished. Because punishment is costly (4 coins per unit to sender, 8 coins deducted per unit from the target), expect punishments to be used sparingly and primarily early on to deter persistent free-riders. Early rounds often feature mixed contributions and some targeted punishments by cooperators. If punishment successfully deters defectors, cooperation tends to rise and remain steady for several rounds. Toward the end (the "endgame"), incentives to punish drop and free-riding can increase, so contributions may fall. Chats are usually short — reminders, short accusations, or thanks. Players can identify who punished them in summaries, which informs their subsequent choices.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-player repeated public goods game with contribution, then optional punishment/reward. Each coin contributed yields 3.7 to the group but only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 back to each individual, so individual incentives favor free-riding (contribute 0) while the group welfare is maximized by full contributions. In early rounds we expect heterogeneous behavior: some players (those inclined toward cooperation or with higher education priors) contribute moderately/highly; others test the waters with low contributions. Because punishment and reward are costly for the sender (4 coins per unit) but impactful on targets (−7 per punishment unit or +5 per reward unit), we expect sparing use of these instruments. Punishments will appear primarily as responses to clear low contributions, and rewards will be used to reinforce high contributors. Over rounds cooperation should gradually rise if rewards/punishments are visible and perceived as effective, but some fluctuations persist because players don't know how many rounds remain and punishments are costly to administer.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 players, private endowments of 20 each round, and an efficient multiplier (3.7) on the public pot. Each round players choose an integer contribution (0–20) without seeing others' current choices; after contributions are revealed and redistributed, players can spend costly reward points (cost 4 each) to give small net gains (5 each) to peers. Players also receive a private summary showing their round net payoff and reward flows, but they do not know who rewarded them nor how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others' behavior induces a mix of moderate cooperation from conditional cooperators and low contributions from selfish types. High-ability or more pro-social personas (e.g., OWL, MONKEY, MOOSE) tend to contribute more; some players (e.g., GORILLA, PARROT, PINGUIN, CROCODILE, CHICK) are more likely to contribute very little.
- Rewarding: Because rewards are costly, players reward sparingly and primarily to reinforce high contributors. Reward flows will be few and targeted toward consistently high contributors (OWL, MONKEY, MOOSE), producing small pockets of positive reciprocity.
- Adjustment over time: Conditional cooperators update their contributions upward if they see others cooperating and downward when exploitation by low contributors persists. With persistent defectors, average contributions drift down slowly across rounds. Rewarding can stabilize cooperation among a core of contributors, but cannot fully offset exploitation by many low contributors.
- Uncertainty about horizon: Since players do not know how many rounds remain, end-game collapse (sharp defection) is less extreme; instead, we expect gradual decline rather than a sudden last-round free ride.
- Overall: expect a stable core of moderately-high contributors (especially OWL, MONKEY, MOOSE) who continue to both contribute and occasionally reward each other, while a set of persistent low contributors free-ride. Rewards remain sparse and targeted.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round binary public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone privately chooses either to contribute their entire 20 coins (cooperate) or keep it (defect). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.9, then split equally. Individually, a player always gets 3.8 coins for each person who contributed; defecting preserves the 20 coins plus the shared return. So defecting strictly dominates contributing in a single-round sense, but if many players contribute the group gets higher total payoffs (and contributors can still be better off than the all-defect outcome).

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players (those with stronger pro-social priors or who want to signal cooperation) will contribute, while some free riders will defect. Because defectors immediately get higher round payoffs, conditional cooperators will be tempted to stop contributing unless a stable coordination emerges.
- Midgame: messages can support coordination. Short campaigns of increased contributions can occur when players promise to contribute together; this produces higher but fragile payoffs. Expect variability across rounds as conditional cooperators respond to observed exploitation.
- Endgame: because the horizon is known and the last-round Nash equilibrium is universal defection, contributions normally fall toward zero in the final rounds. Some stubborn cooperators may keep contributing, but most will defect in rounds 14–15.
- Chat tends to be sparse and tactical: occasional commitments ("I'll contribute if you do"), calls-out for free-riders, and a few attempts to coordinate full contribution. Messages can temporarily increase cooperation but seldom sustain full cooperation through the end.

Transcript follows round-by-round, including short pre/post messages, contributions (in avatar order), and the round summary showing each player's net payoff.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated one-shot public goods game (players do not know the number of rounds). Each round players privately choose how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a group pot; contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across all 15 players. After contributions are revealed, players may spend costly reward units (cost 4 per unit) to give positive transfers (5 per unit) to specific other players. Because rewards are costly but increase others' payoffs, they can be used to sustain cooperation by rewarding high contributors or to signal reciprocity. Uncertainties: players do not see others' choices before deciding, and they do not know how many rounds remain, so early behavior often reflects a mix of conditional cooperation, a desire to test others, and some free-riding.

Likely dynamics in this group:
- Early rounds: moderate average contributions as conditional cooperators (those who like fairness and reciprocity) contribute and test the group; a few low contributors ("free riders") contribute little. Some players will use small rewards to signal approval of high contributors.
- Mid-game: conditional cooperators adjust to observed group behavior. If enough people contribute, cooperation can be sustained; if free-riding is salient, contributions tend to drift downward. Rewards are used sparingly — typically a unit or two to select individuals who contributed more than average.
- Late rounds: because the horizon is unknown, some players may continue cooperating while others reduce contributions anticipating possible endgame effects or exploiting cooperators. Rewards become rarer but are still used occasionally to prop up trusted cooperators.

Given the personas:
- Some players (e.g., CHICKEN, DUCK, PARROT, RABBIT, GORILLA) are more likely to contribute above average and to reward generosity.
- Others (e.g., SLOTH, DOG, SNAKE, COW) are more likely to contribute little and rarely reward.
- Many players are conditional cooperators, increasing contributions if the group seems cooperative and decreasing if they see free-riding.

Below is a simulated, plausible transcript for rounds 1 through 9 that follows these patterns: contributions start moderately, a few reward actions appear each round targeting high contributors, and overall cooperation drifts downward toward later rounds with sparser rewarding.

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round uncertainty: many players test the waters. Several personas predisposed toward cooperation contribute fairly high amounts; a few low contributors test free-riding. A small number of players use single-unit rewards to acknowledge high contributors they observe.

Seeing several generous contributions and some small rewards, many conditional cooperators nudge their contributions up slightly. Known low contributors remain cautious. Rewarding continues but remains sparse — mostly 1-unit acknowledgements.

Some players who saw free-riding (very low contributors) begin cutting back. Overall contributions dip a bit. Rewarding patterns show some reciprocity toward consistently high contributors; still few reward actions.

Mid-game learning: seeing persistent low contributors, many conditional cooperators trim contributions to avoid being exploited. Rewards are used to sustain a core of cooperators but remain limited because each unit is costly.
A modest rebound: some cooperators attempt to shore up the group by slightly increasing contributions and sending a few small rewards to reliable contributors. Free riders remain mostly unchanged.

After observing mixed behavior, players settle into slightly lower contributions than mid-game highs. Rewards are selective: often directed toward those who have been reliably cooperative across rounds.

As the session progresses without certainty about remaining rounds, contributions gradually decline. Rewards become rarer; a couple of players still send single-unit rewards to encourage the most cooperative peers.

Approaching the later stage, many reduce contributions further (anticipating possible endgame or reacting to others' lower cooperation). Rewards are used sparingly to maintain a small cooperative core.

Final simulated round: many players further reduce contributions; a few still contribute to preserve norms or because they are unconditional cooperators. A small number of 1-unit rewards are given to the most consistent contributors.

game_explanation:
This 15-round public goods game pits private incentives (keep 20 coins) against group benefits (contribute 20 to a pot multiplied by 1.9 and split equally). Because contributions are binary (0 or 20) and players do not see others' choices before deciding, we expect:

- Early rounds: some mix of cooperation and free-riding. A few players will try contributing to signal goodwill; opportunists will exploit that and earn higher round payoffs.
- Mid game: cooperation typically declines. Contributors see they earn 20 coins less than non-contributors in the same round (given identical total contributions), so conditional cooperators reduce contributions after being exploited.
- Late rounds (especially final rounds): “end-game” incentives drive more defection because future reciprocity is limited; contributions usually approach zero.
- Communication can moderate decline if trusted peers repeatedly pledge contributions (some personalities here—RABBIT and CHICK—are inclined to keep contributing), but without binding commitments many will still prefer to free-ride.

I’ll simulate a plausible trajectory: modest initial cooperation, a fairly rapid decline as free-riding pays off, a few attempts to restart cooperation, then a collapse toward defection near the end. Chat is sparse and purposeful (pledges, brief reactions).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods setting with a punishment/reward stage after contributions. Each round every player has 20 private coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and then divided equally among the 15 players. The social return to the group from each coin is 3.7 (so the group gains from contributions), but the private return to the contributor is only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 per coin — much less than keeping the coin privately (1). That creates the classic social dilemma: group-optimal high contributions conflict with the individually rational motive to free-ride.

Because players do not know how many rounds will be played, they cannot condition strategies on a known end. The punishment/reward stage introduces a mechanism to enforce cooperation: punishment is costly to impose (4 coins per unit) and costly to the target (-7 per unit), while rewards cost 4 to the giver and add 5 to the recipient per unit. These instruments tend to be used sparingly in real experiments; a few targeted punishments can deter persistent free riding, and occasional rewards help sustain cooperation and signal appreciation.

Uncertainties:
- Players don't see others' choices before deciding, so choices are based on priors about others and observed history (only after each round).
- Punishment/reward is anonymous in the summary, so contributors may respond to aggregate signals (net amounts received/used) rather than direct reciprocity.
- Personas provide priors: some players (younger, college-educated) are slightly more likely to contribute higher amounts and to reward cooperators; others may be more conservative or inclined to free-ride. Expect a mix: moderate initial cooperation, a few low contributions, and sparse but targeted punishment and occasional rewards that push contributions upward over rounds.

Expected dynamics:
- Early rounds: heterogeneous contributions (some moderate/high, some low). A few low contributors may be punished lightly.
- Middle rounds: punishments and visible rewards steer cooperation upward; most players converge toward moderate-high contributions.
- Late rounds: if no explicit end is known, cooperation can remain elevated, supported by occasional rewards; persistent low contributors, if any, are punished occasionally.

Trust in cooperative behavior is building. Many players further increase contributions. With fewer obvious defectors, the group uses rewards to reinforce particularly steady cooperators rather than heavy punishments.

By round 6 most players have converged toward mid-to-high contributions. Small, occasional rewards continue to reinforce cooperative behavior; punishments are rare because the norm is internalized and defectors are fewer.
Cooperation is now common. Small fluctuations occur as players respond to net rewards/punishments in their summaries. A couple of modest rewards help maintain high contributions.

With sustained cooperation, the group’s contributions are high and relatively stable. Players who contributed highly across rounds get occasional small rewards; punishments are minimal because few players deviate strongly.

Near the later rounds (players do not know the horizon), cooperation remains high. Players continue to use small rewards to signal appreciation of consistent cooperators; punishments are limited to rare, persistent deviations (none here). The overall pattern is convergence toward group-beneficial contributions enforced by light, targeted incentives.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 anonymous players each round. Each player decides how many of 20 private coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and distributed equally. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish (cost 4 per unit, -7 to the target per unit) or reward (cost 4 per unit, +5 to the target per unit) specific peers. Players do not know how many rounds remain and cannot identify who targeted them in the summary.

Likely dynamics: rounds typically start with moderate contributions as many players sample others' behavior. Some players will contribute high amounts out of pro-social preferences; some will free-ride. When free-riding is visible, a few cooperators may use punishment sparingly to discipline low contributors; occasional rewards also appear to reinforce high contributors. Because punishment and reward are costly, they remain rare. Over repeated anonymous rounds, conditional cooperators often increase contributions if other contributions and feedback (rewards/punishments) indicate cooperation; persistent low contributors may be punished early and then raise contributions. Expect overall contributions to drift upward modestly across nine rounds with a handful of low-cost punitive or rewarding actions scattered early on and declining later.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round binary public goods game with costly punishment. Each round players choose whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so full cooperation is collectively best but individually tempting to free-ride. Punishment is costly for the punisher (4 coins per unit) but inflicts a larger cost on the target (8 coins per unit). Because players see past punishments and can identify who punished them, punishment can enforce cooperation early on. However, punishment itself reduces the punisher's private payoff, so it tends to be used sparingly and strategically — often early to deter free-riding and less in the final rounds when future retaliation or reputation effects vanish. Expect initial attempts at cooperation with a few tests/free-rides, targeted punishments that discourage repeat defectors, fairly stable cooperation during the middle rounds, and unraveling toward the final rounds as forward-looking incentives vanish. Chats will be sparse and mostly for calling out or requesting cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated anonymous public-goods game with 15 players. Each round players decide how many of 20 private coins to contribute. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 3.7 and then split equally among the 15 players, so the private return on one contributed coin is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 coins — less than the private cost of 1 coin. That creates a tension: socially the group benefits from high contributions, but individually each player is better off free-riding if others contribute.

Because punishment and reward are possible after seeing contributions, players can (at a cost) sanction low contributors or reward high contributors. Punishment units are costly to the source (4 coins per unit) and strongly harmful to the target (-7 coins per unit); rewards cost 4 coins and give +5 to the receiver, so these incentives can support cooperation but are also expensive. Players do not know how many rounds remain and cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, which encourages cautious, often sparse use of costly incentives.

Given the personas and typical PGG patterns, I expect moderately cooperative initial contributions (not full 20), some early punishments targeted at clear free-riders, occasional small rewards to high contributors, and a modest decay of contributions over time as players test others, with punishments used sparingly mostly to deter persistent free-riding. Because punishments and rewards are anonymous and costly, only a few players will use them each round and usually for one unit at a time.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game where each round players choose either to contribute all 20 coins or keep all 20. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players. After redistribution, players may spend punishment units (4 coins per unit) that each reduce a chosen target's earnings by 8 coins per unit. Players see round summaries showing who punished whom and the monetary effects, and chat is allowed.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players will contribute to build the public good. A small number of testers/free-riders are likely; other players may use modest punishment to enforce cooperation.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperation means some players respond to others' defections with punishment; some retaliation and occasional non-contributors emerge, so average contributions decline and punishment appears sporadically.
- Endgame (last few rounds): standard "unraveling" suggests contributions drop as the horizon approaches; punishment becomes less frequent because punishment costs the punisher with no future leverage. Chats are sparse and usually normative or reactive.
- Punishment is used sparingly because it is costly; punishers typically target repeat or visible defectors. Players who are repeatedly punished tend to shift toward cooperating, at least briefly.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a pot multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. Because the per-person return on each contributed coin (3.7/15 ≈ 0.247) is well below 1, the one-shot monetary incentive is to keep coins (free-ride). However, the reward stage lets players spend 4 coins to give 5 coins to someone else; that transfer is pro-social overall (+1 net to the group) but personally costly, so rewards can sustain cooperation only when some players care enough to reward contributors.

Important uncertainties: players do not know how many rounds remain and cannot see who rewarded them, only totals. That makes reputational strategies imperfect but still allows conditional cooperation: many players will respond to observed contributions and received rewards. In practice we expect:
- A cluster of “cooperators” who contribute above average and reward other cooperators sparsely.
- A cluster of low contributors / free-riders who contribute little and rarely reward.
- Rewards will be used sparingly and targeted at visibly high contributors; because reward is costly, each player typically gives 0–2 reward units per round and only to a few peers.
- Over rounds contributions may decline somewhat as free-riding pays off, but rewards can stabilize moderate cooperation among the core cooperators.

Below is a round-by-round transcript (round explanations plus predicted actions). Avatar order for contributions is fixed as: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY.

Those who were rewarded or saw their contributions reciprocated are slightly more inclined to keep or raise contributions; un-rewarded cooperators may reduce a bit. Free-riders remain low. A few reciprocal rewards re-appear, focused on consistent high contributors.

With a small pattern of mutual rewards among a subset, several cooperators hold steady or modestly increase. Low contributors stay low; rewards continue to be sparse and targeted. Players who saw positive net returns from contributing are slightly reinforced.

Some conditional cooperators keep contributing strongly; others reduce a little as they test stability. Because rewards are costly, only a few players continue to reward — typically those who themselves are consistent contributors and want to sustain cooperation among peers.

Sustained reciprocity among a core keeps their contributions high. Those who contributed but weren’t rewarded begin to moderate down. Rewards remain selective — mostly one-unit signals aimed at reliable contributors.

A modest drift downward appears as a few players exploit the cooperative core and some contributors trim back. Rewarding is still used by the cooperative core to support one another, but overall reward frequency dips.

As free-riding continues to pay, some marginal contributors reduce again. The core (several high contributors) still reward each other occasionally, which prevents a complete collapse to zero contributions.

Players settle into patterns: a cooperative core contributes in double digits and exchanges occasional rewards; a group of low contributors persist. Rewarding remains targeted and infrequent — enough to sustain moderate cooperation but not to fully crowd out free-riding.

Over repeated play without known end, many players stick with the equilibrium that balances personal incentives and occasional rewarding. The core remains more cooperative; rewards at the end are modest signals among them. Free-riders remain largely unrewarded.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 players who each get 20 coins per round. Private incentives favor free-riding (each contributed coin returns only ~0.247 coins back to the contributor via the group pot), so without reward mechanisms contributions typically start at moderate levels (some people contribute out of intrinsic cooperativeness or social norms) and then decline over time as free-riding becomes apparent. The reward stage (cost 4 to giver, gives 5 to recipient) can sustain cooperation by selectively rewarding high contributors, but real players tend to reward sparingly because rewards are costly. Because players do not know the end point, cooperation often decays gradually rather than collapsing immediately; consistent contributors (based on personas like RABBIT, MONKEY, MOOSE, CROCODILE) tend to be rewarded occasionally by a few peers, which slows but does not fully stop the downward trend. Uncertainty about others' motives and the limited power of rewards (they are costly) means we expect a modest initial average contribution that drifts downward across rounds, with a small number of one-unit reward acts each round directed at the more consistent contributors.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 10 players against the usual tension: contributing 20 coins to a shared pot benefits the group (contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally), but an individual who withholds (contributes 0) keeps 20 private coins and can earn more that round unless others punish them. Punishment is costly to the punisher (4 coins per punishment unit) and harsher on the target (8 coins lost per unit), so punishment can enforce cooperation but at a private cost. Uncertainties include who will defect, who will pay to punish, and how strong punishment will be; messages can coordinate expectations.

Typical dynamics I predict here:
- Early rounds: many players start cooperative; a small number test the group by free-riding. Some players (those who value fairness or have stronger enforcement tendencies) will punish defectors to deter future free-riding.
- Middle rounds: if punishments are consistent, cooperation stabilizes; defectors switch to contributing. Punishment remains sparse and targeted.
- Endgame (final round): with no future, some players are more likely to defect. Punishment may decline (less incentive to enforce future behavior), or some players may still punish out of fairness/retaliation, producing last-round costs.

Below is a round-by-round transcript (round explanations, sparse chat, contributions, punishment actions, and the summary info each round shows to players). The avatar order for contribution lists is: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone privately chooses to keep their 20 coins (contribute 0) or put all 20 into the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Because the per-capita return (1.9/10 = 0.19) is less than 1, the individual incentive is to free-ride (keep 20); however group payoff is maximized if many contribute. In practice we should expect some early experimentation and modest cooperation from conditional cooperators, followed by erosion as contributors see they earn less than free-riders. Messages can temporarily coordinate higher contributions, producing spikes, but those spikes are often followed by exploitation and decline. Toward the end of the 15 rounds players anticipate a final-round free-ride (endgame effect), so contributions commonly fall in later rounds. reflection:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone privately chooses to keep their 20 coins (contribute 0) or put all 20 into the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Because the per-capita return (1.9/10 = 0.19) is less than 1, the individual incentive is to free-ride (keep 20); however group payoff is maximized if many contribute. In practice we should expect some early experimentation and modest cooperation from conditional cooperators, followed by erosion as contributors see they earn less than free-riders. Messages can temporarily coordinate higher contributions, producing spikes, but those spikes are often followed by exploitation and decline. Toward the end of the 15 rounds players anticipate a final-round free-ride (endgame effect), so contributions commonly fall in later rounds. Chat will be sparse and usually either pledge, complain, or try to coordinate.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 players who each start each round with 20 coins. Each decides privately how much (0–20) to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. After seeing contributions and their own resulting payoff, players can pay to punish or reward others (cost 4 coins per unit sent; punish unit subtracts 7 coins from the target, reward unit adds 5 coins to the target). Players do not know how many rounds the game will have and cannot identify who targeted them.

Likely unfolding and incentives:
- The marginal per-capita return of a contributed coin is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247, below 1, so the one-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, mutual cooperation raises everyone's payoffs, so socially minded and reciprocity-oriented players tend to contribute.
- Because players can punish or reward after seeing contributions, we expect modest use of both to reinforce cooperation and deter persistent low contributors. In real PGGs, punishments and rewards are used sparingly — enough to change behavior but not so often that they dominate payoffs.
- Early rounds typically show heterogeneous contributions as players test each other. If punishments hit obvious low contributors, those players often raise contributions in subsequent rounds. Frequent high contributors may receive occasional small rewards.
- Uncertainty about the number of rounds discourages end-game free-riding; players continue to condition behavior on observed contributions. Overall we expect a moderate-to-high average contribution level that gradually stabilizes, with occasional targeted punishments and occasional small rewards to recognize high contributors.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This 15-round public goods game pits individual incentives (keep 20 privately) against group benefit (contribute 20; pot multiplied by 1.9 and split). Contributing is socially efficient but personally costly each round because a non-contributor gets the same share of the public pot plus keeps their 20. Expect initial uncertainty and some voluntary contributions from pro-social/lead personalities (postgrads, people who signal). Other players (older, lower-education, or risk-averse personas) are more likely to free-ride. Because players see round-by-round payoffs, not binding commitments, we expect conditional cooperation: some players will respond to perceived free-riding by cutting contributions; a few will try to rally the group via short messages. Overall pattern: moderate cooperation early, decline as free-riding is rewarded, occasional brief recoveries when leaders appeal to others, and near-final-round free-riding (end-game) especially by previously exploitative players.

game_explanation: This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with costly punishment and rewarding. Each coin you keep yields 1 privately; each coin contributed produces 3.7 coins to the common pot which are split equally among the 15 players, so each contributed coin returns only about 0.247 to each individual. That creates a private incentive to free-ride (keep money) while the group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes everything. Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding and do not know how many rounds there will be, they face uncertainty about whether cooperation will pay off. The presence of costly punishment (source pays 4 per unit to remove 7 from a target) and costly reward (source pays 4 per unit to add 5 to a target) provides a mechanism to discipline persistent free-riders or to reinforce cooperators; in practice both are used sparingly due to their cost. Early rounds often show a mix of moderate cooperators and a few low contributors; if cooperators reward each other or punish clear free-riders, cooperation tends to rise and stabilize. Individual differences (some players predisposed to cooperate more, others to free-ride) and the anonymity of sanctions will create modest, gradual adjustments rather than dramatic swings. Below I simulate nine rounds consistent with these incentives and typical sparse sanctioning behavior.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual incentives (keep 20 privately) against collective benefits (contribute 20 to a pot that yields 1.9× and is shared equally). Individually, defecting (contributing 0) is dominant because each contributed 20 yields only 3.8 benefit to oneself (less than the 20 cost). However, groups can increase total payoffs if many contribute. Punishment (costly: 4 per punishment unit to sender, 8 deducted per unit from target) lets cooperators sanction defectors and can sustain cooperation, but it also reduces the punishers' payoffs and may provoke retaliation. Because the game has a known finite horizon (15 rounds), expect:

- Early rounds: some experimentation. A moderate level of cooperation is possible if players try a cooperative norm; defectors are likely to be punished occasionally.
- Middle rounds: if punishment successfully disciplines defectors, cooperation can be sustained at high levels, with occasional defections and targeted punishments.
- Late rounds (final 2–3 rounds): cooperation typically unravels (endgame effect). Players reduce punishment because it’s costly and less effective late; hence defection rises and punishment declines.

Given the player personas, expect several cooperative-leaning players (many with postgraduate or college backgrounds) to try to sustain cooperation and issue punishment early on, while some players (SLOTH, DOG, CHICK; varied backgrounds) may test or revert to defecting. Chat will be sparse and focused on norms or calling out defectors. Punishment will be limited and targeted—enough to signal displeasure but not to explode into large cycles—then taper off toward the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with an added costly reward stage. In each round everyone has 20 coins, privately chooses how much to contribute to a pot (0–20), the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly, and then players may spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives the recipient 5 coins). Because contributions are simultaneous and private, players face uncertainty about others' choices each round. However, over repeated rounds players can use rewarding to reinforce cooperative behavior: conditional cooperators tend to increase contributions if they see others contributing and getting rewarded, while free-riders who receive no rewards may be left out of reciprocal benefits and drift toward lower contributions.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Round 1 often shows moderate, heterogeneous contributions as players test each other (many contribute some fraction of their endowment; a few free-ride).
- Sparse rewards will appear to incentivize high contributors. Players who receive rewards (or rewarders themselves) tend to maintain or raise contributions in subsequent rounds.
- Because rewards are costly to the giver, they are used sparingly and mostly directed toward visibly high contributors (even though identities of rewarders are hidden in the summary). Over rounds a small core of reciprocating cooperators can emerge and sustain higher contributions among themselves, while persistent low contributors receive few or no rewards and remain low.
- Uncertainty about the total number of rounds tends to stabilize behavior (no end-game collapse) unless players infer a short horizon; because players do not know the total number of rounds here, we should expect relatively stable patterns by the middle rounds: a core of higher contributors who reward each other and a tail of lower contributors who are rarely rewarded.
