predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start with no history. Many choose a moderate contribution to test reciprocity; a few contribute little. A small number of players who tend toward prosocial preferences (e.g., older/postgrad profiles) may reward high contributors to signal valuing cooperation. Rewards will be sparse and small.

Players who received rewards (or rewarded others) are slightly more likely to keep contributing or increase their contribution. Some marginal contributors raise their offers to test reciprocity. Persistent low contributors may remain low or dip slightly after not being rewarded.

Reciprocity patterns strengthen: a small cooperative core (players who consistently contribute more) begins to exchange rewards occasionally. Players who see this pattern and are conditional cooperators increase contributions; free-riders remain largely unrewarded.

Some players who haven't been rewarded for contributing cut back slightly; the cooperative core still contributes but may be more selective with rewards because rewards are costly. Rewarding remains rare and targeted at consistent high contributors.

Players who benefited from reciprocal ties sustain or slightly raise contributions to maintain access to mutual rewards; isolated low contributors remain unrewarded and continue low contributions. Rewarders continue to be cautious because each unit is costly.

A relatively stable pattern has emerged: a small set of players (high contributors) keeps contributing at higher levels and occasionally reward each other; the rest hover at mid-to-low contributions. Because rewards are costly, they remain infrequent and mostly mutual among the core.

Players have a clearer sense of who belongs to the cooperative core. Rewarding continues but is concentrated among the highest contributors as a way to maintain those relationships; low contributors receive little support and stay low.

With continued uncertainty about the horizon, players avoid dramatic defection. The cooperative core is maintained through occasional reciprocal rewards; marginal players remain unrewarded and contributions stay low for them.

By this late stage players who value long-run cooperation continue to support each other, sometimes increasing rewards slightly to reinforce ties; low contributors have little incentive to change without receiving rewards. Rewarding remains selective and limited in scale.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with private endowments of 20 coins each round. The social return to contributions (multiply by 3.7 and split across 15 players) is greater than the private return per coin (3.7/15 ≈ 0.247), so contributing benefits the group but not the individual acting alone. Typical dynamics: many players start with some mix of cooperation and free-riding. Without strong, reliably targeted sanctions or incentives, contributions tend to drift down over rounds because free-riding is individually rational. Punishment and reward are available but costly: each punishment unit costs the source 4 coins and subtracts 7 from the target; each reward unit costs 4 and gives the target +5. Because interaction is anonymous and players can’t see who punished/rewarded them, incentives for strategic retaliation are limited; punishments more often serve as group enforcement by conditional cooperators and rewards as positive reinforcement for contributors. Real participants punish and reward sparingly because of the costliness.

Uncertainties include: how many players are conditional cooperators (who respond to others' behavior), how many are persistent free riders, and how willing people are to pay to sanction. Early rounds often show mixed contributions; a few players may spend resources on light punishments to deter persistent zero contributors, and occasional rewards encourage high contributors. Over nine rounds we expect an overall gradual decline in average contribution, with occasional small punishments and rewards aimed at the extremes (very low or very high contributors). Below I simulate round-by-round contributions and sparse sanctioning/rewarding, with brief explanations for each round.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players have no history yet. Some (educated, younger, or normative types) are inclined to give moderately to strongly; others prefer to free-ride given the individual incentive. Punishments and rewards are rare but some players use them to signal norms — mostly small 1-unit actions because bigger moves are costly.

Players see contributions from round 1 and modest sanctioning targeted at a few low contributors. Conditional cooperators raise their contributions slightly in response; persistent low contributors increase a little when punished. Rewards encourage high contributors to stay high.

Some stabilization: those rewarded maintain high contributions; a few low contributors remain low and attract occasional single-unit punishments. Cooperation slightly drifts down among some players who realize others aren’t fully cooperating.

A few players drop further toward zero. Group enforcers continue to apply light punishments to clear defectors; rewards persist for consistent contributors. Overall trend: modest decline, with targeted sanctions keeping some cooperators from abandoning the public good entirely.

Persistent zeros (or near-zeros) attract occasional punishment from cooperators who are willing to pay to reduce exploitation. Some contributors slightly reduce their amounts as the group average falls; those who keep contributing are sometimes rewarded.

As rounds accumulate without a binding enforcement mechanism, average contributions continue to slide. Punishment remains sporadic and small (1-unit actions) because of the cost to punishers; a few cooperative acts of rewarding keep some contributors engaged.

The pattern of modest cooperation from a core subset (educated, normative, or reputation-concerned players) and persistent defection from others continues. Rewards are used sparingly to encourage the consistent contributors; punishments target the most persistent zeros.

With repeated low contributions from several players, cooperators reduce their contributions further to limit losses; enforcement continues but is limited to single-unit sanctions or rewards from a few players.

In the final simulated round here, contributions are lower than at the start. A small core of contributors persists, receiving occasional rewards; persistent zeros continue to be lightly punished by multiple players who still prefer to spend a little to reduce exploitation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 players. In each round everyone receives 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among all 15 players, so a single coin contributed returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.25 coins to its contributor (less than 1). Individually, the dominant short-term incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0), but group welfare is maximized when many players contribute. The reward stage lets players spend 4 coins to send a +5 payoff unit to someone else; rewarding is costly to the giver but increases total group payoff by +1 per reward unit, so rewards can be used to encourage cooperators. Players cannot identify who gave them reward units (they only see totals given and received), and they do not know how many rounds remain — both facts create uncertainty about reputation signaling and end-game behavior.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds: heterogeneous contributions as players test others — some modest contributors, some free riders.
- Rewarding is usually sparse and targeted at visible cooperators. Because rewards are visible only in totals (not linkable in the players’ view), rewarders may try to reward cooperators to build norms, but they do so cautiously (small numbers of reward units).
- Over rounds: conditional cooperators tend to increase contributions if they see peers contributing and being rewarded; persistent free riders often remain low and receive few/no rewards. Because the multiplier per player is well below 1, perfect cooperation is fragile; rewards can sustain higher-than-myopic contributions but are used sparingly.
- Uncertainty about the number of rounds makes end-game defection less sharp; behaviour tends to be smoother rather than sharply collapsing at the end.

Players who received rewards or saw consistent cooperation maintain or raise contributions; marginal players (SLOTH, PARROT) creep upward slightly but remain below the core. Some players use rewards to encourage marginal cooperators occasionally.

By now a stable pattern: a core of high contributors (CHICK, COW, DOG, OWL, PINGUIN, RABBIT) keep contributing at high levels and receive most rewards; persistent low contributors (PARROT, CROCODILE) get little attention. Rewarding remains scarce and focused.
Stability continues; small adjustments occur but the cooperative core remains intact. Rewarders stick to modest units, mostly reinforcing those who consistently contribute a lot.

Players still do not know this is the last round. That uncertainty tends to blunt extreme end-game defection; contributions stay similar to previous rounds. Rewards continue to be small and intended to maintain cooperation among the core contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This repeated public goods game (15 rounds) sets up a tension between the private incentive to keep your 20 coins (by contributing 0) and the group benefit of everyone contributing (by contributing 20). The pot multiplier (1.9) makes full-group cooperation socially best (total group payoff higher), but any individual who withholds while others contribute gets a strictly higher round payoff than a cooperator. Because choices are simultaneous and visible only after the round, we should expect a mix of behaviors:
- A subset of players (conditional cooperators / prosocial types) will try contributing early to encourage cooperation (expect participants like MOOSE, OWL, DUCK, CHICK, PARROT to be more willing).
- Some players will free-ride early and persist if they get higher payoffs (players like GORILLA, DOG, RABBIT, and maybe SLOTH are more likely to contribute 0).
- Communication can modestly boost cooperation if credible (promises or calls to cooperate), but because the game length is known, contributions typically drift downward over time (end-game defection).
- Expect a few stable cooperators who keep contributing even as others defect, occasional short-lived rebounds after cooperative messages, and a pronounced decline toward the final rounds. Chat messages will be sparse and strategic (promises, complaints, short encouragements).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 players, 20 coins each round. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among all players, so every coin contributed returns about 0.247 coins to each player (3.7/15) and about 3.7 coins total to the group. Individually, giving a coin costs 1 but only returns ~0.247 to the giver personally, so the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (keep your endowment). However, because the multiplier is >1, group welfare increases with contributions. The reward stage lets players invest 4 coins to give 5 coins to one peer; rewards are costly to the sender and beneficial to the recipient, so they can be used to selectively reinforce cooperation (or reward past generosity). Rewards are typically used sparingly in real games because they are costly, and many players are conditional cooperators: they increase contributions when the group appears cooperative and reduce them when they see free-riding. Some players are persistent free-riders who exploit cooperators, which tends to pull average contributions down unless rewards successfully sustain cooperation.

Given the listed personas: some players (e.g., DUCK, PARROT, PINGUIN, MONKEY, DUCK, RABBIT) are likely to start relatively cooperative and to reward cooperators occasionally; others (e.g., GORILLA, OWL, COW) are more likely to free-ride or contribute little. Over rounds we expect moderate initial contributions, a few persistent low contributors, occasional targeted rewards to high contributors, and a dynamic where conditional cooperators modestly adjust their contributions in response to observed behavior. Because players do not know the horizon, there is little "end-game" collapse, but there will be fluctuation driven by the presence of stable free-riders and sparse rewarding.

Given the listed personas: some players (e.g., DUCK, PARROT, PINGUIN, MONKEY, DUCK, RABBIT) are likely to start relatively cooperative and to reward cooperators occasionally; others (e.g., GORILLA, OWL, COW) are more likely to free-ride or contribute little. Over rounds we expect moderate initial contributions, a few persistent low contributors, occasional targeted rewards to high contributors, and a dynamic where conditional cooperators modestly adjust their contributions in response to observed behavior. Because players do not know the horizon, there is little "end-game" collapse, but there will be fluctuation driven by the presence of stable free-riders and sparse rewarding.

game_explanation:
In this 15-round public goods game each player repeatedly faces the same tension: contributing 20 coins helps the group (because the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and shared) but is costly for the individual in the short run. With 10 players the marginal per-player return on someone’s 20-coin contribution is 0.19 * 20 = 3.8 coins — less than the 20 coins it costs — so the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, if many players contribute, everyone’s payoff can be higher than if nobody contributes, so conditional cooperation and social preferences matter.

Typical unfolding: some players will try cooperation early (pledges, small clusters of contributors). Observing peers’ payoffs (contributors earn less than free-riders), conditional cooperators often reduce contributions over time; free-riding tends to increase. Sparse chat can temporarily coordinate recoveries (pleas, promises, shaming), producing short-lived rebounds. Near the end, a mix of “last-round defection” and a few final cooperative gestures is common. Expect early rounds to show mixed cooperation, a downward drift to mostly 0 contributions in the middle rounds, occasional short rebounds when players try to restore cooperation, and likely many zero contributions in final rounds as strategic incentives dominate.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins, chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally. After seeing contributions, players may spend private coins to punish or reward others: a punishment unit costs the actor 4 coins and removes 7 coins from the target; a reward unit costs the actor 4 coins and gives the target +5 coins. Players do not see who acted on them.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: contributions will be mixed — some high cooperators and some lower contributors. Because contributions are private before choice, players rely on priors (personas) and observed history.
- Mid-game: players who value cooperation (many here are college-educated) will begin to use the punishment/reward stage sparingly to enforce norms: small punishments directed at persistent low contributors and small rewards for reliable high contributors.
- Punishment and reward are typically limited (1–2 units) because costs are high; they are used mostly as signals to steer behavior rather than to extract or give large transfers.
- Over rounds we expect average contributions to drift upward modestly as punishments and rewards shape behavior, with occasional lapses (a low contribution) that prompt a couple of targeted punishments.
- Because players cannot identify who acted on them, retaliation cycles are less likely; instead we see targeted, sparse corrective actions.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round simultaneous public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone privately chooses either to contribute their entire 20 coins to a shared pot or to keep the 20. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and shared equally across all 10 players, so each coin contributed returns 0.19 to each player (marginal per-capita return = 0.19). Because 0.19 < 1, each individual is better off keeping their 20 regardless of the group — there is a private incentive to free-ride. However, if many players contribute, the group total is higher and everyone (including free-riders) benefits.

Typical dynamics combine an early period of conditional cooperation (some players contribute hoping others will) with gradual decline as free-riding pays off and people adjust expectations. Communication can sustain cooperation somewhat if trusting players coordinate, but the finite 15-round horizon increases the chance of end-game unraveling (late-round defection). Uncertainties include which players are conditional cooperators versus selfish free-riders, and whether early signals and brief social pressure will deter free-riding.

Given the personas, I predict several moderately committed cooperators (DUCK, PARROT, MOOSE) who will contribute repeatedly; some players will try cooperating early but then defect when they see free-riding (SLOTH, GORILLA, DOG); a subset will free-ride early or opportunistically (CHICKEN, RABBIT, CHICK). Chat will be sparse: short commitments, thanks, occasional complaints, and eventual resignation. Overall we expect initial positive contributions that decay toward near-zero before the game ends.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player decides whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (contribute = 20) or keep it (contribute = 0). Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Because the multiplier (1.9) is less than the group size (10), individual incentives favor free-riding (keeping the 20) while group payoff is maximized by everyone contributing. Punishment is available after seeing contributions: each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and reduces the punished player's earnings by 8 coins. Players see who punished them and summary statistics each round; the game length (15 rounds) is common knowledge, so cooperation is likely to be higher early on and decay toward the end (endgame effect). Typical dynamics:

- Early rounds: many players will try to cooperate; a few may free-ride, and cooperators who care about fairness will punish defectors sparingly to enforce cooperation.
- Middle rounds: cooperation can stabilize if punishments are credible, but punishers will avoid excessive spending. Defectors punished earlier may switch to contributing.
- Late rounds: as the final round approaches, incentives to punish fall (no future to influence), and some players will begin to defect more often (endgame collapse). Punishment frequency tends to decline toward the end.

Given the provided personas, expect some players to be consistent cooperators (e.g., GORILLA, DUCK, CHICK, OWL, CHICKEN, RABBIT), a few sporadic defectors/testing players (SLOTH, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE), and punishments targeted mainly at repeated or surprising defections. Chat is sparse and purposeful (requests to cooperate, short accusations or confirmations).

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game pits 15 players against the temptation to free-ride. Each round everyone privately chooses how many of 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 to each player. That means individual incentives favor contributing 0, while group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes 20. Because players see only the round summaries (others' contributions, and reward amounts given/received) after decisions, behavior tends to start somewhat mixed and — unless strong reciprocity is established — drift toward lower contributions as selfish payoffs for free-riding become salient.

The reward stage lets players spend 4 coins per reward unit to give a 5-coin benefit to another player. Because giving rewards is costly, rewards are typically sparse and used selectively to encourage consistent contributors or reciprocate previous generosity. Players can't identify who rewarded them, so strategic signaling is noisy. Over multiple rounds we expect:
- A small core of consistently cooperative players (high contributors) who may continue contributing despite low immediate payoff.
- A larger group of conditional cooperators who moderate their contributions based on observed peer behavior and reward patterns.
- Many players drifting toward low or zero contributions because free-riding gives higher private payoffs.
- Rewards remain rare and targeted (1 unit) towards persistently high contributors; overall reward activity decreases as contributions fall.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with anonymous punishment and reward. Each round players privately decide how much of 20 coins to contribute; contributed coins are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across all 15 players. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish or reward others (each unit of punishment costs 4 to the sender and removes 7 from the target; each reward unit costs 4 and gives the target +5). Players see round summaries (who contributed how much and the aggregate punish/reward amounts they and others used/received) but not who specifically targeted whom. They also do not know how many rounds will be played.

Likely dynamics:
- Many players will behave as conditional cooperators: they tend to match group norms and respond to feedback. When others contribute moderately-high amounts, most will contribute fairly high too. When they see free-riding, some will punish to enforce cooperation.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly because they are costly. Typical use is 0–2 units per actor per round, often targeted at clear low contributors (punishment) or at consistently high contributors (reward).
- Because contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split across 15 players, the marginal per-player benefit of contributing 1 coin is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.2467 coins — less than the private cost of 1 coin. So purely self-interested short-run incentive is to free-ride, but social preferences and sanctions can sustain much higher contributions.
- Uncertainty: players don't know how many rounds remain, which promotes cautious conditional cooperation. Early punishments can quickly change a low contributor's behavior. Over repeated rounds, contributions often settle into a moderate-to-high level with occasional deviations and occasional small sanctions or rewards.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game pits 15 anonymous players against the standard social dilemma: each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses how many coins (0–20) to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so the social return on each contributed coin (3.7/15 ≈ 0.247) is less than the private cost (1 coin). That creates a private incentive to free-ride while a collective incentive favors higher contributions. After contributions are revealed, players may spend costly reward units (4 coins each) to give others +5 coins per unit. Rewards can promote cooperation by rewarding high contributors, but they are costly and typically used sparingly.

Key uncertainties: players do not know the total number of rounds, so there is no known endgame. Players see peers' contributions and how much peers spent on rewards and how much they received overall, but they cannot see who rewarded whom. Given the provided personas and typical lab patterns, I expect: an initial moderately cooperative phase (many contribute around mid-range), some persistent low contributors (who free-ride), sparse positive rewards targeted at the consistent high contributors, and a gradual drift downward as some conditional cooperators reduce contributions when they feel exploited. Rewards will be used selectively and usually in single-unit amounts to acknowledge or reciprocate cooperators rather than to punish defectors (since only positive transfers are allowed).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits private incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a social multiplier (contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split among all players). The group-efficient outcome is high contributions; the individually dominant move (if you assume others contribute) is to contribute less or zero. Because contributions are simultaneous and players do not know the total number of rounds, expectations about others' behavior matter—and the punishment/reward stage introduces a way for players to enforce or encourage norms: punishment is costly to the sender (4 coins per unit) but inflicts a larger loss on the target (7 coins per unit), while rewards also cost 4 to send and give 5 to the recipient. In practice, we expect:
- Initial contributions to be moderate and somewhat heterogeneous (some quick cooperators, some free-riders).
- Punishments to be infrequent and targeted at clear low contributors; rewards to be used sparingly to reinforce cooperators.
- Over rounds, small upward drift in contributions as selective rewarding/punishing nudges behavior toward higher cooperation, but a few players may continue to lag.
- Because players can't see individual targets of punishment/reward, they only see totals (how much each person spent and how much they lost/received), which encourages reputation-building but leaves ambiguity about who targeted whom.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round binary public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone decides privately whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.9, and then split equally among all 10 players. That means a contributor's round payoff equals the group share (3.8 times the number of contributors), while a non-contributor gets that same share plus the 20 they kept. The individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) because, within a round, keeping your 20 always gives you 20 more than contributing. However, if enough players contribute, everyone can earn more than 20 on average.

Typical dynamics: some players will try cooperation early; free-riding often looks attractive because defectors earn more within rounds, so contributions tend to fall over repeated rounds unless players can coordinate and reciprocate. People who value fairness or who are conditional cooperators may keep contributing for a while, prompting occasional partial recoveries. Near the end (final few rounds), many players often stop contributing because the repeated-game incentive to build reputation disappears. Chat can help sustain cooperation if participants make credible commitments, but messages will be sparse. Expect an early moderate level of contributions, a decline as free-riding reinforces itself, a few intermittent attempts to restart cooperation, and low contributions by the end.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-player repeated public goods game lasting 17 rounds. Each round every player privately chooses how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. Because the per-coin private return (4.5/16 ≈ 0.281) is much less than the private cost (1), the dominant private strategy is to contribute 0, while the group optimum is full contribution. That creates a tension: cooperative players who contribute benefit the group but are vulnerable to free riders.

The reward stage lets players spend 4 coins to give 2 coins to another player per unit — an expensive way to reward cooperators or punish defectors. Because rewards are costly, we expect them to be used sparingly and mostly early on by those who care about promoting cooperation. Chat messages are also possible but typically few; players who are cooperative may try to encourage others or thank rewarders, while defectors often stay silent.

Given the player personas, we expect a mix: some consistently pro-social players (e.g., CHICKEN, OWL, COW, DUCK, DOG) start off contributing relatively high amounts; others (e.g., SLOTH, GORILLA, CROCODILE, SNAKE, PINGUIN) tend to contribute little. Early rounds will show moderate-to-high average contributions and occasional targeted rewards to high contributors. Over the middle rounds, without strong or widespread costly enforcement, contributions are likely to drift downward as free-riding becomes more attractive. Rewards taper off as they are costly, and by the final rounds many players will reduce contributions substantially — though a few may keep contributing out of principled cooperation or reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This public goods game will likely start with heterogeneous contributions as players test the group. Some players are inclined to cooperate (steady, mid-to-high contributions), others will test or free-ride (low contributions). Because contributions are private until revealed and there is a costly punishment/reward stage afterward, early rounds are often used to signal intentions and to establish norms. Punishment here is severe to the target (−15 per punishment unit) and relatively costly to the punisher (−4 per unit), so punishments should be used sparingly and typically only against persistent low contributors. Rewards are also costly to the giver and give only a small benefit to the receiver, so they will be rare and targeted to reinforce strong cooperators.

Given these dynamics and the 17-round horizon, I predict an initial phase of mixed contributions and a few targeted punishments aimed at persistent low contributors. Conditional cooperators will increase their contributions as they see others cooperating; persistent defectors will be punished occasionally. Midgame will show higher average contributions as enforcement and reciprocal rewards take effect. Toward the final round, some players will reduce contributions due to endgame effects, and punishment will decline since there's no future to enforce. Chat will be sparse and mostly instrumental: brief threats, requests, thanks, or explanations after punishments/rewards.

game_explanation:
This 17-round public goods game pits 16 players against a classic social dilemma: contributing to the public pot helps the group but is individually costly. Because each coin contributed yields only 4.5/16 = 0.28125 coins back to each contributor via the pot, the immediate personal incentive is to contribute 0 while hoping others contribute. However, the presence of a costly reward stage (each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins) creates a mechanism for positive reciprocity: players can reinforce cooperative behavior by rewarding high contributors. Over repeated rounds, we should expect:

- Early rounds: higher initial contributions by many players (socially optimistic or testing cooperativeness), with a few low contributors (free-riders). Players who contribute well are occasionally rewarded by others who value cooperation.
- Middle rounds: dynamics respond to observed patterns. Rewarding can sustain cooperation among a core of contributors. Free-riders who persist may be ignored in rewards and possibly ostracized verbally. Overall average contributions may drift down unless rewards are consistently targeted and valued.
- Late rounds: some decline in cooperation is typical (endgame effects), because future reciprocity matters less toward the last rounds. However, if rewarding has successfully built norms, a subset of players may maintain high contributions through the end.
- Chat is usually sparse and functional: brief thanks, calls for fairness, or responses to perceived unfairness. Rewarding is typically sparse and targeted, not widespread.

I will simulate the full transcript round-by-round. Each round begins with an explanation of expected incentives for that round, followed by any short chat, the contribution vector in the exact avatar order, optional chat, the reward actions (only non-zero edges shown), the summary stage, and occasional follow-up chat.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG </PLAYERS>

This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. The private marginal return on a single coin is 4.5/16 = 0.28125, so selfish incentives push toward contributing 0. However, the group is better off if many contribute. After contributions are revealed, players can punish (cost 4 per unit to sender, -15 per unit to target) or reward (cost 4 per unit to sender, +2 per unit to target). Both punishment and reward are costly to the sender and overall inefficient for total welfare, but players may still use them to enforce norms or signal appreciation. Messaging is allowed and will be used rarely to encourage cooperation or register complaints.

Likely unfolding: early rounds show modest-to-moderate contributions as some players try to establish cooperation. A few consistent low contributors (or zeros) provoke occasional targeted punishments early on; because punishment is costly, it will be used sparingly and mainly against persistent free-riders. Rewards will appear occasionally to acknowledge steady contributors. Over the middle rounds contributions will hover around moderate levels with fluctuations as punishments deter some free-riding, but an end-game effect will emerge: in the final rounds many players will reduce contributions toward zero, and punishments become less frequent because their cost is no longer worthwhile (and because punishment can't change future rounds). Chats are sparse and purposeful (requests to cooperate, short complaints, a few thank-yous).

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round, players decide privately how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a public pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so contributions benefit the group but are individually costly; pure monetary incentives favor free-riding. After contributions are revealed, players can pay (4 coins per reward unit) to give others bonus (2 coins per unit). Rewards are expensive to the giver and only partially return value to the recipient, so they are most likely to be used sparingly to recognize high contributors or to signal approval. Because the game has a known finite horizon (17 rounds), we expect some cooperation early and midgame from intrinsically cooperative players, with free-riding by others. Rewards can sustain cooperation if used selectively by pro-social players; withholding rewards typically functions as a mild punishment. In the final rounds, especially round 17, many players will reduce contributions because no future reciprocity can be bought. Chat is available but typically sparse; it will be used for brief appeals, promises, or praise, and occasionally to announce reward intentions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 17 rounds. Each round, players privately decide how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players may spend part of their round earnings to reward or punish others. Punishment is costly and harsh (costs 4 to the punisher per unit, removes 15 from the punished), while rewards are cheaper for the receiver (costs 4 to the giver per unit, gives 2 to the receiver). Players can send brief messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: without history, average contributions tend to be moderate. A few players (habitual free-riders) may contribute little, provoking sparse punishment from cooperators.
- Midgame: social norms can form. Punishment and occasional rewards will be used sparingly to enforce contributions; conditional cooperators increase contributions, and cooperators reward each other occasionally.
- Late game: "end-game" thinking leads many to reduce contributions. Punishment becomes less frequent because it is costly and has no future benefit; some players still punish repeat defectors out of fairness. Rewards remain occasional to reinforce cooperation but are used less.
- Overall: contributions will cluster around mid-to-high values for many cooperators (12–16) for most of the middle rounds, with a subset of players contributing less (0–8) persistently or intermittently. Punishments are rare and targeted; rewards are also rare and used to recognize high contributors.

This is a 17-round public goods game with costly punishment and rewards available after contributions are revealed. Each coin contributed returns less to the individual than it adds to the group (each coin yields 4.5 to the collective, but only 4.5/16 ≈ 0.28 back to any one player), so the dominant selfish choice is to contribute 0. However, many players start with social preferences or conditional cooperation: they give positive amounts hoping others do the same. Because punishments (very costly to targets: -15 per punishment unit) and rewards (smaller effect: +2 per reward unit) can be directed and are fully attributable, a small number of enforcement-minded players can sustain higher contributions by punishing low contributors early. Punishment is costly to the sender (4 per unit), so it's used sparingly and mainly against clear free-riders. Rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce high contributors.

We should expect:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions, some high cooperators and some low/free-riders. A few punishments aimed at obvious free-riders will appear to deter them.

- Endgame (last few rounds): backward induction and known finite horizon push contributions down; cooperation unravels and punishments/rewards taper off since senders anticipate the final rounds.
- Chats will be sparse, often short admonitions, promises to punish free-riders, or thanks when rewarded.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round repeated public goods game with costly reward choices. Because contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, group-level payoffs are maximized when most players contribute a lot, but individual incentives push toward free-riding (keeping your 20 and letting others contribute). Repetition and the ability to reward after seeing contributions create scope for conditional cooperation and social enforcement: players who consistently contribute may be rewarded by others (small costly transfers) to sustain cooperation; conversely, low contributors get no reward and thus lose a channel of reciprocity. Expect a modest initial level of contributions from many players (conditional cooperators), a small set of steady high contributors (socially motivated or reputation-conscious), and persistent low contributors (either strategic free-riders or disengaged players). Over rounds, absent strong, costly reward-based enforcement, average contributions typically drift down — especially toward the end when the "shadow of the future" fades. Chat will be sparse and mostly used to appeal for cooperation or to acknowledge behavior. Rewarding will be relatively rare and targeted to recognized high contributors; as the game moves toward the final rounds, rewards and contributions will both decline.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 16 players, fixed group membership, and 17 rounds. Each round everyone privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so cooperative contributions benefit the group but are individually costly; free-riding (contributing little or nothing) can increase an individual's private payoff if enough others contribute. After contributions are revealed, players can pay to reward others: each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the receiver 2 coins. Rewards are costly and thus tend to be used sparingly — typically directed at notable cooperators to sustain cooperation or at times to reciprocate past generosity.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often start with moderate-to-strong cooperation as players test each other and try to establish trust.
- Because free-riders can earn more privately, contributions commonly decline after some rounds unless social mechanisms (chat, rewards) encourage continued cooperation.
- Rewards, when used, are targeted and small (1–2 units) because of their high cost; they are usually reserved for those who consistently contribute.
- Chat messages can help coordinate and praise cooperation, but they are limited and strategically used.
- Toward the final rounds, standard game-theory reasoning predicts a decline in contributions (end-game effect). However, some players may maintain contributions out of fairness, reputation concerns within the experiment, or reciprocity if others also continue to give rewards.

Given the player personas, expect a mix: well-educated or older players (OWL, CHICKEN, MOOSE, CHICK, COW) often contribute more and reward cooperators; some younger/high-school players may contribute less (DOG, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG), and a few may free-ride strategically (CROCODILE, SNAKE). Reward usage will be sparse and mostly to acknowledge consistent contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 identifiable players. Each round every player privately chooses how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so each player's share equals 9/32 of the group total that round. After seeing contributions, players can costly-punish or costly-reward others: each punishment or reward unit costs the source 4 coins; punishments deduct 15 coins per unit from the target, rewards add 2 coins per unit to the target. Players see their own round summary (net payoff and their personal pun/reward activity), but not peers' personal summaries.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: modest-to-moderate contributions (some free riders). A few players punish clear under-contributors to signal norms; a few positive rewards reinforce high contributors. This tends to raise average contributions across early-to-mid rounds.
- Middle rounds: punishment and reward stabilize cooperation. Average contributions rise or plateau as enforcement and reciprocity take effect.
- Last rounds: with a known finite horizon, contributions typically erode (endgame effect). Punishment and reward drop off (it’s costly and less strategic late), so cooperation declines toward the end. A handful of persistent punishers may keep targeting repeat low contributors, but overall enforcement weakens.

I will simulate this plausible trajectory over 17 rounds. Chat is sparse and focused: calls for cooperation, short accusations, or brief praise. Punishment/reward actions are kept sparse and modest, reflecting real experimental behavior.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 16-player public goods game over 17 rounds. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split evenly, then players may spend costly reward units to give small benefits to specific others. Key dynamics and uncertainties to expect:

- Incentives: The social-welfare maximizing move is to contribute everything (each contributed coin returns 4.5/16 = 0.28125 coins to each player, so group return per coin is 4.5, but each individual keeps only 0.28125 — private incentive is to free-ride). Thus, unilateral defection pays off in the short run, while mutual cooperation benefits the group.
- Typical unfolding: Many groups start with moderately high contributions (trust/exploration), then contributions tend to decline as free-riding appears and players respond. Because rewards are costly to the giver, they'll be used sparingly — usually to reinforce cooperators or reciprocate helpful behavior rather than to punish defectors aggressively.
- Role of rewards/messages: Targeted positive rewards can sustain cooperation if cooperators reciprocate or if rewarders are consistent. Chat is usually sparse — a few commitments, thanks, or complaints can shape expectations, but it rarely fully prevents decline.
- Endgame effects: Knowing the game has a finite horizon (17 rounds) typically weakens cooperation toward the end as strategic incentives to free-ride increase; some players may try to extract payoff in final rounds, while a few may stick to principled contribution or attempt last-minute reciprocity.

Given the 16 distinct personas, we expect a mix: a small core of reliably high contributors (e.g., PARROT, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, MOOSE, MONKEY, COW) who start high but taper off, a cluster of moderate contributors (DUCK, DOG, SNAKE, PINGUIN, FROG), and a few habitual low contributors/free riders (SLOTH, GORILLA, CROCODILE, CHICKEN). Rewards will be occasional and directed to those who contribute more; chat will be short but may include calls to cooperate and a few thank-yous or complaints.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game for 17 rounds with 16 players. Each round players decide simultaneously how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players may spend costly punishment or reward tokens: each punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and removes 15 coins from the target; each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the target +2 coins. Players see only their own round summary (their net payoff, how much they spent on punishment/reward, how much they lost to punishment, and how much they received in rewards) but can identify who punished/rewarded them.

Likely dynamics: players often begin with moderate contributions and sometimes reward high contributors or punish clear free-riders. Because punishment is very costly to targets (−15 per unit), senders tend to use punishment sparingly (usually 0 or 1 units). Rewards are also used sparingly. If some players contribute very little repeatedly, a few others may punish them consistently; punished players often increase contributions thereafter. Over repeated rounds many groups converge to a stable pattern (moderate to high contributions maintained by occasional sanctioning). Given the large multiplier (4.5), group returns to cooperative contributions are attractive; with only sparse punishment/reward, expect contributions to be similar across rounds with a few repeated targeted sanctions (especially against persistently low contributors).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 17 rounds. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, then players may punish or reward specific others. Typical dynamics: early rounds feature tentative or moderate cooperation as players test each other; conditional cooperators increase contributions if others contribute, while free riders prompt selective punishment. Punishment is costly to the sender and very costly to the recipient, so it is used sparingly — often by a few players to enforce cooperation. Rewards are cheaper for targets but still cost the sender and are used occasionally to reinforce high contributors. Over time there is often a gradual decline in cooperation as the end approaches (an “endgame” effect); if punishments are applied early and consistently, mid-game cooperation can be sustained, but many players reduce contributions in the final rounds and punish less because the cost may not be worth it so late.

Expectations in this group given the mix of personas: a few players (those more engaged or with postgraduate background) will try to sustain cooperation and occasionally punish visible low contributors; some players (names like SLOTH and GORILLA) will start lower and adjust upward after being sanctioned; many will be conditional cooperators who match the group average. Chat will be sparse and focused (greetings, appeals to cooperate, short complaints after being sanctioned, brief thanks after rewards). Punishments and rewards will be rare and targeted.

This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round players privately decide how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are revealed, multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, then players can spend some of their round earnings to give costly positive rewards to other players (1 reward unit costs 4 coins to the giver and gives 2 coins to the receiver). Messages may be posted at any time.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players attempt to lead cooperation by contributing highly; others test by free-riding or contributing little. Because the group return per coin (4.5/16 = 0.28125 per player per contributed coin) is less than 1, selfish incentives push toward lower contributions, but social preferences and rewards can sustain cooperation among a core subset.
- Rewards are costly for the giver but signal appreciation and encourage reciprocation. Expect sparse, targeted rewards: cooperators reward other cooperators; free-riders receive little or no reward.
- Over rounds: contributions typically decline from initial optimism as free-riding is observed. Some players (leaders) keep contributing relatively high and get rewarded by a small number of reciprocating players. Mid-game may show small rebounds when rewards are used intentionally; toward the final rounds contributions often decline again (end-game effect).
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful: leaders call for cooperation, cooperators thank each other, and some players call out free-riders. Rewards are used sparingly to amplify prosocial behavior.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players who each get 20 coins per round and decide how many to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so collective payoffs rise with contributions but each player's private incentive is to free-ride (contribute less) while still benefiting from others' contributions. Because the game repeats with feedback each round, we expect an interplay of cooperation, conditional cooperation, and occasional enforcement:

- Early rounds: many players (especially those with postgraduate backgrounds who favor cooperation) will contribute moderately to highly as a social norm and to signal cooperativeness; a few players with lower prior inclination may contribute little or nothing.
- Mid game: players who value cooperation will start to use punishment (costly to sender but strong deterrent) and selective rewards to discipline persistent low contributors and reinforce high contributors. Punishment will be used sparingly (1–2 units typical) and targeted at repeat low contributors; rewards will be rarer and small.
- Dynamics: contributions should rise for those who are punished or rewarded; some players will respond by increasing contributions, others may retaliate occasionally. Overall group contribution tends to stabilize at an intermediate-high level when punishment/reward enforcement is used.
- Endgame: as the final rounds approach (rounds 16–17), the incentive to enforce drops because future rounds are fewer; contributions often decline a bit and punishment usage falls off.

Uncertainties: individual variability (some persistently low contributors may ignore sanctions), occasional retaliatory punishments can appear, and chat can shift norms if strongly worded. But the dominant pattern will be initial cooperation, targeted enforcement in mid-game, and a slight drop-off at the end.

reflection:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players who each get 20 coins per round and decide how many to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so collective payoffs rise with contributions but each player's private incentive is to free-ride (contribute less) while still benefiting from others' contributions. Because the game repeats with feedback each round, we expect an interplay of cooperation, conditional cooperation, and occasional enforcement:

- Early rounds: many players (especially those with postgraduate backgrounds who favor cooperation) will contribute moderately to highly as a social norm and to signal cooperativeness; a few players with lower prior inclination may contribute little or nothing.
- Mid game: players who value cooperation will start to use punishment (costly to sender but strong deterrent) and selective rewards to discipline persistent low contributors and reinforce high contributors. Punishment will be used sparingly (1–2 units typical) and targeted at repeat low contributors; rewards will be rarer and small.
- Dynamics: contributions should rise for those who are punished or rewarded; some players will respond by increasing contributions, others may retaliate occasionally. Overall group contribution tends to stabilize at an intermediate-high level when punishment/reward enforcement is used.
- Endgame: as the final rounds approach (rounds 16–17), the incentive to enforce drops because future rounds are fewer; contributions often decline a bit and punishment usage falls off.

Uncertainties: individual variability (some persistently low contributors may ignore sanctions), occasional retaliatory punishments can appear, and chat can shift norms if strongly worded. But the dominant pattern will be initial cooperation, targeted enforcement in mid-game, and a slight drop-off at the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round players decide how many of 20 private coins to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so the group benefits from contributions but each individual's private return on their own contribution is less than the cost (private marginal return < 1), creating an incentive to free-ride. Because the game lasts a finite known number of rounds (17), end-game effects (declining cooperation near the last rounds) are likely. The punishment and reward stage changes incentives: players can punish free-riders (costly to sender, harmful to target) or reward cooperators (also costly, but benefits target modestly). In practice this usually produces:

- Early rounds: mixed contributions as players test each other. A few low contributors may get low-level punishments; some players may send supportive rewards to signal reciprocity.
- Mid game: if punishments are used credibly against low contributors, cooperation tends to rise and stabilize around moderate-to-high contributions. Messages (sparse) can help coordinate.
- Late rounds: some unraveling is typical as forward-looking incentives decline; some players reduce contributions and punishment becomes less effective or less frequent. A few targeted punishments and some rewards may still appear.

Throughout, chat is typically sparse and punishments/rewards are used sparingly. Players will usually punish clear or repeated low contributions or send occasional small rewards to encourage high contributors. Summaries after each round report each player's net payoff and their own spending/receipts in the punishment/reward stage (players do not see peers' summaries except for identification of who punished/rewarded them).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players for 17 rounds. Each round every player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players may pay to reward others (each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins). Players also may chat at any time. Because rewards are costly, they will be used sparingly — mostly to encourage consistent contributors or to reciprocate past generosity. Over 17 rounds we expect some cooperation (moderate contributions) early on from several players (those with college/postgrad backgrounds and reputations of being cooperative), but free riders (those who consistently contribute little) will benefit from others' cooperation. Without very strong, frequent rewards, contributions will tend to drift downward over the repeated rounds as players respond to the payoffs and the finite horizon. Rewards will be relatively sparse and targeted: a few 1-unit rewards each round aimed at high contributors; reciprocal pairs may emerge between a couple of players. Chat messages will be few and mostly short signals (promises, thanks, or short calls for coordination). The endgame (final rounds) will likely show the lowest contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round players choose how much (0–20) of their private 20 coins to contribute to a public pot; that pot is multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally. After contributions are revealed, players can spend costly reward units (each unit costs 4 to the sender and gives 2 to the recipient) to reward others. Players see who rewarded them, but not other players' full round summaries.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds are exploratory. Some players contribute moderately to test reciprocity; others start low (or very high) based on their priors/persona. Because rewards are costly, they will be used sparingly to encourage cooperators rather than to punish defectors.
- If a subset consistently contributes more and uses a few reward units to reinforce each other, cooperation can stabilize at a moderate level (contributions ~10–15). If cooperators fail to coordinate or free riders dominate, contributions decline.
- Messages (chat) will be infrequent and used mainly to signal intentions or to call out patterns ("I'll reward cooperators", "Why did you free-ride?").
- Near the end (rounds 16–17), standard end-game effects make unilateral cooperation less attractive; some players reduce contributions. However, because rewards still happen after contributions and players care about immediate payoffs, small targeted rewards may continue to maintain cooperation among committed players in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is amplified (×4.5) and split equally, so the private marginal return to contributing (4.5/16 ≈ 0.28 coins per coin contributed) is well below the private cost (1 coin). That creates a tension: free-riding (contributing 0) is individually dominant each round, but group payoffs are highest if many contribute. Because the game is finite (17 rounds), forward-looking theory predicts declining contributions toward the end, but the presence of costly punishment (each punishment unit costs the sender 4 and removes 15 from the target) and costly rewards (each reward unit costs the sender 4 and gives 2 to the target) allows players to try to enforce cooperation or reward cooperators.

Expectations for dynamics:
- Early rounds: some tentative cooperation as players test each other; occasional promises or public messages increase the chance of moderate contributions.
- Mid game: conditional cooperators may sustain higher contributions if they can punish persistent free-riders and reward reliable contributors. Punishment is costly to the sender but highly damaging to the target, so it will be used sparingly and typically directed at repeated low contributors.
- Late rounds: contributions typically decline as the shadow of the future shrinks; punishments and rewards drop off because the long-term enforcement value is limited. Occasional last-ditch reinforcements or a few targeted punishments may occur, but overall decay is expected.

Uncertainties:
- How many players are conditional cooperators vs. stubborn free-riders cannot be known initially; chats (voluntary statements) and observed punishments/rewards provide noisy signals.
- The extent to which players are willing to pay to punish (cost 4) despite limited future returns is uncertain; this affects whether mid-game cooperation can be sustained.

With those priors, below is a simulated transcript following the avatar order in <PLAYERS>.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 16 identifiable players against a standard social dilemma: each round everyone can keep private coins or contribute them to a shared pot that is multiplied and split. The incentive for any individual is to free-ride (contribute little) because the marginal private return from contributing is less than keeping the coin, while the group benefit is maximized when everyone contributes. Two institutional features change dynamics here: (1) costly punishment that strongly deducts from targets (one punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and removes 15 coins from the target) and (2) costly rewards that modestly benefit recipients (one reward unit costs 4 coins and gives the recipient +2 coins). Players may also send short messages.

Typical unfolding to expect:
- Early rounds: relatively high initial contributions (many players cooperate by social norms or optimism). Low-level messaging can set cooperative tone.
- Mid game: some players experiment with lowering contributions (free-riding). Other players respond sometimes with punishment or occasional reward. Because punishment is powerful (big hit to target), a small amount of targeted punishment can deter repeat free-riding and stabilize cooperation, but punishment is personally costly so it is used sparingly.
- Late game: contributions tend to drift downward as the end of the finite horizon approaches (backward induction). In the final round many players often defect because no future repercussions remain; moralizing punishments may still occur but are rarer as punishers pay costs without future leverage.
- Uncertainties: who will bear punishment costs, how many players coordinate to punish free-riders, and whether players prefer rewarding cooperators to sustain cooperation. Messaging can help reinforce norms, but with private decision-making, responses vary.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 17 rounds, fixed group of 16 players, private endowments (20 coins each per round), a public-pot multiplier of 4.5, and the option to send costly positive rewards to others after seeing contributions. Players know the horizon is finite (17 rounds). Typical dynamics to expect here:
- Early rounds: many players start by contributing moderately (conditional cooperators and prosocial types), while a minority free-ride or contribute little. Because contributions are private until revealed, players rely on priors and early signals (rewards and chat) to form expectations.
- Rewards: since rewards are visible (recipients see who rewarded them), rewards are most likely used sparingly to reinforce high contributors (rewarding cooperation) rather than to punish. Rewarding stabilizes cooperation for a while but is costly to the sender, so only some will reward consistently.
- Mid-game: If free-riding persists and rewards are insufficiently widespread, contributions typically decline as conditional cooperators reduce contributions. Strategic types may shift to low contributions if they perceive exploitation.
- End-game: With a known finite horizon, cooperation tends to unravel in the last rounds (players shift toward self-interested zero contributions). Rewards also decline toward the end because senders don't expect future reciprocity.
- Communication: Chats are usually sparse and used to signal intentions or gratitude; they can slightly sustain cooperation but rarely fully prevent decline in a fixed finite game.

I will simulate these tendencies with sparse chat, moderate early cooperation reinforced by a few rewards, gradual decline in contributions through the middle rounds, and near-zero contributions by the final rounds. Rewards will be used selectively to praise high contributors early and will taper off later.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players. Each round each player either keeps the full 20 coins (contributes 0) or leaves the full 20 in the common pot (contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all players. The key incentives:
- Collective incentive: if everyone contributes, each player gets a large return (everyone is better off).
- Individual incentive: for any fixed number of other contributors, withholding your 20 always gives you 20 extra that round (you keep the 20 and still share the pot). The personal return per coin contributed (11.5 / 19 ≈ 0.605) is less than 1, so contributing is privately costly but socially beneficial.
Uncertainties and dynamics:
- Players do not see others’ choices before choosing in a round, but after each round they see the round payoffs for every player. Because payoffs reveal whether a player contributed (contributors get only the pot share; defectors get pot share + 20), players can infer others' actions and adapt.
- In early rounds, social preferences and uncertainty about others often produce a moderate level of contribution. Once some players are seen to be freeriding and earning more, conditional cooperators tend to switch to withholding, producing a decline in contributions over time. Some players may remain steady cooperators (due to strong pro-social norms) and some remain steady defectors.
Given the mix of personas (a mix of likely cooperators, defectors, and conditional players) and the payoff structure, I expect relatively high cooperation in round 1, then a gradual decline as players observe exploitation and switch to withholding. Below I simulate one such plausible trajectory for rounds 1–10.

Given those priors, a fairly high fraction contribute in round 1. Payoff feedback for each player will make the identities of cooperators and defectors visible to others after the round.

Players now observe who earned 20 more last round (the defectors). Conditional cooperators respond: a few switch to withholding to capture the advantage; others continue cooperating. Cooperation falls but remains moderate.
The pattern of defectors earning consistently more encourages more withholding. Several conditional players defect this round. Some cooperators still remain, so the pot is smaller but nontrivial.

Cooperation continues to decline as more players choose the individually better action given the observed history. A few players who started cooperative still contribute, but the group pot shrinks further.
After several rounds showing a persistent advantage to withholding, many conditional cooperators give up contributing. A core group of steady cooperators remains, but total cooperation settles lower.
With the pattern established, most conditional players continue withholding. The same core of cooperators contributes; cooperation remains low and stable.

Same dynamics: conditional cooperators have settled into withholding; a consistent minority continues to contribute out of preference or principle.
With no new information and no coordination channel, the low-cooperation equilibrium persists; the same small group contributes and most continue to withhold.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players, each endowed with 20 coins per round and choosing either to contribute all 20 to the group pot or keep all 20 privately. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 11.5, and split equally across all players. Because the individual return from contributing (11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 per coin) is less than 1, each player faces a strong private incentive to free-ride (keep their 20) even though the group as a whole is better off if many contribute. Typical dynamics in this environment: an initial phase with some level of cooperation (driven by prosocial priors, uncertainty about others, or hope of mutual gain), followed by decay as players observe that defectors receive higher round payoffs and adjust. Because players see each other’s round payoffs, contribution decisions are effectively revealed (contributors earn the group share; defectors earn that share plus 20), enabling quick inference and conditional strategies (punishing defectors by not contributing). No communication is allowed, so adjustments are purely behavior-driven.

Given the player personas (a mix of postgraduate, college-educated, and some with less formal education), I predict a modest initial number of contributors (people with stronger prosocial priors start by contributing), then a steady decline as free-riding pays off and others switch to keeping their 20. Occasional attempts to reintroduce cooperation are possible but short-lived. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there's no predictable endgame spike in contributions.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start without knowledge of others’ choices and with heterogeneous priors. A handful of participants with stronger cooperative priors (postgraduates and those with college-level education who tend to favor public benefit) are likely to contribute on round 1, while many will defect to test the waters. Payoffs this round will clearly reveal who contributed (they will have the lower payoff equal to the group share), so players will immediately learn who cooperated and who free-rode.

Players observe round 1 payoffs: defectors made 20 coins more than contributors. That immediate advantage encourages switching to defection. Some conditional cooperators may still contribute if they see enough others who cooperated, but overall contributions should drop. Expect a small core of steady cooperators (those who weigh group benefit more heavily) to remain, while many switch to keeping the 20.

The advantage for defectors persists and is visible in the summary. Conditional cooperators who saw many defectors in round 2 are likely to switch to defection. A small committed minority (who strongly favor cooperation or expect reciprocity) may still contribute, but numbers should drop further.

After two rounds that highlighted the defectors’ advantage, many previously cooperative players abandon contributing. At this point the group is mostly defectors; only a couple of consistently cooperative players (perhaps strongly prosocial postgraduates) keep contributing. The trend is toward low cooperation unless coordinated punishment or strong reciprocity emerges.

Seeing persistent defection, a few players might attempt to restart cooperation (hoping to trigger some reciprocity), and a couple of previously steady cooperators might return. This can produce a small uptick, but it’s fragile: defectors still earn more per round, so the attempt is unlikely to be sustained.

The brief uptick in round 5 shows contributors receiving lower payoffs than defectors; most of those who briefly cooperated will revert to defection. It’s plausible only a very committed single contributor remains, producing a tiny group benefit and making cooperation economically unattractive for the rest.

Once players observe that almost everyone defects and that contributors fare worse, mutual defection becomes the stable outcome: nobody contributes. With no contributors, everyone simply keeps their 20 each round. Expect all-defect from here unless a coordinated change occurs.
With prior rounds showing persistent defection and no coordination mechanism, players continue to defect. All keep their endowment.
Same incentives remain. Without communication or coordinated moves, defection persists.
Final simulated round continues the established pattern of universal defection. With no mechanism for trust-building or enforcement, keeping the endowment dominates individual incentives.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public-goods game with 19 players. Each round each player either withdraws the full 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leaves the 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and divided equally, so each contributor increases the per-player return by a fixed amount (230/19 ≈ 12.105263 per contributor). Because an individual who withdraws keeps the private 20 coins in addition to the equal share, withdrawing (defecting) strictly dominates contributing in a one-shot sense: a defector always gets 20 more than a cooperator, given the same number of contributors.

However, in repeated play with observable round payoffs (players see their own net payoff and peers' corresponding payoffs each round), contributions can be inferred and conditional cooperation (or attempts to build cooperation) can appear. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, backward induction pressures and the temptation to free-ride typically push behavior toward defection over time. Still, some players may be motivated by norms, intrinsic cooperation, or attempts to restart cooperation after collapse. The likely trajectory is: some initial contributions from a subset of players, a visible payoff differential that reveals who contributed, subsequent decline in contributions as many respond to being exploited, occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation, and eventual convergence toward widespread defection by later rounds.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players are starting without knowledge of the total horizon. A subset of players with cooperative priors or who want to test the group's responsiveness will try contributing. Because payoffs will reveal who contributed (contributors earn ≈20 less than defectors), early cooperation is likely to be visible and then punished by most players choosing to withdraw in subsequent rounds. I expect a moderate set of initial contributors (some committed cooperators join in round 1).

Round 1's payoffs make contributions obvious (contributors ~96.84, defectors ~116.84). Most players see they earned 20 more by withdrawing; many respond by withdrawing in round 2. A small group of continued cooperators (who either persist in trying to build cooperation or value group payoffs) may remain, but the number of contributors should drop substantially.

Seeing the pattern from round 2, most players further reduce contributions. A couple of players who are persistent cooperators (or testing if cooperation can be sustained between a tiny set) may still contribute, but the group payoff advantage to defecting remains clear.

With only a tiny cooperative core left and contributors earning less than defectors, most of those remaining cooperators face strong incentives to stop. It's plausible that only one committed contributor persists (prefers contributing despite personal cost).

The sole contributor from round 4 is clearly disadvantaged relative to others; they are likely to stop in round 5. A period of near-universal withdrawal (defection) is the expected stable outcome once cooperation collapses.

After a round of universal defection, a few players may attempt to restart cooperation (testing whether others will reciprocate). Because players do not know when the game ends, occasional attempts can occur even late, though they are risky. Expect a small number of renewed contributors.

Round 6's contributors are again visibly earning less than defectors, so cooperation contracts again. A couple of persistent cooperators might still contribute in hopes of re-starting a stable cooperative cluster; overall contributors decline.
With cooperation repeatedly punished, we expect almost everyone to withdraw. A single committed cooperator might remain (perhaps testing whether they'll be alone or whether others will rejoin).
By now, the incentives and the visible history strongly favor withdrawal for nearly everyone. Expect universal withdrawal in round 9.

With repeated rounds of visible defection and unknown remaining horizon, players are likely to continue withdrawing. The game typically ends with persistent defection; a lone attempt at cooperation is unlikely at this late stage.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players. In each round each player either keeps the full 20 (contributes 0) or leaves it in the common pot (contributes 20). The common pot is multiplied by 11.5 and shared equally. After payoffs are revealed, players may spend resources to punish others: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and reduces the target's payoff by 3 coins. Punishment is observable in aggregate (who spent how much punishing, and how much was deducted from each player), but players cannot directly see who punished them.

Typical dynamics here: many players will initially try contributing to test others (hoping for the higher shared return), while a subset may free-ride. Because punishments are costly, they are used sparingly and mostly by cooperators to deter persistent defectors. Punishment tends to increase cooperation in subsequent rounds, but occasional short-term defection or “testing” behavior can reappear. Because players can't see the exact punisher-target link from each individual's perspective, retaliatory spirals are limited; most punishment is directed at clear and repeated free-riders. Over 10 anonymous rounds (players don’t know how many rounds remain), expect cooperation to rise after early punishment and stabilize at a high level, with occasional one-off defections and correspondingly sparse punishments.
