game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with binary contributions: each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them all (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by a generous 11.5 and shared equally, so group contribution is highly socially efficient — when many contribute, every player benefits by a large shared return. However, individual incentives favor withdrawing (free-riding) because a defector avoids the private cost while still getting a share of others' contributions. Punishment is available: players can spend resources to reduce others' payoffs (each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and reduces the target by 3 coins). Punishment is anonymous (players cannot see who punished them), which makes direct retaliation less likely but still supports norm enforcement.

Given these factors, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: uncertainty. Some participants test boundaries; a mix of contributors and defectors is common. Punishment may be used sparingly to signal that free-riding will be costly.
- Middle rounds: targeted punishment of clear defectors tends to increase cooperation as players learn that defection draws costly sanctions. Because punishment is anonymous, it enforces norms without obvious pairwise retaliation.
- Later rounds: cooperation often rises and stabilizes, with occasional lapses by opportunistic or stubborn defectors who receive small punishments. Players with education/age priors (e.g., college/postgrad profiles) are slightly more likely to cooperate early; others may follow once punishment clarifies expectations.

Uncertainties remain: players do not know how many rounds the game has, so there is no known final round to exploit; this tends to sustain cooperation when punishment is credible. However, the anonymity and discrete binary choice leave room for occasional experimentation (single-round defections) even late in the sequence.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players, where each player effectively decides either to keep the 20 coins for themselves (contribute 0) or to leave the 20 coins in the communal pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all players each round. Individually, contributing is costly: each contributor sacrifices 20 immediate coins but only brings an extra 11.5 * 20 / 19 ≈ 12.11 coins back to each player, so the personal return from contributing (≈12.11) is less than the private cost (20). That makes defection (keeping the 20) the dominant action in one-shot reasoning. However, because the multiplication makes the group richer when people contribute, conditional cooperation and social preferences can motivate some players to contribute, especially early on or if they expect others to reciprocate.

Uncertainties in this setting:
- Players do not know how many rounds will be played, so they may behave more cooperatively early on to test reciprocity or establish a reputation.
- Feedback is limited to net payoffs per player (everyone sees every player's round payoff), which reveals who likely contributed (contributors earn ≈12.11–121.05 less than defectors depending on totals) but does not show explicit messages or promises.
- Because defecting yields a higher private payoff whenever others contribute, we expect initial cooperation by some (either altruistic or conditional cooperators), followed by erosion of cooperation as the exploitation by defectors becomes visible in the payoff information.

Predicted overall unfolding: modest initial cooperation by several players, followed by steady decline in contributions as players observe that defecting yields higher individual payoffs. A few persistent cooperators (often older or more prosocial personas in the prior) might keep contributing for several rounds, but by the middle to late rounds most players will be defecting. Occasional attempts to re-start cooperation may appear but are likely to be short-lived because the private incentive to defect is strong.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with 19 players. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the group pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them all for private use (contribute 0). Every coin left in the pot is multiplied by 11.5 and then split equally among all players; that multiplication means each coin contributed returns only ~0.605 coins to its contributor (11.5/19), so individually people gain by withdrawing (free-riding) but the group gains if many contribute. After the public-good returns are distributed, players may pay a cost (2 coins per punishment unit) to deduct 3 coins per unit from specific other players — a targeted, costly sanction that can sustain cooperation but itself is costly and therefore used sparingly.

Typical dynamics and uncertainties to expect here:
- Early rounds: many players are uncertain about others. Some will contribute out of pro-social preference or conditional cooperation; others will free-ride. Observed punishment in early rounds can rapidly increase cooperation because it raises the expected cost of free-riding.
- Punishment use is usually sparse and targeted (a few cooperators sanction a few persistent defectors). Because punishers pay a cost and punishments are anonymous to targets, senders tend to use just enough punishment to change behavior rather than punish many players heavily.
- Over multiple rounds, a pattern often emerges: a core of contributors who punish occasional defectors, defectors who experience punishment will often switch to contributing, and overall contribution rates may rise. However, some stubborn free-riders may persist for several rounds and draw intermittent punishment.
- Uncertainty: players do not know how many rounds remain, so they cannot time a last-round free-ride. This tends to support longer-run cooperation if punishment deters defection.

Below I simulate a plausible 10-round trajectory: an initially mixed outcome with sparse punishment that pushes the group toward near-universal contribution by the middle rounds, and then stable cooperation.

This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with 19 players. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the group pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them all for private use (contribute 0). Every coin left in the pot is multiplied by 11.5 and then split equally among all players; that multiplication means each coin contributed returns only ~0.605 coins to its contributor (11.5/19), so individually people gain by withdrawing (free-riding) but the group gains if many contribute. After the public-good returns are distributed, players may pay a cost (2 coins per punishment unit) to deduct 3 coins per unit from specific other players — a targeted, costly sanction that can sustain cooperation but itself is costly and therefore used sparingly.

Typical dynamics and uncertainties to expect here:
- Early rounds: many players are uncertain about others. Some will contribute out of pro-social preference or conditional cooperation; others will free-ride. Observed punishment in early rounds can rapidly increase cooperation because it raises the expected cost of free-riding.
- Punishment use is usually sparse and targeted (a few cooperators sanction a few persistent defectors). Because punishers pay a cost and punishments are anonymous to targets, senders tend to use just enough punishment to change behavior rather than punish many players heavily.
- Over multiple rounds, a pattern often emerges: a core of contributors who punish occasional defectors, defectors who experience punishment will often switch to contributing, and overall contribution rates may rise. However, some stubborn free-riders may persist for several rounds and draw intermittent punishment.
- Uncertainty: players do not know how many rounds remain, so they cannot time a last-round free-ride. This tends to support longer-run cooperation if punishment deters defection.

Below I simulate a plausible 10-round trajectory: an initially mixed outcome with sparse punishment that pushes the group toward near-universal contribution by the middle rounds, and then stable cooperation.

Seeing continued sanctions, a couple more players switch to contributing. A small set of cooperators escalate slightly against persistent defectors to increase deterrence.

Heavy-targeted punishment of some repeat defectors leads several to switch; a few stubborn free-riders remain and draw occasional sanctions. Punishment stays concentrated on the persistent noncontributors.

Most players now contribute; a handful of holdouts still free-ride. Cooperators punish sparingly — usually single-unit sanctions — to target remaining defectors rather than exhaustively punishing everyone.

Some of the punished remaining defectors switch; persistent defection continues for a few players, but the overall level of contribution is high. Punishment is used only against those who repeatedly defect.
Peer pressure and prior punishments reduce the number of defectors further. Punishment continues to be sparse and focused: a couple of cooperators send small sanctions to holdouts.

With repeated sanctions many remaining defectors give up free-riding and start contributing. Only a couple of stubborn players remain and draw a brief wave of punishment intended to bring them into line.

Punishment has been an effective deterrent; by this point almost everyone contributes. Once near-unanimous contribution is established, punishment drops because there are few targets and senders avoid unnecessary cost.

game_explanation:
This is a classic public goods game with a strong individual incentive to withdraw the shared pot (take 20) each round: anyone who withdraws keeps 20 privately and still receives their share of the multiplied public pot. The public return per contributing player is large (20 × 11.5 = 230), but that total is divided among all participants. Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20) and choices are simultaneous and anonymous, free-riding is individually dominant: a player who expects others to contribute still gets more by withdrawing 20 themselves. At the same time, some players (older participants, postgraduate-educated, or those portrayed as more prosocial in the personas) may be predisposed to contribute early on — they act as conditional cooperators or altruists. Because players see only the net payoffs (not messages) at the summary stage, they can infer whether others contributed (contributors and withdrawers will typically have distinct payoff levels), and adjust in later rounds.

Uncertainty factors:
- Players do not know how many rounds there will be, which can encourage cooperation early (hopes of reciprocity) but also reduce endgame certainty.
- Because net payoffs give indirect signals, players can update beliefs but cannot perfectly observe intentions.
- Heterogeneous predispositions (some persist in contributing despite being exploited; others quickly switch to withdrawing) produce oscillations or a gradual collapse of cooperation.

Predicted broad dynamics: a modest initial level of contributions (a set of altruists/conditional cooperators) followed by rapid decline as those contributors see they earn less than withdrawers. A few persistent cooperators may briefly keep contributions nonzero, but absent binding commitments or communication, contributions trend down and often end near zero. Occasional experiments (players trying to restart cooperation) can produce small revivals mid-game.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players and a binary contribution choice each round: either contribute the full 20 coins to the shared pot or keep all 20 privately (so contributions are 0 or 20). The shared pot (sum of all contributions) is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all active players. Because payoffs shown at the end of each round reveal each player's net payoff, observers can infer who contributed: contributors receive only the shared share, defectors receive that same share plus 20 private coins. That observability makes conditional (reciprocal) strategies possible: some players will cooperate if others do, and punish (defect) if many others defect.

Incentives:
- A single contribution increases the total group payoff substantially (everyone benefits), but each contributor is individually strictly better off withholding the 20 (free-riding) because defecting always yields 20 more than contributing in the same round.
- Thus the social optimum is many contributors, but individual incentive is to defect. Typical dynamics: initial moderate cooperation from more pro-social or optimistic players, then a drift toward more defection as free-riding pays off unless enough players are committed to reciprocity. Because players see payoffs including peers' payoffs each round, conditional cooperators can respond: sustained cooperation is possible but fragile without coordination.

Uncertainties:
- Players do not know the total number of rounds; beliefs about whether the play will continue can affect contributions (e.g., some might try to build cooperation over time).
- Individual dispositions (from the provided personas) suggest some players are more likely to start cooperating (those with higher education or declared pro-social leaning) and some are more likely to free-ride. But these are soft priors: behavior can change in reaction to observed outcomes.

Predicted overall pattern: moderate initial cooperation by several pro-social players, a partial decline as defectors exploit the situation, occasional attempts to reestablish cooperation by a small core of conditional cooperators. Over ten rounds we expect fluctuation with a general downward tendency in the number of contributors, punctuated by brief recoveries when a small cooperative core attempts to re-start contributions.

Because contributions will be visible via payoffs at the end of the round, this first outcome will strongly shape expectations about others' conditionality.

Some conditional cooperators (who saw enough contributors) may continue contributing; others who preferred the safe private payoff will defect. Expect a drop in contributors as a few initial cooperators switch to free-riding.

The reduction in round 2 confirms to many that defecting is popular and profitable, so more conditional cooperators reduce contributions. Those who remain contributing are likely committed cooperators or trying to sustain a cooperative core.

With cooperation reduced further, many players prefer to defect. A small core of persistent cooperators may remain (they may be motivated by fairness, long-term strategy, or optimism that cooperation can be rebuilt), but overall contributions are likely low.

The modest success of round 6 encourages a few previously defecting players to return to contributing (hoping to capture the larger shared share). This can temporarily boost contributions more noticeably. But because defecting still yields +20 relative to contributing in the same share, the boost may be short-lived.

Approaching the later rounds without knowing the end, a few players may try once more to rebuild cooperation; some conditional cooperators respond to the previous small cores and rejoin. Expect a small uptick from the persistent core.

As the sequence advances without guaranteed continuation, the tension between private incentive and collective benefit persists. Some players stick with the small cooperative core; others defect, and in many such sessions the last observable rounds show reduced cooperation as free-riding dominates. Expect a relatively low number of contributors in this round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated-style public goods game with 19 players. Each round every player faces a binary decision: withdraw all 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leave all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20). The total shared contribution is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally across all players. Because the private return on a contributed coin (11.5/19 ≈ 0.575) is less than 1, a purely self-interested player gains more by withdrawing (free-riding) in any single round. The social optimum — everyone leaving their 20 in the pot — would produce large collective returns, but individual incentives favor withdrawing.

Uncertainties and strategic considerations here:
- Players do not know how many rounds remain, so early rounds often see more cooperation (hope of future reciprocity) than would a one-shot game.
- Players cannot communicate, but at the end of each round everyone sees each player’s net payoff for that round. Because a contributor’s payoff is exactly 20 less than a withdrawer’s payoff (same shared share, withdrawer also keeps 20), players can infer who contributed from the payoffs shown.
- Given that information, many players behave as conditional cooperators: they start with some cooperation, then switch to withdrawing if they see too many free-riders. A few players may remain steadfast cooperators for moral or heuristic reasons (preferences or higher social orientation).
- Over rounds we should expect an initial level of cooperation followed by decline as free-riding rewards become clear; occasional experimental returns to contribution are possible but fragile without communication.

Below I simulate a plausible unfolding over ten rounds using the provided personas as priors for likely tendencies (some players more likely to persist in cooperation, others more likely to defect). The transcript shows each round’s expected dynamics, the contribution choices (0 or 20), and the round summary everyone sees (net payoff per player).

game_explanation:
This is a typical public goods game with 18 players, where contributing 20 coins to the shared pot is socially beneficial but personally costly: each coin contributed returns only 11.5/18 ≈ 0.639 coins to the contributor, so the one-shot incentive favors withdrawing (free-riding). Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there is no guaranteed end‑game to force universal defection; that uncertainty often supports some cooperation early on. Punishment is available: each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target. Punishment is costly and anonymous (players cannot identify who punished them), so it is usually used sparingly and mostly by those who care about enforcing cooperation rather than by widespread retaliation.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: a minority of players contribute while many free-ride. Cooperators will sometimes use one or two punishment units to sanction visible defectors; because punishment is anonymous, defectors can't target punishers directly, but may respond by punishing randomly in later rounds.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is used effectively and not too costly, more players will switch to contributing to avoid being punished. Cooperation typically rises but rarely reaches full cooperation.
- Later rounds: cooperation may plateau or slowly erode as the costs of punishment and occasional retaliatory or spiteful punishments reduce net gains. Because the number of rounds is unknown, there may be a modest decline but not a sharp end-game collapse.

This is a one-pot public goods game with a binary contribution choice each round: each player either leaves all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and evenly split, so an individual who contributes receives back less than they kept privately (per-capita return from a contributed 20 is 20*11.5/19 ≈ 12.1), creating a clear individual incentive to withdraw (defect) even though group payoff is maximized by everyone contributing.

Because punishment is available (each punishment unit costs 2 to the sender and removes 3 from the target), we expect the following typical dynamics:
- Early rounds: mixed behavior. Some players (often those with stronger pro-social priors or higher education in these personas) contribute, while others free-ride. Punishment will be used sparingly at first to signal that defection can be costly.
- Mid-game: if punishers are willing to pay the cost, cooperation tends to increase as defectors are selectively punished. Punishment is usually targeted and low in intensity (many 1-unit punishments rather than heavy attacks) because punishment is costly to the punisher.
- Later rounds: behavior stabilizes around a higher cooperation rate if punishers keep punishing occasional defectors; if punishment is sporadic or punishing players tire, some defection can persist. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we do not expect a sharp end-game collapse, but some fatigue can reduce punishment frequency later on.
- Uncertainties: identities of punishers are not revealed, which weakens precise retaliation/reciprocity and encourages more generalized norms (punishers are anonymous). Some defectors may retaliate indiscriminately because they cannot identify their punishers. Overall, punishments will be few and mostly single-unit; cooperation will rise gradually and then plateau with a small persistent set of defectors.

Occasional lapses occur: a normally cooperative player experiments with defection or yields to temptation. Cooperators respond with measured punishment to discourage that behavior.
After being punished, the experimenting player returns to contributing. Cooperators continue to target the persistent defectors (especially those who never adjusted).
Players do not know this is the final round, so behavior remains in line with established norms. Punishments are focused on the remaining defectors; intensity stays low.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 19 players. Each round every player decides whether to keep the 20 coins privately (withdraw them → contribution 0) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The total contributed is multiplied by 11.5 and then split equally among all 19 players. A unilateral defection (withdrawing 20) gives the defector an immediate private gain of 20 while reducing the public pot, so defecting always gives a higher individual payoff in a single round when others' contributions are fixed: the personal return from contributing one’s 20 is the added share from the multiplied pot, which is 230/19 ≈ 12.11 per additional contributor — less than the 20 you could keep privately. That creates a social dilemma: group welfare is maximized if many contribute, but individual incentives favor withdrawing.

Players cannot see others' current decisions before choosing, but after each round everyone sees each player's net payoff for that round. From those payoffs players can infer (imperfectly) whether peers likely contributed, because contributors will have lower round payoffs than defectors given the same total contributed. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect an initial openness to cooperation by some players (uncertainty, hope of reciprocity) followed by a drift toward withdrawal as people detect that withdrawers earn more that round and reciprocation is limited. Some players may persist in contributing out of other-regarding preferences or experimentation, causing occasional temporary cooperation spikes; overall cooperation is likely to decline across rounds with occasional brief recoveries.

predicted_rounds.explanation: No prior feedback yet. Some players start cooperatively (hoping others will reciprocate or because they prefer group gains); others defect immediately to secure the private 20. The large multiplier means the total group return can be big if many cooperate, but the individual incentive to withdraw is still stronger. I predict a majority leaning toward contributing but a significant minority withdrawing in round 1.

Players see round 1 payoffs: withdrawers earned ~165.26 while contributors earned ~145.26. Selfish incentive is visible: withdrawing pays ~20 more. Many conditional cooperators will switch to withdrawing; some persistent cooperators (or experimenters) may continue to contribute. I predict a clear drop in contributors.

The visible pattern (withdrawers earn more per round) pushes more players to withdraw. A small core of persistent cooperators may remain motivated by norms or testing whether cooperation can be rebuilt. Expect only a few contributors.

Contributors see smaller payoffs relative to withdrawers again; many who had been contributing may stop. A few players who value cooperation or test strategies might still contribute; but it is likely nearly everyone withdraws. I predict a single persistent contributor this round.

Seeing that even lone contributors earn much less than withdrawers, the remaining cooperators are likely to switch to withdrawing. It's typical for cooperation to collapse toward all-withdraw in a finite repeated setting when people follow selfish incentives or retaliate. Expect everyone to withdraw this round.

After an all-withdraw round, a few players may experiment to see if cooperation can be re-established; a couple of persistent cooperators may try contributing again. I predict a small experiment: two players contribute, others mostly withdraw.

The small experiment shows contributors still earn less than withdrawers in that round, so many will revert to withdrawing. A single experimenter may try again but widespread cooperation is unlikely. I predict one contributor.
Given repeated evidence that withdrawing yields a higher per-round payoff and limited coordination, most players will stick with withdrawing. Expect another all-withdraw round.

Near the late middle of the session, a couple of players may test whether a small cooperative cluster can be formed, particularly those who previously cooperated occasionally. Expect a two-person contribution experiment.

If this were a game with an unknown horizon, many players will conservatively withdraw in late rounds after observing the persistent advantage of withdrawing in prior rounds. Expect everyone to withdraw in the final round, as free-riding is the dominant safe strategy when cooperation has repeatedly failed to be sustained.

game_explanation:
This is a large-group public-goods game with 19 players per round. Each player effectively chooses to contribute their whole endowment (20) to the group pot or to withdraw it (contribute 0). The group pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all players; with 19 players that means each player receives (11.5 * 20 * k) / 19 = (230 * k) / 19 from the pot when k players contributed. A contributor's private payoff from the public good is -20 + (230*k)/19 (k includes themselves); a non-contributor receives (230*k)/19 without paying 20. That creates a strong private incentive to free-ride when others contribute, but the group benefit increases with more contributors.

Punishment is the only costly sanction available: each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and reduces the target's payoff by 3 coins. Punishment is anonymous (players don't know who punished them), and players do not know the total number of rounds in advance. These features create uncertainty: early rounds often see experimentation and norm establishment; mid-game can move toward high cooperation if defectors are deterred; the unknown horizon can dampen final-round unraveling but a last-round drop in cooperation is still common.

Given these incentives and the personas (which provide weak priors about cooperativeness), I expect an initial mix of cooperation and defection, a period of increasing cooperation as a few players punish defectors sparsely, and then occasional lapses (especially toward the end). Punishment will be used sparingly: typically 1-unit sanctions targeted at persistent or lone defectors rather than heavy, repeated punishment.

Punished defectors are likely to revise toward cooperation to avoid continued penalties; some initial cooperators stay cooperative. A small set of remaining defectors may attract a few more 1-unit sanctions.

game_explanation: This is a repeated, simultaneous public-goods game with 19 players. Each round every player chooses either to leave all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or to withdraw all 20 for private use (contribute 0). The shared pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among the 19 players. Individually, contributing is costly: a single 20-coin contribution returns only (20*11.5)/19 ≈ 12.11 coins to the contributor, so a unilateral contributor loses about 7.89 coins compared with withholding. That makes defection the dominant strategy in a one-shot game. But in a repeated (unknown-length) setting, some players with social preferences or conditional-cooperator priors may contribute early, hoping others will too; seeing higher payoffs for defectors tends to cause conditional cooperators to abandon contribution in later rounds. Because choices are simultaneous each round and players only learn round payoffs (their own and peers'), dynamics will typically show some initial cooperation by altruistic or uncertain players, followed by a decline in contributions as free-riding is observed. With the high multiplier, public returns can be large if many contribute, which creates a tension between group benefit and individual incentive to defect. Below I simulate one plausible unfolding across ten rounds, using the provided personas as priors for who is likely to persist in contributing versus who is likelier to free-ride.

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round: players are uncertain about others. Some players (postgraduate-educated and those who appear more prosocial in the priors) may try contributing to test reciprocity; others defect immediately. No one sees contemporaneous choices, only the next-round summaries, so expect a noticeable but not majority level of cooperation.

Players see round 1 payoffs: defectors earned ~116.84 while contributors earned ~96.84. This payoff gap encourages more defection from conditional cooperators. A smaller core of contributors (the more persistent altruists/conditional cooperators) may continue to contribute, but at a reduced rate.

The pattern from round 2 reinforces defection's short-term advantage (defectors earn ~80.53 vs contributors ~60.53). Fewer players remain willing to risk contributing. A couple of persistent contributors may continue out of intrinsic motivation; a small number of conditional cooperators might still test cooperation again.

The visible advantage to defecting persists (defectors would earn ~44.21 vs contributors ~24.21 if two contribute). Most conditional cooperators have switched to defecting; only a couple of steadfast contributors remain.

After several rounds where defectors consistently earn more, most contributors stop contributing. One particularly persistent cooperator (e.g., a very prosocial persona) may still try a single-person contribution, but their action is unlikely to be rewarded by reciprocity.

Seeing that single-person contributions are dominated by withholding, many (or all) players switch to the individually dominant strategy of defecting. With k = 0, everyone simply keeps 20; this is a stable outcome in finite or indefinite repeated play when reciprocity breaks down.

Once everyone sees a round of universal defection and receives 20 each, there's little incentive to reintroduce contribution absent external changes or strong altruism. The group is likely to remain in the all-defect equilibrium.

With repeated evidence of defection being individually better, the equilibrium of zero contributions persists. Any attempt to restart cooperation is unlikely unless players care strongly about future reciprocity beyond the observed history.
No new information appears to change incentives; universal defection continues to be the stable outcome in this simulated path.

By round 10, the group has settled into the individually rational pattern of withholding. Unless players have unobserved long-term social preferences or expect outside enforcement, contributions remain at zero.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players. Each round every player either keeps 20 coins privately (defects) or leaves those 20 in the pot (contributes). The total contributed coins are multiplied by 11.5 and divided equally among all 19 players. Individually, withdrawing 20 always gives a higher immediate private return than contributing: each contributed coin returns only 11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 to the contributor, so rational self-interest pushes toward withdrawing (defecting). However, group welfare is higher when players contribute; conditional cooperators and people with stronger pro-social priors can start by contributing.

Key dynamic features and uncertainties:
- Learning and inference are strong: after each round everyone sees each player's net payoff, so players can infer exactly who contributed (contributors earn 40 coins less than defectors given the same total number of contributors). That transparent feedback accelerates coordination — typically toward defection if many see free-riding.
- Because the horizon is unknown, there is no clear final-round trigger (but players still form beliefs about how long cooperation can last). Expect some initial cooperation from conditional or pro-social players, followed by a decline as defectors earn systematically more each round and others react.
- Some players (based on persona priors) are more likely to be persistent cooperators (e.g., DUCK, DOG, ELEPHANT, WHALE), while others (e.g., SLOTH) are more likely to defect immediately. The visible payoff feedback will likely produce a cascade: early cooperation erodes once contributors see consistent payoff disadvantage.

Prediction summary: modest cooperation in the first round from a cluster of pro-social or conditional cooperators, a clear gap in payoffs (defectors earn ~40 more) that causes several cooperators to switch in round 2–3, and a steady decline to near-universal defection by the mid rounds. A few stubborn cooperators may linger briefly, but the game will likely reach all-defect by the late rounds.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players enter blind to others' choices. Others (SLOTH, GORILLA, RABBIT, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, PINGUIN, FROG, HORSE) prefer the safe private withdrawal. No one has yet seen payoff feedback, so cooperation is plausible but fragile.

After DUCK's solitary loss in round 5, even persistent cooperators abandon contributing. By now, the observable pattern strongly favors defecting; expect everyone to withdraw privately from here on.
With everyone defecting in round 6 and no communication channel, there's no incentive to deviate. Expect all-defect to continue.
No new information to incentivize cooperation. The all-defect equilibrium persists.
Uniform defection is now the established pattern; no incentives to change absent outside signals.

With ten rounds completed and no cooperation resurgence, the group remains in the all-defect equilibrium. If the game were to continue longer, a rare experimenter or an attempt at a coordinated restart might appear, but absent communication this is unlikely.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public‑goods setting with 19 active players per round. Each round starts with a 20‑coin pot that each player can either leave intact (cooperate: contribution = 20) or withdraw entirely for private use (defect: contribution = 0). The total of all contributions is multiplied by 11.5 and divided equally among the 19 players. That means:
- If you withdraw (defect) you immediately keep 20 private coins plus your equal share of the multiplied public pot.
- If you leave the pot (cooperate) you get no private coins but still receive your equal share of the multiplied public pot.

Incentives and likely dynamics
- Individual incentive: with the public share given to everyone regardless of their action, withdrawing 20 strictly increases a player's private take while reducing the group total — so defection is individually profitable in the short term.
- Collective incentive: if many players leave the pot, the multiplied public pot yields large shares for everyone; full cooperation is socially optimal.
- Learning and uncertainty: players do not know how many rounds remain, cannot communicate, and do not see others' choices before deciding. They only observe round summaries with net payoffs (their own and peers’). From those payoffs, players can infer whether many others cooperated and adjust behavior over time.
- Typical pattern: initial rounds often show moderate cooperation (some players try to benefit the group or test others). Observing that defectors receive higher net payoffs tends to trigger more defections in later rounds — contribution rates commonly decline across rounds toward universal defection.

Using the given persona priors as soft guides: players with postgraduate education are slightly more likely (but not guaranteed) to sustain cooperation longer; more “slothful” or less invested players are more likely to defect early. Because there is no communication and the horizon is unknown, expect cooperation to erode across rounds as players respond to payoff evidence.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start with no direct information about others' behavior; some try cooperating (especially those whose priors suggest higher pro‑social tendencies) while others defect immediately to secure the private 20. Early cooperative experiments are plausible from several postgraduate profiles; others are cautious or opportunistic, leading to a mixed outcome.

Players compare round‑1 net payoffs. Defectors (who kept 20) earned more than cooperators, which many interpret as a signal that defection is profitable. Some prior cooperators switch to defect; a core of more consistently pro‑social players remains contributing to test whether cooperation can be sustained.

The pattern is clearer: defectors continue to earn more. Several more players defect. A smaller subset — often those with stronger pro‑social priors — continues cooperating, either out of intrinsic motivation or to see whether cooperation can recover.

By now the higher payoff to defectors is obvious. Most players switch to defect. A small loyal set of contributors (often those with strong pro‑social priors) may persist, but their presence produces only modest shared returns.

Some players briefly test returning to cooperation (hoping to get a higher group payoff if enough others do the same), producing a small temporary uptick in contributions. But defections remain common because defecting still yields higher individual payoffs in these mixed groups.
Seeing that defectors again earned more in the previous round, most players revert to defection. The small cooperative minority remains but contributes little to overall returns.

Cooperation continues to shrink as players react to the pattern of payoffs — only a couple of consistently pro‑social players persist, but with diminishing returns the dominant strategy (and experienced outcome) becomes defection for most.
Only a tiny cooperative core remains. With contributions so low, everyone’s public share is small — defecting still gives a higher private payoff, reinforcing the defection trend.
Cooperation is now nearly extinct; only one player continues to contribute, perhaps out of principle or error. The public share is negligible, and defectors enjoy noticeably higher net payoffs.

By this stage (and with no coordination and an unknown horizon), almost everyone chooses the individually dominant option: withdraw the 20. With no contributions, the public pot disappears and each player's payoff equals the private 20.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits a clear individual incentive to withdraw the whole 20 coins (free-ride) against a collective gain if many players leave their 20 in the shared pot. Each contribution of 20 is multiplied by 11.5 (so each contributing coin effectively becomes 230 total coins split across the 19 players, ≈12.11 per person per contribution), which means a contributor gets back less than they put in—so selfishly there's always pressure to withdraw. Punishment allows cooperators to impose a cost on defectors (each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target), so punishment can sustain cooperation but is itself costly and noisy (players don't see who punished them).

Given these incentives and the player personas, I expect:
- Round 1: a mixed start — several experienced/educated or reliably cooperative personas contribute, while others free-ride out of uncertainty.
- Early punishment will be targeted at clear free-riders; because punishment is visible only in net outcomes (not who did it), some punished defectors will be deterred in following rounds, producing a bump in cooperation.
- Punishers pay a cost; over time some punishers reduce activity, and a small fraction of players drift back to free-riding, producing fluctuations rather than monotonic dynamics.
- Punishment is used sparingly (typical lab behavior) and mostly directed at obvious defectors; occasional antisocial or mistaken punishment is possible but rare in this simulation.
- Without a known end to the game, conditional cooperation and occasional sanctions produce wave-like cooperation: an early rise after initial sanctions, then some erosion as sanctioning costs accumulate, and a modest recovery or stabilization as norms reassert or some players return to contributing.

Punishment in the previous round deters additional defectors; many who saw deductions or expect enforcement are more likely to contribute. Only a few players still withdraw. Punishment focuses on the remaining outliers.

Some players tire of the cost of contributing or try to exploit the higher contribution level; a few previously contributing players revert to withdrawing. Punishment responds to those backsliding defections, but senders are selective because punishment is costly.

Punishment from Round 4 deters some of the new defectors, but a few others keep withdrawing. Punishment remains targeted at persistent defectors; punishing is somewhat reduced as senders feel the cost.

Cooperation falls further as some players stop contributing; the pool of punishers narrows (punishment is costly), so enforcement weakens and defecting looks profitable for some. A couple of focused punishments target long-term defectors.

Some players who briefly withdrew return to contributing after seeing targeted sanctions and relative payoffs; others remain defectors. Punishment is selective and often aimed at recurring free-riders.
Cooperation holds for a core of players; a handful of others cycle in and out. Punishment is used to discourage opportunistic withdrawals but senders are careful because each sanction costs them.
A modest rebound in contributions occurs as some defectors return; persistent defectors still attract focused punishment. Overall pattern is fluctuating cooperation rather than collapse.

Players still don't know when the game will end. Some try to re-establish cooperation; others exploit momentary lax enforcement. Punishment continues to be used sparingly to signal costs of persistent free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with binary contribution choices (each player either contributes the whole 20 coins to the public pot or withdraws it for private use). The public pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all players, so cooperating (contributing 20) generates a large group benefit but is individually exploitable: if everyone else contributes, a single defector gets a higher immediate private payoff than a cooperator. Because players do not know how many rounds there will be, there is no guaranteed end-game unraveling; behavior likely evolves through learning and informal enforcement.

Key dynamics and uncertainties to expect:
- Early rounds: substantial uncertainty about others’ behavior. Many players with cooperative priors (higher education or those who value group outcomes) tend to contribute. Some players with less cooperative priors or who test the waters will defect (withdraw privately).
- Public-return calculation makes cooperation collectively attractive: as more players contribute, every player’s round payoff increases. But the individual incentive to free-ride can still lure some players.
- Punishment is the only enforcement mechanism. It is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and costly to the punished (3 coins lost per unit). Because punishment is anonymous (players cannot identify who specifically punished them), punishment typically is used sparingly and targeted at obvious defectors. Punishment often stabilizes cooperation over repeated rounds by deterring persistent free-riding.
- Given the player personas, expect a cluster of steady cooperators (many postgraduates and college-educated players) and a smaller cluster of sporadic defectors (some high-school/some-college backgrounds). Punishment will be modest and concentrated on persistent defectors; after a few rounds many defectors tend to switch to contributing.
- Because players see peers’ round summaries (their net payoff, how much they spent on punishment, and how much punishment they received), they can infer who is enforcing norms and who is freeriding at the aggregate level. That will nudge behavior toward more cooperation over rounds, with occasional deviations and a small number of punitive acts.
reflection:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with binary contribution choices (each player either contributes the whole 20 coins to the public pot or withdraws it for private use). The public pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all players, so cooperating (contributing 20) generates a large group benefit but is individually exploitable: if everyone else contributes, a single defector gets a higher immediate private payoff than a cooperator. Because players do not know how many rounds there will be, there is no guaranteed end-game unraveling; behavior likely evolves through learning and informal enforcement.

Key dynamics and uncertainties to expect:
- Early rounds: substantial uncertainty about others’ behavior. Many players with cooperative priors (higher education or those who value group outcomes) tend to contribute. Some players with less cooperative priors or who test the waters will defect (withdraw privately).
- Public-return calculation makes cooperation collectively attractive: as more players contribute, every player’s round payoff increases. But the individual incentive to free-ride can still lure some players.
- Punishment is the only enforcement mechanism. It is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and costly to the punished (3 coins lost per unit). Because punishment is anonymous (players cannot identify who specifically punished them), punishment typically is used sparingly and targeted at obvious defectors. Punishment often stabilizes cooperation over repeated rounds by deterring persistent free-riding.
- Given the player personas, expect a cluster of steady cooperators (many postgraduates and college-educated players) and a smaller cluster of sporadic defectors (some high-school/some-college backgrounds). Punishment will be modest and concentrated on persistent defectors; after a few rounds many defectors tend to switch to contributing.
- Because players see peers’ round summaries (their net payoff, how much they spent on punishment, and how much punishment they received), they can infer who is enforcing norms and who is freeriding at the aggregate level. That will nudge behavior toward more cooperation over rounds, with occasional deviations and a small number of punitive acts.

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round with maximum uncertainty. A set of players who lean cooperative will contribute immediately; a handful will test by defecting. Early punishments will be exploratory and small — targeted at clear defectors to signal that free-riding may be costly.
Some defectors punished in round 1 respond by switching to contribute (learning from cost signals); a few continue to defect. Punishment remains sparse, concentrated on the remaining noncontributors.

Punishment in earlier rounds leads more tentative defectors to contribute; a few stubborn defectors persist and draw some targeted punishment. Punishment units remain small (1 unit) except where repeated offenses occur.
As cooperation becomes the norm for most, remaining defectors face somewhat stronger punishment to encourage compliance. A small uptick in punishment intensity may appear toward persistent defectors.

Most defectors have been deterred; only one or two players resist switching. Punishment continues but is limited — mostly single-unit responses to maintain incentives without over-investing in costly punishment.

With cooperation now common, a few opportunistic deviations can occur (testing whether others will still cooperate). If a regular cooperator defects unexpectedly, they are likely to be met with quick, modest punishments from visible enforcers.
After being punished, the occasional deviator commonly returns to cooperation; by now most players are cooperating and punishment is unnecessary. Expect a quiet round with universal contribution.
High cooperation persists. With few or no defectors, players conserve resources and refrain from punitive spending. This is a cooperative plateau.

Stable cooperation continues. Because players still do not know the experiment horizon, there is no predictable unwind; the norm remains to contribute and avoid costly punishment unless a defector appears.

Even late in the sequence, small deviations can occur. A last-minute tester or two may defect; that usually provokes modest punitive responses from established cooperators who want to protect the public good, but punishment remains limited because it is individually costly.
